BD-PB-BADSHIFT.title: Avoid incorrect shift operations [BD-PB-BADSHIFT]
BD-PB-BADSHIFT.description:
This rule detects incorrect shift operations. This rule is dependent on several parameterizable
conditions.
BD-PB-BADSHIFT.parameters:
"Report violation when negative value is shifted": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation when the left
operand of the shift operator is negative.

"Report violation when shifting by negative value": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation when the right
operand of the shift operator is negative.

"Report violation when shifting by too big value": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation when the right
operand of the shift operator is greater or equal to the number of bits in the left operand.

"Number of bits of the left hand side operand calculated based on its": This parameter determines
how Flow Analysis engine calculates the number of bits of the left operand. Possible options are:
* "Standard type"
* "MISRA C essential type"
* "MISRA C++ underlying type"

"Report violation when shifting will result in overflow": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation 
when the result of the shift operation will cause overflow.

"Report violation when shifting an unsigned value will result in overflow": Setting this parameter to true 
forces a violation when the result of the shift operation will cause overflow and the left operand is of unsigned type.
references: MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-8-1
MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 12.2
MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 4.1.3 There shall be no occurrence of undefined or critical unspecified behaviour
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 12.2 The right hand operand of a shift operator shall lie in the range zero to one less than the width in bits of the essential type of the left hand operand
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT34-C. Do not shift an expression by a negative number of bits or by greater than or equal to the number of bits that exist in the operand
JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.21 Operators, AV Rule 164
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
4.2.2 Ensure that data loss does not demonstrably occur in an integral expression
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-8-1. The right hand operand of a shift operator shall lie between zero and one less than the width in bits of the underlying type of the left hand operand

BD-PB-INFREC.title: Avoid infinite recursion [BD-PB-INFREC]
BD-PB-INFREC.description:
This rule detects infinitely recursive methods. A method is infinitely recursive when every
path that results from the method being entered leads to a recursive call. This rule also
detects indirect recursion.
references: CWE-674: Uncontrolled Recursion

CDD-DUPC.title: Avoid code duplication [CDD-DUPC]
CDD-DUPC.benefits:
Rule improves readability and maintainability. Simplifies and reduce code size.
Copying and pasting code will increase the cost of code maintenance because 
each time a modification is required, multiple instance of the same code 
will need to be identified and then modified. Reducing or eliminating the amount 
of duplicated code can prevent this problem.
CDD-DUPC.parameters:
This rule can be parameterized to achieve best results. 

* Minimal number of duplicated tokens (default value is 80) 
  This parameter value defines minimum size of code fragment which may be reported 
  by this rule.
  Variable names, string and numeric literals, keywords, operators are count as one token.   
  For example:
   
  if (param1 == 0) {   // 7 tokens
      return a;        // 3 tokens
  }                    // 1 token

"Ignore ..." parameters allows some differences to be ignored 
and reported as duplicated code:    

* Ignore string literals (default is off) 
  Turn it on to ignore string literals differences
     
* Ignore number literals (default is off) 
  Turn it on to ignore number literals differences
       
* Ignore boolean literals (default is off) 
  Turn it on to ignore boolean literals differences
     
* Ignore identifiers (local/global variables, function names, ...) (default is off) 
  Turn it on to ignore identifiers differences   

"Report code duplicate found" parameter allows to change scope of duplicated code findings:

* Anywhere in the test scope (default)
  All code duplicates in the test scope are reported

* In the same project
  Only code duplicates located in the same project are reported
  
* In the same file
  Only code duplicates located in the same file are reported
references: N/A

CERT_C-API00-a.title: The validity of parameters must be checked inside each function [CERT_C-API00-a]
CERT_C-API00-a.description:
"The validity of parameters must be checked inside each function."
The rule reports a violation if a parameter is not checked before the first
use inside function.
CERT_C-API00-a.benefits: The rule prevents use incorrect value of parameter.
references: 1. The Power of Ten - Rules for Developing Safety Critical Code.
Rule 7
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API00-C. Functions should validate their parameters

CERT_C-API00.title: CERT-API00_C (REC) Functions should validate their parameters [CERT_C-API00]

CERT_C-API01-a.title: Avoid overflow when writing to a buffer [CERT_C-API01-a]
CERT_C-API01-a.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-API01-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and it is an array
with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is enabled by default.

CERT_C-API01-b.title: Avoid using unsafe string functions which may cause buffer overflows [CERT_C-API01-b]
CERT_C-API01-b.description:
This rule detects code that uses unsafe string functions from C library:
strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets
According to David A. Wheeler (see reference below),
"C functions users must avoid using dangerous functions
that do not check bounds unless they've ensured
that the bounds will never get exceed.
Functions to avoid in most cases (or ensure protection) include the functions
strcpy(), strcat(), sprintf() (with cousin vsprintf()), and gets().
These should be replaced with functions such as
strncpy(), strncat(), snprintf(), fgets(), respectively."
See also; SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13, SECURITY-16
CERT_C-API01-b.benefits: Prevents the use of functions which may cause buffer overflows.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/dangers-c.html
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API01-C. Avoid laying out strings in memory directly before sensitive data
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API02-C. Functions that read or write to or from an array should take an
argument to specify the source or target size
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV01-C. Do not make assumptions about the size of an environment variable
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC24-C. Do not use deprecated or obsolescent functions
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR31-C. Guarantee that storage for strings has sufficient space for
character data and the null terminator

CERT_C-API01.title: CERT-API01_C (REC) Avoid laying out strings in memory directly before sensitive data [CERT_C-API01]

CERT_C-API02-b.title: Don't use unsafe C functions that do write to range-unchecked buffers [CERT_C-API02-b]
CERT_C-API02-b.description:
Don't use the unsafe C legacy: strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets,
strncpy, strncat, snprintf, fgets - functions that do write to range-unchecked
buffers, and/or do not check and correctly handle out-of-bounds errors.
See also: SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13,SECURITY-16
CERT_C-API02-b.benefits: Rule prevents using functions which may cause overflow.
CERT_C-API02-b.example_repair: Don't use the unsafe C legacy.

CERT_C-ARR01-a.title: Do not call 'sizeof' on a pointer type [CERT_C-ARR01-a]
CERT_C-ARR01-a.description:
Sizeof should not be used on a pointer type.
Using the pointer of the type as the argument of the sizeof operator
makes the sizeof operator return the size of pointer
(which is 4 in a 32-bit platform).
See also: MRM-45
CERT_C-ARR01-a.benefits:
Rule prevents situations in which instead of using the actual type as the
argument of the sizeof operator, the pointer of the type is mistakenly used.
CERT_C-ARR01-a.example_repair: Do not use sizeof on pointers.
references: 1. ISO/DIS 26262
point 8.4.4
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ARR01-C. Do not apply the sizeof operator to a pointer when taking the size
of an array

CERT_C-ARR01.title: CERT-ARR01_C (REC) Do not apply the sizeof operator to a pointer when taking the size of an array [CERT_C-ARR01]

CERT_C-ARR02-a.title: Explicitly specify array bounds in array declarations with initializers [CERT_C-ARR02-a]
CERT_C-ARR02-a.description:
"If an array of unknown size is initialized, its size is determined by the
largest indexed element with an explicit initializer." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]
"Although compilers can compute the size of an array on the basis of its
initialization list, explicitly specifying the size of the array provides
a redundancy check, ensuring that the array's size is correct. It also enables
compilers to emit warnings if the array's size is less than the size implied
by the initialization." [CERT-ARR02-C]

This rule detects when an array of unknown size is initialized with an explicit
aggregate initializer.

See also: CODSTA-197
CERT_C-ARR02-a.benefits:
This rule helps you to detect if the array's size is incorrect.
If the size of the array is explicitly specified and if the number
of initializers exceeds the number of elements to initialize,
then some compilers emit warnings or errors.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ARR02-C. Explicitly specify array bounds, even if implicitly defined by an
initializer
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.8 Initialization
3. ISO/IEC 14882:2003 Programming languages -- C++
8.5.1 Aggregates

CERT_C-ARR02.title: CERT-ARR02_C (REC) Explicitly specify array bounds, even if implicitly defined by an initializer [CERT_C-ARR02]

CERT_C-ARR30-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [CERT_C-ARR30-a]
CERT_C-ARR30-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
CERT_C-ARR30-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-ARR30-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
CERT_C-ARR30-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

CERT_C-ARR32-a.title: Ensure the size of the variable length array is in valid range [CERT_C-ARR32-a]
CERT_C-ARR32-a.description:
This rule detects declarations of variable-length arrays whose size argument may be
outside a valid range.
The rule triggers if the size argument can have a negative value, or it is not known
whether the size argument is limited by an upper bound.
CERT_C-ARR32-a.security_relevance:
"If the size argument supplied to a variable length array is not a positive integer value,
the behavior is undefined. Additionally, if the magnitude of the argument is excessive, the
program may behave in an unexpected way. An attacker may be able to leverage this behavior
to overwrite critical program data."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, ARR32-C. Ensure size arguments for variable length arrays are in a valid range]
CERT_C-ARR32-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent potential runtime errors caused by incorrect
memory allocation.
CERT_C-ARR32-a.parameters:
The "Report violation when upper size limit is set, but its value is unknown" parameter enables
reporting rule violation when there is an upper bound for the size argument, but the exact value
of the limit is not known when analyzing a given flow path.

This option is disabled by default.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ARR32-C. Ensure size arguments for variable length arrays are in a valid range
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MEM05-C. Avoid large stack allocations

CERT_C-ARR32.title: CERT-ARR32_C (RULE) Ensure size arguments for variable length arrays are in a valid range [CERT_C-ARR32]

CERT_C-ARR36-a.title: Do not subtract two pointers that do not address elements of the same array [CERT_C-ARR36-a]
CERT_C-ARR36-a.description:
This rule detects when two pointers that do not address elements of the same
array are subtracted.
CERT_C-ARR36-a.security_relevance:
Subtracting pointers that do not address elements of the same array may cause
undefined behavior.
CERT_C-ARR36-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior that may be caused by
subtracting pointers that do not address elements of the same array.
CERT_C-ARR36-a.parameters:
If "Aggressive mode" is enabled, the rule additionally reports violations 
in cases when Flow Analysis is unable to positively verify that the subtracted
pointers address elements of the same array and there is probability that
reported violations are false positives. Enabling this parameter results in better
bug detection, but it may increase the number of false alarms.
With this parameter disabled, the rule only triggers when Flow Analysis can
conclusively determine that the subtracted pointers do not address elements of
the same array.
references: AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

Rule M0-3-1. Minimization of run-time failures shall be ensured by the use of at least one of: (a) static analysis tools/techniques; (b) dynamic analysis tools/techniques; (c) explicit coding of checks to handle run-time faults
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
Rule M5-0-17. Subtraction between pointers shall only be applied to pointers that address elements of the same array

CERT_C-ARR36-b.title: Do not compare two unrelated pointers [CERT_C-ARR36-b]
CERT_C-ARR36-b.description:
This rule detects when two unrelated pointers are compared.

The rule assumes that two pointers are related if they store addresses of two elements
of the same array. Whether two pointers that point to two elements of the same structure
object are related or not depends on the rule parameterization (see PARAMETERS).
CERT_C-ARR36-b.security_relevance: Comparison of unrelated pointers may cause undefined behavior.
CERT_C-ARR36-b.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior that may be caused by
comparing unrelated pointers.
CERT_C-ARR36-b.parameters:
If the "Report when the pointers are compared using relational operators"
parameter is enabled, the rule detects when pointers are compared with
the following operators:
	operator>
	operator<
	operator>=
	operator<=
	
If the "Report when the pointers are compared using standard functions" parameter 
is enabled, the rule detects when pointers are compared with the following
operators:
	std::less()
	std::greater()
	std::less_equal()
	std::greater_equal()
	
If the "Treat pointers addressing fields of the same object as related"
parameter is enabled, the rule assumes that that two pointers that address
elements of the same structure object (for example, two fields of the same
object) are related.

If "Aggressive mode" is enabled, the rule additionally reports pointer
comparison in cases when Flow Analysis is unable to positively verify that
the compared pointers are related and there is probability that reported
violations are false positives. Enabling this parameter results in better
bug detection, but it may increase the number of false alarms. 
With this parameter disabled, the rule only triggers when Flow Analysis can
conclusively determine that the compared pointers are not related.
references: AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

CERT_C-ARR37-a.title: Pointer arithmetic shall not be applied to pointers that address variables of non-array type [CERT_C-ARR37-a]
CERT_C-ARR37-a.description:
"Pointer arithmetic shall only be applied to pointers that address an array or
array element.
Addition and subtraction of integers (including increment and decrement) from
pointers that do not point to an array or array element results in undefined
behaviour."
See also: MISRA2004-17_1
CERT_C-ARR37-a.benefits: Rule makes the code more readable and less confusing.

Chapter 6, Section 17
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard

CERT_C-ARR38-a.title: Avoid overflow when reading from a buffer [CERT_C-ARR38-a]
CERT_C-ARR38-a.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-ARR38-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and it is an array
with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is enabled by default.

CERT_C-ARR38-d.title: Avoid overflow due to reading a not zero terminated string [CERT_C-ARR38-d]

The rule detects cases of possible overflow due to running a string function on a string that
was not properly zero terminated.

The rule will report violation when the parameter passed to certain functions(see PARAMETERS section)
is not zero terminated. There are cases when such behavior may not lead to overflow or undefined
behavior(e.g. using 'snprintf' function with specific format and correct maximum number of bytes).

execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.

CERT_C-ARR39-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [CERT_C-ARR39-a]
CERT_C-ARR39-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
CERT_C-ARR39-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-ARR39-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
CERT_C-ARR39-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

CERT_C-ARR39-b.title: Pointer arithmetic should not be used [CERT_C-ARR39-b]
CERT_C-ARR39-b.description:
"This rule refers to explicitly calculating pointer values. Any such pointer
value then has the potential to access unintended or invalid memory addresses.
This is an area where there is a danger of serious errors occuring in the code
at run time. Pointers may go out of bounds of arrays or structures, or may even
point to effectively arbitrary locations."
See also: MISRA2004-17_1, MISRA2004-17_2, MISRA2004-17_4
CERT_C-ARR39-b.benefits: Prevents from access to unintended or invalid memory addresses.
CERT_C-ARR39-b.example_repair:
"If pointer arithmetic is necessary, then appropriate reviews and/or dynamic
checking code will be required to ensure that the resulting pointer can not
point to unintended or invalid memory addresses"
references: 1. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 101
2. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.28 Portable Code, AV Rule 215
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ARR39-C. Do not add or subtract a scaled integer to a pointer
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP08-C. Ensure pointer arithmetic is used correctly

CERT_C-ARR39-c.title: Do not add or subtract a scaled integer to a pointer [CERT_C-ARR39-c]
CERT_C-ARR39-c.description:
Adding or subtracting a scaled integer value to or from a pointer automatically
scales the size of the value to the size of the type of the referenced object.
This may yield a pointer that points to an invalid memory block.

This rule detects when an expression with the sizeof() operator is added or
subtracted to or from a pointer or array of a type other than character, except:
- the A + sizeof(B) / sizeof(C) idiom, where B is an array and the types A, B
  and C are the same types.
- expressions where sizeof is a divider.
CERT_C-ARR39-c.benefits: This rule helps you avoid using pointers that point to an invalid memory block.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ARR39-C. Do not add or subtract a scaled integer to a pointer
2. CWE-468: Incorrect Pointer Scaling

CERT_C-ARR39.title: CERT-ARR39_C (RULE) Do not add or subtract a scaled integer to a pointer [CERT_C-ARR39]

CERT_C-CON01-a.title: Do not abandon unreleased locks [CERT_C-CON01-a]
CERT_C-CON01-a.description:
This rule detects cases where a mutex is locked but not unlocked at an
appropriate place, which could cause an application deadlock.

The list of supported multithreading APIs can be found and modified in test
configuration "Flow Analysis Advanced Settings" -> "Multi-threading".
The rule will report a violation if one of the functions defined as 
"Functions for locking" is used without a proper use of a function defined
as "Functions for unlocking".
CERT_C-CON01-a.parameters:
The "Locked object may be unlocked from callers up to specified level" parameter
allows you to manage the depth of analysis of the call graph. Lower numbers are 
recommended to enforce the best practice that locking and unlocking occur at the
same nesting level. This practice is required by MISRA C/C++ & JSF standards.

Higher numbers of the parameter value may be preferred for (rare) cases where 
locking and unlocking have to be performed on different levels as well as in the
case where certain functions serve as wrappers to standard locking and unlocking
functions. The parameter of the value is set to 1  by default. This ensures the
detection of most bugs related to direct uses of standard locking functions
as well as the use of custom wrappers around them - without producing
unnecessary false positives.

To demonstrate this, let's assume the following wrapper functions:

void mutex_lock()
{
    pthread_mutex_lock(mutex);
    // do something else
}

void mutex_unlock()
{
    pthread_mutex_unlock(mutex);
    // do something else
}

With the value of parameter set to 0, Flow Analysis will see the call to
pthread_mutex_lock inside the wrapper function "mutex_lock" and no corresponding
call to pthread_mutex_unlock inside the function. Consequently, it will report a 
violation which is in fact a false positive because mutex_lock is not meant to 
call unlocking function by design and pthread_mutex_unlock is called from the 
other wrapper function named "mutex_unlock". Setting the depth to values higher
than 0 prevents false positives for such wrapper functions while still ensuring
that violations will be reported if the code doesn't call "mutex_unlock".
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.13
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.16

Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.16 All mutex objects locked by a thread shall be explicitly unlocked by the same thread

CERT_C-CON05-a.title: Do not use blocking functions while holding a lock [CERT_C-CON05-a]
CERT_C-CON05-a.description:
This rule detects cases where functions that cease thread execution for a certain time
(such as sleep()) are called from critical sections--thus unnecessarily increasing
resource contention between threads.
CERT_C-CON05-a.benefits:
Helps prevent unnecessary resource contention between threads as well as
thread starvation.
CERT_C-CON05-a.parameters:
The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON05-C. Do not perform operations that can block while holding a lock
SEI CERT C Coding Standard

CERT_C-CON05.title: CERT-CON05_C (REC) Do not perform operations that can block while holding a lock [CERT_C-CON05]

CERT_C-CON30-a.title: Ensure resources are freed [CERT_C-CON30-a]
CERT_C-CON30-a.description:
This rule helps to ensure that allocated resources are deallocated on all paths.
Violations of the rule identify execution paths in an application where resources
are leaked.
CERT_C-CON30-a.security_relevance:
Failure to close resources may lead to starvation, which could have
security implications.
CERT_C-CON30-a.benefits:
Having a strict policy with respect to closing resources helps to make an application more
robust by preventing resource starvation.
CERT_C-CON30-a.parameters:
The "Assume resource references can be stored by third-party methods of the following
types" set of parameters allows you to determine how strict the rule is when a reference
to a resource may be stored by a third-party function. For example, if there is a
third-party function SpecialCollection::add() and a resource is passed as a parameter
to this function, it will be stored in a collection and may later be closed by iterating
over the collection. Since Flow Analysis does not know the exact behavior of arbitrary
third-party functions, it behaves in either of the following ways:

   * With "Any functions" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that any third-party
     function may store a reference to a resource if the resource is passed into the function.
     This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource is later closed using
     the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are
     not reported as violations) if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
    
   * With "Functions that accept parameters which could host resource reference" parameter
     enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that a function may store a reference to a resource if
     it takes an additional parameter that can be used to store a pointer or a reference to
     the given resource. This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource
     is later closed using the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false
     negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations) if the function does not store
     a reference to the resource.
   
   * With "Functions with names matching the pattern" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis
     assumes that any third-party method which matches regex may store a reference to
     a resource if the resource is passed into the method. This prevents the rule from reporting
     false positives if the resource is later closed using the stored reference. However,
     this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations)
     if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
   
   * With all the parameters disabled, Flow Analysis takes a more aggressive approach and it
     assumes that third-party methods do not affect resources in any way. This approach
     may lead to some false positives, but it will not overlook any of the real leaks
     that may be missed with enabled parameters.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

CERT_C-CON31-a.title: Do not destroy another thread's mutex [CERT_C-CON31-a]
CERT_C-CON31-a.description:
This rule detects when a lock is destroyed without ensuring that all the threads that may be using
this lock have finished. 
The rule must be parameterized with a list of user-defined functions that are called to ensure
that the threads have finished. If the list is not provided, the rule will report a violation
each time a lock-destroying function is used in code.
CERT_C-CON31-a.security_relevance:
"Destroying a mutex while it is locked may result in invalid control flow and data corruption".
SEI CERT C Coding Standard,
CON31-C. Do not destroy a mutex while it is locked
CERT_C-CON31-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent data corruption and violation of data integrity, as well as maintain
proper control flow.
CERT_C-CON31-a.parameters:
The "Functions that ensure that all the threads have finished" parameter allows you to specify a list
of functions that can be used to check if all the threads that use a lock have finished. When one of these
functions is called before destroying a lock, a violation will not be reported.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON31-C. Do not destroy a mutex while it is locked,
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS48-C. Do not unlock or destroy another POSIX thread's mutex,
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON50-CPP. Do not destroy a mutex while it is locked

CERT_C-CON31-b.title: Do not use resources that have been freed [CERT_C-CON31-b]
CERT_C-CON31-b.description:
This rule detects uses of resources that have been freed. In particular, a violation is reported
when a pointer or reference to a freed resource is:

   * returned from a function,
   * passed as a parameter to a function,
   * used in an arithmetic operation,
   * dereferenced, or
   * assigned to a variable/field/array element.
CERT_C-CON31-b.benefits: Prevents strange, often non-deterministic errors related to the use of freed resources.
CERT_C-CON31-b.parameters:
The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to null" check box
unsupresses/suppresses  violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to a null
pointer. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to other pointers" check box
unsuppresses/suppresses violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to other
pointers. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

CERT_C-CON31-c.title: Do not free resources using invalid pointers [CERT_C-CON31-c]
CERT_C-CON31-c.description:
This rule detects attempts to free memory using an invalid pointer.
A violation is reported when the pointer used to free a resource meets any of
the following conditions:

   * It is a pointer to a string constant.
   * It is acquired as a result of a cast of an integer constant to pointer type (wild pointer).
   * It is acquired via an address operation applied to a parameter, local, global
     or static variable (attempt to free resource in non-heap memory).
   * It does not point to the beginning of the allocated chunk.
   * It is not allocated pointer.

CERT_C-CON32-a.title: Use locks to prevent race conditions when modifying bit fields [CERT_C-CON32-a]
CERT_C-CON32-a.description:
When multiple threads access or modify a shared variable, they may inadvertently
access other variables that are adjacent in memory. This rule detects when a bit field
that has another bit field adjacent in the same byte is accessed outside of the critical
section.
CERT_C-CON32-a.benefits: Helps you prevent data corruption due to unintended modifications of adjacent bit fields.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON32-C. Prevent data races when accessing bit-fields from multiple threads
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS49-C. When data must be accessed by multiple threads, provide a mutex and guarantee no adjacent data is also accessed
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON52-CPP. Prevent data races when accessing bit-fields from multiple threads
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.

CERT_C-CON32.title: CERT-CON32_C (RULE) Prevent data races when accessing bit-fields from multiple threads [CERT_C-CON32]

CERT_C-CON33-a.title: Avoid using thread-unsafe functions [CERT_C-CON33-a]
CERT_C-CON33-a.description:
"Some C standard library functions are not guaranteed to be reentrant with
respect to threads. Multiple threads invoking the same function can cause
concurrency problems, which often result in abnormal behavior and can cause
more serious vulnerabilities, such as abnormal termination, denial-of-service
attack, and data integrity violations." [CERT-CON33-C]

This rule detects when a call to any of the following functions is used
or when the address of any of the following functions is taken:
asctime(), basename(), catgets(), crypt(), ctime(), dbm_clearerr(),
dbm_close(), dbm_delete(), dbm_error(), dbm_fetch(), dbm_firstkey(),
dbm_nextkey(), dbm_open(), dbm_store(), dirname(), dlerror(), drand48(),
encrypt(), endgrent(), endpwent(), endutxent(), ftw(), getdate(), getenv(),
getgrent(), getgrgid(), getgrnam(), gethostent(), getlogin(), getnetbyaddr(),
getnetbyname(), getnetent(), getopt(), getprotobyname(), getprotobynumber(),
getprotoent(), getpwent(), getpwnam(), getpwuid(), getservbyname(),
getservbyport(), getservent(), getutxent(), getutxid(), getutxline(), gmtime(),
hcreate(), hdestroy(), hsearch(), inet_ntoa(), l64a(), lgamma(), lgammaf(),
lgammal(), localeconv(), localtime(), lrand48(), mblen(), mbtowc(), mrand48(),
nftw(), nl_langinfo(), ptsname(), putenv(), pututxline(), rand(), readdir(),
setenv(), setgrent(), setkey(), setlocale(), setpwent(), setutxent(),
strerror(), strsignal(), strtok(), system(), ttyname(), unsetenv(), wctomb(),
srand(), getenv_s(), ATOMIC_VAR_INIT(), atomic_init(), tmpnam(), mbrtoc16(),
c16rtomb(), mbrtoc32(), c32rtomb().
CERT_C-CON33-a.benefits:
"Race conditions caused by multiple threads invoking the same library function
can lead to abnormal termination of the application, data integrity violations,
or a denial-of-service attack." [CERT-CON33-C]

This rule helps you avoid race conditions.

CERT_C-CON33.title: CERT-CON33_C (RULE) Avoid race conditions when using library functions [CERT_C-CON33]

CERT_C-CON34-a.title: Declare objects shared between POSIX threads with appropriate storage durations [CERT_C-CON34-a]
CERT_C-CON34-a.description:
This rule detects when objects are shared between POSIX threads without 
appropriate storage durations. Accessing the stack of a thread from another 
thread can cause invalid memory access, as the referenced stack frame may 
not be valid when the other thread is trying to access it. Regular shared variables
should be protected by a call to the pthread_join(pthread_t thread, void **retval)
method, which is a thread synchronization mechanism.
CERT_C-CON34-a.benefits: Helps you prevents invalid memory access.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON34-C. Declare objects shared between threads with appropriate storage durations
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS50-C. Declare objects shared between POSIX threads with appropriate storage durations

CERT_C-CON34.title: CERT-CON34_C (RULE) Declare objects shared between threads with appropriate storage durations [CERT_C-CON34]

CERT_C-CON35-a.title: Do not acquire locks in different order [CERT_C-CON35-a]
CERT_C-CON35-a.description:
This rule detects cases where two mutexes are locked in different orders, which
can cause a deadlock. The violation will be reported if there exist at least
two locks and at least two paths on which they are acquired in different orders.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.2
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.2 There shall be no deadlocks between threads
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON35-C. Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS51-C. Avoid deadlock with POSIX threads by locking in predefined order
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON53-CPP. Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order
CWE-833: Deadlock
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
18.3.2 Ensure that order of nesting of locks in a project forms a DAG

CERT_C-CON35.title: CERT-CON35_C (RULE) Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order [CERT_C-CON35]

CERT_C-CON36-a.title: Wrap functions that can spuriously wake up in a loop [CERT_C-CON36-a]
CERT_C-CON36-a.description:
Execution of threads are paused using appropriate functions. A waiting thread
resumes execution only after it has been notified that the condition predicate
is true. A condition predicate is an expression constructed from the variables
of a function that must be true for a thread to be allowed to continue
execution.

When functions that pause and resume threads are called inside an 'if' block
that checks the condition predicate, the condition predicate is checked only
after the notification is received. As a result, if the notification is
spurious or malicious, the thread would wake up prematurely. For this reason,
it is recommended to use a loop to ensure that the condition predicate is
checked both before and after the call.

This rule detects when one of the following functions is called outside of any
loop (while, do-while, for): cnd_wait(), cnd_timedwait(), cond_wait(),
cond_timedwait(), cond_reltimedwait(), pthread_cond_wait(),
pthread_cond_timedwait(), pthread_cond_reltimedwait_np(),
std::condition_variable::wait(), std::condition_variable::wait_for(),
std::condition_variable::wait_until().

The rule does not verify if a loop contains a correct condition.
CERT_C-CON36-a.benefits:
The rule prevents indefinite blocking and denial of service attack (an attempt
to make a computer resource unavailable to its intended users).
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON36-C. Wrap functions that can spuriously wake up in a loop
2. SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON54-CPP. Wrap functions that can spuriously wake up in a loop

CERT_C-CON36.title: CERT-CON36_C (RULE) Wrap functions that can spuriously wake up in a loop [CERT_C-CON36]

CERT_C-CON37-a.title: The signal handling facilities of <signal.h> shall not be used [CERT_C-CON37-a]
CERT_C-CON37-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'signal()' or 'raise()' global function declared
with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard library,
or locally by the user) is called or when its address is taken.

This rule also detects when identifiers with names of types and macros defined
in <signal.h> are used: sig_atomic_t, SIGABRT, SIGFPE, SIGILL, SIGINT, SIGSEGV,
SIGTERM, SIG_DFL, SIG_IGN, SIG_ERR.
CERT_C-CON37-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent implementation-defined and undefined behavior.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M18-7-1. The signal handling facilities of <csignal> shall not be used
2.  ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
7.7.1 Specify signal handling
3.  ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.14.1 Specify signal handling

5.  MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 18, Rule 18-7-1
6.  MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 21.5
7.  MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.5 The standard header file <signal.h> shall not be used
8.  MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 20, Rule 20.8
9.  Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 123

CERT_C-CON37.title: CERT-CON37_C (RULE) Do not call signal() in a multithreaded program [CERT_C-CON37]

CERT_C-CON38-a.title: Use the 'cnd_signal()' function with a unique condition variable [CERT_C-CON38-a]
CERT_C-CON38-a.description:
"The 'cnd_signal()' function unblocks one of the threads that are blocked
on the specified condition variable at the time of the call. If multiple
threads are waiting on the same condition variable, the scheduler can select
any of those threads to be awakened (assuming that all threads have the same
priority level). The 'cnd_broadcast()' function unblocks all of the threads
that are blocked on the specified condition variable at the time of the call."
[CERT-CON38-C]

"The use of 'cnd_signal()' is safe if each thread uses a unique condition
variable. If multiple threads share a condition variable, the use of
'cnd_signal()' is safe only if the following conditions are met:
- All threads must perform the same set of operations after waking up, which
  means that any thread can be selected to wake up and resume for a single
  invocation of 'cnd_signal()'.
- Only one thread is required to wake upon receiving the signal.
The 'cnd_broadcast()' function can be used to unblock all of the threads
that are blocked on the specified condition variable if the use of
'cnd_signal()' is unsafe." [CERT-CON38-C]

This rule detects when the address of a global condition variable is passed
as an argument to the 'cnd_signal()' function. If multiple threads share
the same condition variable, the liveness property may be violated.
The compliant solution is to use the 'cnd_broadcast()' function, or to ensure
that a unique condition variable is used for each thread.
CERT_C-CON38-a.benefits:
This rule helps you preserve the liveness when using condition variables.
"The liveness property requires that every operation or function invocation
execute to completion without interruption; for example, there is no deadlock."
[CERT-CON38-C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON38-C. Preserve thread safety and liveness when using condition variables

CERT_C-CON38.title: CERT-CON38_C (RULE) Preserve thread safety and liveness when using condition variables [CERT_C-CON38]

CERT_C-CON39-a.title: Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached [CERT_C-CON39-a]
CERT_C-CON39-a.description:
"The C Standard, 7.26.5.6 [ISO/IEC 9899:2011], states that a thread shall not be joined 
once it was previously joined or detached. Similarly, subclause 7.26.5.3 states that a thread 
shall not be detached once it was previously joined or detached".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, CON39-C. Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached]

This rule detects code flow paths where a thread object that is joined or detached was previously
joined or detached.
CERT_C-CON39-a.security_relevance:
"Joining or detaching a previously joined or detached thread is undefined behavior".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, CON39-C. Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached]
CERT_C-CON39-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior (see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.11
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.11 A thread that was previously either joined or detached shall not be subsequently joined nor detached
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON39-C. Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached

CERT_C-CON39.title: CERT-CON39_C (RULE) Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached [CERT_C-CON39]

CERT_C-CON40-a.title: Do not refer to an atomic variable twice in an expression [CERT_C-CON40-a]
CERT_C-CON40-a.description:
"If the same atomic variable appears twice in an expression, then two atomic
reads, or an atomic read and an atomic write, are required. Such a pair of
atomic operations is not thread-safe, as another thread can modify the atomic
variable between the two operations." [CERT-CON40-C]

The rule detects when an atomic global variable is used more than once in
a full expression.
CERT_C-CON40-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid incorrect results when an atomic global variable
changes between two atomic reads, or between an atomic read and an atomic
write in an expression.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON40-C. Do not refer to an atomic variable twice in an expression
2. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.8 Statements and blocks
7.17 Atomics <stdatomic.h>

CERT_C-CON40.title: CERT-CON40_C (RULE) Do not refer to an atomic variable twice in an expression [CERT_C-CON40]

CERT_C-CON41-a.title: Wrap functions that can fail spuriously in a loop [CERT_C-CON41-a]
CERT_C-CON41-a.description:
"A weak compare-and-exchange operation may fail spuriously. That is, even when
the contents of memory referred to by 'expected' and 'object' are equal, it may
return false and store back to 'expected' the same memory contents that were
originally there. Note: This spurious failure enables implementation of
compare-and-exchange on a broader class of machines, e.g., load-locked
store-conditional machines. A consequence of spurious failure is that nearly
all uses of weak compare-and-exchange will be in a loop." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

This rule detects when one of the following functions is called outside of any
loop (while, do-while, for): atomic_compare_exchange_weak(),
atomic_compare_exchange_weak_explicit().

The rule does not verify if a loop contains a correct condition.
CERT_C-CON41-a.benefits:
"Failing to wrap the atomic_compare_exchange_weak()
and atomic_compare_exchange_weak_explicit() functions
in a loop can result in incorrect values and control flow." [CERT-CON41-C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON41-C. Wrap functions that can fail spuriously in a loop
2. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
7.17.7.4 The atomic_compare_exchange generic functions

CERT_C-CON41.title: CERT-CON41_C (RULE) Wrap functions that can fail spuriously in a loop [CERT_C-CON41]

CERT_C-CON43-a.title: Do not use global variable with different locks set [CERT_C-CON43-a]
CERT_C-CON43-a.description:
Rule checks how many times global variables are used in critical sections. Global 
variables are regularly used in critical sections that hold some set of mutexes. 
When the variables are used in this way and subsequently used outside of a critical 
section or with a different set of locking objects then its possible that that 
variable can be accessed without synchronization, putting the application in an 
incorrect state.
CERT_C-CON43-a.benefits: Prevents unsynchronized access to global variables.
CERT_C-CON43-a.parameters:
"Disallowed usages ratio in critical section" defines the usage threshold for reporting violations.
For example, if a variable is used 10 times in a method - 7 times in a critical section with the
same set of locking objects, and 3 times outside of critical sections or with a different set of
locking objects - 3 violations will be reported when the threshold is set to 0.3. This is because
there is one common pattern of variable usage (it is used 70% of the time in that way), but there
are also 3 other cases when the variable is used in a different way.
    
"Compare usages in critical sections and outside of them regardless the lock object being used" turns off 
checking locking objects. The rule will count usages in all critical sections and compare it to usages 
without critical section.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')

MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.1 There shall be no data races between threads

18.2.2 Synchronize access to data shared between threads using a single lock
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON43-C. Do not allow data races in multithreaded code
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.

CERT_C-CON43.title: CERT-CON43_C (RULE) Do not allow data races in multithreaded code [CERT_C-CON43]

CERT_C-DCL00-a.title: Declare local variable as const whenever possible [CERT_C-DCL00-a]
CERT_C-DCL00-a.description:
"If data is declared to be const or constexpr then its value can not be changed
by mistake. Also, such declaration can offer the compiler optimization
opportunities." [AUTOSAR A7-1-1]

The rule detects immutable local variables declared without the 'const'
qualifier.

See also: CODSTA-CPP-43, MISRA2004-16_7
CERT_C-DCL00-a.benefits:
It's safe, it's checked at compile time, and it's integrated
with C++'s type system. It also prevents future revisions
from unintentional changing the caller's data.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A7-1-1. Constexpr or const specifiers shall be used for immutable data
declaration
2. General Principles of Software Validation;
Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff

5. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 7, Rule 7-1-1
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL00-C. Const-qualify immutable objects

CERT_C-DCL00-b.title: Declare parameters as const whenever possible [CERT_C-DCL00-b]
CERT_C-DCL00-b.description:
"If data is declared to be const or constexpr then its value can not be changed
by mistake. Also, such declaration can offer the compiler optimization
opportunities." [AUTOSAR A7-1-1]

The rule detects immutable function parameters declared without the 'const'
qualifier.

See also: CODSTA-CPP-43, MISRA2004-16_7
CERT_C-DCL00-b.benefits:
It's safe, it's checked at compile time, and it's integrated
with C++'s type system. It also prevents future revisions
from unintentional changing the caller's data.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A7-1-1. Constexpr or const specifiers shall be used for immutable data
declaration
2. General Principles of Software Validation;

CERT_C-DCL01-a.title: Identifier declared in a local or function prototype scope shall not hide an identifier declared in a global or namespace scope [CERT_C-DCL01-a]
CERT_C-DCL01-a.description:
"If an identifier is declared in an inner scope but is not distinct
from an identifier that already exists in an outer scope, then the inner-most
declaration will 'hide' the outer one. This may lead to developer confusion."

The rule checks if identifiers declared in local or function prototype scope
have the same name as an identifier declared in a global or namespace scope.

See also: CODSTA-CPP-83, CODSTA-CPP-84, CODSTA-CPP-85, MISRA2004-5_1
CERT_C-DCL01-a.benefits: Hiding names of global identifiers can lead to errors and confusions.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A2-10-1. An identifier declared in an inner scope shall not hide an
identifier declared in an outer scope
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
3.1.1. Do not hide declarations
3. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.15 Declarations and Definitions, AV Rule 135

systems, Chapter 6, Section 2, Rule 2-10-2

Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.3

CERT_C-DCL01-b.title: Identifiers declared in an inner local scope should not hide identifiers declared in an outer local scope [CERT_C-DCL01-b]
CERT_C-DCL01-b.description:
"If an identifier is declared in an inner scope but is not distinct
from an identifier that already exists in an outer scope, then the inner-most
declaration will 'hide' the outer one. This may lead to developer confusion."

The rule checks if an identifier declared in an inner local scope has the same
name as an identifier declared in an outer local scope.

See also: CODSTA-CPP-83, CODSTA-CPP-84, CODSTA-CPP-85, MISRA2004-5_1
CERT_C-DCL01-b.benefits: Hiding names of identifiers can lead to errors and confusions.

CERT_C-DCL02-a.title: Use visually distinct identifiers [CERT_C-DCL02-a]
CERT_C-DCL02-a.description:
"Use visually distinct identifiers with meaningful names to eliminate errors
resulting from misreading the spelling of an identifier during the development
and review of code. . . Depending on the fonts used, certain characters appear
visually similar or even identical." [CERT-DCL02-C]

The rule detects when two identifiers vary only with respect to one or more
visually similar characters.

The rule assumes that the following characters are visually similar:
- the letter 'O' and the number '0'
- the letter 'O' and the letter 'D'
- the letter 'O' and the letter 'Q'
- the letter 'D' and the number '0'
- the letter 'D' and the letter 'Q'
- the letter 'Q' and the number '0'
- the letter 'I' and the number '1'
- the letter 'I' and the letter 'l'
- the letter 'l' and the number '1'
- the letter 'Z' and the number '2'
- the letter 'S' and the number '5'
- the letter 'B' and the number '8'
- the letter 'n' and the letter 'h'
- the letter cluster 'rn' ('r' followed by 'n') and the letter 'm'
CERT_C-DCL02-a.benefits:
This rule helps you improve code readability and avoid errors that may result
from confusing identifiers.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL02-C. Use visually distinct identifiers

CERT_C-DCL04-a.title: Each variable should be declared in a separate declaration statement [CERT_C-DCL04-a]
CERT_C-DCL04-a.description:
"Each variable is to be declared in a separate declaration statement."
Determining the types of variables becomes confusing when pointers and access
specifiers are used for multiple declarations in the same statement.
See also: FORMAT-29
CERT_C-DCL04-a.benefits: Rule prevents confusion and makes source code more readable.
references: 1. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 11 Variables - Rule 39, 6 Style - 6.5 Pointers and References - Rec. 26
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A7-1-7. Each expression statement and identifier declaration shall be
placed on a separate line
3. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M8-0-1. An init-declarator-list or a member-declarator-list shall consist
of a single init-declarator or member-declarator respectively
4. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
7.1.1. Declare each identifier on a separate line in a separate declaration
5. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 8, Rule 8-0-1
6. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 10.0.1 A declaration should not declare more than one variable or member variable
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL04-C. Do not declare more than one variable per declaration

CERT_C-DCL04.title: CERT-DCL04_C (REC) Do not declare more than one variable per declaration [CERT_C-DCL04]

CERT_C-DCL05-a.title: Declare a type of parameter as typedef to pointer to const if the pointer is not used to modify the addressed object [CERT_C-DCL05-a]
CERT_C-DCL05-a.description:
The rule reports a violation if a parameter in a function prototype is declared
as typedef to pointer to non-const object and the pointer is not used to modify
the addressed object. Then the type of parameter could be changed to typedef
to pointer to const object.
See also: CODSTA-14, CODSTA-CPP-43, CODSTA-CPP-53, MISRA-104
CERT_C-DCL05-a.benefits:
Rule prevents unintentional change of data and improves precision in the
definition of the function interface.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M7-1-2. A pointer or reference parameter in a function shall be declared as
pointer to const or reference to const if the corresponding object is not
modified
2.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.13 Function, AV Rule 118
3.  MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 7, Rule 7-1-2
4.  MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 10.1.1 The target type of a pointer or lvalue reference parameter should be const-qualified appropriately
5.  MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.13
6.  MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.13 A pointer should point to a const-qualified type whenever possible
7.  MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 16

CERT_C-DCL06-a.title: Use meaningful symbolic constants to represent literal values [CERT_C-DCL06-a]
CERT_C-DCL06-a.description:
"When used in program logic, literals can reduce the readability of source code.
As a result, literals, in general, and integer constants, in particular,
are frequently called magic numbers because their purpose is often obscured.
Magic numbers can be constant values that represent either an arbitrary value
(such as a determined appropriate buffer size) or a malleable concept
(such as the age at which a person is considered an adult, which can change
between geopolitical boundaries). Rather than embed literals in program logic,
use appropriately named symbolic constants to clarify the intent of the code.
The C programming language has several mechanisms for creating named, symbolic
constants: const-qualified objects, enumeration constants, and object-like macro
definitions." [CERT DCL06-C]

This rule detects integer and floating constants with the following exceptions:
- Constants with '0' and '1' values are not detected independently from their
  representations.
- Constants used in initializers of enumeration constants or global variables
  are not detected.
- Constants used in macro and pragma definitions are not detected.

See also: CODSTA-26
CERT_C-DCL06-a.benefits:
"Using numeric literals makes code more difficult to read and understand.
Buffer overruns are frequently a consequence of a magic number being changed
in one place (such as in an array declaration) but not elsewhere (such as in
a loop through an array)." [CERT DCL06-C]

This rule helps you improve readability and maintainability of the code.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL06-C. Use meaningful symbolic constants to represent literal values
2. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 10 Constants - Rule 37

CERT_C-DCL06.title: CERT-DCL06_C (REC) Use meaningful symbolic constants to represent literal values [CERT_C-DCL06]

CERT_C-DCL10-a.title: The number of format specifiers in the format string and the number of corresponding arguments in the invocation of a string formatting function should be equal [CERT_C-DCL10-a]
CERT_C-DCL10-a.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule detects function calls where the number of format specifiers
in the argument of string type is different than the number of optional
arguments that are passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type
CERT_C-DCL10-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent abnormal program termination or unintended
information disclosure.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.19.6 Formatted input/output functions
2. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/printf/
3. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/scanf/
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO47-C. Use valid format strings
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL10-C. Maintain the contract between the writer and caller of variadic
functions
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL11-C. Understand the type issues associated with variadic functions
7. CWE-685: Function Call With Incorrect Number of Arguments

CERT_C-DCL10.title: CERT-DCL10_C (REC) Maintain the contract between the writer and caller of variadic functions [CERT_C-DCL10]

CERT_C-DCL11-a.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%s' and '%c' format specifiers in the format string and their corresponding arguments in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-DCL11-a]
CERT_C-DCL11-a.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule identifies a mismatch between the type specified by the format
specifier included in the string argument and the type of the corresponding
argument that is passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

The rule detects 'printf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%s" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a signed/unsigned/plain char or void type
- array of signed/unsigned/plain char type
- class that contains 'String' in its name and a member function conversion
  operator
- typedefs or references to the above types
- constant '0' (null)

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%c" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- signed/unsigned/plain char type
- typedefs or references to the above types
- integer constant whose value is positive and less than 256

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%ls" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to wchar_t type
- array of wchar_t type
- typedefs or references to the above types

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%lc" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- wchar_t or wint_t type
- typedefs or references to the above types

The rule detects 'scanf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%s" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a signed/unsigned/plain char type
- array of signed/unsigned/plain char type
- typedefs or references to the above types

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%c" or "%[characters]"
format specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to plain char type
- array of plain char type
- typedefs or references to the above types

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%ls" or "%lc" format
specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to wchar_t type
- array of wchar_t type
- typedefs to the above types

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type
CERT_C-DCL11-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent abnormal program termination or unintended
information disclosure.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.19.6 Formatted input/output functions
2. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/printf/
3. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/scanf/
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO47-C. Use valid format strings
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL11-C. Understand the type issues associated with variadic functions
6. CWE-688: Function Call With Incorrect Variable or Reference as Argument

CERT_C-DCL11-b.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%f' format specifier in the format string and its corresponding argument in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-DCL11-b]
CERT_C-DCL11-b.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule identifies a mismatch between the type specified by the format
specifier included in the string argument and the type of the corresponding
argument that is passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

The rule detects 'printf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%f" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- float type
- double type

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%Lf" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- long double type

The rule detects 'scanf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%f" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a float type
- pointer to a double type

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%Lf" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- long double type

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type
CERT_C-DCL11-b.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent abnormal program termination or unintended
information disclosure.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.19.6 Formatted input/output functions
2. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/printf/
3. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/scanf/
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO47-C. Use valid format strings
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL11-C. Understand the type issues associated with variadic functions

CERT_C-DCL11-c.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%i' and '%d' format specifiers in the string and their corresponding arguments in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-DCL11-c]
CERT_C-DCL11-c.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule identifies a mismatch between the type specified by the format
specifier included in the string argument and the type of the corresponding
argument that is passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

The rule detects 'printf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%i", "%d", "%li", "%ld", "%Ii",
"%Id", "%I32i", or "%I32d" format specifier in the string argument is other
than:
- parameter/variable of type signed int, signed long, short, char, enum,
  or bool
- expression of type int, long, short, char, enum, or bool
- typedefs to the above types
- constant of int or long type in the range of -2147483648 to 2147483647

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hi" or "%hd" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- parameter/variable of type signed short, char, enum, or bool
- expression of type short, char, enum, or bool
- typedefs to the above types
- constant in the range of -32768 to 32767

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hhi" or "%hhd" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- parameter/variable of type signed char
- expression of type char, enum, or bool
- typedefs to the above types
- constant in the range of -128 to 127

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%lli" or "%lld" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- signed long long
- typedef to the above type
- constant of long long type

The rule detects 'scanf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%i", "%d", "%li", "%ld", "%Ii",
"%Id", "%I32i", or "%I32d" format specifier in the string argument is other
than:
- pointer to signed int or signed long type

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hi" or "%hd" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to signed short type

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hhi" or "%hhd" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to signed char type

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%lli" or "%lld" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a signed long long type

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type
CERT_C-DCL11-c.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent abnormal program termination or unintended
information disclosure.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.19.6 Formatted input/output functions
2. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/printf/
3. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/scanf/
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO47-C. Use valid format strings
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL11-C. Understand the type issues associated with variadic functions

CERT_C-DCL11-d.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%u' format specifier in the format string and its corresponding argument in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-DCL11-d]
CERT_C-DCL11-d.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by

promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule identifies a mismatch between the type specified by the format

The rule detects 'printf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%u", "%lu", "%Iu", or "%I32u"
format specifier in the string argument is other than:
- parameter/variable/expression of type unsigned char, unsigned short,

- typedefs to the above types
- constant of int or long type in the range of 0 to 4294967295

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hu" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- parameter/variable/expression of type unsigned char or unsigned short
- typedefs to the above types
- constant in the range of 0 to 65535

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hhu" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- parameter/variable/expression of type unsigned char
- typedef to unsigned char type
- constant in the range of 0 to 255

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%llu" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- parameter/variable/expression of type unsigned long long
- typedef to the above type
- constant of long long type

The rule detects 'scanf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%u", "%lu", "%Iu", or "%I32u"
format specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to unsigned char, unsigned short, unsigned int, or unsigned long type

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hu" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to unsigned char or unsigned short type

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hhu" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to unsigned char type

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%llu" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to unsigned long long type

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type

CERT_C-DCL12-a.title: If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced within a translation unit, then the implementation of the object should be hidden [CERT_C-DCL12-a]
CERT_C-DCL12-a.description:
"Hiding the implementation details creates an opaque type that may
be referenced via a pointer whose contents may not be accessed. The
implementation of an object should be hidden by means of a pointer
to an incomplete type." [MISRA C:2012]

This rule reports a violation if the implementation of the structure
or the union is not required in a translation unit. If the structure
is defined in a header file, then its implementation can be required
in one translation unit and not required in the other, so the user
should take care when refactoring the code.
CERT_C-DCL12-a.benefits:
"If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced, then the
implementation details of the object are not needed and its contents
should be protected from unintentional changes." [MISRA C:2012]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.8
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.8 If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced within a translation unit, then the implementation of the object should be hidden

CERT_C-DCL13-a.title: A pointer parameter in a function prototype should be declared as pointer to const if the pointer is not used to modify the addressed object [CERT_C-DCL13-a]
CERT_C-DCL13-a.description:
"A pointer parameter in a function prototype should be declared as pointer to
const if the pointer is not used to modify the addressed object. The const
qualification should be applied to the object pointed to, not to the pointer,
since it is the object itself that is being protected."
See also: CODSTA-14, CODSTA-CPP-43, CODSTA-CPP-53, MISRA-104
CERT_C-DCL13-a.benefits:
Rule prevents unintentional change of data and improves precision in the
definition of the function interface.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M7-1-2. A pointer or reference parameter in a function shall be declared as
pointer to const or reference to const if the corresponding object is not
modified

CERT_C-DCL15-a.title: Objects or functions with external linkage shall be declared in a header file [CERT_C-DCL15-a]
CERT_C-DCL15-a.description:
"Declarations at file scope are external by default. Therefore if two files
both declare an identifier with the same name at file scope, the linker will
either give an error, or they will be the same variable, which may not be what
the programmer intended.

Placing the declarations of objects and functions with external linkage
in a header file documents that they are intended to be accessible from other
translation units. If external linkage is not required, then the object or
function shall either be declared in an unnamed namespace or declared static."

The rule reports a violations on variables or functions with external linkage
defined in implementation file that do not have declarations in a header file.
CERT_C-DCL15-a.benefits:
The rule minimizes dependencies between translation units and prevents
confusions.
CERT_C-DCL15-a.example_violation:
// file.cpp
int i1;        // Violation
int i2 = 0;    // Violation

void foo1(){}  // Violation
void foo2(){}  // Violation
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A3-3-1. Objects or functions with external linkage (including members of
named namespaces) shall be declared in a header file

Rule 6.5.1 A function or object with external linkage should be introduced in a header file
