CERT_C-DCL16-a.title: The lowercase form of 'L' shall not be used as the first character in a literal suffix [CERT_C-DCL16-a]
CERT_C-DCL16-a.description:
The lowercase letter 'l' can easily be confused with the number '1'
when it is used as the first character in a literal suffix.

This rule detects when the lowercase 'l' is used as the first character
in a literal suffix.
CERT_C-DCL16-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent confusion and improve the readability
of the code.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 5.13.5 The lowercase form of "L" shall not be used as the first character in a literal suffix
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL16-C. Use "L," not "l," to indicate a long value

CERT_C-DCL16.title: CERT-DCL16_C (REC) Use "L," not "l," to indicate a long value [CERT_C-DCL16]

CERT_C-DCL18-a.title: Octal and hexadecimal escape sequences shall be terminated [CERT_C-DCL18-a]
CERT_C-DCL18-a.description:
"An octal or hexadecimal escape sequence shall be terminated by either:
  - The start of another escape sequence, or
  - The end of the character constant or the end of a string literal.
There is potential for confusion if an octal or hexadecimal escape sequence
is followed by other characters. For example, the character constant '\x1f'
consists of a single character whereas the character constant '\x1g' consists
of the two characters '\x1' and 'g'. The manner in which multi-character
constants are represented as integers is implementation-defined.
The potential for confusion is reduced if every octal or hexadecimal escape
sequence in a character constant or string literal is terminated."

See also: MISRA2004-4_1
CERT_C-DCL18-a.example_violation:
/* Examples of incorrectly terminated escape sequences: */
int c1 = '\141t';            /* Violation */
const char *s1 = "\x41g";    /* Violation */
s1 = "\01 \02";              /* Violation */
CERT_C-DCL18-a.example_repair:
/* Examples of correctly terminated escape sequences: */
int c1 = '\141\t';           /* OK */
const char *s1 = "\x41" "g"; /* OK */
s1 = "\001 \002";            /* OK */
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.4.4.4 Character constants
6.4.5 String literals
2. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 5.13.2 Octal escape sequences, hexadecimal escape sequences and universal character names shall be terminated

Rule 4.1 Octal and hexadecimal escape sequences shall be terminated

DCL18-C. Do not begin integer constants with 0 when specifying a decimal
value

CERT_C-DCL18-b.title: Octal constants (other than zero) shall not be used [CERT_C-DCL18-b]
CERT_C-DCL18-b.description:
"Any integer constant beginning with a '0' (zero) is treated as octal.
Because of this, it is possible for a zero-prefixed constant that is intended
to be a decimal number to be incorrectly entered as an octal number, contrary
to developer expectations.

The integer constant zero (written as a single numeric digit) is, strictly
speaking, an octal constant, but is a permitted exception to this rule."
CERT_C-DCL18-b.benefits:
Rule increases readability and maintainability.
Rule prevents using implementation-defined values.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

(other than "\0" ) shall not be used

CERT_C-DCL19-a.title: Declare variables as locally as possible [CERT_C-DCL19-a]
CERT_C-DCL19-a.description:
The rule detects when a local variable is used only within a block that is
inside the block in which it was declared.
CERT_C-DCL19-a.benefits:
This rule helps you improve code readability and maintainability.

A variable ought to be declared with the smallest possible scope to improve
the readability of the code and so that variables are not unnecessarily
allocated. When a variable that is declared at the beginning of a function
is used somewhere in the code, it is not easy to directly see the type
of the variable.
In addition, there is a risk that such a variable is inadvertently hidden
if a local variable, having the same name, is declared in an internal block.

Many local variables are only used in special cases which seldom occur.
If a variable is declared at the outer level, memory will be allocated
even if it is not used. In addition, when variables are initialized
upon declaration, more efficient code is obtained than if values are assigned
when the variable is used.
references: 1. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 11 Variables - Rule 41
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M3-4-1. An identifier declared to be an object or type shall be defined in
a block that minimizes its visibility

Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff

CERT_C-DCL20-a.title: The number of arguments passed to a function shall match the number of parameters [CERT_C-DCL20-a]
CERT_C-DCL20-a.description:
"If the expression that denotes the called function has a type that includes
a prototype, the number of arguments shall agree with the number of parameters.
If the expression that denotes the called function has a type that does not
include a prototype, (...) if the number of arguments does not agree with
the number of parameters, the behavior is undefined."

The rule reports a violation if the number of arguments in a function call
is different than the number of parameters in function prototype or in function
definition.

If the function does not have neither a prototype (declaration of a function
that declares the types of its parameters) nor definition, then violation
is not reported. However, it is recommended always declare function prototype
before the function is called (see rule MISRA2004-8_1_b).

See also: MISRA2004-8_1_b, MISRA2004-8_4
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 16
2. Misra Guidelines - Rule 78
3. ISO/IEC 9899: 1990, 6.3.2.2 Function calls
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL20-C. Explicitly specify void when a function accepts no arguments

CERT_C-DCL20.title: CERT-DCL20_C (REC) Explicitly specify void when a function accepts no arguments [CERT_C-DCL20]

CERT_C-DCL22-a.title: Avoid unused values [CERT_C-DCL22-a]
CERT_C-DCL22-a.description:
This rule detects when a local variable or function parameter is assigned with 
a value that is never subsequently read because either:
- variable is unused, i.e. there is no possible path on which the variable is
  subsequently used, or
- value is overwritten, i.e. the variable is always assigned a new value before
  it is read
CERT_C-DCL22-a.security_relevance:
"This weakness could be an indication of a bug in the program or a deprecated
variable that was not removed and is an indication of poor quality. This could
lead to further bugs and the introduction of weaknesses." [CWE-563]
CERT_C-DCL22-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid unused values to improve code quality and security.
CERT_C-DCL22-a.parameters:
The "Reporting on variable declarations" group of parameters allows you to
specify how the rule should behave regarding variable declarations.
- If the "Report on variable declarations" parameter is enabled, the rule
  checks variable declarations. With this parameter disabled, no violations
  are reported on variable declarations.
- If the "including unused non-initialized primitives and pointers" parameter
  is enabled, the rule triggers on declarations of variables of primitive and
  pointer types, which are not assigned any explicit value, if these variables
  are not used on any reachable path.
  Note: This parameter requires the "Reporting on variable declarations"
  parameter to be enabled.
- If the "including overwritten values of primitives" parameter is enabled,
  the rule triggers on declarations of primitive type variables if the variables
  are initialized with values that are never subsequently used, because they are
  overwritten on all paths where they are used.
  Note: This parameter requires the "Reporting on variable declarations"
  parameter to be enabled.

The "Reporting on function parameters" group of parameters allows you to
specify how the rule should behave regarding function parameters.
- If the "Report on function parameters" parameter is enabled, the rule checks 
  function parameters.
- If the "including overwritten initial values" parameter is enabled, the
  rule triggers on unused(overwritten) initial values of function parameters.
  Note: This parameter requires the "Report on function parameters" parameter
  to be enabled.
  
If the "Report when null value is assigned to a pointer" parameter is enabled,
the rule triggers when null is assigned to a pointer.

If the "Report when there is at least one path where the value of the variable
is not used" parameter is enabled, the rule triggers when there is one or more
paths where the value of a non-volatile variable is never used.
references: CWE-563: Assignment to Variable without Use

CERT_C-DCL30-a.title: The address of an object with automatic storage shall not be returned from a function [CERT_C-DCL30-a]
CERT_C-DCL30-a.description:
"If the address of an automatic object is returned from a function, then
the object containing the address may exist beyond the time when the original
object ceases to exist (and its address becomes invalid)." [MISRA C:2004]

The rule reports a violation if a function returns a reference or a pointer
to a non-static local variable or to a parameter.

See also: MRM-23
CERT_C-DCL30-a.benefits:
"Automatic variables are destroyed at the end of the function call.
Returning a reference or pointer to such a variable allows it
to be used after its destruction, leading to undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C++:2008]

CERT_C-DCL31-a.title: All functions shall be declared before use [CERT_C-DCL31-a]
CERT_C-DCL31-a.description:
This rule detects when a function call is not preceded by any function
declaration in the translation unit.
CERT_C-DCL31-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.

A declaration provides information to the compiler about the type of function.
If undeclared function is found, the compiler will have to make assumptions
about the function prototype, and will not verify if actual parameter types
match types specified in function declaration. This may lead to undefined
behavior, which can result in hard-to-find errors at runtime.
references: 1. MISRA-C:1998 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 7, Rule 20
2. Multiplatform Porting to 64 Bits
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL31-C. Declare identifiers before using them

CERT_C-DCL31.title: CERT-DCL31_C (RULE) Declare identifiers before using them [CERT_C-DCL31]

CERT_C-DCL36-a.title: Identifiers shall not simultaneously have both internal and external linkage in the same translation unit [CERT_C-DCL36-a]
CERT_C-DCL36-a.description:
"For an identifier declared with the storage-class specifier 'extern'
in a scope in which a prior declaration of that identifier is visible,
if the prior declaration specifies internal or external linkage, the
linkage of the identifier at the later declaration is the same as the
linkage specified at the prior declaration. If no prior declaration
is visible, or if the prior declaration specifies no linkage, then
the identifier has external linkage." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

"If, within a translation unit, the same identifier appears with both
internal and external linkage, the behavior is undefined." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

This rule detects when:
- a function or an object is declared with the explicit 'extern' specifier,
  while it has another declaration with internal linkage in the same
  translation unit
- a function or an object is declared with the explicit 'extern' specifier
  in the unnamed namespace
- an object is declared with the explicit 'static' specifier, while it
  has another declaration with external linkage in the same translation unit
CERT_C-DCL36-a.benefits:
"Having names with both internal and external linkage can be confusing
since the objects to which they actually refer may not be obvious." [JSF]

This rule helps you improve readability of code and prevent confusion
or undefined behavior.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.2.2 Linkages of identifiers
2. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.15 Declarations and Definitions, AV Rule 138
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 6.5.2 Internal linkage should be specified appropriately
4. Origin:  Misra Guidelines - Rule 24
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL36-C. Do not declare an identifier with conflicting linkage
classifications

CERT_C-DCL36.title: CERT-DCL36_C (RULE) Do not declare an identifier with conflicting linkage classifications [CERT_C-DCL36]

CERT_C-DCL37-b.title: Identifiers that begin with an underscore and either an uppercase letter or another underscore should not be declared [CERT_C-DCL37-b]
CERT_C-DCL37-b.description:
The rule detects identifiers whose names begin with an underscore followed
by an uppercase letter or another underscore.
CERT_C-DCL37-b.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.

"All identifiers that begin with an underscore and either an uppercase letter
or another underscore are always reserved for any use" [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"The behavior of a program that declares or defines an identifier in a context
in which it is reserved or that defines a reserved identifier as a macro name
is undefined." [SEI CERT C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL37-C. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier
7.31 Future library directions

CERT_C-DCL37-c.title: Avoid declaring file-scoped objects whose names begin with an underscore [CERT_C-DCL37-c]
CERT_C-DCL37-c.description:
The rule detects declarations of identifiers with file scope whose names begin
with an underscore.
CERT_C-DCL37-c.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.

"All identifiers that begin with an underscore are always reserved for use
as identifiers with file scope in both the ordinary and tag name spaces."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"The behavior of a program that declares or defines an identifier in a context
in which it is reserved or that defines a reserved identifier as a macro name
is undefined." [SEI CERT C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL37-C. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier
7.1.3 Reserved identifiers
6.2.1 Scopes of identifiers

CERT_C-DCL37-d.title: The names of standard library macros should not be reused (C11 code) [CERT_C-DCL37-d]
CERT_C-DCL37-d.description:
The rule detects when an identifier, a macro, or a macro parameter has the same
name as a macro defined in the C11 Standard Library, or when its name matches
a macro name pattern described in future library directions.
CERT_C-DCL37-d.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL37-C. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier
7.1.3 Reserved identifiers
7.31 Future library directions

CERT_C-DCL37-e.title: The names of standard library identifiers with file scope should not be reused (C11 code) [CERT_C-DCL37-e]
CERT_C-DCL37-e.description:
The rule detects:
- when a macro or a macro parameter has the same name as an identifier defined
  with file scope in the C11 Standard Library, or when its name matches a name
  pattern described in future library directions
- when an identifier declared with file scope has the same name as an identifier
  defined with file scope in the same name space in the C11 Standard Library,
  or when its name matches a name pattern described in future library
  directions.
CERT_C-DCL37-e.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL37-C. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier
7.1.3 Reserved identifiers
7.31 Future library directions

CERT_C-DCL37-f.title: The standard library identifiers with external linkage should not be reused (C11 code) [CERT_C-DCL37-f]
CERT_C-DCL37-f.description:
The rule detects when an identifier declared with external linkage has the same
name as an identifier defined with external linkage in the C11 Standard Library,
or when its name matches a name pattern described in future library directions.
CERT_C-DCL37-f.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
CERT_C-DCL37-f.example_violation:
#include <stdlib.h>
int isupper(char);    // Violation
extern int errno;     // Violation
void string(int);     // Violation
void my_string(int);  // OK
static int strfunc(); // OK - the rule does not apply
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL37-C. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier
7.1.3 Reserved identifiers

CERT_C-DCL37-g.title: Macros that begin with an underscore and either an uppercase letter or another underscore should not be defined [CERT_C-DCL37-g]
CERT_C-DCL37-g.description:
The rule detects macros and macro parameters whose names begin with an
underscore followed by an uppercase letter or another underscore.
CERT_C-DCL37-g.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.

"All identifiers that begin with an underscore and either an uppercase letter
or another underscore are always reserved for any use" [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"The behavior of a program that declares or defines an identifier in a context
in which it is reserved or that defines a reserved identifier as a macro name
is undefined." [SEI CERT C]
CERT_C-DCL37-g.example_violation:
#define _MY_MACRO                            // Violation
#define __one 1                              // Violation
#define ADD(_MAX, __min) ((_MAX) + (__min))  // Violation * 2
#define _my_macro                            // OK - the rule does not apply
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL37-C. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier
7.1.3 Reserved identifiers

CERT_C-DCL37.title: CERT-DCL37_C (RULE) Do not declare or define a reserved identifier [CERT_C-DCL37]

CERT_C-DCL38-a.title: The final member of a structure should not be an array of size '0' or '1' [CERT_C-DCL38-a]
CERT_C-DCL38-a.description:
"Flexible array members are a special type of array in which the last
element of a structure with more than one named member has an incomplete
array type; that is, the size of the array is not specified explicitly
within the structure. Before the introduction of flexible array members
in the C Standard, structures with a one-element array as the final member
were used to achieve similar functionality." [CERT-DCL38-C]

This rule detects when the final member of a structure is an array of size
'1' or '0'.
CERT_C-DCL38-a.benefits:
"Failing to use the correct syntax when declaring a flexible array member
can result in undefined behavior, although the incorrect syntax will work
on most implementations." [CERT-DCL38-C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL38-C. Use the correct syntax when declaring a flexible array member
2. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.7.2.1 Structure and union specifiers
3. McCluskey 2001 "Flexible Array Members and Designators in C9X"

CERT_C-DCL38.title: CERT-DCL38_C (RULE) Use the correct syntax when declaring a flexible array member [CERT_C-DCL38]

CERT_C-DCL39-a.title: A pointer to a structure should not be passed to a function that can copy data to the user space [CERT_C-DCL39-a]
CERT_C-DCL39-a.description:
"When passing a pointer to a structure across a trust boundary to a different
trusted domain, the programmer must ensure that the padding bytes and bit-field
storage unit padding bits of such a structure do not contain sensitive
information." [CERT-DCL39-C]

This rule detects when a pointer to a structure is passed as an argument to one
of the following functions which can copy data across a trust boundary:
bcopy(), memcpy(), _memccpy(), memccpy(), memmove(), mempcpy(),
fwrite(), fread(), WriteFile(), WriteFileEx(), ReadFile(),
ReadFileEx(), send(), sendto(), recv(), recvfrom().
CERT_C-DCL39-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent data leaks.
"Padding units might contain sensitive data because the C Standard allows
any padding to take unspecified values. A pointer to such a structure
could be passed to other functions, causing information leakage." [CERT-DCL39-C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL39-C. Avoid information leakage when passing a structure across a trust
boundary
2. SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
DCL55-CPP. Avoid information leakage when passing a class object across a
trust boundary
3. OWASP API Security Top 10-2019
API3-Excessive Data Exposure
4. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-002380: Applications must prevent unauthorized and unintended information transfer
via shared system resources
APSC-DV-003120: The application must have the capability to mark sensitive/classified
output when required

CERT_C-DCL39.title: CERT-DCL39_C (RULE) Avoid information leakage when passing a structure across a trust boundary [CERT_C-DCL39]

CERT_C-DCL40-a.title: All declarations of an object or function shall have compatible types [CERT_C-DCL40-a]
CERT_C-DCL40-a.description:
This rule detects when declarations of an object or function with external
linkage in different translation units do not have compatible types.

"Compatible types are types that, for the purpose of declaration matching,
are treated as the same. Two identical types are compatible but two compatible
types need not be identical. For example, 'short int' and 'short' are
compatible." [MISRA C++:2008 Appendix C: Glossary]

"The easiest way of ensuring object or function types are compatible
is to make the declarations identical." [MISRA C++:2008 Rule 3-2-1]
CERT_C-DCL40-a.benefits:
"It is undefined behaviour if the declarations of an object or function
in two different translation units do not have compatible types."
[MISRA C++:2008 Rule 3-2-1]
CERT_C-DCL40-a.example_violation:
Assumption: the testing scope contains only 'test1.cpp' and 'test2.cpp'.

/* test1.cpp */
extern int g1;                   /* Violation */
extern short g2[5];              /* Violation */
extern void (*fp)(int*, int*);   /* Violation */
extern int* f1(int*);            /* Violation */
                                    
                              
/* test2.cpp */
extern short g1;                 /* Violation */
extern short g2[4];              /* Violation */
extern void (*fp)(char*, int*);  /* Violation */
extern int* const f1(int*);      /* Violation */
CERT_C-DCL40-a.example_repair:
Assumption: the testing scope contains only 'test1.cpp' and 'test2.cpp'.

/* test1.cpp */
extern int g1;                    /* OK */
extern short g2[5];               /* OK */
extern void (*fp)(char*, int*);   /* OK */
extern int* f1(int*);             /* OK */
                                    
                              
/* test2.cpp */
extern int g1;                    /* OK */
extern short g2[5];               /* OK */            
extern void (*fp)(char*, int*);   /* OK */
extern int* f1(int*);             /* OK */
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M3-2-1. All declarations of an object or function shall have compatible types
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Section 6: Rules, Rule 3-2-1
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 6.2.2 All declarations of a variable or function shall have the same type
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.3
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.3 All declarations of an object or function shall use the same names and type qualifiers
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL40-C. Do not create incompatible declarations of the same function or
object

CERT_C-DCL40-b.title: If objects or functions are declared more than once their types shall be compatible [CERT_C-DCL40-b]
CERT_C-DCL40-b.description:
"Two types have compatible type if their types are the same. For two qualified
types to be compatible, both shall have the identically qualified version
of a compatible type. For two pointer types to be compatible, both shall
be identically qualified and both shall be pointers to compatible types.
For two array types to be compatible, both shall have compatible element types,
and if both size specifiers are present, and are integer constant expressions,
then both size specifiers shall have the same constant value.
For two function types to be compatible, both shall specify compatible return
types. Moreover, the parameter type lists, if both are present, shall agree
in the number of parameters and in use of the ellipsis terminator;
corresponding parameters shall have compatible types."

The rule reports a violation if two declarations of object or function are not
compatible. The rule assumes that typedefs to the same type are compatible
and signed short/int/long type is the same as short/int/long type.

See also: MISRA2004-8_3_a, MISRA2004-8_3_b, CODSTA-67
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.4, Rule 8.3
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.3 All declarations of an object or function shall use the same names and type qualifiers
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.4 A compatible declaration shall be visible when an object or function with external linkage is defined
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 8
5. Origin: Misra Guidelines rule 26
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL40-C. Do not create incompatible declarations of the same function or
object

CERT_C-DCL40.title: CERT-DCL40_C (RULE) Do not create incompatible declarations of the same function or object [CERT_C-DCL40]

CERT_C-DCL41-a.title: A switch statement shall only contain switch labels and switch clauses, and no other code [CERT_C-DCL41-a]
CERT_C-DCL41-a.description:
"A switch statement shall only contain switch labels
and switch clauses, and no other code.
Switch label is either a case label or default label.
Switch clause is either a case clause or a default clause.
Case clause is the code between any two switch labels.
Default clause is the code between the default label
and the end of the switch statement."
CERT_C-DCL41-a.benefits: The rule enforces a simple and consistent structure on to the switch statement.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.4.2 The structure of a switch statement shall be appropriate
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.1
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 16.1 All switch statements shall be well-formed
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL41-C. Do not declare variables inside a switch statement before the
first case label

CERT_C-DCL41.title: CERT-DCL41_C (RULE) Do not declare variables inside a switch statement before the first case label [CERT_C-DCL41]

CERT_C-ENV01-a.title: Don't use unsafe C functions that do write to range-unchecked buffers [CERT_C-ENV01-a]
CERT_C-ENV01-a.description:
Don't use the unsafe C legacy: strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets,
strncpy, strncat, snprintf, fgets - functions that do write to range-unchecked
buffers, and/or do not check and correctly handle out-of-bounds errors.
See also: SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13,SECURITY-16
CERT_C-ENV01-a.benefits: Rule prevents using functions which may cause overflow.
CERT_C-ENV01-a.example_repair: Don't use the unsafe C legacy.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A27-0-4. C-style strings shall not be used.
2. Herb Sutter, Andrei Alexandrescu, "C++ Coding Standards," Addison-Wesley,
(C) 2005 Pearson Education, Inc.
Chapter: "Type Safety", Rule 99
3. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API02-C. Functions that read or write to or from an array should take an
argument to specify the source or target size
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV01-C. Do not make assumptions about the size of an environment variable
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC24-C. Do not use deprecated or obsolescent functions

CERT_C-ENV01-b.title: Avoid using unsafe string functions which may cause buffer overflows [CERT_C-ENV01-b]
CERT_C-ENV01-b.description:
This rule detects code that uses unsafe string functions from C library:
strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets
According to David A. Wheeler (see reference below),
"C functions users must avoid using dangerous functions
that do not check bounds unless they've ensured
that the bounds will never get exceed.
Functions to avoid in most cases (or ensure protection) include the functions
strcpy(), strcat(), sprintf() (with cousin vsprintf()), and gets().
These should be replaced with functions such as
strncpy(), strncat(), snprintf(), fgets(), respectively."
See also; SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13, SECURITY-16
CERT_C-ENV01-b.benefits: Prevents the use of functions which may cause buffer overflows.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/dangers-c.html
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API01-C. Avoid laying out strings in memory directly before sensitive data
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API02-C. Functions that read or write to or from an array should take an
argument to specify the source or target size
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV01-C. Do not make assumptions about the size of an environment variable
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC24-C. Do not use deprecated or obsolescent functions
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR31-C. Guarantee that storage for strings has sufficient space for
character data and the null terminator

CERT_C-ENV01-c.title: Avoid overflow when writing to a buffer [CERT_C-ENV01-c]
CERT_C-ENV01-c.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-ENV01-c.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and it is an array
with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is enabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-119: Improper Restriction of Operations within the Bounds of a Memory Buffer
2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

CERT_C-ENV01.title: CERT-ENV01_C (REC) Do not make assumptions about the size of an environment variable [CERT_C-ENV01]

CERT_C-ENV02-a.title: Usage of system properties (environment variables) should be restricted [CERT_C-ENV02-a]
CERT_C-ENV02-a.description:
This rule detects code that uses system properties, or environment variables:
a violation is reported on each call of getenv()/putenv() functions from
stdlib.h header.

There are multiple issues and implementation defined behavior related to
environment variables:

- The string pointer returned by getenv() may be modified (overwritten) by
  subsequent calls to the getenv() or other environment modifying functions.
  The string shall not be modified by the program. If you keep using it without
  making a copy you may end up with invalid (dangling) pointer or a pointer to
  a different string then the one returned by the initial getenv() call. Even
  making copy immediately is not completely safe - there's a race window
  between getenv() call and copying the string.

- Depending on the platform the variables may either be or not be
  case-sensitive. The matching conditions are not well defined, for example
  "VAR=value" might be matched on some systems but not on others.

- Names of environment variables are not standard so depending on their
  presence or values or setting them might not give expected results.

- The functions for altering the environment are implementation defined,
  for example putenv() is not a standard C99 function. putenv() need not be
  reentrant (and therefore not required to be thread-safe)

- Attackers can often change the value of environment variables, so limited
  trust should be placed in their values.

- Certain variables expected to be present in the environment can cause
  insecure program behavior if they are missing or improperly set.
CERT_C-ENV02-a.benefits:
By controlling use of environment variables in the applications, they are made
more secure. Environment variables can be used as security holes.
If a malicious user passes a specifically crafted string into a running program
via an environment variable, a buffer overflow attack can be launched.
On the other hand, if a program writes sensitive information
(such as a password) into an environment variable with putenv, it can be
captured and used to gain unauthorized privileges or access to sensitive data.
CERT_C-ENV02-a.example_repair:
Review each use of getenv/putenv functions and if it is correct
suppress the violation.
references: 1. The Open Group Base Specifications
2. Robert C. Seacord
CERT CS 15-392 Secure Programming - Environment Variables
3. Build Security In - GetEnv
4. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV02-C. Beware of multiple environment variables with the same effective
name
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS34-C. Do not call putenv() with a pointer to an automatic variable as
the argument
7. OWASP API Security Top 10-2019
API3-Excessive Data Exposure
8. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-002485: The application must not store sensitive information in hidden fields

CERT_C-ENV02.title: CERT-ENV02_C (REC) Beware of multiple environment variables with the same effective name [CERT_C-ENV02]

CERT_C-ENV30-a.title: The pointers returned by the Standard Library functions 'localeconv', 'getenv', 'setlocale' or, 'strerror' shall only be used as if they have pointer to const-qualified type [CERT_C-ENV30-a]
CERT_C-ENV30-a.description:
"The 'localeconv' function returns a pointer of type 'struct lconv*'. This
pointer shall be regarded as if it had type 'const struct lconv*'.
A 'struct lconv' object includes pointers of type 'char*' and the 'getenv',
'setlocale', and 'strerror' functions each return a pointer of type 'char*'.
These pointers are used to access strings (null terminated arrays of type char).
For the purpose of this rule, these pointers shall be regarded as if they had
type 'const char*'

The Standard states that undefined behaviour occurs if a program modifies:
- The structure pointed to by the value returned by 'localeconv';
- The strings returned by 'getenv', 'setlocale' or 'strerror'.

The Standard does not specify the behaviour that results if the strings
referenced by the structure pointed to by the value returned by 'localeconv'
are modified. This rule prohibits any changes to these strings as they are
considered to be undesirable." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]

This rule detects the following cases:
- the pointers returned by the functions 'localeconv', 'getenv', 'setlocale'
or 'strerror' are assigned to pointers to non-const-qualified types,
- the members of the structure 'lconv' referenced by the function 'localeconv'
are assigned to pointers to non-const-qualified types.
CERT_C-ENV30-a.benefits:
"Treating the pointers returned by the various functions as if they were
const-qualified allows an analysis tool to detect any attempt to modify an
object through one of the pointers. Additionally, assigning the return values
of the functions to const-qualified pointers will result in the compiler
issuing a diagnostic if an attempt is made to modify an object."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 25.5.2 The pointers returned by the C++ Standard Library functions localeconv, getenv, setlocale or strerror must only be used as if they have pointer to const-qualified type
2. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.19
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.19 The pointers returned by the Standard Library functions localeconv, getenv, setlocale or strerror shall only be used as if they have pointer to const-qualified type
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV30-C. Do not modify the object referenced by the return value of certain
functions

CERT_C-ENV30.title: CERT-ENV30_C (RULE) Do not modify the object referenced by the return value of certain functions [CERT_C-ENV30]

CERT_C-ENV31-a.title: Do not rely on an environment pointer following an operation that may invalidate it [CERT_C-ENV31-a]
CERT_C-ENV31-a.security_relevance:
"Unanticipated results may occur if setenv() changes the external variable 
environ. In particular, if the optional envp argument to main() is present, 
it is not changed, and thus may point to an obsolete copy of the 
environment (as may any other copy of environ)."
[Portable Operating System Interface (POSIX), Base Specifications, Issue 7]
CERT_C-ENV31-a.benefits:
'envp' that points to an obsolete environment may cause undefined behavior.
This rule helps you ensure that 'envp' points to the current environment.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV31-C. Do not rely on an environment pointer following an operation that may invalidate it

CERT_C-ENV31.title: CERT-ENV31_C (RULE) Do not rely on an environment pointer following an operation that may invalidate it [CERT_C-ENV31]

CERT_C-ENV32-a.title: Properly define exit handlers [CERT_C-ENV32-a]
CERT_C-ENV32-a.description:
This rule detects when functions registered as exit handlers do not return normally.

Functions can be registered as exit handlers by calling the atexit() or at_quick_exit() functions.

"Two specific issues include nested calls to an exit function and terminating a call to an exit 
handler by invoking longjmp. A nested call to an exit function is undefined behavior . . .
If a call to the longjmp() function is made that would terminate the call to a function 
registered with atexit(), the behavior is undefined."
[ENV32-C. All exit handlers must return normally]
CERT_C-ENV32-a.security_relevance:
"Exit handlers must terminate by returning. It is important and potentially safety-critical for all
exit handlers to be allowed to perform their cleanup actions."
[ENV32-C. All exit handlers must return normally]
CERT_C-ENV32-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
references: MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C+ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15-5-3
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
Rule A15-5-3 The std::terminate() function shall not be called implicitly
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV32-C. All exit handlers must return normally
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
ERR50-CPP. Do not abruptly terminate the program

CERT_C-ENV32.title: CERT-ENV32_C (RULE) All exit handlers must return normally [CERT_C-ENV32]

CERT_C-ENV33-a.title: The 'system()' function from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library should not be used with an argument other than '0' (null pointer) [CERT_C-ENV33-a]
CERT_C-ENV33-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'system()' global function declared with external
C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard library, or locally by the
user) is called with an argument other than '0'.
CERT_C-ENV33-a.benefits:
This rule helps you reduce the number of application vulnerabilities and
prevents undefined and implementation-defined behavior.

"The C Standard system() function executes a specified command by invoking an
implementation-defined command processor, such as a UNIX shell or CMD.EXE in
Microsoft Windows. Use of the system() function can result in exploitable
vulnerabilities, in the worst case allowing execution of arbitrary system
commands." [CERT-ENV33-C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV33-C. Do not call system()

CERT_C-ENV34-a.title: Pointers returned by certain Standard Library functions should not be used following a subsequent call to the same or related function [CERT_C-ENV34-a]
CERT_C-ENV34-a.description:
This rule detects when a pointer returned by one of the Standard Library functions listed below
is used following a subsequent call to the same function.

"The Standard Library functions asctime, ctime, gmtime, localtime, localeconv, getenv, setlocale
and strerror return a pointer to an object within the Standard Library. Implementations are 
permitted to use static buffers for any of these objects and a second call to the same function 
may modify the contents of the buffer. The value accessed through a pointer held by the program 
before a subsequent call to a function may therefore change unexpectedly".
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.20]

<time.h>
    char* asctime (const struct tm* timeptr)
    char* ctime (const time_t* timer)
    struct tm* gmtime (const time_t* timer)
    struct tm* localtime (const time_t* timer)
    
<locale.h>
    struct lconv* localeconv ()
    char* setlocale (int category, const char* locale)
    
<stdlib.h>
    char* getenv (const char* name)
    
<string.h>
    char* strerror (int errnum)
CERT_C-ENV34-a.security_relevance:
Subsequent calls to the same function may change the value referenced by the pointer that is returned
by the first call. Relying on such a value following a subsequent call may cause unexpected results.
CERT_C-ENV34-a.benefits:
Following this rule helps you prevent changes of values that may be caused by subsequent calls
the same function. This could result in incorrect application behavior.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.20
MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems

Rule 25.5.3 The pointer returned by the C++ Standard Library functions asctime, ctime, gmtime, localtime, localeconv, getenv, setlocale or strerror must not be used following a subsequent call to the same function
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

Rule 21.20 The pointer returned by the Standard Library functions asctime, ctime, gmtime, localtime, localeconv, getenv, setlocale or strerror shall not be used following a subsequent call to the same function

CERT_C-ERR01-a.title: The error indicator 'errno' shall not be used [CERT_C-ERR01-a]
CERT_C-ERR01-a.description:
" 'errno' is a facility of C [and C++], which in theory should be useful,
but which in practice is poorly defined by the standard. A non zero value
may or may not indicate that a problem has occurred; as a result it shall
not be used.
Even for those functions for which the behaviour of 'errno' is well defined,
it is preferable to check the values of inputs before calling the function
rather than rely on using 'errno' to trap errors" [MISRA-C:2004]

This rule detects when the 'errno' identifier is used in code.
CERT_C-ERR01-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent spurious error reporting.

CERT_C-ERR04-c.title: The 'quick_exit()' and '_Exit()' functions from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library shall not be used [CERT_C-ERR04-c]
CERT_C-ERR04-c.description:
"The 'std::abort()', 'std::quick_exit()', and 'std::_Exit()' functions are used
to terminate the program in an immediate fashion. They do so without calling
exit handlers registered with 'std::atexit()' and without executing destructors
for objects with automatic, thread, or static storage duration. Because these
functions can leave external resources, such as files and network
communications, in an indeterminate state, they should be called explicitly only
in direct response to a critical error in the application." [CERT-ERR50-CPP]

This rule detects when the 'quick_exit()' or '_Exit()' global function
declared with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard
library, or locally by the user) is called or when its address is taken.

See also: PB-75
CERT_C-ERR04-c.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent implementation-defined behavior. It allows you
to ensure that open streams with unwritten buffered data are flushed, open
streams are closed, and temporary files are removed.

CERT_C-ERR06-a.title: Do not use assertions [CERT_C-ERR06-a]
CERT_C-ERR06-a.description:
"The assert macro puts diagnostic tests into programs; it expands to a void
expression. When it is executed, if expression (which shall have a scalar type)
is false (that is, compares equal to 0), the assert macro writes information
about the particular call that failed (including the text of the argument,
the name of the source file, the source line number, and the name
of the enclosing function - the latter are respectively the values
of the pre-processing macros __FILE__ and __LINE__ and of the identifier
__func__) on the standard error stream in an implementation-defined format.
It then calls the abort function." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"If the intention of the programmer is to properly clean up in the case
of a failed assertion, then runtime assertions should be replaced with static
assertions where possible. When the assertion is based on runtime data,
the assert should be replaced with a runtime check that implements the adopted
error strategy." [CERT-ERR06-C]

This rule detects each use of 'assert()' in the tested code.
CERT_C-ERR06-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent unexpected behavior.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ERR06-C. Understand the termination behavior of assert() and abort()
2. 2023 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
CWE-617: Reachable Assertion

CERT_C-ERR06.title: CERT-ERR06_C (REC) Understand the termination behavior of assert() and abort() [CERT_C-ERR06]

is taken.

undefined behavior when the string cannot be converted.

CERT_C-ERR30-a.title: Properly use errno value [CERT_C-ERR30-a]
CERT_C-ERR30-a.security_relevance:
The improper use of errno may result in failing to detect an error condition or in 
incorrectly identifying an error condition when none exists.
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, Rule 12: Error Handling (ERR), ERR30-C]
CERT_C-ERR30-a.parameters:
If the "Report a violation when errno is not set to 0 before an errno-setting-function call"
option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specifiedin point 1) (see the DESCRIPTION section).
This covers Rule 22.8 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

If the "Report a violation when errno is not tested against 0 after an errno-setting-function call"
option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specified in point 2) (see the DESCRIPTION section)
This covers Rule 22.9 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

If the "Report a violation when errno is tested against 0 after calling a function that is not
an errno-setting-function" option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specified in point 3)
(see the DESCRIPTION section)
This covers Rule 22.10 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

"Additional errno setting functions" allows user to define additional functions which should be considered
errno-setting-functions.
The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name of the type or namespace
  where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace,
  or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
* The "Correct return value constraint" should be specified when function returns an out-of-band error indicator, 
  which means that when error occurs, it returns value that cannot be returned by any successful call.
  The errno value does not have to be tested against 0 if the check of such return value is present instead.
  Possible values are : >A / >=A / <A / <=A / ==B / !=B, where A is constant, B is constant/"true"/"false",
  e.g. ">=-1", "!=0", "==true"
references: OWASP Top 10-2021
A05:2021 - Security Misconfiguration
OWASP Top 10 2017
A6-Security Misconfiguration
OWASP API Security Top 10-2023
API8-Security Misconfiguration
OWASP API Security Top 10-2019
API7-Security Misconfiguration

Section 2: New rules, Rule 22.8, 22.9, 22.10

Rule 22.8 The value of errno shall be set to zero prior to a call to an errno-setting-function
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.9 The value of errno shall be tested against zero after calling an errno-setting-function
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

CERT_C-ERR32-b.title: Properly define signal handlers [CERT_C-ERR32-b]
CERT_C-ERR32-b.description:
This rule analyzes functions registered as signal handlers, and triggers in the following cases: 

 * A signal handler calls a function that is not asynchronous-signal-safe
 * A signal handler accesses a shared object that is not of atomic type

The function is considered to be asynchronous-signal-safe on one of the following conditions:
 * It is required by the standard (see the PARAMETERS section)
 * It calls only asynchronous-signal-safe functions, and does not access any shared objects

The rule reports all signal handler calls to functions that are not asynchronous-signal-safe.

As a rule, shared data should not be accessed in signal handlers.
"The two exceptions (C Standard, 5.1.2.3, paragraph 5) to this rule are the ability to read 
from and write to lock-free atomic objects and variables of type volatile sig_atomic_t. 
Accessing any other type of object from a signal handler is undefined behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]

The rule reports each access to a shared object which is not of atomic type.
CERT_C-ERR32-b.security_relevance:
"Accessing or modifying shared objects in signal handlers can result in race conditions that can leave 
data in an inconsistent state"
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]
CERT_C-ERR32-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
CERT_C-ERR32-b.parameters:
The "Consider asynchronous-signal-safe functions from POSIX standard" parameter allows you to specify 
the Posix Standard the rule should consider, as different standards added or removed requirements for
functions to be asynchronous-signal-safe. Alternatively, you can select the "Strict" option:
"For strictly conforming programs, only the C standard library functions abort(), _Exit(), quick_exit(), 
and signal() can be safely called from within a signal handler."
[SIG30-C. Call only asynchronous-safe functions within signal handlers]

The available options are:
- None
- Strict
- POSIX.1-2001
- POSIX.1-2003
- POSIX.1-2004
- POSIX.1-2008
- POSIX.1-2013
- POSIX.1-2016(default)

See references for more details about the list of signal-asynchronous-free functions required by each standard.

The "Additional functions which are asynchronous-signal-safe" parameter allows to define additional, third-party
functions that the rule should consider to be signal-asynchronous-free. The rule will not trigger when the specified
functions are called from signal handlers.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table:

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name 
  of the type or namespace where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any
  type or namespace, or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
The "Report violations when signal() function is called from within signal handler" parameter allows you to enable
reporting violations when the signal() function is called from within the signal handler.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard

SEI CERT C Coding Standard

SEI CERT C Coding Standard
SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
SIG34-C. Do not call signal() from within interruptible signal handlers
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
MSC54-CPP. A signal handler must be a plain old function
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.
CWE-364: Signal Handler Race Condition

CERT_C-ERR32.title: CERT-ERR32_C (RULE) Do not rely on indeterminate values of errno [CERT_C-ERR32]

CERT_C-ERR33-a.title: The value returned by a standard library function that may return an error should be used [CERT_C-ERR33-a]
CERT_C-ERR33-a.description:
"The majority of the standard library functions, including I/O functions and
memory allocation functions, return either a valid value or a value of the
correct return type that indicates an error (e.g. -1 or a null pointer).
Assuming that all calls to such functions will succeed and failing to check
the return value for an indication of an error is a dangerous practice that
may lead to unexpected or undefined behavior when an error occurs. It is
essential that programs detect and appropriately handle all errors in
accordance with an error-handling policy." [SEI CERT C Coding Standard]

This rule detects when the value returned from the following standard library
functions is not used:
aligned_alloc, asctime_s, at_quick_exit, atexit, bsearch, bsearch_s, btowc,
c16rtomb, c32rtomb, calloc, clock, cnd_broadcast, cnd_init, cnd_signal,
cnd_timedwait, cnd_wait, ctime_s, fclose, fflush, fgetc, fgetpos, fgets,
fgetwc, fopen, fopen_s, fprintf, fprintf_s, fputc, fputs, fputwc, fputws,
fread, freopen, freopen_s, fscanf, fscanf_s, fseek, fsetpos, ftell, fwprintf,
fwprintf_s, fwrite, fwscanf, fwscanf_s, getc, getchar, getenv, getenv_s,
gets_s, getwc, getwchar, gmtime, gmtime_s, localtime, localtime_s, malloc,
mblen, mbrlen, mbrtoc16, mbrtoc32, mbrtowc, mbsrtowcs, mbsrtowcs_s, mbstowcs,
mbstowcs_s, mbtowc, memchr, mktime, mtx_init, mtx_lock, mtx_timedlock, rename,
mtx_trylock, mtx_unlock, printf_s, putc, putwc, raise, realloc, remove,
setlocale, setvbuf, scanf, scanf_s, signal, snprintf, snprintf_s, sprintf,
sprintf_s, sscanf, sscanf_s, strchr, strerror_s, strftime, strpbrk, strrchr,
strstr, strtod, strtof, strtoimax, strtok, strtok_s, strtol, strtold, strtoll,
strtoumax, strtoul, strtoull, strxfrm, swprintf, swprintf_s, swscanf, time,
swscanf_s, thrd_create, thrd_detach, thrd_join, thrd_sleep, timespec_get,
tmpfile, tmpfile_s, tmpnam, tmpnam_s, tss_create, tss_get, tss_set, ungetc,
ungetwc, vfprintf, vfprintf_s, vfscanf, vfscanf_s, vfwprintf, vfwprintf_s,
vfwscanf, vfwscanf_s, vprintf_s, vscanf, vscanf_s, vsnprintf, vsnprintf_s,
vsprintf, vsprintf_s, vsscanf, vsscanf_s, vswprintf, vswprintf_s, vswscanf,
vswscanf_s, vwprintf_s, vwscanf, vwscanf_s, wcrtomb, wcschr, wcsftime, wcstok,
wcsrchr, wcsrtombs, wcsrtombs_s, wcsstr, wcstod, wcstof, wcstoimax, wcspbrk,
wcstok_s, wcstol, wcstold, wcstoll, wcstombs, wcstombs_s, wcstoumax, wcstoul,
wcstoull, wcsxfrm, wctob, wctomb, wctomb_s, wctrans, wctype, wmemchr,
wprintf_s, wscanf, wscanf_s.
CERT_C-ERR33-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent unexpected or undefined behavior when an error
occurs during the call to the standard library function.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ERR33-C. Detect and handle standard library errors

CERT_C-ERR33-d.title: Always check the returned value of non-void function [CERT_C-ERR33-d]
CERT_C-ERR33-d.description:
This rule detects cases when returned values of functions are not checked.
The list of library functions that should have their returned values checked includes:
- void* bsearch(const void* key, const void* base, size_t num, size_t size,
                 int (*compar)(const void*, const void*))
- void* calloc(size_t num, size_t size)
- int fgetc(FILE* stream)
- char* fgets(char* str, int num, FILE* stream)
- FILE* fopen(const char* filename, const char* mode)
- size_t fread(void* ptr, size_t size, size_t count, FILE* stream)
- void* malloc(size_t size)
- void* aligned_alloc(size_t alignment, size_t size)
- (const)void* memchr ((const)void* ptr, int value, size_t num)
- void* realloc (void* ptr, size_t size)
- (const)char* strchr((const)char * str, int character)
- (const)char* strpbrk((const)char* str1, const char* str2)
- (const)char* strrchr((const)char* str, int character)
- (const)char* strstr((const)char* str1, const char* str2)
- char* strtok(char* str, const char* delimiters)
- (const)wchar_t* wcschr((const)wchar_t* ws, wchar_t wc)
- (const)wchar_t* wcspbrk((const)wchar_t* wcs1, const wchar_t* wcs2)
- (const)wchar_t* wcsrchr((const)wchar_t* ws, wchar_t wc)
- (const)wchar_t* wcsstr((const)wchar_t* wcs1, const wchar_t* wcs2)
- wchar_t* wcstok(wchar_t* wcs, const wchar_t* delimiters(, wchar_t **ptr))
- (const)wchar_t* wmemchr((const)wchar_t* ptr, wchar_t wc, size_t num)

Returned values can be checked in one of the following ways:
- the returned value is used in a conditional statement (if, switch, while etc.)
- the returned value is used in a binary expression with >, <, >=, <=, !=, == operators
- the returned value is passed to a validating method as a parameter.

Using the returned value means:
- passing it as a parameter to a method other than a validating method
- dereferencing a pointer, accessing its fields, invoking its methods etc.

The returned value is considered unchecked if:
- it is used before it has been checked
- it has not been checked at all
- it is not used.

This rule works only on a scope of one method. For this reason, the returned values that are
passed further to another method are counted neither as checked nor as unchecked.
CERT_C-ERR33-d.security_relevance:
If returned values are unchecked, then an attacker may force the function to fail or return a value
that is not expected. This may lead to a software state that the programmer assumed would not be possible.
For example, if a program calls a function to drop privileges, but does not check the return code to ensure
that privileges were successfully dropped, then the program may continue to operate with the higher privileges.
CERT_C-ERR33-d.benefits:
Checking the value returned form a function can prevent unexpected states and conditions that may
lead to crashes or other unintended behaviors.
CERT_C-ERR33-d.parameters:
The "Analyze library functions" parameter allows you to specify if the default library functions
(listed above) should always have their values checked.

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify a list of functions that can be used to check returned values.
The returned value will be considered to be checked if it is passed to one of the listed functions as a parameter.

The "Additional functions which return value should be checked" parameter allows you to specify additional functions
whose returned values should always be checked.

The "Library functions which return value does not need to be checked" parameter allows you to specify the builtin 
functions whose returned values should not be checked.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives being reported. In this mode, Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.
references: CWE-252: Unchecked Return Value
MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.7
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.7 If a function returns error information, then that error information shall be tested
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ERR33-C. Detect and handle standard library errors

CERT_C-ERR33-e.title: Provide error handling for file opening errors right next to the call to fopen [CERT_C-ERR33-e]
CERT_C-ERR33-e.description:
This rule detects when the return value from an 'fopen()' function call
is not checked immediately after the call.
CERT_C-ERR33-e.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent unexpected and undefined behavior.

"The majority of the standard library functions, including I/O functions
and memory allocation functions, return either a valid value or a value
of the correct return type that indicates an error (for example, -1
or a null pointer). Assuming that all calls to such functions will succeed
and failing to check the return value for an indication of an error
is a dangerous practice that may lead to unexpected or undefined behavior
when an error occurs. It is essential that programs detect and appropriately
handle all errors in accordance with an error-handling policy." [CERT-ERR33-C]
references: 1. http://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/252.html
2. General Principles of Software Validation;
Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff
Document issued on: January 11, 2002
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ERR33-C. Detect and handle standard library errors

CERT_C-ERR33.title: CERT-ERR33_C (RULE) Detect and handle standard library errors [CERT_C-ERR33]

CERT_C-ERR34-a.title: The 'atof', 'atoi', 'atol' and 'atoll' functions from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library should not be used [CERT_C-ERR34-a]
CERT_C-ERR34-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'atof()', 'atoi()', 'atol()' or 'atoll()' global
function declared with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib'
standard library, or locally by the user) is called or when its address
is taken.
CERT_C-ERR34-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior. Using these functions results in
undefined behavior when the string cannot be converted.

shall be checked

CERT_C-ERR34.title: CERT-ERR34_C (RULE) Detect errors when converting a string to a number [CERT_C-ERR34]

CERT_C-EXP00-a.title: Use parenthesis to clarify expression order if operators with precedence lower than arithmetic are used [CERT_C-EXP00-a]
CERT_C-EXP00-a.description:
"No dependence shall be placed on operator precedence rules,
below arithmetic operators, in expressions. Parentheses should
be used to clarify operator precedence rules to enhance readability
and reduce mistakes. However, overuse of parentheses can clutter
an expression thereby reducing readability. Requiring parenthesis below
arithmetic operators strikes a reasonable balance between readability
and clutter."

Following operators have precedence lower than arithmetic operators:
- shifts  ">>", ">>"
- comparison "<", "<=", ">", ">=", "==", "!="
- bitwise "&", "^", "|"
- logical "&&", "||"
- conditional "?:"
- assignment "=", "*=", "/=", "%=", "+=", "-=", "<<=", ">>=", "&=", "|=", "^="
- throw "throw"
- comma ","
references: 1. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.28 Portable Code, AV Rule 213
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP00-C. Use parentheses for precedence of operation

CERT_C-EXP00.title: CERT-EXP00_C (REC) Use parentheses for precedence of operation [CERT_C-EXP00]

CERT_C-EXP02-a.title: The right-hand operand of a logical && or || operator shall not contain side effects [CERT_C-EXP02-a]
CERT_C-EXP02-a.description:
The rule detects when the right-hand operand of the logical operators '&&'
and '||' contains an expression that causes a persistent side effect.
CERT_C-EXP02-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent conditional evaluation of the right-hand operand,
which can easily cause problems if the developer relies on a side effect
occurring.

"There are some situations in C or C++ where certain parts of expressions may
not be evaluated. If these sub-expressions contain side effects then those side
effects may or may not occur, depending on the values of other sub expressions.
The operators which can lead to this problem are && and || where the evaluation
of the right-hand operand is conditional on the value of the left-hand operand."
[MISRA C:2012]

M5-14-1. The right hand operand of a logical &&, || operators shall not
contain side effects

CERT_C-EXP05-a.title: A cast shall not remove any 'const' or 'volatile' qualification from the type of a pointer or reference [CERT_C-EXP05-a]
CERT_C-EXP05-a.description:
"Any attempt to remove the qualification associated with the addressed type
by using casting is a violation of the principle of type qualification."
[MISRA-C:2004]

This rule detects when a const or volatile qualification is explicitly
cast away on an object of pointer or reference type.
CERT_C-EXP05-a.benefits:
"Removal of the const or volatile qualification may not meet developer
expectations as it may lead to undefined behaviour." [MISRA C++:2008]
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

the type of a pointer or reference
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard

explicit constructor call
3.  ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.5.3 Type qualifiers
4.  ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.3 Type qualifiers

systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-2-5

CERT_C-EXP08-a.title: Pointer arithmetic should not be used [CERT_C-EXP08-a]
CERT_C-EXP08-a.description:
"This rule refers to explicitly calculating pointer values. Any such pointer
value then has the potential to access unintended or invalid memory addresses.
This is an area where there is a danger of serious errors occuring in the code
at run time. Pointers may go out of bounds of arrays or structures, or may even
point to effectively arbitrary locations."
See also: MISRA2004-17_1, MISRA2004-17_2, MISRA2004-17_4
CERT_C-EXP08-a.benefits: Prevents from access to unintended or invalid memory addresses.
CERT_C-EXP08-a.example_repair:
"If pointer arithmetic is necessary, then appropriate reviews and/or dynamic
checking code will be required to ensure that the resulting pointer can not
point to unintended or invalid memory addresses"
references: 1. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 101
2. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.28 Portable Code, AV Rule 215
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ARR39-C. Do not add or subtract a scaled integer to a pointer
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP08-C. Ensure pointer arithmetic is used correctly

CERT_C-EXP08-b.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [CERT_C-EXP08-b]
CERT_C-EXP08-b.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
CERT_C-EXP08-b.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-EXP08-b.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
CERT_C-EXP08-b.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

CERT_C-EXP10-a.title: The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of evaluation that the standard permits [CERT_C-EXP10-a]
CERT_C-EXP10-a.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) and also used in another way in a statement, where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
CERT_C-EXP10-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

Since the increment, decrement, or assignment operator modifies its operand,
using such an operand more than once within the same expression can produce
undefined results.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A5-0-1. The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of
evaluation that the standard permits
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.1.2. Do not rely on the sequence of evaluation within an expression
3.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.25 Expressions, AV Rule 204.1

systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-1

EXP10-C. Do not depend on the order of evaluation of subexpressions or the

CERT_C-EXP10-b.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of function arguments [CERT_C-EXP10-b]
CERT_C-EXP10-b.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) in a function call argument, and the same object is used
in another argument of the same function call, or the same volatile object
is used in different arguments of the same function call.
CERT_C-EXP10-b.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

The order of evaluation of function arguments is unspecified. This means that
if the arguments have side effects, the order in which those side effects occur
is also unspecified. A function call can produce different results depending
on which of the function's arguments is evaluated first.

CERT_C-EXP10-c.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of the function designator and function arguments [CERT_C-EXP10-c]
CERT_C-EXP10-c.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) and also used in another way in a function designator and function
arguments in a function call.
CERT_C-EXP10-c.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"If a function is called via a function pointer there shall be no dependence
on the order in which function designator and function arguments are evaluated."

CERT_C-EXP10-d.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of expressions involving a function call [CERT_C-EXP10-d]
CERT_C-EXP10-d.description:
This rule detects when an object is passed to a function call by a pointer
or a reference, modified (incremented, decremented, or assigned) in that
function, and also used in another way in a statement where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
CERT_C-EXP10-d.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"Functions may have additional effects when they are called (e.g. modifying some
global data). Dependence on order of evaluation could be avoided by invoking the
function prior to the expression that uses it, making use of a temporary
variable for the value." [MISRA-C:2004]

CERT_C-EXP12-a.title: The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used [CERT_C-EXP12-a]
CERT_C-EXP12-a.description:
"It is possible to call a function without using the return value, which may be
an error. If the return value of a function is intended not to be used
explicitly, it should be cast to the void type." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.7]

This rule does not check the first operand of comma operator (","). It is
checked by a separate rule: CODSTA-122_b

See also: CODSTA-122_b
CERT_C-EXP12-a.benefits: Rule prevents errors caused by not using or checking function return value.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.7 The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP12-C. Do not ignore values returned by functions

CERT_C-EXP12-b.title: The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used [CERT_C-EXP12-b]
CERT_C-EXP12-b.description:
"It is possible to call a function without using the return value, which may be
an error. If the return value of a function is intended not to be used
explicitly, it should be cast to the void type." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.7]

This rule checks the first operand of comma operator (","). All other cases are
checked by a separate rule: CODSTA-122_a

See also: CODSTA-122_a
CERT_C-EXP12-b.benefits: Rule prevents errors caused by not using or checking function return value.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.7

EXP12-C. Do not ignore values returned by functions
