CERT_C-EXP14-a.title: Avoid mixing arithmetic of different precisions in the same expression [CERT_C-EXP14-a]
CERT_C-EXP14-a.description:
"C performs many type conversions implicitly and silently, so as to harmonize
types within an expression before evaluating it. Some of these conversions can
result in loss of information. Such implicit conversions shall not be used,
but explicit casts should be used instead.
As a general principle, avoid mixing arithmetic of different precisions in the
same expression."
The rule reports a violation if as operands of binary arithmetic, relational
equality or bitwise operator are used two non-constant operands of different
types.
See also: MISRA-043, CODSTA-99, CODSTA-100, MISRA2004-10_1_b, MISRA2004-10_2_a
CERT_C-EXP14-a.benefits:
The rule prevents unexpected behaviours.
"Mixed arithmetic normally entails implicit promotions and balancing
of types (i.e. conversions), some of which can lead to unexpected behaviour."
references: 1. Origin: Misra Guidelines rule 43
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP14-C. Beware of integer promotion when performing bitwise operations on
integer types smaller than int
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT02-C. Understand integer conversion rules

CERT_C-EXP14.title: CERT-EXP14_C (REC) Beware of integer promotion when performing bitwise operations on integer types smaller than int [CERT_C-EXP14]

CERT_C-EXP15-a.title: Suspicious use of semicolon [CERT_C-EXP15-a]
CERT_C-EXP15-a.description:
Rule reports a violation message if the semicolon constituting
the statement body is placed in the same line as closing ")"
parentheses (e.g., if(e); or while(e); or for(e;e;e);.
CERT_C-EXP15-a.benefits: Rule prevents accidental termination of 'if', 'while', or 'for' constructs.
references: 1. General Principles of Software Validation;
Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff
Document issued on: January 11, 2002
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP15-C. Do not place a semicolon on the same line as an if, for, or while
statement

CERT_C-EXP15.title: CERT-EXP15_C (REC) Do not place a semicolon on the same line as an if, for, or while statement [CERT_C-EXP15]

CERT_C-EXP16-a.title: Function address should not be compared to zero [CERT_C-EXP16-a]
CERT_C-EXP16-a.description:
Comparing an address of a function to '0' typically indicates programmer
error and can result in unexpected behavior.
The address of a function should never be '0', so such a comparison
always has a fixed result.

This rule reports a violation if an address of a function is explicitly
(in the equality or relational operator) or implicitly (used directly
in the condition of a conditional operator) compared to '0' or 'NULL'
(if 'NULL' is defined as '0' or '(cast)0').

See also: MISRA2004-16_9
CERT_C-EXP16-a.benefits: Following this rule prevents programming mistakes.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP16-C. Do not compare function pointers to constant values

CERT_C-EXP16.title: CERT-EXP16_C (REC) Do not compare function pointers to constant values [CERT_C-EXP16]

CERT_C-EXP19-a.title: The statement forming the body of a 'switch', 'while', 'do...while' or 'for' statement shall be a compound statement [CERT_C-EXP19-a]
CERT_C-EXP19-a.description:
This rule detects when a 'while', 'do', 'for' or 'switch' statement
is not immediately followed by a compound statement enclosed in
braces.
CERT_C-EXP19-a.benefits:
This rule helps you improve the readability and maintainability of your code.

A developer might mistakenly assume that a sequence of statements forms
the body of an iteration-statement due to their indentation.
Accidentally adding a semicolon after the control expression
is particularly risky, as it results in an empty statement.
Using a block statement clearly specifies which statements are included
in the body.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-3-1. The statement forming the body of a switch, while, do ... while or
for statement shall be a compound statement
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.1.1. Enclose the body of a selection or an iteration statement in a
compound statement
3.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.9 Style, AV Rule 59
4.  MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 15.6 The body of an iteration-statement or a selection-statement
shall be a compound-statement
5.  MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.3.1 The body of an iteration-statement or a selection-statement
shall be a compound-statement
6.  MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 15.6
7.  MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-3-1
8.  MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 14, Rule 14.8
9.  MISRA-C:1998 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 59
10. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP19-C. Use braces for the body of an if, for, or while statement
11. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 6 Style - 6.4 Flow Control Statements - Rec. 25

CERT_C-EXP19.title: CERT-EXP19_C (REC) Use braces for the body of an if, for, or while statement [CERT_C-EXP19]

CERT_C-EXP20-a.title: Avoid comparing values with the 'TRUE' identifier using equality operators ("==", "!=") [CERT_C-EXP20-a]
CERT_C-EXP20-a.description:
This rule detects when the direct operand of a comparison expression
is an identifier with the name 'TRUE'.
CERT_C-EXP20-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid mistakes and improves the readability of the code.

When the 'TRUE' identifier (other than the 'true' boolean value) is used
in a comparison expression, it is unclear whether the comparison is valid
because it can represent many possible values (any non-zero value may be true).
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP20-C. Perform explicit tests to determine success, true and false, and
equality

CERT_C-EXP20-b.title: Tests of a value against zero should be made explicit, unless the operand is effectively Boolean [CERT_C-EXP20-b]
CERT_C-EXP20-b.description:
"Where a data value is to be tested against zero then the test should be made
explicit. The exception to this rule is when data represents a Boolean value,
even though in C this will in practice be an integer."

An 'effectively Boolean' expression which is either 'Boolean-by-construct'
or 'Boolean-by-enforcement' as defined below.

Boolean-by-construct values are produced by the following operators:
- equality operators (== and !=)
- logical operators (!, && and ||)
- relational operators (<, >, <= and >=)

Boolean-by-enforcement values can be introduced by implementing a specific type
enforcement mechanism using a tool. A Boolean type could be associated with
a specific typedef, and would then be used for any objects that are Boolean."

Rule does not report violation if as an operand of logical operator is used:
- Boolean-by-construct values
- boolean constant
- integer or enum constant defined as '0' or '1'
- variable, parameter or expression of type:
  - bool,
  - typedef to bool,
  - an enumeration type whose name begins with 'bool' (case insensitive)
    or contains 'boolean' (case insensitive) and contains exactly 2 enumeration
    constants
  - typedef whose name begins with 'bool' (case insensitive)
    or contains 'boolean' (case insensitive) to:
    -- a char type defined explicitly as signed or unsigned
    -- a short or int type
    -- an enumeration type which contains exactly 2 enumeration constants
  - reference to above types
CERT_C-EXP20-b.benefits:
"This rule is in the interests of clarity, and makes clear the distinction
between integers and logical values."
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 14.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 14.4 The controlling expression of an if statement and the controlling expression of an iteration-statement shall have essentially Boolean type
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 13
4. Misra Guidelines - Rule 49
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP20-C. Perform explicit tests to determine success, true and false, and
equality

CERT_C-EXP20.title: CERT-EXP20_C (REC) Perform explicit tests to determine success, true and false, and equality [CERT_C-EXP20]

CERT_C-EXP30-d.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of expressions involving a function call [CERT_C-EXP30-d]
CERT_C-EXP30-d.description:
This rule detects when an object is passed to a function call by a pointer
or a reference, modified (incremented, decremented, or assigned) in that
function, and also used in another way in a statement where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
CERT_C-EXP30-d.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"Functions may have additional effects when they are called (e.g. modifying some
global data). Dependence on order of evaluation could be avoided by invoking the
function prior to the expression that uses it, making use of a temporary
variable for the value." [MISRA-C:2004]

CERT_C-EXP32-a.title: A cast shall not remove any 'const' or 'volatile' qualification from the type of a pointer or reference [CERT_C-EXP32-a]
CERT_C-EXP32-a.description:
"Any attempt to remove the qualification associated with the addressed type
by using casting is a violation of the principle of type qualification."
[MISRA-C:2004]

This rule detects when a const or volatile qualification is explicitly
cast away on an object of pointer or reference type.
CERT_C-EXP32-a.benefits:
"Removal of the const or volatile qualification may not meet developer
expectations as it may lead to undefined behaviour." [MISRA C++:2008]
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

CERT_C-EXP33-a.title: Avoid use before initialization [CERT_C-EXP33-a]
CERT_C-EXP33-a.description:
Objects with static storage duration are always initialized - either explicitly or implicitly.
Objects with automatic storage duration, if not initialized explicitly, have indeterminate value.
Reading the value of such an object results in undefined behavior.

This rule reports a violation when:
 - a non-initialized object is used, or
 - a non-initialized element of an array is used, or
 - a non-initialized field of a class/struct is used, or
 - a field of a non-initialized union object is used

See also the PARAMETERS section for other situations when the rule may report violations.

For the purpose of this rule, an object is non-initialized when:
 - it is an uninitialized object of numeric, enum or union type, or
 - it is an array where all elements are non-initialized, or
 - it is a class/struct object with all non-static members non-initialized
CERT_C-EXP33-a.security_relevance:
Reading the value of a non-initialized object results in undefined behavior.
Additionally, it can allow an attacker to control the behavior of the program, or to gain access to sensitive data.
CERT_C-EXP33-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
CERT_C-EXP33-a.parameters:
The "Report when non-initialized object is passed to a third-party function as const" 
parameter allows you to specify if the rule should trigger when a non-initialized object 
is passed by pointer or reference to const to a function whose source code is not available.
Since such an object cannot be modified inside the function, it is assumed that its value will be read.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Report when non-initialized structure is copied" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a non-initialized object of class/struct/union type is:
 - used as the rhs operand of the assignment operator, or
 - passed to a function by value, or
 - returned from a function by value, or
 - thrown by value.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Report when partially-initialized object is copied" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a partially-initialized object is:
 - used as the rhs operand of the assignment operator, or
 - passed to a function by value, or
 - returned from a function by value, or
 - thrown by value.

The "Report when potentially non-initialized element of an array is used" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a potentially non-initialized element of an array is used.
This parameter is disabled by default.

CERT_C-EXP34-a.title: Avoid null pointer dereferencing [CERT_C-EXP34-a]
CERT_C-EXP34-a.description: This rule detects cases when a null pointer may be dereferenced.
CERT_C-EXP34-a.parameters:
The "Reporting violations where variable is known to be null due to a null check" group of parameters:

Enable or disable this check box to specify which violations are reported:
   * If disabled, the rule will report violations only on variables that are certain to be assigned with a null value.
   * If enabled, there rule will report violations not only for null value assignment, 
     but also when a check for a null value that stipulates null pointer dereferencing at the point of use is detected.

Here is an example of the second type of violations:

    int b = (ptr == 0); /* null check that stipulates null dereferencing at violation point */
    /* ... */
    if (b) {
        /* ... */
        int i = *ptr; /* violation reported if the check box is ticked */
    }

The "Exclude cases when a null check is performed inside:" set of parameters helps you reduce the number of
unwanted violations caused by a null check. For cases when a function or a macro performs a null check,
these parameters determine whether it should be assumed that the corresponding variable can be null
in the callers of the function.

The "a macro invocation" parameter determines how the rule handles null checks in macros.
Consider the following example:
 
    #define FOO(P) if (P == 0) { /* ... */ }
    
    void bar(int* p)
    {
        FOO(p);
        *p = 10; /* Violation or not? */
    }
    
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported for the above example.   

Other parameters in this set are related to the visibility of functions.
Consider the following example:

    int* foo(int* p)
    {
        if (p == 0) {
            /* ... */
        }
        return p; 
    }
    
    void bar(int* p)
    {
        foo(p);
        *p = 10; /* Violation or not? */
    }

Whether the violation inside bar() will be reported or not depends on the visibility of foo()'s 
and parameterization of the rule.

"a called function with internal linkage"
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported in the above example if the foo() function is 
a function with internal linkage (a function that is visible only in the compilation unit where it is defined).
Static global functions and functions from anonymous namespaces are functions with internal linkage. 

"a called global function"
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported in the above example if the foo() function is
a global non-member function. 

"a called member function of the following visibility:"
This parameter defines the behavior of the rule for cases where foo() is a member function.
If this option is disabled, a violation will always be reported in the above example if foo() is a member function.
If this option is enabled, the following radio group of parameters is available:

   * "public" 
   * "public, protected"
   * "any visibility"

The table below shows all the possible options of the radio group of parameters. Each row represents one of
the parameters. The "+" character indicates that a violation will be reported in the above example.

                                  member function visibility (foo)
Parameterization:                 | public | protected | private |
"public"                          |    -   |     +     |   +     |
"public, protected"               |    -   |     -     |   +     |
"any visibility"                  |    -   |     -     |   -     |



The "Functions that do not accept NULL as their parameters" table allows you to list functions
whose parameters are not allowed to accept null.

The "External functions that may return NULL" table allows you to handle external functions
that can potentially return null.
To achieve better results, the rule provides a predefined set of such null returners from
standard libraries. Those predefined functions are used by default. They cannot be edited
or removed from the list, but they can be enabled/disabled in the "Enabled" column. Select or clear
the check box next to the function name to enable or disable the function.
Additionally, you can extend the set of null returners to include additional functions that
you assume may return null. 

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.

* The 'Non-NULL parameters' column allows you to specify 
  the list of ordinal numbers of the function parameters that are not allowed to accept null.
  List the indexes of parameters separated by a comma or use '*' to specify that all parameters are affected.

A step-by-step example of how to create your own null returner is provided in the 'EXAMPLE' 
section of this help page.

The "Treat "dynamic_cast" conversion as a source of the null value" parameter allows you
to specify if "dynamic_cast" conversion will be considered to be a source of null. 
This option is enabled by default.

class A {virtual void f();};  
class B : public A {void bar();}; 

static void foo(A* p)
{
    B* d = dynamic_cast<B*>(p); // the source of null if the option is enabled
    d->bar(); // a violation is reported if the option is enabled
}

CERT_C-EXP35-a.title: Do not modify objects with temporary lifetime [CERT_C-EXP35-a]
CERT_C-EXP35-a.description:
"An array which is a member of a non-lvalue expression with structure
or union type shall not be used as a value, other than as the immediate
postfix-expression operand to a subscript operator. The subscript operator
shall not be used to produce a modifiable lvalue." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

"A non-lvalue expression with structure or union type, where the structure
or union contains a member with array type (including, recursively, members
of all contained structures and unions) refers to an object with automatic
storage duration and temporary lifetime. Its lifetime begins when
the expression is evaluated and its initial value is the value
of the expression. Its lifetime ends when the evaluation of the containing
full expression or full declarator ends. Any attempt to modify an object with
temporary lifetime results in undefined behavior." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

This rule detects access to an array member of a structure or union object
with a temporary lifetime that was created as a result of a function call,
or by using a conditional, assignment, or comma operator, except when it is
used as the immediate postfix-expression operand to a subscript operator
that does not produce a modifiable lvalue.
CERT_C-EXP35-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined and erroneous program behavior.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.2.4 Storage durations of objects
2. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.16 Rule 18.9
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 18.9 An object with temporary lifetime shall not undergo array-to-pointer conversion
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP35-C. Do not modify objects with temporary lifetime

CERT_C-EXP35.title: CERT-EXP35_C (RULE) Do not modify objects with temporary lifetime [CERT_C-EXP35]

CERT_C-EXP36-a.title: Do not cast pointers into more strictly aligned pointer types [CERT_C-EXP36-a]
CERT_C-EXP36-a.description:
"A pointer to an object or incomplete type may be converted to a pointer to
a different object or incomplete type. If the resulting pointer is not
correctly aligned for the referenced type, the behavior is undefined."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

This rule detects when a pointer to an object type is cast to a pointer to
an object type which can be more strictly aligned.
CERT_C-EXP36-a.benefits:
"If the misaligned pointer is dereferenced, the program may terminate
abnormally. On some architectures, the cast alone may cause a loss of
information even if the value is not dereferenced if the types involved have
differing alignment requirements." [CERT-EXP36-C]

This rule helps you avoid undefined and implementation-defined behavior.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP36-C. Do not cast pointers into more strictly aligned pointer types
6.3.2.3, "Pointers"

CERT_C-EXP36.title: CERT-EXP36_C (RULE) Do not cast pointers into more strictly aligned pointer types [CERT_C-EXP36]

CERT_C-EXP37-a.title: Conversions shall not be performed between non compatible pointer to a function types [CERT_C-EXP37-a]
CERT_C-EXP37-a.description:
"A pointer to a function shall only be converted into or from a pointer
to a function with a compatible type. If a function is called by means
of a pointer whose type is not compatible with the called function,
the behaviour is undefined." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.1]

This rule reports a violation if a pointer to function is converted
(implicit or explicit) into a pointer to function of a non-compatible type.

The implicit conversion of a pointer is detected in the following cases:
- assignment expression
- comparison expression
- initialization (only simple variables)
- passing argument to function call
- returning value from function

"For two function types to be compatible, both shall specify compatible
return types. Moreover, the parameter type lists, if both are present,
shall agree in the number of parameters and in use of the ellipsis
terminator; corresponding parameters shall have compatible types."
[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.3.4 Cast operators, 6.5.2 Type specifiers, 6.5.3 Type qualifiers
6.5.4 Declarators
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.3.2.3 Pointers, 6.5.2.2 Function calls, 6.5.4 Cast operators
6.7.2 Type specifiers, 6.7.3 Type qualifiers, 6.7.5 Declarators
3. CWE-704: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 11.1
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 11.1 Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to a function and any other type
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP37-C. Call functions with the correct number and type of arguments

CERT_C-EXP37-b.title: Specify the access permission bits if a file is created using the 'open' or 'openat' system call [CERT_C-EXP37-b]
CERT_C-EXP37-b.description:
If the second argument of the open() call contains the O_CREAT flag, then the
file will be created if it does not yet exist. In this case you should specify
the access permission bits in the third argument of the open() call.

Similarly, you should also specify the access permission bits in the fourth
argument of the openat() call if the O_CREAT flag is specified in the third
argument of the call.

When the access permission bits are not set, then the effect is unspecified
and the file can have random permissions.

This rule reports a violation if:
- open() is called with O_CREAT flag and 3rd argument is missing, or
- openat() is called with O_CREAT flag and 4th argument is missing
CERT_C-EXP37-b.benefits: This rule can help minimize security risks.
references: 1. The Open Group Base Specifications Issue 7
IEEE Std 1003.1, 2013 Edition
2. 2023 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
2022 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
CWE-732: Incorrect Permission Assignment for Critical Resource
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP37-C. Call functions with the correct number and type of arguments

CERT_C-EXP37-d.title: Functions shall always have visible prototype at the function call [CERT_C-EXP37-d]
CERT_C-EXP37-d.description:
"Provided that a function call is made in the presence of a prototype,
a constraint ensures that the number of arguments matches the number
of parameters and that each argument can be assigned to its corresponding
parameter." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.3]

The rule requires that prototype (declaration or definition of function
that explicitly declares types of all parameters or uses explicit 'void'
if the function does not have parameters) must be declared before the
function is called.

This rule is enabled only for C language.

See also: MISRA-071_a, MISRA2004-8_2_b, MISRA2004-8_3_b, MISRA2004-16_4,
          MISRA2004-16_5, MISRA2004-16_6
CERT_C-EXP37-d.benefits:
"If a function is declared implicitly, a C90 compiler will assume that
the function has a return type of int. Since an implicit function declaration
does not provide a prototype, a compiler will have no information about
the number of function parameters and their types. Inappropriate type
conversions may result in passing the arguments and assigning the return
value, as well as other undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.3]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.3
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.3 A function shall not be declared implicitly
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 8, Rule 8.1
4. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 71
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP37-C. Call functions with the correct number and type of arguments

CERT_C-EXP37.title: CERT-EXP37_C (RULE) Call functions with the correct number and type of arguments [CERT_C-EXP37]

CERT_C-EXP39-a.title: There shall be no implicit conversions from integral to floating type [CERT_C-EXP39-a]
CERT_C-EXP39-a.description:
"Conversions from integral types to floating point types may not result
in an exact representation, which may not be consistent with developer
expectations."
The rule reports a violation if variable/parameter/constant/expression
of integral type is implicitly converted to floating type.
CERT_C-EXP39-a.benefits: The rule prevents confusions and possible loss of information.

EXP39-C. Do not access a variable through a pointer of an incompatible type

CERT_C-EXP39-b.title: A cast should not be performed between a pointer to object type and a different pointer to object type [CERT_C-EXP39-b]
CERT_C-EXP39-b.description:
"A cast should not be performed between a pointer to object type and a different
pointer to object type. Conversions of this type may be invalid if the new
pointer type requires a stricter alignment.
Pointer types can be classified as follows:
- Pointer to object
- Pointer to function
- Pointer to void
- The null pointer constant (the value 0 cast to type void *)"

CERT_C-EXP39-c.title: Avoid accessing arrays and pointers out of bounds [CERT_C-EXP39-c]
CERT_C-EXP39-c.description:
This rule detects pointer dereference operations that may result in accessing allocated memory that 
is out of bounds. A violation is reported when pointer is dereferenced and points to an array
(address of array variable was assigned to it or array was dynamically created by new[] operator).
Also, a violation is triggered if memory is allocated using new() operator and the pointer is moved
and accessed.

See also : BD-PB-ARRAY
CERT_C-EXP39-c.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat. If an
application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to execute arbitrary code
and gain full control over the application.

CERT_C-EXP39-d.title: Avoid buffer overflow from tainted data due to defining incorrect format limits [CERT_C-EXP39-d]
CERT_C-EXP39-d.description:
This rule detects possible overflow when writing to a buffer as a result of 
insufficient buffer size for holding the string that results from the sprintf function.

To prevent buffer overflow errors, ensure that the destination buffer has sufficient 
size to hold both the input and the null-terminator character.

The rule will report a violation when the following conditions are detected:
   * Destination buffer size is known or when it's smallest size can be approximated  
   * Input size was not compared to buffer size
   * Buffer input is considered tainted
   * Formatting the string did not restrict the size of the input or restrictions are 
     incorrect (allows larger input than size of buffer)
CERT_C-EXP39-d.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to execute 
arbitrary code, enabling the exploiter to gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-EXP39-d.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators
  
The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.
references: CWE-120: Buffer Copy without Checking Size of Input ('Classic Buffer Overflow')

CERT_C-EXP39-e.title: Avoid buffer read overflow from tainted data [CERT_C-EXP39-e]
CERT_C-EXP39-e.description:
The rule detects cases when code reads from a buffer and the read operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary.

Specifically, a violation is reported if the 'size' value passed to the function that reads from a buffer
comes from a tainting function (e.g. user input) that can return malicious data, but has not not been
checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types) and not greater than the size
of the buffer.
CERT_C-EXP39-e.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-EXP39-e.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

CERT_C-EXP39-f.title: Avoid buffer write overflow from tainted data [CERT_C-EXP39-f]
CERT_C-EXP39-f.description:
The rule detects when code writes to a buffer and the write operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary.

Specifically, a violation is reported if a 'size' value passed to a function that writes to a buffer
comes from a tainting function (e.g., user input) that can return malicious data, but has not been 
checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types) and not greater than the size
of the buffer.
CERT_C-EXP39-f.security_relevance:
A possible buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.

CERT_C-EXP39.title: CERT-EXP39_C (RULE) Do not access a variable through a pointer of an incompatible type [CERT_C-EXP39]

CERT_C-EXP40-a.title: A cast shall not remove any 'const' or 'volatile' qualification from the type of a pointer or reference [CERT_C-EXP40-a]
CERT_C-EXP40-a.description:
"Any attempt to remove the qualification associated with the addressed type
by using casting is a violation of the principle of type qualification."
[MISRA-C:2004]

This rule detects when a const or volatile qualification is explicitly
cast away on an object of pointer or reference type.
CERT_C-EXP40-a.benefits:
"Removal of the const or volatile qualification may not meet developer
expectations as it may lead to undefined behaviour." [MISRA C++:2008]
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A5-2-3. A cast shall not remove any const or volatile qualification from
the type of a pointer or reference
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.4.1. Only use casting forms: static cast (excl. void*), dynamic cast or
explicit constructor call
3.  ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.5.3 Type qualifiers
4.  ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.3 Type qualifiers
5.  MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-2-5
6.  MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 11, Rule 11.5
7.  SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP05-C. Do not cast away a const qualification
8.  SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP32-C. Do not access a volatile object through a nonvolatile reference
9.  SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP40-C. Do not modify constant objects
10. SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
EXP55-CPP. Do not access a cv-qualified object through a cv-unqualified
type

CERT_C-EXP40.title: CERT-EXP40_C (RULE) Do not modify constant objects [CERT_C-EXP40]

CERT_C-EXP42-a.title: Don't memcpy or memcmp non-PODs [CERT_C-EXP42-a]
CERT_C-EXP42-a.description:
"Don't use memcpy or memcmp to copy or compare anything more structured than raw
 memory. Memcpy and memcmp violate the type system. Using memcpy is a serious
 violation of information hiding, and often leads to memory and resource leaks,
 crashes, or undefined behavior."
CERT_C-EXP42-a.benefits: Rule improves safety and reliability of code.
CERT_C-EXP42-a.example_repair: Memcpy and memcmp should not be used.
references: 1. Herb Sutter, Andrei Alexandrescu, "C++ Coding Standards," Addison-Wesley,
(C) 2005 Pearson Education, Inc.
Chapter: "Type Safety", Rule 96
2. General Principles of Software Validation;
Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff
Document issued on: January 11, 2002
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP42-C. Do not compare padding data

CERT_C-EXP42.title: CERT-EXP42_C (RULE) Do not compare padding data [CERT_C-EXP42]

CERT_C-EXP43-a.title: The restrict type qualifier shall not be used [CERT_C-EXP43-a]
CERT_C-EXP43-a.description:
"When used with care the restrict type qualifier may improve the efficiency
of code generated by a compiler. It may also allow improved static analysis.
However, to use the restrict type qualifier the programmer must be sure that
the memory areas operated on by two or more pointers do not overlap.
There is a significant risk that a compiler will generate code that does not
behave as expected if restrict is used incorrectly."

The rule reports a violation on each use of 'restrict' type qualifier in code.
Any compiler extensions (e.g. __restrict) are not reported.

The rule is enabled only for C language (C++ standard does not define 'restrict'
type qualifier).
CERT_C-EXP43-a.example_violation:
/* The code is compilable with compilers which support
   the 'restrict' type qualifier */
typedef int* restrict INT_R;     /* Violation */
INT_R var1;                      /* OK - Violation on typedef declaration */
void foo(void* restrict p){}     /* Violation */

/* If 'restrict' is not a type qualifier (C90) this defines a variable */
  int restrict = 0;              /* Violation */
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.3.1 Formal definition of restrict
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.14
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.14 The restrict type qualifier shall not be used
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP43-C. Avoid undefined behavior when using restrict-qualified pointers

CERT_C-EXP43.title: CERT-EXP43_C (RULE) Avoid undefined behavior when using restrict-qualified pointers [CERT_C-EXP43]

CERT_C-EXP44-a.title: Object designated by a volatile lvalue should not be accessed in the operand of the sizeof operator [CERT_C-EXP44-a]
CERT_C-EXP44-a.description:
"The operand of a sizeof operator may be either an expression or may specify
a type. If the operand contains an expression, a possible programming error
is to expect that expression to be evaluated when it is actually not evaluated
in most circumstances." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.6]

The rule reports a violation if an object designated by a volatile lvalue
is accessed in the operand of the sizeof operator.


Exception:

"An expression of the form sizeof ( V ), where V is an lvalue with a volatile
qualified type that is not a variable-length array, is permitted."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.6]
CERT_C-EXP44-a.benefits: The rule improves maintainability of code and prevents confusions.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-3-4. Evaluation of the operand to the sizeof operator shall not contain
side effects
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.1.6. Do not code side effects into the right-hand operands of: &&, ||,
sizeof, typeid or a function passed to condition variable::wait
3.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.21 Operators, AV Rule 166
4.  MISRA C++:2008 - Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-3-4
5.  MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 13.6
6.  MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 13.6 The operand of the sizeof operator shall not contain any expression which has potential side effects
7.  MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 12
8.  Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 40
9.  SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP44-C. Do not rely on side effects in operands to sizeof, _Alignof, or
_Generic
10. SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
EXP52-CPP. Do not rely on side effects in unevaluated operands

CERT_C-EXP44-b.title: The function call that causes the side effect shall not be the operand of the sizeof operator [CERT_C-EXP44-b]
CERT_C-EXP44-b.description:
"A possible programming error in C is to apply the sizeof operator
to an expression and expect the expression to be evaluated.
However the expression is not evaluated: sizeof only acts on the type
of the expression. To avoid this error, sizeof shall not be used
on expressions that contain side effects, as the side effects
will not occur." [MISRA C:2004 Rule 12.3]

"Accessing a volatile object, modifying an object, modifying a file,
or calling a function that does any of those operations are all side
effects, which are changes in the state of the execution environment."
[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

"When a function is called, it may have side effects. Modifying a file
or accessing a volatile object are persistent as viewed by the calling
function. However any objects modified by the called function, whose
lifetimes have ended by the time it returns, do not affect the caller's
execution state. Any side effects arising from modifying such objects
are not persistent as viewed by the caller." [MISRA C:2012 Appendix J]

The rule reports a violation if in the operand of the sizeof operator
is called the function that has a persistent side effect.

See also: CODSTA-137
CERT_C-EXP44-b.benefits: The rule improves maintainability of code and prevents confusions.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-3-4. Evaluation of the operand to the sizeof operator shall not contain
side effects
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.1.6. Do not code side effects into the right-hand operands of: &&, ||,
sizeof, typeid or a function passed to condition variable::wait
3.  ISO/IEC 14882:1998 Programming languages -- C++
5.3.3 Sizeof
1.9 Program execution
4.  ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.3.3.4 The sizeof operator
5.1.2.3 Program execution

Appendix J Glossary

Chapter 6, Section 12, Rule 12.3

CERT_C-EXP44.title: CERT-EXP44_C (RULE) Do not rely on side effects in operands to sizeof, _Alignof, or _Generic [CERT_C-EXP44]

CERT_C-EXP45-b.title: Assignment operators shall not be used in conditions without brackets [CERT_C-EXP45-b]
CERT_C-EXP45-b.description:
Assignment operators shall not be used in conditions without brackets.
Missing the brackets causes that only one branch is executed and the
assignment may lead to unexpected program behavior.
See also: MISRA2004-13_1
references: 1. Recommended by ParaSoft
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP45-C. Do not perform assignments in selection statements

CERT_C-EXP45-d.title: Assignment operators shall not be used in expressions that yield a Boolean value [CERT_C-EXP45-d]
CERT_C-EXP45-d.description:
"No assignments are permitted in any expression which is considered to have a
Boolean value. This precludes the use of both simple and compound assignment
operators in the operands of a Boolean-valued expression. However, it does not
preclude assigning a Boolean value to a variable. If assignments are required
in the operands of a Boolean-valued expression then they must be performed
separately outside of those operands. This helps to avoid getting "=" and "=="
confused, and assists the static detection of mistakes."
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-2-1. Assignment operators shall not be used in sub-expressions
2. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.21 Operators, AV Rule  160
3. MISRA C++:2008 - Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-2-1
4. Misra Guidelines - Rule 35
5. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 13
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP45-C. Do not perform assignments in selection statements

CERT_C-EXP45.title: CERT-EXP45_C (RULE) Do not perform assignments in selection statements [CERT_C-EXP45]

CERT_C-EXP46-b.title: Expressions that are effectively Boolean should not be used as operands to operators other than (&&, ||, !, =, ==, !=, ?:) [CERT_C-EXP46-b]
CERT_C-EXP46-b.description:
"Expressions that are effectively Boolean should not be used as operands
 to operators other than (&&, ||, !, =, ==, !=, ?:).
An 'effectively Boolean' expression which is either 'Boolean-by-construct'
or 'Boolean-by-enforcement' as defined below.
Boolean-by-construct values are produced by the following operators:
- equality operators (== and !=)
- logical operators (!, && and ||)
- relational operators (<, >, <= and >=)
Boolean-by-enforcement values can be introduced by implementing a specific type
enforcement mechanism using a tool. A Boolean type could be associated with
a specific typedef, and would then be used for any objects that are Boolean."

Rule reports violation if as an operand of operator
other than (&&, ||, !, =, ==, !=, ?:) is used:
- Boolean-by-construct values
- boolean constant
- variable, parameter or expression of type:
  - bool,
  - typedef to bool,
  - enum that name begins with 'bool' (ignoring case)
    and that contains exactly 2 enum constant in body
  - typedef to char/short/int/enum which name begins with 'bool' (ignoring case)
  - reference to above types
CERT_C-EXP46-b.benefits:
Rule improves readability and maintainability.
Rule prevents confusion between logical and bitwise operators.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M4-5-1. Expressions with type bool shall not be used as operands to built-
in operators other than the assignment operator =, the logical operators
&&, ||, !, the equality operators == and !=, the unary & operator, and the
conditional operator
2. MISRA C++:2008 - Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 4, Rule 4-5-1
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 12
4. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 36
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP46-C. Do not use a bitwise operator with a Boolean-like operand

CERT_C-EXP46.title: CERT-EXP46_C (RULE) Do not use a bitwise operator with a Boolean-like operand [CERT_C-EXP46]

CERT_C-EXP47-a.title: Do not call va_arg with an argument of the incorrect type [CERT_C-EXP47-a]
CERT_C-EXP47-a.description:
"The variable arguments passed to a variadic function are accessed by calling
the 'va_arg()' macro. This macro accepts the 'va_list' representing the variable
arguments of the function invocation and the type denoting the expected argument
type for the argument being retrieved. The macro is typically invoked within
a loop, being called once for each expected argument. However, there are no type
safety guarantees that the type passed to 'va_arg' matches the type passed by
the caller" [CERT-EXP47-C]

Incompatibility of the type passed to the 'va_arg()' macro and the type
passed to the variadic function after default argument promotions have been
applied results in undefined behavior.

"Since the prototype doesn't specify types for optional arguments, in a call
to a variadic function the default argument promotions are performed on the
optional argument values. This means the objects of type 'char' or 'short int'
(whether signed or not) are promoted to either 'int' or 'unsigned int',
as appropriate; and that objects of type 'float' are promoted to type 'double'.
So, if the caller passes a 'char' as an optional argument, it is promoted
to an 'int'" [The GNU C Library]

Due to default argument promotions, variadic functions will never receive
arguments of types 'char, 'short' or 'float'. For this reason, passing the type
names 'char', 'short', or 'float' as the second argument to the va_arg() macro
is incorrect.

This rule detects when the second argument passed to the 'va_arg()' macro
is a type name 'char, 'short', or 'float', or a typedef name to these types.
CERT_C-EXP47-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.5.2.2 Function calls
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP47-C. Do not call va_arg with an argument of the incorrect type
3. The GNU C Library
A.2.2.4 Calling Variadic Functions

CERT_C-EXP47.title: CERT-EXP47_C (RULE) Do not call va_arg with an argument of the incorrect type [CERT_C-EXP47]

CERT_C-FIO01-a.title: Don't use chmod(), chown(), chgrp() [CERT_C-FIO01-a]
CERT_C-FIO01-a.description:
Don't use chmod(), chown(), chgrp().
Use fchmod(), fchown() instead.
fchmod() is more efficient because it does not have to do
a separate look-up of the name, whereas chmod() must do so.
CERT_C-FIO01-a.benefits: Rule prevents using unsafe chmod(), chown(), chgrp() functions.
references: 1. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO01-C. Be careful using functions that use file names for identification

CERT_C-FIO01-c.title: Be careful using functions that use file names for identification [CERT_C-FIO01-c]
CERT_C-FIO01-c.description:
The rule detects when 'remove', 'rename', 'fopen', 'freopen', 'link', 'unlink',
'mkdir', 'rmdir', 'mount', 'unmount', 'lstat', 'mknod', 'symlink', and 'utime'
functions are used.
CERT_C-FIO01-c.benefits:
"Accessing files via file descriptors or FILE pointers rather than file names
provides a greater degree of certainty as to which object is actually acted
upon. It is recommended that files be accessed through file descriptors or
FILE pointers where possible.
The following C functions rely solely on file names for file identification:
'remove()', 'rename()', 'fopen()', and 'freopen()'. Use these functions with
caution." [SEI CERT FIO01-C]

"Many file-related security vulnerabilities result from a program accessing an
unintended file object because file names are only loosely bound to underlying
file objects. File names provide no information regarding the nature of the
file object itself. Furthermore, the binding of a file name to a file object
is reasserted every time the file name is used in an operation. File
descriptors and FILE pointers are bound to underlying file objects by the
operating system." [SEI CERT FIO01-C]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO01-C. Be careful using functions that use file names for identification

CERT_C-FIO01.title: CERT-FIO01_C (REC) Be careful using functions that use file names for identification [CERT_C-FIO01]

CERT_C-FIO21-b.title: Use secure temporary file name functions [CERT_C-FIO21-b]
CERT_C-FIO21-b.description:
This rule detects when the following functions are used: 'mktemp', 'tempnam',
'tmpnam', 'tmpnam_r', 'tmpnam_s', 'tmpfile', and 'tmpfile_s'.
CERT_C-FIO21-b.benefits:
This rule helps you secure your code against unintended access.

"Securely creating temporary files is error prone and dependent on the version
of the C runtime library used, the operating system, and the file system. Code
that works for a locally mounted file system, for example, may be vulnerable
when used with a remotely mounted file system. Moreover, none of these functions
('mktemp', 'tempnam', 'tmpnam', 'tmpnam_r', 'tmpnam_s', 'tmpfile', 'tmpfile_s')
are without problems. The only secure solution is to not create temporary files
in shared directories." [CERT C-FIO21]
references: 1. C standard ISO/IEC 9899:1999
7.19.4.3 The tmpfile function
7.19.4.4 The tmpnam function
2. The Open Group Base Specifications Issue 7, IEEE Std 1003.1, 2013 Edition
3. The Open Group Base Specifications Issue 6, IEEE Std 1003.1, 2004 Edition
4. CWE-377: Insecure Temporary File
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO21-C. Be careful using functions that use file names for identification
6. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-000060: The application must clear temporary storage and cookies when the session
is terminated

CERT_C-FIO21.title: CERT-FIO21_C (REC) Do not create temporary files in shared directories [CERT_C-FIO21]

CERT_C-FIO22-a.title: Ensure resources are freed [CERT_C-FIO22-a]
CERT_C-FIO22-a.description:
This rule helps to ensure that allocated resources are deallocated on all paths.
Violations of the rule identify execution paths in an application where resources
are leaked.
CERT_C-FIO22-a.security_relevance:
Failure to close resources may lead to starvation, which could have
security implications.
CERT_C-FIO22-a.benefits:
Having a strict policy with respect to closing resources helps to make an application more
robust by preventing resource starvation.
CERT_C-FIO22-a.parameters:
The "Assume resource references can be stored by third-party methods of the following
types" set of parameters allows you to determine how strict the rule is when a reference
to a resource may be stored by a third-party function. For example, if there is a
third-party function SpecialCollection::add() and a resource is passed as a parameter
to this function, it will be stored in a collection and may later be closed by iterating
over the collection. Since Flow Analysis does not know the exact behavior of arbitrary
third-party functions, it behaves in either of the following ways:

   * With "Any functions" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that any third-party
     function may store a reference to a resource if the resource is passed into the function.
     This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource is later closed using
     the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are
     not reported as violations) if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
    
   * With "Functions that accept parameters which could host resource reference" parameter
     enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that a function may store a reference to a resource if
     it takes an additional parameter that can be used to store a pointer or a reference to
     the given resource. This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource
     is later closed using the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false
     negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations) if the function does not store
     a reference to the resource.
   
   * With "Functions with names matching the pattern" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis
     assumes that any third-party method which matches regex may store a reference to
     a resource if the resource is passed into the method. This prevents the rule from reporting
     false positives if the resource is later closed using the stored reference. However,
     this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations)
     if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
   
   * With all the parameters disabled, Flow Analysis takes a more aggressive approach and it
     assumes that third-party methods do not affect resources in any way. This approach
     may lead to some false positives, but it will not overlook any of the real leaks
     that may be missed with enabled parameters.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

CERT_C-FIO24-a.title: Avoid race conditions while accessing files [CERT_C-FIO24-a]
CERT_C-FIO24-a.description:
"A program that performs two or more file operations on a single file name or path name creates 
a race window between the two file operations. This race window comes from the assumption that the
file name or path name refers to the same resource both times. If an attacker can modify the file,
remove it, or replace it with a different file, then this assumption will not hold".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO45-C. Avoid TOCTOU race conditions while accessing files]

This rule detects when the same file is being opened more than once, which may lead to race condition.
CERT_C-FIO24-a.security_relevance:
"An attacker can exploit the race window between the two calls to fopen()..." or other opening 
function "...to overwrite an existing file".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO45-C. Avoid TOCTOU race conditions while accessing files]
CERT_C-FIO24-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid unexpected behavior and prevent hackers from accessing files
(see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO24-C. Do not open a file that is already open
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO45-C. Avoid TOCTOU race conditions while accessing files

CERT_C-FIO30-a.title: Avoid calling functions printf/wprintf with only one argument other than string constant [CERT_C-FIO30-a]
CERT_C-FIO30-a.description:
"Never use unfiltered data from an untrusted user as the format parameter.
Failing to follow this guideline usually results in a format string
vulnerability" [Secure Programming HOWTO]
"A formatting string should almost always be a constant string, possibly
involving a function call to implement a lookup for internationalization"
[Secure Programming HOWTO]

This rule detects when functions printf/wprintf are called with only one
argument other than a string constant.

See also: BD-SECURITY-TDCONSOLE
CERT_C-FIO30-a.benefits:
This rule prevents security attacks with format strings.

"When an attacker can modify an externally-controlled format
string, this can lead to buffer overflows, denial of service,
or data representation problems." [CWE-134]
references: 1. "Secure Programming HOWTO", David A. Wheeler
9.4. Control Data Formatting (Format Strings)
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A27-0-1. Inputs from independent components shall be validated
3. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO30-C. Exclude user input from format strings

CERT_C-FIO30-b.title: Avoid using functions fprintf/fwprintf with only two parameters, when second parameter is a variable [CERT_C-FIO30-b]
CERT_C-FIO30-b.description:
This rule detects the use of the functions fprintf/wfprintf with two parameters
and no format specifiers, when second parameter is a variable parameter.
CERT_C-FIO30-b.benefits:
This rule prevents security attack using format strings.
If a fprintf functions is passed a variable without format specifiers,
the variable can contain a cleverly formatted string
(including format characters %n, %x, and %s) that can be used to execute
malicious instructions or crash the application.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/control-formatting.html
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO30-C. Exclude user input from format strings

CERT_C-FIO30-c.title: Never use unfiltered data from an untrusted user as the format parameter [CERT_C-FIO30-c]
CERT_C-FIO30-c.description:
Never use unfiltered data from an untrusted user as a format parameter.
Failing this guideline usually results in a format string vulnerability.
A particularly nasty case is printf's %n conversion specification,
which writes the number of characters written so far into the pointer argument;
using this, an attacker can overwrite a value that was intended for printing.

The rule reports a violation if an argument passed as format parameter in call
of 'printf' function (or other similar function, e.g. 'sprintf', 'fprintf', ...)
contains parameter, variable or function's call.
CERT_C-FIO30-c.benefits: The rule prevents attempts of overwriting a value that was intended for printing.
references: 1. David A. Wheeler, Programming Secure Applications for Unix-like Systems (pdf),
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO30-C. Exclude user input from format strings
4. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)

CERT_C-FIO32-a.title: Protect against file name injection [CERT_C-FIO32-a]
CERT_C-FIO32-a.description:
This rule detects cases when possibly tainted data is used as a filename or a path in file
manipulating functions, which could lead to file name injection. The rule supports file manipulation
functions from the standard C and C++ libraries as well as those defined by POSIX.
CERT_C-FIO32-a.security_relevance:
Unverified data in file names may enable a potential attacker to gain access
to any file on the system with specially-prepared malicious data.
CERT_C-FIO32-a.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

CERT_C-FIO34-a.title: The macro EOF should be compared with the unmodified return value from the Standard Library function [CERT_C-FIO34-a]
CERT_C-FIO34-a.description:
This rule detects comparisons of the EOF macro with the value returned
by a Standard Library function capable of returning EOF that has been
converted to another type before the comparison.


The list of function that are capable of returning EOF:
	int fclose ( FILE * stream )
	int fflush ( FILE * stream )
	int fgetc ( FILE * stream )
	int fputc ( int character, FILE * stream )
    int fputs ( const char * str, FILE * stream )
	int fputws (const wchar_t* ws, FILE* stream)
	int fscanf ( FILE * stream, const char * format, ... )
	int fwscanf (FILE* stream, const wchar_t* format, ...)
	int getc ( FILE * stream )
	int getchar ( void )
	int putc ( int character, FILE * stream )
	int putchar ( int character )
	int puts ( const char * str )
	int scanf ( const char * format, ... )
	int sscanf ( const char * s, const char * format, ...)
	int swscanf (const wchar_t* ws, const wchar_t* format, ...)
	int ungetc ( int character, FILE * stream )
	int vfscanf ( FILE * stream, const char * format, va_list arg )
	int vfwscanf (FILE* stream, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
	int vscanf ( const char * format, va_list arg )
	int vsscanf ( const char * s, const char * format, va_list arg )
	int vswscanf (const wchar_t* ws, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
	int wscanf (const wchar_t* format, ...)
	int wctob (wint_t wc)
	int wscanf (const wchar_t* format, ...)
CERT_C-FIO34-a.security_relevance:
"An EOF return value from these functions is used to indicate that a stream
is either at end-of-file or that a read or write error has occurred. The EOF
value may become indistinguishable from a valid character code if the value
returned is converted to another type. In such cases, testing the converted
value against EOF will not reliably identify if the end of file has been
reached or if an error has occurred". 
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 22.7]
CERT_C-FIO34-a.benefits:
This rule helps you ensure that the comparison between the EOF macro and
the value returned by the functions listed above is reliable.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 22.7
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.7 The macro EOF shall only be compared with the unmodified return value from any Standard Library function capable of returning EOF
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO34-C. Distinguish between characters read from a file and EOF or WEOF

CERT_C-FIO34.title: CERT-FIO34_C (RULE) Distinguish between characters read from a file and EOF or WEOF [CERT_C-FIO34]

CERT_C-FIO37-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [CERT_C-FIO37-a]
CERT_C-FIO37-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
CERT_C-FIO37-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-FIO37-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
CERT_C-FIO37-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

CERT_C-FIO38-a.title: A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced [CERT_C-FIO38-a]
CERT_C-FIO38-a.description:
"A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced directly or indirectly
(e.g. by a call to memcpy or memcmp)." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]

"The direct manipulation of a FILE object is prohibited as this may be
incompatible with its use as a stream designator." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]

This rule detects cases when a pointer to a FILE object is dereferenced
directly (i.e. used as the operand 'a' in expressions: '*a', 'a->b', 'a[b]').
CERT_C-FIO38-a.benefits:
"The Standard (C90 Section 7.9.3(6), C99 Section 7.19.3(6) ) states that the
address of a FILE object used to control a stream may be significant
and a copy of the object may not give the same behaviour. This rule ensures
that such a copy cannot be made." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]
CERT_C-FIO38-a.example_repair: Do not dereference pointers to FILE objects.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 22.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.5 A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO38-C. Do not copy a FILE object

CERT_C-FIO38.title: CERT-FIO38_C (RULE) Do not copy a FILE object [CERT_C-FIO38]

CERT_C-FIO39-a.title: Do not alternately input and output from a stream without an intervening flush or positioning call [CERT_C-FIO39-a]
CERT_C-FIO39-a.description:
"When a file is opened with update mode [...], both input and output may be performed 
on the associated stream. However, output shall not be directly followed by input 
without an intervening call to the fflush function or to a file positioning 
function (fseek, fsetpos, or rewind), and input shall not be directly followed 
by output without an intervening call to a file positioning function, unless 
the input operation encounters end-of-file."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2017]

This rule detects when a reading function is directly followed by a writing function,
or a writing function is directly followed by a reading function, without an intervening
call to a positioning function between these calls. 

A list of the writing functions:
	int fprintf(FILE* stream, const char* format, ...)
	int fputs(const char* str, FILE* stream)
	int fputc(int character, FILE* stream)
	int putc(int character, FILE* stream)
	size_t fwrite(const void* ptr, size_t size, size_t count, FILE* stream)
	ostream& ostream::operator<<()

A list of the reading functions:
	int fscanf(FILE* stream, const char* format, ...)	
	char* fgets(char* str, int num, FILE* stream)
	int fgetc(FILE* stream)
	int getc(FILE* stream)
	size_t fread(void* ptr, size_t size, size_t count, FILE* stream)
	istream& istream::operator>>()
	std::getline(istream& stream, basic_string& out);
	std::getline(istream& stream, basic_string& out, CharT delim)
	
A list of the positioning functions:
	int fseek(FILE* stream, long int offset, int origin)
	int fsetpos(FILE* stream, const fpos_t* pos)
	void rewind(FILE* stream)
	pos_type pubseekoff(off_type off, std::ios_base::seekdir dir, std::ios_base::openmode which = ios_base::in | ios_base::out)
	pos_type pubseekpos(pos_type pos, std::ios_base::openmode which = std::ios_base::in | std::ios_base::out)
    basic_istream& seekg(pos_type pos)
    basic_istream& seekg(off_type off, std::ios_base::seekdir dir)
    pos_type tellg()
    basic_ostream& seekp(pos_type pos)

CERT_C-FIO40-a.title: Reset strings on fgets() or fgetws() failure [CERT_C-FIO40-a]
CERT_C-FIO40-a.description:
"If either of the C Standard fgets() or fgetws() functions fail, the contents of the array being written 
is indeterminate. It is necessary to reset the string to a known value to avoid errors on subsequent string 
manipulation functions".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO40-C. Reset strings on fgets() or fgetws() failure]

This rule detects when arrays are used that were not reset after fgets() or fgetws() failed.
CERT_C-FIO40-a.security_relevance:
"Making invalid assumptions about the contents of an array modified by fgets() or fgetws() can result in 
undefined behavior and abnormal program termination".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO40-C. Reset strings on fgets() or fgetws() failure]
CERT_C-FIO40-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior (see SECURITY RELEVANCE).

CERT_C-FIO41-a.title: Do not call 'getc()', 'putc()', 'getwc()', or 'putwc()' with a stream argument containing assignments, increment or decrement operators [CERT_C-FIO41-a]
CERT_C-FIO41-a.description:
"Do not invoke 'getc()' or 'putc()' or their wide-character analogues 'getwc()'
and 'putwc()' with a stream argument that has side effects. The stream argument
passed to these macros may be evaluated more than once if these functions are
implemented as unsafe macros" [CERT-C-FIO41]

This rule detects when a stream argument passed to the call to the 'getc()',
'putc()', 'getwc()', or 'putwc()' functions or function-like macros contains
assignments, increment or decrement operators. The use of such operators might
cause unwanted side effects.
CERT_C-FIO41-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected and undefined behavior.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard

argument that has side effects

CERT_C-FIO41-b.description:
"Do not invoke getc() or putc() or their wide-character analogues getwc()
and putwc() with a stream argument that has side effects. The stream argument
passed to these macros may be evaluated more than once if these functions are

CERT_C-FIO42-a.title: Ensure resources are freed [CERT_C-FIO42-a]
CERT_C-FIO42-a.description:
This rule helps to ensure that allocated resources are deallocated on all paths.
Violations of the rule identify execution paths in an application where resources
are leaked.
CERT_C-FIO42-a.security_relevance:
Failure to close resources may lead to starvation, which could have
security implications.
CERT_C-FIO42-a.benefits:
Having a strict policy with respect to closing resources helps to make an application more
robust by preventing resource starvation.
CERT_C-FIO42-a.parameters:
The "Assume resource references can be stored by third-party methods of the following
types" set of parameters allows you to determine how strict the rule is when a reference
to a resource may be stored by a third-party function. For example, if there is a
third-party function SpecialCollection::add() and a resource is passed as a parameter
to this function, it will be stored in a collection and may later be closed by iterating
over the collection. Since Flow Analysis does not know the exact behavior of arbitrary
third-party functions, it behaves in either of the following ways:

   * With "Any functions" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that any third-party
     function may store a reference to a resource if the resource is passed into the function.
     This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource is later closed using
     the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are
     not reported as violations) if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
    
   * With "Functions that accept parameters which could host resource reference" parameter
     enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that a function may store a reference to a resource if
     it takes an additional parameter that can be used to store a pointer or a reference to
     the given resource. This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource
     is later closed using the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false
     negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations) if the function does not store
     a reference to the resource.
   
   * With "Functions with names matching the pattern" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis
     assumes that any third-party method which matches regex may store a reference to
     a resource if the resource is passed into the method. This prevents the rule from reporting
     false positives if the resource is later closed using the stored reference. However,
     this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations)
     if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
   
   * With all the parameters disabled, Flow Analysis takes a more aggressive approach and it
     assumes that third-party methods do not affect resources in any way. This approach
     may lead to some false positives, but it will not overlook any of the real leaks
     that may be missed with enabled parameters.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

CERT_C-FIO44-a.title: Only use values for fsetpos() that are returned from fgetpos() [CERT_C-FIO44-a]
CERT_C-FIO44-a.description:
"The C Standard, 7.21.9.3 [ISO/IEC 9899:2011], defines the following behavior for fsetpos():
  The fsetpos function sets the mbstate_t object (if any) and file position indicator for
  the stream pointed to by stream according to the value of the object pointed to by pos,
  which shall be a value obtained from an earlier successful call to the fgetpos function
  on a stream associated with the same file.
Invoking the fsetpos() function with any other values for pos is undefined behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO44-C. Only use values for fsetpos() that are returned from fgetpos()]

This rule detects the following cases:
 * the value passed to fsetpos() was not previously initialized by the call to fgetpos()
 * the value passed to fsetpos() was previously initialized by the call to fgetpos() using a different stream object
CERT_C-FIO44-a.security_relevance:
"Only the return value of an fgetpos() call is a valid argument to fsetpos(); passing a value
of type fpos_t that was created in any other way is undefined behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO44-C. Only use values for fsetpos() that are returned from fgetpos()]
CERT_C-FIO44-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent potential problems caused by undefined behavior.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO44-C. Only use values for fsetpos() that are returned from fgetpos()

CERT_C-FIO44.title: CERT-FIO44_C (RULE) Only use values for fsetpos() that are returned from fgetpos() [CERT_C-FIO44]

CERT_C-FIO45-a.title: Avoid race conditions while accessing files [CERT_C-FIO45-a]
CERT_C-FIO45-a.description:
"A program that performs two or more file operations on a single file name or path name creates 
a race window between the two file operations. This race window comes from the assumption that the
file name or path name refers to the same resource both times. If an attacker can modify the file,
remove it, or replace it with a different file, then this assumption will not hold".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO45-C. Avoid TOCTOU race conditions while accessing files]

This rule detects when the same file is being opened more than once, which may lead to race condition.
CERT_C-FIO45-a.security_relevance:
"An attacker can exploit the race window between the two calls to fopen()..." or other opening 
function "...to overwrite an existing file".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, FIO45-C. Avoid TOCTOU race conditions while accessing files]
CERT_C-FIO45-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid unexpected behavior and prevent hackers from accessing files
(see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
