CERT_C-FIO46-a.title: Do not use resources that have been freed [CERT_C-FIO46-a]
CERT_C-FIO46-a.description:
This rule detects uses of resources that have been freed. In particular, a violation is reported
when a pointer or reference to a freed resource is:

   * returned from a function,
   * passed as a parameter to a function,
   * used in an arithmetic operation,
   * dereferenced, or
   * assigned to a variable/field/array element.
CERT_C-FIO46-a.benefits: Prevents strange, often non-deterministic errors related to the use of freed resources.
CERT_C-FIO46-a.parameters:
The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to null" check box
unsupresses/suppresses  violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to a null
pointer. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to other pointers" check box
unsuppresses/suppresses violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to other
pointers. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

CERT_C-FIO47-a.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%s' and '%c' format specifiers in the format string and their corresponding arguments in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-FIO47-a]
CERT_C-FIO47-a.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule identifies a mismatch between the type specified by the format
specifier included in the string argument and the type of the corresponding
argument that is passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

The rule detects 'printf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%s" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a signed/unsigned/plain char or void type
- array of signed/unsigned/plain char type
- class that contains 'String' in its name and a member function conversion
  operator
- typedefs or references to the above types
- constant '0' (null)

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%c" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- signed/unsigned/plain char type
- typedefs or references to the above types
- integer constant whose value is positive and less than 256

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%ls" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to wchar_t type
- array of wchar_t type
- typedefs or references to the above types

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%lc" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- wchar_t or wint_t type
- typedefs or references to the above types

The rule detects 'scanf' function calls where:
a) a function argument that corresponds to a "%s" format specifier in
the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a signed/unsigned/plain char type
- array of signed/unsigned/plain char type
- typedefs or references to the above types

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%c" or "%[characters]"
format specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to plain char type
- array of plain char type
- typedefs or references to the above types

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%ls" or "%lc" format
specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to wchar_t type
- array of wchar_t type
- typedefs to the above types

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type
CERT_C-FIO47-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent abnormal program termination or unintended
information disclosure.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.19.6 Formatted input/output functions
2. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/printf/
3. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/scanf/
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO47-C. Use valid format strings
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL11-C. Understand the type issues associated with variadic functions
6. CWE-688: Function Call With Incorrect Variable or Reference as Argument

CERT_C-FIO47-c.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%i' and '%d' format specifiers in the string and their corresponding arguments in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-FIO47-c]
CERT_C-FIO47-c.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

than:
- parameter/variable of type signed int, signed long, short, char, enum,
  or bool
- expression of type int, long, short, char, enum, or bool

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%lli" or "%lld" format specifier

b) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hi" or "%hd" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to signed short type

c) a function argument that corresponds to a "%hhi" or "%hhd" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to signed char type

d) a function argument that corresponds to a "%lli" or "%lld" format specifier
in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to a signed long long type

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),

CERT_C-FIO47-d.description:

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following
conditions:
- their names include "printf" or "scanf", for example, printf(), fprintf(),
scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)
- they are variadic functions with the ellipsis preceded by a parameter of
string type
CERT_C-FIO47-d.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent abnormal program termination or unintended
information disclosure.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.19.6 Formatted input/output functions
2. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/printf/
3. http://www.cplusplus.com/reference/clibrary/cstdio/scanf/
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO47-C. Use valid format strings
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL11-C. Understand the type issues associated with variadic functions

CERT_C-FIO47-e.title: There should be no mismatch between the '%p' format specifier in the format string and its corresponding argument in the invocation of a string formatting function [CERT_C-FIO47-e]
CERT_C-FIO47-e.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule identifies a mismatch between the type specified by the format
specifier included in the string argument and the type of the corresponding
argument that is passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

The rule detects 'printf' function calls where a function argument that
corresponds to a "%p" format specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer type
- typedef to pointer type

The rule detects 'scanf' function calls where a function argument that
corresponds to a "%p" format specifier in the string argument is other than:
- pointer to pointer type
- typedef pointer to pointer type

This rule checks the string formatting functions that fulfill the following

scanf(), fscanf() (case insensitive)

string type

information disclosure.

CERT_C-FIO47-f.title: The number of format specifiers in the format string and the number of corresponding arguments in the invocation of a string formatting function should be equal [CERT_C-FIO47-f]
CERT_C-FIO47-f.description:
"The variable arguments to a variadic function are not checked for type by
the compiler. As a result, the programmer is responsible for ensuring that
they are compatible with the corresponding parameter after the default argument
promotions." [DCL11-C.]

This rule detects function calls where the number of format specifiers
in the argument of string type is different than the number of optional
arguments that are passed to the invocation of a string formatting function.

CERT_C-FLP00-a.title: Floating-point expressions shall not be tested for equality or inequality [CERT_C-FLP00-a]
CERT_C-FLP00-a.description:
"Floating-point expressions shall not be tested for equality or inequality.
The recommended method for achieving deterministic floating-point comparisons is
to write a library that implements the comparison operations. The library should
take into account the floating-point granularity (FLT_EPSILON) and the magnitude
of the numbers being compared."
CERT_C-FLP00-a.benefits:
"The inherent nature of floating-point types is such that comparisons of
equality will often not evaluate to true even when they are expected to. In
addition the behaviour of such a comparison cannot be predicted before
execution, and may well vary from one implementation to another. "
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-2-2. Floating-point expressions shall not be directly or indirectly
tested for equality or inequality
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.7.1. Do not write code that expects floating point calculations to yield
exact results
3. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS

systems, Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-2-2

needed

CERT_C-FLP03-a.title: Avoid division by zero [CERT_C-FLP03-a]
CERT_C-FLP03-a.description:
This rule detects execution paths where zero may potentially be used as a denominator
in a division operation. Division by zero may lead to divide-by-zero errors and should
be avoided.
CERT_C-FLP03-a.benefits:
Division by zero behavior is specified as undefinded in the language standard. For this
reason, it may result in divide-by-zero errors and abnormal program termination.
CERT_C-FLP03-a.parameters:
The "Sensitive mode" parameter allows you to enable reporting violations
when divisors are possibly equal to zero.
With this option enabled, Flow Analysis detects not only when the actual division
by zero occurs, but also when it may (but may not) occur. This results in more
violations being reported.

This option is disabled by default.
references: CWE-369: Divide By Zero

CERT_C-FLP03-b.title: Avoid implicit conversions from wider to narrower floating type [CERT_C-FLP03-b]
CERT_C-FLP03-b.description:
"C performs many type conversions implicitly and silently,
so as to harmonize types within an expression before evaluating it.
Some of these conversions can result in loss of information.
Such implicit conversions shall not be used, but explicit casts
should be used instead." [Misra C:1998 Rule 43]

The above applies also to the C++ standard.

This rule detects cases when the value of an expression of floating type
is assigned to an object with a narrower 'underlying type'.

The rule does not report a violation if a non-complex floating constant
is implicitly converted to a narrower floating type. These cases are reported
by the MISRA2004-10_2_d rule.

See also: MISRA-043, PORT-27, PORT-28
CERT_C-FLP03-b.benefits:
"Explicit casts should normally only be used in the case where a conversion
which could result in a loss of information is specifically required by the
programmer. If the static checking of implicit conversions is overridden by
the use of explicit casts in this way, then the programmer should be aware
of the issues of truncation and lost of precision associated with the operation,
and should provide appropriate checking of values in the code."
[Misra C:1998 Rule 43]

the size of the underlying type

narrower floating point type

CERT_C-FLP03-c.title: Avoid implicit conversions from narrower to wider floating type [CERT_C-FLP03-c]
CERT_C-FLP03-c.description:
"The value of an expression of floating type shall not be implicitly
converted to a different type if:
- it is not a conversion to a wider floating type, or
- the expression is complex, or
- the expression is a function argument, or
- the expression is a return expression."

The rule reports a violation if a conversion from narrower to wider floating
type is used and the converted expression is complex, function argument
or return expression.

Conversions from wider to narrower floating type are detected
by rule MISRA2004-10_2_b.
CERT_C-FLP03-c.benefits:
"By observing the principle whereby all operations are performed in
a consistent type, it is possible to avoid programmer confusion"
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 10
2. ISO/DIS 26262
point 8.4.4
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FLP03-C. Detect and handle floating-point errors

CERT_C-FLP03-d.title: Avoid implicit conversions of floating point numbers from wider to narrower floating type [CERT_C-FLP03-d]
CERT_C-FLP03-d.description:
"The value of an expression of floating type shall
not be implicitly converted to a different type if:
a) it is not a conversion to a wider floating type, or
b) the expression is complex, or
c) the expression is a function argument, or
d) the expression is a return expression."
[MISRA C:2004 Rule 10.2]

This rule detects cases when the floating point number is assigned
to an object with a narrower 'underlying type'.

"The term 'underlying type' is defined as describing the type that would
be obtained from evaluating an expression if it were not for the effects
of integral promotion." [MISRA C:2004 Rule 10.1]

The complete definition of the 'underlying type' is described
by the MISRA C:2004 (6.10.4 Underlying type) and the MISRA C++:2008
(6.5 Expressions - Underlying type) standards.

See also: MISRA-043, PORT-27, PORT-28
CERT_C-FLP03-d.benefits:
"An implicit conversion that results in the size of a type being reduced
may result in a loss of information." [MISRA C++:2008 Rule 5-0-6]

CERT_C-FLP06-a.title: Implicit conversions from integral to floating type which may result in a loss of information shall not be used [CERT_C-FLP06-a]
CERT_C-FLP06-a.description:
"C and C++ perform many type conversions implicitly and silently,
so as to harmonize types within an expression before evaluating it.
Some of these conversions can result in loss of information.
Such implicit conversions shall not be used, but explicit casts
should be used instead."

The rule reports a violation if an expression of integral type
is implicitly converted to a floating type and the loss of data
is possible. The rule checks sizes for types and reports violation
only if the size of integral type is greater or equal to size of
floating type.

See also: MISRA2004-10_1_b, MISRA2004-10_1_d, MISRA2004-10_2_a,
MISRA2004-10_2_b, PORT-27, PORT-28
CERT_C-FLP06-a.benefits:
"Explicit casts should normally only be used in the case where a conversion
which could result in a loss of information is specifically required by the
programmer. If the static checking of implicit conversions is overridden by
the use of explicit casts in this way, then the programmer should be aware
of the issues of truncation and lost of precision associated with the operation,
and should provide appropriate checking of values in the code"

CERT_C-FLP06-b.title: Implicit conversions from integral constant to floating type which may result in a loss of information shall not be used [CERT_C-FLP06-b]
CERT_C-FLP06-b.description:
"When a value of integer type is converted to a real floating type,
if the value being converted can be represented exactly in the new type,
it is unchanged. If the value being converted is in the range of values
that can be represented but cannot be represented exactly, the result
is either the nearest higher or nearest lower representable value,
chosen in an implementation-defined manner. If the value being converted
is outside the range of values that can be represented, the behavior
is undefined."

This rule assumes the representation of floating point numbers follows
specifation of IEEE 754 (IEC 60559), where single precision numbers ("float")
has significand with a precision of 24 bits, and double precision numbers
("double") has significand with a precision of 53 bits. This means that any
integer with absolute value less than 2^24 can be exactly represented in the
single precision format, and any integer with absolute value less than 2^53 can
be exactly represented in the double precision format.

This means that float and double types should be able to exactly represent
integer values in following ranges:
- float: from -16777215 to 16777215
- double: from -9007199254740991 to 9007199254740991

The rule reports a violation if an integer constant is implicitly converted to
a floating type and its value is not in the specified range (the loss of data
is possible).

Implicit conversion can happen when assigning, comparing or mixing integer and
floating types.

See also: MISRA2004-10_1_b, PORT-28
CERT_C-FLP06-b.benefits: The rule prevents possible loss of data.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A4-7-1. An integer expression shall not lead to data loss
2. IEEE Standard for Floating-Point Arithmetic (IEEE 754)
3. ISO/IEC 9899: 6.3.1.4 Real floating and integer, p. 2
4. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.23 Type Conversions, AV Rule 180
5. Origin: Misra Guidelines rule 43

FLP06-C. Convert integers to floating point for floating-point operations

FLP36-C. Preserve precision when converting integral values to floating-

CERT_C-FLP32-a.title: Validate values passed to library functions [CERT_C-FLP32-a]
CERT_C-FLP32-a.security_relevance:
Passing a value that is out of the expected range as a function parameter
violates API. This may lead to runtime errors.
CERT_C-FLP32-a.benefits:
This rule helps prevent API violations caused by passing an unexpected
parameter to a function. Such violations could cause runtime errors.
CERT_C-FLP32-a.parameters:
If the "Use heuristics to detect calls to functions from the ctype.h header" parameter
is enabled, the rule will use heuristics to determine whether the code contains any calls
to functions from the <ctype.h> header.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.11
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.11 The validity of values passed to library functions shall be checked

CERT_C-FLP34-a.title: Avoid implicit conversions from wider to narrower floating type [CERT_C-FLP34-a]

"C performs many type conversions implicitly and silently,
so as to harmonize types within an expression before evaluating it.
Some of these conversions can result in loss of information.
Such implicit conversions shall not be used, but explicit casts
should be used instead." [Misra C:1998 Rule 43]

The above applies also to the C++ standard.

This rule detects cases when the value of an expression of floating type
is assigned to an object with a narrower 'underlying type'.

The rule does not report a violation if a non-complex floating constant
is implicitly converted to a narrower floating type. These cases are reported
by the MISRA2004-10_2_d rule.

See also: MISRA-043, PORT-27, PORT-28
CERT_C-FLP34-a.benefits:
"Explicit casts should normally only be used in the case where a conversion
which could result in a loss of information is specifically required by the
programmer. If the static checking of implicit conversions is overridden by
the use of explicit casts in this way, then the programmer should be aware
of the issues of truncation and lost of precision associated with the operation,
and should provide appropriate checking of values in the code."
[Misra C:1998 Rule 43]

FLP36-C. Preserve precision when converting integral values to floating-
point type
6. CWE-704: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast

CERT_C-FLP36-b.title: Implicit conversions from integral constant to floating type which may result in a loss of information shall not be used [CERT_C-FLP36-b]
CERT_C-FLP36-b.description:
"When a value of integer type is converted to a real floating type,
if the value being converted can be represented exactly in the new type,
it is unchanged. If the value being converted is in the range of values
that can be represented but cannot be represented exactly, the result
is either the nearest higher or nearest lower representable value,
chosen in an implementation-defined manner. If the value being converted
is outside the range of values that can be represented, the behavior
is undefined."

This rule assumes the representation of floating point numbers follows
specifation of IEEE 754 (IEC 60559), where single precision numbers ("float")
has significand with a precision of 24 bits, and double precision numbers
("double") has significand with a precision of 53 bits. This means that any
integer with absolute value less than 2^24 can be exactly represented in the
single precision format, and any integer with absolute value less than 2^53 can
be exactly represented in the double precision format.

This means that float and double types should be able to exactly represent
integer values in following ranges:
- float: from -16777215 to 16777215
- double: from -9007199254740991 to 9007199254740991

The rule reports a violation if an integer constant is implicitly converted to
a floating type and its value is not in the specified range (the loss of data
is possible).

Implicit conversion can happen when assigning, comparing or mixing integer and
floating types.

See also: MISRA2004-10_1_b, PORT-28
CERT_C-FLP36-b.benefits: The rule prevents possible loss of data.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A4-7-1. An integer expression shall not lead to data loss
2. IEEE Standard for Floating-Point Arithmetic (IEEE 754)
3. ISO/IEC 9899: 6.3.1.4 Real floating and integer, p. 2
4. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.23 Type Conversions, AV Rule 180
5. Origin: Misra Guidelines rule 43
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FLP06-C. Convert integers to floating point for floating-point operations
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FLP36-C. Preserve precision when converting integral values to floating-
point type
8. CWE-704: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast

CERT_C-FLP36.title: CERT-FLP36_C (RULE) Preserve precision when converting integral values to floating-point type [CERT_C-FLP36]

CERT_C-FLP37-c.title: Do not use object representations to compare floating-point values [CERT_C-FLP37-c]
CERT_C-FLP37-c.description:
"Do not compare floating-point object representations directly, such as by
calling memcmp() or its moral equivalents. Instead, the equality operators
(== and !=) should be used to determine if two floating-point values are
equivalent." [CERT-FLP37-C]

"All floating-point object representations use specific bit patterns to encode
the value of the floating-point number being represented. However, equivalence
of floating-point values is not encoded solely by the bit pattern used to
represent the value." [CERT-FLP37-C]

This rule detects when the memcmp() or bcmp() functions compare objects that
are of the floating-point type or contain members that are of the floating-point
type.
CERT_C-FLP37-c.benefits:
Using object representations of floating-point values in comparison
may result incorrect equality results. This rule helps you ensure
that object represenations are not used to compare floating-point values.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Dir 0.3.1 Floating-point arithmetic should be used appropriately
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FLP37-C. Do not use object representations to compare floating-point values

CERT_C-FLP37.title: CERT-FLP37_C (RULE) Do not use object representations to compare floating-point values [CERT_C-FLP37]

CERT_C-INT02-a.title: Implicit conversions from wider to narrower integral type which may result in a loss of information shall not be used [CERT_C-INT02-a]
CERT_C-INT02-a.description:
"C and C++ perform many type conversions implicitly and silently,
so as to harmonize types within an expression before evaluating it.
Some of these conversions can result in loss of information.
Such implicit conversions shall not be used, but explicit casts
should be used instead."

The rule reports a violation if an expression of integral type
is implicitly converted to a narrower integral type.

See also: MISRA2004-10_1_b, MISRA2004-10_1_d, MISRA2004-10_2_a,
MISRA2004-10_2_b, PORT-27, PORT-28, PB-56
CERT_C-INT02-a.benefits:
"Explicit casts should normally only be used in the case where a conversion
which could result in a loss of information is specifically required by the
programmer. If the static checking of implicit conversions is overridden by
the use of explicit casts in this way, then the programmer should be aware
of the issues of truncation and lost of precision associated with the operation,
and should provide appropriate checking of values in the code"
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A4-7-1. An integer expression shall not lead to data loss
2. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.23 Type Conversions, AV Rule 180
3. Origin: Misra Guidelines rule 43
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT02-C. Understand integer conversion rules
5. CWE-196: Unsigned to Signed Conversion Error
6. CWE-704: Incorrect Type Conversion or Cast

CERT_C-INT02-b.title: Avoid mixing arithmetic of different precisions in the same expression [CERT_C-INT02-b]
CERT_C-INT02-b.description:
"C performs many type conversions implicitly and silently, so as to harmonize
types within an expression before evaluating it. Some of these conversions can
result in loss of information. Such implicit conversions shall not be used,
but explicit casts should be used instead.
As a general principle, avoid mixing arithmetic of different precisions in the
same expression."
The rule reports a violation if as operands of binary arithmetic, relational
equality or bitwise operator are used two non-constant operands of different
types.
See also: MISRA-043, CODSTA-99, CODSTA-100, MISRA2004-10_1_b, MISRA2004-10_2_a
CERT_C-INT02-b.benefits:
The rule prevents unexpected behaviours.
"Mixed arithmetic normally entails implicit promotions and balancing
of types (i.e. conversions), some of which can lead to unexpected behaviour."
references: 1. Origin: Misra Guidelines rule 43
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP14-C. Beware of integer promotion when performing bitwise operations on
integer types smaller than int
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT02-C. Understand integer conversion rules

CERT_C-INT04-a.title: Avoid potential integer overflow/underflow on tainted data [CERT_C-INT04-a]
CERT_C-INT04-a.description:
This rule detects cases when unvalidated input (tainted data) is used in an arithmetic
operation that can result in numeric overflow or underflow.

A numeric overflow is a condition that occurs when the mathematical result of an operation
exceeds the maximum value that can be represented by the result type of the operation.
A numeric underflow occurs when the result of an operation is smaller than the minimum value
that can be represented by the result type of the operation.

A violation is reported at the point of potential overflow.

See also BD-SECURITY-INTOVERF
CERT_C-INT04-a.security_relevance:
Integer overflow is a serious security threat as it may be used to crash an application or
cause denial of service.
CERT_C-INT04-a.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter allows you to enable or disable
reporting unvalidated violations. If enabled, Flow  Analysis does not check
whether the path of the violation can be reached from the beginning of
the function. Enabling this parameter may be useful in complex cases when
restricted analysis depth prevents Flow Analysis from completing the violation
validation procedure, but it may result in reporting false positives.

CERT_C-INT04-b.title: Avoid buffer read overflow from tainted data [CERT_C-INT04-b]
CERT_C-INT04-b.description:
The rule detects cases when code reads from a buffer and the read operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary.

Specifically, a violation is reported if the 'size' value passed to the function that reads from a buffer
comes from a tainting function (e.g. user input) that can return malicious data, but has not not been
checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types) and not greater than the size
of the buffer.
CERT_C-INT04-b.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-INT04-b.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

CERT_C-INT04-c.title: Avoid buffer write overflow from tainted data [CERT_C-INT04-c]
CERT_C-INT04-c.description:
The rule detects when code writes to a buffer and the write operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary.

Specifically, a violation is reported if a 'size' value passed to a function that writes to a buffer
comes from a tainting function (e.g., user input) that can return malicious data, but has not been 
checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types) and not greater than the size
of the buffer.
CERT_C-INT04-c.security_relevance:
A possible buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.

sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify

   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)

CERT_C-INT05-a.title: Avoid using unsafe string functions that do not check bounds [CERT_C-INT05-a]
CERT_C-INT05-a.description:
This rule detects code that uses unsafe string functions:
scanf, fscanf, sscanf, vscanf, vsscanf, vfscanf, realpath,
getopt, getpass, streadd, strecpy, strtrns, getwd, snprintf
According to David A. Wheeler (see reference below),
"C functions users must avoid using dangerous functions
that do not check bounds unless they've ensured
that the bounds will never get exceeded.
The function strlen() should be avoided
unless you can ensure that there will be a terminating NIL character to find.
The scanf() family (scanf(), fscanf(), sscanf(), vscanf(), vsscanf(),
and vfscanf()) is often dangerous to use;
do not use it to send data to a string without controlling the maximum length
(the format %s is a particularly common problem).
Other dangerous functions that may permit buffer overruns
(depending on their use) include realpath(), getopt(), getpass(), streadd(),
strncpy(), and strtrns().
You must be careful with getwd();
the buffer sent to getwd() must be at least PATH_MAX bytes long.
The select() helper macros FD_SET(), FD_CLR(), and FD_ISSET()
do not check that the index fd is within bounds;
make sure that fd >= 0 and fd <= FD_SETSIZE
(this particular one has been exploited in pppd).
Unfortunately, snprintf()'s variants have additional problems.
Officially, snprintf() is not a standard C function
in the ISO 1990 (ANSI 1989) standard,
though sprintf() is, so not all systems include snprintf()
others call sprintf directly what could make horrible thing."
See also: SECURITY-07,SECURITY-12, SECURITY-16
CERT_C-INT05-a.benefits: Prevents the use of functions which may cause overflows.
CERT_C-INT05-a.example_repair: Remove usage of unsafe string functions.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/dangers-c.html
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A27-0-4. C-style strings shall not be used.
3. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT05-C. Do not use input functions to convert character data if they
cannot handle all possible inputs
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR07-C. Use the bounds-checking interfaces for string manipulation

CERT_C-INT05.title: CERT-INT05_C (REC) Do not use input functions to convert character data if they cannot handle all possible inputs [CERT_C-INT05]

CERT_C-INT07-a.title: The plain char type shall be used only for the storage and use of character values [CERT_C-INT07-a]
CERT_C-INT07-a.description:
"There are three distinct char types, plain char, signed char and unsigned char.
Signed char and unsigned char shall be used for numeric data and plain char
shall be used for character data. The signedness of the plain char type
is implementation-defined and should not be relied upon.

The permissible operators on plain char types are the simple assignment
operator (=), equality operators( ==, != ) and explicit casts to integral
types. Additionally, the second and third operands of the ternary conditional
operator may both be of plain char type."

The rule reports a violation if parameter, variable or expression of plain char
type is used for numerical data. If a plain char is used as operand of operator
other than assignment (=), equality ( ==, != ), explicit cast or ternary
conditional operator then violation is reported as well.

See also: MISRA2004-6_2
CERT_C-INT07-a.benefits: Rule makes the code more readable and less confusing.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

character values

7.1.6. Use class types or typedefs to abstract scalar quantities and
standard integer types

systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-12
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT07-C. Use only explicitly signed or unsigned char type for numeric
values
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR00-C. Represent characters using an appropriate type
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR04-C. Use plain char for characters in the basic character set

CERT_C-INT07-b.title: signed and unsigned char type shall be used only for the storage and use of numeric values [CERT_C-INT07-b]
CERT_C-INT07-b.description:
"There are three distinct char types, plain char, signed char and unsigned char.
Signed char and unsigned char shall be used for numeric data and plain char
shall be used for character data. The signedness of the plain char type
is implementation-defined and should not be relied upon."

The rule reports a violation if signed char or unsigned char is used
for character data.

See also: MISRA2004-6_1

CERT_C-INT08-a.title: Avoid data loss when converting between integer types [CERT_C-INT08-a]
CERT_C-INT08-a.description:
This rule detects when casting an integer value to a narrower type results in
a data loss.
A violation is reported when:
* The given value cannot be represented in the result type, and
* The width of the result type is smaller than the width of the source type

See also BD-PB-INTVC
CERT_C-INT08-a.security_relevance:
Typically, converting an integer to a smaller type results in the truncation of
the high-order bits. If the high-order bits store some data, that data may be
lost. Therefore, the result of integer casting may be incorrect and data loss
makes it impossible to retrieve the correct result. This becomes
security-critical when the result is used to control looping, make a security
decision, or determine the offset or size in behaviors such as memory
allocation, copying, concatenation, etc.
CERT_C-INT08-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent data loss.
CERT_C-INT08-a.parameters:
"Aggressive mode" - when enabled, violations will also be reported when the
value being cast is -1.
This option is disabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

CWE-191: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)

CERT_C-INT08-b.title: Avoid signed integer overflows [CERT_C-INT08-b]
CERT_C-INT08-b.description:
An integer overflow occurs when an integer value is incremented to a value that
is too large to store in the associated representation. When this occurs for
signed integers, it causes undefined behavior.

Similarly, an integer underflow occurs when an integer value is decremented to
a value that is too small to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs for signed integers, it causes undefined behavior.

This rule reports a violation when an overflow or underflow occurs in the signed
integer arithmetic operation.

See also BD-PB-INTWRAP
CERT_C-INT08-b.security_relevance:
Signed integer overflow leads to undefined behavior.

Integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous if the result value is used in
a sensitive area, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
memory allocation function)
- Loop conditions
- In security-critical code
CERT_C-INT08-b.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
CERT_C-INT08-b.parameters:
'Violate on overflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
overflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is higher than the
upper boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.

'Violate on underflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
underflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is lower than the
lower boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-190: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
CWE-191: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)

CERT_C-INT08-c.title: Avoid value change when converting between integer types [CERT_C-INT08-c]
CERT_C-INT08-c.description:
This rule detects when casting an integer value between signed and unsigned
types results in a value change.

A violation is reported when:
* The given value cannot be represented in the result type, and
* The width of the result type is greater than or equal to the width of the
source type

See also BD-PB-INTDL
CERT_C-INT08-c.security_relevance:
Converting an integer value to a type with a different sign may result in an
unexpected value change. This becomes security-critical when the value is used
to control looping, make a security decision, or determine the offset or size in
behaviors such as memory allocation, copying, concatenation, etc.
CERT_C-INT08-c.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected value changes.
CERT_C-INT08-c.parameters:
"Aggressive mode" - when enabled, violations will also be reported when the
value being cast is -1.
This option is disabled by default.
references: CWE-195: Signed to Unsigned Conversion Error
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT08-C. Verify that all integer values are in range
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or misinterpreted data
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
4.2.2 Ensure that data loss does not demonstrably occur in an integral expression
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
Rule A4-7-1 An integer expression shall not lead to data loss

CERT_C-INT08-d.title: Avoid wraparounds when performing arithmetic integer operations [CERT_C-INT08-d]
CERT_C-INT08-d.description:
An integer overflow occurs when an integer value is incremented to a value that
is too large to store in the associated representation. When this occurs for
unsigned integers, the value can wrap to become a very small number.
For example: UINT_MAX + 1

Similarly, an integer underflow occurs when an integer value is decremented to
a value that is too small to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs for unsigned integers, the value can wrap to become a very large number.
For example: UINT_MIN - 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on
wrapping, it can have serious consequences if the wrap is unexpected.

This rule reports a violation when an overflow or underflow occurs in the
unsigned integer arithmetic operation.

See also BD-PB-INTUB
CERT_C-INT08-d.security_relevance:
Integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous if the result value is used in
a sensitive area, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
memory allocation function)
- Loop conditions
- In security-critical code
CERT_C-INT08-d.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected behavior (see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
CERT_C-INT08-d.parameters:
'Violate on overflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
overflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is higher than the
upper boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.

'Violate on underflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
underflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is lower than
zero.
This option is enabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

CWE-191: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)

CERT_C-INT09-a.description:
"In enumerator list, the '=' construct shall not be used
to explicitly initialize members other than the first,
unless all items are explicitly initialized."

enumerators shall be initialized

CERT_C-INT10-a.title: The operands of the remainder operator '%' should be of unsigned integer types [CERT_C-INT10-a]
CERT_C-INT10-a.description:
"In C89 (and historical K&R implementations), the meaning of the remainder
operator for negative operands was implementation-defined. This behavior was
changed in C99, and the change remains in C11. Because not all C compilers
are strictly C-conforming, programmers cannot rely on the behavior of the %
operator if they need to run on a wide range of platforms with many different
compilers." [CERT-INT10-C]

This rule detects when an operand of the remainder operator is of integer type
other than an unsigned type.
CERT_C-INT10-a.benefits:
"Incorrectly assuming that the result of the remainder operator for signed
operands will always be positive can lead to an out-of-bounds memory accessor
other flawed logic." [CERT-INT10-C]

This rule helps you avoid undefined and implementation-defined behavior.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT10-C. Do not assume a positive remainder when using the % operator

CERT_C-INT10.title: CERT-INT10_C (REC) Do not assume a positive remainder when using the % operator [CERT_C-INT10]

CERT_C-INT12-a.title: Bit fields shall only be defined to be of type unsigned int or signed int [CERT_C-INT12-a]
CERT_C-INT12-a.description:
"Bit fields shall only be defined to be of type unsigned int or signed int.
Using int is implementation-defined because bit fields of type int can be
either signed or unsigned. The use of enum, short or char types for bit fields
is not allowed because the behaviour is undefined." [MISRA C:2004]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 6.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 6.1 Bit-fields shall only be declared with an appropriate type
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6.4
4. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 111
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT12-C. Do not make assumptions about the type of a plain int bit-field
when used in an expression

CERT_C-INT12.title: CERT-INT12_C (REC) Do not make assumptions about the type of a plain int bit-field when used in an expression [CERT_C-INT12]

CERT_C-INT13-a.title: Operands of bitwise and complement operators shall have an unsigned type [CERT_C-INT13-a]
CERT_C-INT13-a.description:
This rule reports when the operands of bitwise operators and complement operator
do not have an unsigned type.

The following operators are checked:
* the binary bitwise operators and their compound versions: &, |, ^, &=, |=, ^=
* the unary complement operator ~
CERT_C-INT13-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid unexpected or undefined behavior.
The result of bit operations is only guaranteed to be defined
if the sign bit is not affected.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 7.0.4 The operands of bitwise operators and shift operators shall be appropriate
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT13-C. Use bitwise operators only on unsigned operands

CERT_C-INT13-b.title: Operands of shift operators shall have an unsigned type [CERT_C-INT13-b]
CERT_C-INT13-b.description:
This rule reports when operands of shift operators (<<, >>) and
their compound assignment versions (<<=, >>=) do not have an unsigned
type.
CERT_C-INT13-b.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid unexpected or undefined behavior.
The result of shift operations is only guaranteed to be defined
if the sign bit is not affected.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 7.0.4 The operands of bitwise operators and shift operators shall be appropriate
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT13-C. Use bitwise operators only on unsigned operands

CERT_C-INT13.title: CERT-INT13_C (REC) Use bitwise operators only on unsigned operands [CERT_C-INT13]

CERT_C-INT15-a.title: Use intmax_t or uintmax_t for formatted IO on programmer-defined integer types [CERT_C-INT15-a]
CERT_C-INT15-a.description:
"A programmer-defined integer type might be any type supported
by the implementation, even a type larger than unsigned long long . . .
Furthermore, the definition of programmer-defined types may change,
which creates a problem when these types are used with formatted
output functions, such as printf(), and formatted input functions,
such as scanf().

The C intmax_t and uintmax_t types can represent any value representable
by any other integer types of the same signedness. This capability allows
conversion between programmer-defined integer types of the same signedness
and intmax_t and uintmax_t.
Formatted I/O functions can be used to input and output greatest-width
integer typed values. The 'j' length modifier in a format string indicates
that the following 'd', 'i', 'o', 'u', 'x', 'X', or 'n' conversion specifier
will apply to an argument with type intmax_t or uintmax_t." [CERT INT15-C]

This rule detects when a programmer-defined integer type that is not based
on the standard types (signed or unsigned) char, short, int, long, and
long long is passed to the printf() or scanf() family of functions as
an argument to the '%i', '%d', and '%u' specifiers.
CERT_C-INT15-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent failures in use of an appropriate conversion
specifier when inputting or outputting programmer-defined integer types
which can result in buffer overflow and lost or misinterpreted data.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT15-C. Use intmax_t or uintmax_t for formatted IO on programmer-defined
integer types

CERT_C-INT15.title: CERT-INT15_C (REC) Use intmax_t or uintmax_t for formatted IO on programmer-defined integer types [CERT_C-INT15]

CERT_C-INT16-a.title: Bitwise operators shall only be applied to operands of unsigned underlying type (with exceptions) [CERT_C-INT16-a]
CERT_C-INT16-a.description:
This rule detects when the type of an operand of a bitwise operator (~, <<,
<<=, >>, >>=, &, &=, ^, ^=, |, |=) is different than the unsigned underlying
type.
CERT_C-INT16-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined and implementation-defined behavior.
CERT_C-INT16-a.example_repair:
void f1(signed short s1, unsigned short us1, unsigned short us2)
{
    us1 = us2 & (unsigned short)s1;   // OK
    us1 = (unsigned short)s1 | us2;   // OK
    us1 = ~us2;                       // OK
    us1 = us2 >> 7u;                  // OK
    us1 = 2u | 3u & 4u;               // OK
}
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-0-21. Bitwise operators shall only be applied to operands of unsigned
underlying type
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.6.1. Do not use bitwise operators with signed operands
3. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-21
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT16-C. Do not make assumptions about representation of signed integers

CERT_C-INT16-b.title: Bitwise operators shall not use positive integer literals as operands [CERT_C-INT16-b]
CERT_C-INT16-b.description:
This rule detects when an operand of a bitwise operator (~, <<, <<=, >>, >>=,
&, &=, ^, ^=, |, |=) is a positive signed integer-literal constant
or a positive enumeration constant.
CERT_C-INT16-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined and implementation-defined behavior.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-0-21. Bitwise operators shall only be applied to operands of unsigned
underlying type
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.6.1. Do not use bitwise operators with signed operands
3. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-21
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT16-C. Do not make assumptions about representation of signed integers

CERT_C-INT16.title: CERT-INT16_C (REC) Do not make assumptions about representation of signed integers [CERT_C-INT16]

CERT_C-INT18-a.title: Avoid possible integer overflow in expressions in which the result is assigned to a variable of a wider integer type [CERT_C-INT18-a]
CERT_C-INT18-a.description:
"If an exceptional condition occurs during the evaluation of an expression
(that is, if the result is not mathematically defined or not in the range
of representable values for its type), the behavior is undefined."
[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

"If an integer expression involving an operation is assigned to a larger
integer size, that integer expression should be evaluated in that larger
size by explicitly casting one of the operands." [CERT C: INT18-C]

If the result of integer multiplication, addition, subtraction,
or left-shift operation is converted to a wider integer type,
then unexpected integer overflow is possible.

This rule reports a violation if the result of multiplication, addition,
subtraction, or left-shift operation is implicitly converted from an
integer type to a wider integer type in a simple assignment statement or
during initialization.

If the result of integer multiplication, addition, subtraction,

CERT_C-INT18-c.title: Avoid possible integer overflow in expressions in which the result is cast to a wider integer type [CERT_C-INT18-c]
CERT_C-INT18-c.description:
"In an expression, sub-expressions are evaluated at the precision appropriate
to the types of the operands. This may be less than the precision of the final
result. It is therefore possible to be misled into creating sub-expressions
which are evaluated at the wrong precision, which may result in values which
are not as the programmer intended." [MISRA C:1998]

"If an integer expression involving an operation is assigned to a larger
integer size, that integer expression should be evaluated in that larger
size by explicitly casting one of the operands." [CERT C: INT18-C]

If the result of integer multiplication, addition, subtraction,
or left-shift operation is converted to a wider integer type,
then unexpected integer overflow is possible.

This rule reports a violation if the result of multiplication, addition,
subtraction or left-shift operation is explicitly cast from an integer
type to a wider integer type.

CERT_C-INT30-a.title: Avoid wraparounds when performing arithmetic integer operations [CERT_C-INT30-a]
CERT_C-INT30-a.description:
An integer overflow occurs when an integer value is incremented to a value that
is too large to store in the associated representation. When this occurs for
unsigned integers, the value can wrap to become a very small number.
For example: UINT_MAX + 1

Similarly, an integer underflow occurs when an integer value is decremented to
a value that is too small to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs for unsigned integers, the value can wrap to become a very large number.
For example: UINT_MIN - 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on
wrapping, it can have serious consequences if the wrap is unexpected.

This rule reports a violation when an overflow or underflow occurs in the
unsigned integer arithmetic operation.

See also BD-PB-INTUB
CERT_C-INT30-a.security_relevance:
Integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous if the result value is used in
a sensitive area, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
memory allocation function)
- Loop conditions
- In security-critical code
CERT_C-INT30-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected behavior (see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
CERT_C-INT30-a.parameters:
'Violate on overflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
overflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is higher than the
upper boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.

'Violate on underflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
underflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is lower than
zero.
This option is enabled by default.

CERT_C-INT30-b.title: Integer overflow or underflow in constant expression in '+', '-', '*' operator [CERT_C-INT30-b]
CERT_C-INT30-b.description:
An integer overflow or wraparound occurs when an integer value is incremented to
a value that is too large to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs, the value may wrap to become a very small or negative number.
For example: INT_MAX + 1

A similar situation happens when an integer value is decremented to a value that
is too small to store in the associated representation. When this occurs, the
value may wrap to become a very large or a positive number.
For example: INT_MIN - 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on wrapping, it
can have serious consequences if the wrap is unexpected.

See also: PB-66_b
CERT_C-INT30-b.security_relevance:
Unexpected integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.
CERT_C-INT30-b.benefits:
Unexpected integer overflow generally leads to undefined behavior that may
result in crashes. In the case of overflows involving loop index variables,
the likelihood of infinite loops is also high.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous, as the computed (incorrect)
value can be used in multiple sensitive areas, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
  square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array
  object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
  memory allocation function)
- In security-critical code

to wrap-around

CERT_C-INT30-c.description:
An integer overflow or wraparound occurs when an integer value is incremented to
a value that is too large to store in the associated representation. When this

For example: INT_MAX << 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on wrapping, it

CERT_C-INT30-c.security_relevance:

be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.
CERT_C-INT30-c.benefits:
Unexpected integer overflow generally leads to undefined behavior that may
result in crashes. In the case of overflows involving loop index variables,
the likelihood of infinite loops is also high.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous, as the computed (incorrect)
value can be used in multiple sensitive areas, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
  square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array
  object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a

- In security-critical code

CERT_C-INT31-a.title: An expression of essentially Boolean type should always be used where an operand is interpreted as a Boolean value [CERT_C-INT31-a]
CERT_C-INT31-a.description:
This rule detects cases when the following operands are expressed as integers,
or floating types different than "essentially Boolean" type:
  - the operand of the logical operator '!'
  - the right hand or the left hand operand of logical operators '&&' and '||'
  - the first operand of the conditional operator '?:'

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" type.
CERT_C-INT31-a.benefits:
This rule prevents confusion and helps distinguish between integers
and logical values.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-b.title: An operand of essentially Boolean type should not be used where an operand is interpreted as a numeric value [CERT_C-INT31-b]
CERT_C-INT31-b.description:
This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators
have "essentially Boolean" type:
[], +, +=, ++, --, -, -=, *, *=, /, /=, %, %=, <, >, <=, >=, >>, >>=,
<<, <<=, ~, &, &=, |, |=, ^, ^=
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-c.title: An operand of essentially character type should not be used where an operand is interpreted as a numeric value [CERT_C-INT31-c]
CERT_C-INT31-c.description:
"An operand of essentially character type should not be used where an operand
is interpreted as a numeric value. The numeric values of character data are
implementation-defined." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators
have have essentially character type:
[], +(unary), -(unary), *, /, %, >>, <<, ~, &, |, ^, +=, -=, /=, *=,
^=, |=, &=, <<=, >>=, %=

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
CERT_C-INT31-c.benefits: This rule prevents confusion and implementation-defined behaviours.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-d.title: An operand of essentially enum type should not be used in an arithmetic operation [CERT_C-INT31-d]
CERT_C-INT31-d.description:
"An operand of essentially enum type should not be used in an arithmetic
operation because an enum object uses an implementation-defined integer type.
An operation involving an enum object may therefore yield a result with an
unexpected type. Note that an enumeration constant from an anonymous enum has
essentially signed type." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators
have essentially enum type:
 +, -, *, /, %, >>, <<, ~, &, |, ^, ++, --, +=, -=, /=, *=, ^=, |=, &=, <<=,
 >>=, %=

This rule assumes that the enumeration type defined as "essentially Boolean"
type is not essentially enum type.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
CERT_C-INT31-d.benefits: This rule prevents confusion and implementation defined behaviours.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-e.title: Shift and bitwise operations should not be performed on operands of essentially signed or enum type [CERT_C-INT31-e]
CERT_C-INT31-e.description:
"Shift and bitwise operations should only be performed on operands
of essentially unsigned type. The numeric value resulting from their
use on essentially signed types may be undefined or implementation-defined."
[MISRA C:2012 with Technical Corrigendum 2 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects when the following operands have essentially signed
or essentially enum type:
- the left hand operand of the following operators: >>, <<, <<=, >>=
- the left hand or the right hand operand of the following operators:
  ~, &, |, ^, &=, |=, ^=

This rule assumes that:
- the enumeration type defined as "essentially Boolean" type is not
  essentially enum type.
- the signed type defined as "essentially Boolean" type is not
  essentially signed type.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard, but tools
may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially  Boolean" types.
See "Notes".
CERT_C-INT31-e.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent developer confusion and undefined
or implementation-defined behaviors.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2012 Technical Corrigendum 2, Technical clarification of MISRA C:2012
Section 3: Clarification of rules, 3.8 Rule 10.1
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-f.title: An operand of essentially signed or enum type should not be used as the right hand operand to the bitwise shifting operator [CERT_C-INT31-f]
CERT_C-INT31-f.description:
"The right hand operand of a shift operator should be of essentially unsigned
type to ensure that undefined behaviour does not result from a negative shift."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the right hand operand of a bitwise shift
operator (<<, >>, <<=, >>=) has essentially signed or essentially enum type.

This rule assumes that the enumeration type defined as "essentially Boolean"
type is not essentially enum type.

This rule assumes that the signed type defined as "essentially Boolean" type
is not essentially signed type.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-g.title: An operand of essentially unsigned type should not be used as the operand to the unary minus operator [CERT_C-INT31-g]
CERT_C-INT31-g.description:
"An operand of essentially unsigned type should not be used as the operand to
the unary minus operator, as the signedness of the result is determined by the
implemented size of int" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the operand of a unary minus operator
has "essentialy unsigned type".

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
CERT_C-INT31-g.benefits: The rule prevents unexpected results which may follow integer promotion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-h.title: The value of an expression shall not be assigned to an object with a narrower essential type [CERT_C-INT31-h]
CERT_C-INT31-h.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
assignments between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision. The use of stronger
typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model, reduces the likelihood
of these problems occurring." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.3]

This rule detects cases when the value of an expression is assigned to an
object with a narrower essential type. A violation is not reported if an
expression is converted to a different essential type category (such cases
are reported by the CODSTA-163_b rule).

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
CERT_C-INT31-h.benefits: This rule prevents unintended results and data loss.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.3
2. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.8 Rule 10.3
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.3 The value of an expression shall not be assigned to an object with a narrower essential type or of a different essential type category
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-i.title: The value of an expression shall not be assigned to an object of a different essential type category [CERT_C-INT31-i]
CERT_C-INT31-i.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
assignments between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision. The use of stronger
typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model, reduces the likelihood
of these problems occurring." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.3]

This rule detects cases when the value of an expression is assigned to an
object with a different essential type category or if the constant expression
in a switch statement's case label is converted to a different essential type
category.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
CERT_C-INT31-i.benefits: This rule prevents the loss of value, sign or precision.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-j.title: Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed shall have the same essential type category [CERT_C-INT31-j]
CERT_C-INT31-j.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
conversions between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision."

"Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions
are performed shall have the same essential type category. This rule applies
to operators that are described in usual arithmetic conversions. This includes
all the binary operators, excluding the shift, logical &&, logical || and comma
operators. The increment and decrement operators are not covered by this rule."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators have
different essential types:
+(binary), -(binary), *, /, %, <, >, <=, >=, !=, ==, &(binary), |, ^, +=, -=,
/=, *=, ^=, |=, &=, %=

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
CERT_C-INT31-j.benefits:
"The use of stronger typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model,
allows implicit conversions to be restricted to those that should then produce
the answer expected by the developer." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.4

Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features

3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.4 Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed shall have the same essential type category
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

CERT_C-INT31-k.title: The second and third operands of the ternary operator shall have the same essential type category [CERT_C-INT31-k]
CERT_C-INT31-k.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
conversions between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision."

"Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions
are performed shall have the same essential type category. The second
and third operands of the ternary operator are covered by this rule."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]

This rule detects cases when the second and third operands of the ternary
operator have different essential types.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
CERT_C-INT31-k.benefits:
"The use of stronger typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model,
allows implicit conversions to be restricted to those that should then produce
the answer expected by the developer." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]
