CERT_C-MSC17-a.title: Missing break statement between cases in a switch statement [CERT_C-MSC17-a]
CERT_C-MSC17-a.description:
A switch statement consists of several 'case' labels, plus an optional 'default'
label. A series of statements following those labels conventionally ends with
a break statement; if omitted, control flow falls through to the next label in
the switch statement block. Because the break statement is not required,
omitting it does not produce compiler diagnostics. If the omission was
unintentional, it can result in an unexpected control flow.

This rule reports a violation if there is no statement which would break
the control flow between case or default labels. The following statements
are recognized as breaking the control flow:
- break, return
- exit(), abort(), _Exit()
- continue, goto, throw (if those statements cause control flow to jump out
  of the switch label)

The rule also recognizes a "fall through" comment placed at the end of the case.
The regular expression recognized is: falls?.?thro?u (ignoring case).
(e.g. following comments are acceptable: 'falls through', 'fall through',
 'fall thru', 'FALLS THROUGH', 'FALL THRU')

No violation is reported on the last label.

See also: CODSTA-56, CODSTA-64, MISRA2004-15_2
CERT_C-MSC17-a.benefits:
Following this rule helps prevent logic errors and unexpected application
behavior.
references: 1. CWE-484: Omitted Break Statement in Switch
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC17-C. Finish every set of statements associated with a case label with a
break statement

CERT_C-MSC17.title: CERT-MSC17_C (REC) Finish every set of statements associated with a case label with a break statement [CERT_C-MSC17]

CERT_C-MSC19-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [CERT_C-MSC19-a]
CERT_C-MSC19-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
CERT_C-MSC19-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-MSC19-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
CERT_C-MSC19-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

CERT_C-MSC19-b.title: Avoid null pointer dereferencing [CERT_C-MSC19-b]
CERT_C-MSC19-b.description: This rule detects cases when a null pointer may be dereferenced.
CERT_C-MSC19-b.parameters:
The "Reporting violations where variable is known to be null due to a null check" group of parameters:

Enable or disable this check box to specify which violations are reported:
   * If disabled, the rule will report violations only on variables that are certain to be assigned with a null value.
   * If enabled, there rule will report violations not only for null value assignment, 
     but also when a check for a null value that stipulates null pointer dereferencing at the point of use is detected.

Here is an example of the second type of violations:

    int b = (ptr == 0); /* null check that stipulates null dereferencing at violation point */
    /* ... */
    if (b) {
        /* ... */
        int i = *ptr; /* violation reported if the check box is ticked */
    }

The "Exclude cases when a null check is performed inside:" set of parameters helps you reduce the number of
unwanted violations caused by a null check. For cases when a function or a macro performs a null check,
these parameters determine whether it should be assumed that the corresponding variable can be null
in the callers of the function.

The "a macro invocation" parameter determines how the rule handles null checks in macros.
Consider the following example:
 
    #define FOO(P) if (P == 0) { /* ... */ }
    
    void bar(int* p)
    {
        FOO(p);
        *p = 10; /* Violation or not? */
    }
    
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported for the above example.   

Other parameters in this set are related to the visibility of functions.
Consider the following example:

    int* foo(int* p)
    {
        if (p == 0) {
            /* ... */
        }
        return p; 
    }
    
    void bar(int* p)
    {
        foo(p);
        *p = 10; /* Violation or not? */
    }

Whether the violation inside bar() will be reported or not depends on the visibility of foo()'s 
and parameterization of the rule.

"a called function with internal linkage"
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported in the above example if the foo() function is 
a function with internal linkage (a function that is visible only in the compilation unit where it is defined).
Static global functions and functions from anonymous namespaces are functions with internal linkage. 

"a called global function"
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported in the above example if the foo() function is
a global non-member function. 

"a called member function of the following visibility:"
This parameter defines the behavior of the rule for cases where foo() is a member function.
If this option is disabled, a violation will always be reported in the above example if foo() is a member function.
If this option is enabled, the following radio group of parameters is available:

   * "public" 
   * "public, protected"
   * "any visibility"

The table below shows all the possible options of the radio group of parameters. Each row represents one of
the parameters. The "+" character indicates that a violation will be reported in the above example.

                                  member function visibility (foo)
Parameterization:                 | public | protected | private |
"public"                          |    -   |     +     |   +     |
"public, protected"               |    -   |     -     |   +     |
"any visibility"                  |    -   |     -     |   -     |



The "Functions that do not accept NULL as their parameters" table allows you to list functions
whose parameters are not allowed to accept null.

The "External functions that may return NULL" table allows you to handle external functions
that can potentially return null.
To achieve better results, the rule provides a predefined set of such null returners from
standard libraries. Those predefined functions are used by default. They cannot be edited
or removed from the list, but they can be enabled/disabled in the "Enabled" column. Select or clear
the check box next to the function name to enable or disable the function.
Additionally, you can extend the set of null returners to include additional functions that
you assume may return null. 

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.

* The 'Non-NULL parameters' column allows you to specify 
  the list of ordinal numbers of the function parameters that are not allowed to accept null.
  List the indexes of parameters separated by a comma or use '*' to specify that all parameters are affected.

A step-by-step example of how to create your own null returner is provided in the 'EXAMPLE' 
section of this help page.

The "Treat "dynamic_cast" conversion as a source of the null value" parameter allows you
to specify if "dynamic_cast" conversion will be considered to be a source of null. 
This option is enabled by default.

class A {virtual void f();};  
class B : public A {void bar();}; 

static void foo(A* p)
{
    B* d = dynamic_cast<B*>(p); // the source of null if the option is enabled
    d->bar(); // a violation is reported if the option is enabled
}

CERT_C-MSC22-a.title: The facilities provided by <setjmp.h> should not be used [CERT_C-MSC22-a]
CERT_C-MSC22-a.description:
"The standard header file <setjmp.h> shall not be #included, and none of the
features that are specified as being provided by <setjmp.h> shall be used."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 21.4 with Amendment 3]

"'setjmp' and 'longjmp' allow the normal function call mechanisms to be
bypassed. Their use may lead to undefined and unspecified behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012]

The rule detects the use of the following facilities:
- the 'longjmp' function
- the 'jmp_buf' type
- the 'setjmp' macro
CERT_C-MSC22-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent implementation-defined and undefined behavior.
CERT_C-MSC22-a.example_repair: Do not use the facilities from the <setjmp.h> header.

CERT_C-MSC24-a.title: The 'atof', 'atoi', 'atol' and 'atoll' functions from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library should not be used [CERT_C-MSC24-a]
CERT_C-MSC24-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'atof()', 'atoi()', 'atol()' or 'atoll()' global
function declared with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib'
standard library, or locally by the user) is called or when its address
is taken.
CERT_C-MSC24-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior. Using these functions results in
undefined behavior when the string cannot be converted.

shall be checked

CERT_C-MSC24-b.title: The 'getenv()' function from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library shall not be used [CERT_C-MSC24-b]
CERT_C-MSC24-b.description:
"The library functions 'abort', 'exit', 'getenv' and 'system' from library
<stdlib.h> shall not be used." [MISRA-C:2004]
"The library functions 'abort', 'exit', 'getenv' and 'system' from library
<cstdlib> shall not be used. The use of these functions leads to
implementation-defined behaviour." [MISRA C++:2008]

This rule detects calls to the 'getenv()' function from the 'stdlib.h'
and 'cstdlib' standard libraries.

See also: CODSTA-185_a, BD-PB-INVRET, SECURITY-48_b, PB-75, PB-75_b

CERT_C-MSC24-c.title: Avoid using unsafe string functions which may cause buffer overflows [CERT_C-MSC24-c]
CERT_C-MSC24-c.description:
This rule detects code that uses unsafe string functions from C library:
strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets
According to David A. Wheeler (see reference below),
"C functions users must avoid using dangerous functions
that do not check bounds unless they've ensured
that the bounds will never get exceed.
Functions to avoid in most cases (or ensure protection) include the functions
strcpy(), strcat(), sprintf() (with cousin vsprintf()), and gets().
These should be replaced with functions such as
strncpy(), strncat(), snprintf(), fgets(), respectively."
See also; SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13, SECURITY-16
CERT_C-MSC24-c.benefits: Prevents the use of functions which may cause buffer overflows.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/dangers-c.html
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API01-C. Avoid laying out strings in memory directly before sensitive data
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
API02-C. Functions that read or write to or from an array should take an
argument to specify the source or target size
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV01-C. Do not make assumptions about the size of an environment variable
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC24-C. Do not use deprecated or obsolescent functions
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR31-C. Guarantee that storage for strings has sufficient space for
character data and the null terminator

CERT_C-MSC24-d.title: Don't use unsafe C functions that do write to range-unchecked buffers [CERT_C-MSC24-d]
CERT_C-MSC24-d.description:
Don't use the unsafe C legacy: strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets,
strncpy, strncat, snprintf, fgets - functions that do write to range-unchecked
buffers, and/or do not check and correctly handle out-of-bounds errors.
See also: SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13,SECURITY-16
CERT_C-MSC24-d.benefits: Rule prevents using functions which may cause overflow.
CERT_C-MSC24-d.example_repair: Don't use the unsafe C legacy.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A27-0-4. C-style strings shall not be used.
2. Herb Sutter, Andrei Alexandrescu, "C++ Coding Standards," Addison-Wesley,
(C) 2005 Pearson Education, Inc.
Chapter: "Type Safety", Rule 99

Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications

argument to specify the source or target size

CERT_C-MSC30-a.title: Do not use the rand() function for generating pseudorandom numbers [CERT_C-MSC30-a]
CERT_C-MSC30-a.description:
"The C Standard rand() function makes no guarantees as to the quality
of the random sequence produced. The numbers generated by some
implementations of rand() have a comparatively short cycle and the numbers
can be predictable." [CERT-MSC30-C]

This rule detects when the rand() function from the C standard library
or the std::rand() function from the C++ standard library are used.
CERT_C-MSC30-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid using the rand() function, which produces predictable
numbers and has limited randomness.
The rand() function uses the last generated number as the seed to create
the next number. This may enable an attacker to predict or calculate
the following numbers and gain access to privileged data.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A26-5-1. Pseudorandom numbers shall not be generated using std::rand()
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC30-C. Do not use the rand() function for generating pseudorandom numbers
3. SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
MSC50-CPP. Do not use std::rand() for generating pseudorandom numbers
4. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-002290: The application must use the Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 140-2-validated cryptographic modules
and random number generator if the application implements encryption, key exchange, digital signature, and hash functionality

CERT_C-MSC30.title: CERT-MSC30_C (RULE) Do not use the rand() function for generating pseudorandom numbers [CERT_C-MSC30]

CERT_C-MSC32-d.title: Properly seed pseudorandom number generators [CERT_C-MSC32-d]
CERT_C-MSC32-d.description:
"Initializing random number engines by default initializes pseudo-random
generator with a default_seed constant value. However, this can be not obvious
for a developer and can lead to unexpected program behaviour." [AUTOSAR A26-5-2]

If a pseudorandom number generator is called with the same initial state, it
generates an identical sequence of random numbers in different runs of a program.
This enables an attacker to predict the sequence of random numbers that will be
generated in the future runs. For this reason, calling the 'rand' and 'random'
functions should be preceded with a call to 'srand' or 'srandom' respectively,
which should take a different argument every time a program is run. 

This rule detects when:
- a call to 'rand' or 'random' is not preceded by a call to 'srand' or 'srandom'
  respectively.
- 'srand' or 'srandom' is called with the same argument in different runs
  of a program.
- a pseudo-random number engine from the C++ standard library is initialized with
  a 'default_seed' constant value.
CERT_C-MSC32-d.security_relevance:
"If the PRNG is subsequently seeded with the same initial seed value, then it
will generate the same sequence. As a result, after the first run of
an improperly seeded PRNG, an attacker can predict the sequence of random
numbers that will be generated in the future runs. Improperly seeding or
failing to seed the PRNG can lead to vulnerabilities, especially in security
protocols". [MSC32-C.]
CERT_C-MSC32-d.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid vulnerabilities by preventing an attacker form
predicting the random numbers that will be generated in the future runs
of the program.
references: OWASP Top 10-2021
A02:2021 - Cryptographic Failures
OWASP Top 10 2017
A3-Sensitive Data Exposure
OWASP API Security Top 10-2019
API3-Excessive Data Exposure
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC32-C. Properly seed pseudorandom number generators
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
MSC51-CPP. Ensure your random number generator is properly seeded
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
Rule A26-5-2 Random number engines shall not be default-initialized.
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-002050: Applications making SAML assertions must use FIPS-approved random numbers in the generation of SessionIndex in the SAML element AuthnStatement.

APSC-DV-002290: The application must use the Federal Information Processing Standard (FIPS) 140-2-validated cryptographic modules and random number generator if the application implements encryption, key exchange, digital signature, and hash functionality.

CERT_C-MSC32.title: CERT-MSC32_C (RULE) Properly seed pseudorandom number generators [CERT_C-MSC32]

CERT_C-MSC33-a.title: The 'asctime()' and 'asctime_r()' functions should not be used [CERT_C-MSC33-a]
CERT_C-MSC33-a.description:
"The POSIX Base Specifications [IEEE Std 1003.1:2013] says the following about
the 'asctime()' and 'asctime_r()' functions:
These functions are included only for compatibility with older implementations.
They have undefined behavior if the resulting string would be too long, so the
use of these functions should be discouraged. On implementations that do not
detect output string length overflow, it is possible to overflow the output
buffers in such a way as to cause applications to fail, or possible system
security violations. Also, these functions do not support localized date
and time formats. To avoid these problems, applications should use 'strftime()'
to generate strings from broken-down times." [SEI CERT C Coding Standard]

This rule detects when the 'asctime()' and 'asctime_r()' functions are used.

See also: MISRA2004-20_12, MISRA2004-20_12_b, MISRA2004-20_12_c
CERT_C-MSC33-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
CERT_C-MSC33-a.example_repair:
You can improve your code by removing the 'asctime()' and 'asctime_r()'
functions or replacing them with the 'asctime_s()' function.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC33-C. Do not pass invalid data to the asctime() function
2. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
7.27.3.1 The asctime function

CERT_C-MSC33.title: CERT-MSC33_C (RULE) Do not pass invalid data to the asctime() function [CERT_C-MSC33]

CERT_C-MSC37-a.title: All exit paths from a function, except main(), with non-void return type shall have an explicit return statement with an expression [CERT_C-MSC37-a]
CERT_C-MSC37-a.description:
"All exit paths from a function with non-void return type shall have
an explicit return statement with an expression.
In a function with non-void return type, return expression gives the value
that the function returns. The absence of a return with an expression leads to
undefined behavior (and the compiler may not give an error)." [AUTOSAR A8-4-2]

This rule detects functions, othen than main(), that fail to return a value
from all exit paths.
CERT_C-MSC37-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A8-4-2. All exit paths from a function with non-void return type shall have
an explicit return statement with an expression
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.3.2. Ensure that execution of a function with a non-void return type ends
in a return statement with a value
3. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
4. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems

5. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

7. Programming languages -- C++ ISO/IEC 14882:2011
3.6.1 Main function
8. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC37-C. Ensure that control never reaches the end of a non-void function
9. SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
MSC52-CPP. Value-returning functions must return a value from all exit
paths

CERT_C-MSC38-a.title: A function-like macro shall not be invoked without all of its arguments [CERT_C-MSC38-a]
CERT_C-MSC38-a.description:
"This is a constraint error, but preprocessors have been known to ignore this
problem. Each argument in a function-like macro must consist of at least one
preprocessing token otherwise the behaviour is undefined."
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19

be implemented as a macro

CERT_C-MSC39-a.title: Use macros for variable arguments correctly [CERT_C-MSC39-a]
CERT_C-MSC39-a.description:
This rule detects when a variable argument list is used incorrectly. 
The rule assumes that the following uses are incorrect:
- Calling va_start() with an incorrect object as the second argument:
	* object other than the last named parameter of the function
	* object of a reference type
	* object of a type that undergoes default argument promotion
	* object of a class type that has a nontrivial copy constructor, 
	  a nontrivial move constructor, or a nontrivial destructor
- Calling functions that require initialized object with an uninitialized va_list object: 
  va_arg(), va_copy() - as the second argument, va_end(), the library methods (listed below).
- Calling functions that require an uninitialized object with an initialized va_list object: 
  va_start(), va_copy() - as the first argument.
- Failing to terminate an initialized va_list object with a call to va_end().

List of checked library methods using initialized va_list object as argument :
- void verr(int eval, const char* format, va_list arg)
- void verrx(int eval, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vfprintf(FILE* stream, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vfscanf(FILE* stream, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vfwprintf(FILE* stream, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
- int vfwscanf (FILE* stream, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
- int vprintf(const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vscanf(const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vslogf(int opcode, int severity, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vsnprintf(char* s, size_t n, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vsprintf(char* s, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vsscanf(const char* s, const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vswprintf(wchar_t* ws, size_t len, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
- int vswscanf(const wchar_t* ws, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
- void vsyslog(int priority, const char* format, va_list arg)
- void vwarn(const char* format, va_list arg)
- void vwarnx(const char* format, va_list arg)
- int vwprintf(const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
- int vwscanf(const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
CERT_C-MSC39-a.parameters:
The "Aggressive mode" encourages Flow Analysis to report a violation any time that Flow Analysis 
suspects a problem - even if there is high probability that such a case may be a false positive.
Applying this configuration will result in more bugs being reported, but it can also increase
the number of false alarms. 
For this rule for some compilers (e.g., gcc) aggressive mode is recommended, as it should
find more good violations. If you are getting false positives with this rule, switch off aggressive
mode. By default it is turned off.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC39-C. Do not call va_arg() on a va_list that has an indeterminate value
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
EXP58-CPP. Pass an object of the correct type to va_start

CERT_C-MSC39.title: CERT-MSC39_C (RULE) Do not call va_arg() on a va_list that has an indeterminate value [CERT_C-MSC39]

CERT_C-MSC40-a.title: An inline definition of a function with external linkage shall not contain definitions and uses of static objects [CERT_C-MSC40-a]
CERT_C-MSC40-a.description:
"An inline definition of a function with external linkage shall not contain
a definition of a modifiable object with static or thread storage duration,
and shall not contain a reference to an identifier with internal linkage."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"[I]f a function has an external and inline definition, implementations
are free to choose which definition to invoke (two distinct invocations
of the function may call different definitions, one the external definition,
the other the inline definition). Therefore, issues can arise when these
definitions reference internally linked objects or mutable objects with static
or thread storage duration." [CERT-MSC40-C]

This rule detects when inline functions with external linkage contain
one of the following:
- a definition of a modifiable object with static storage duration
- a reference to an identifier with internal linkage
CERT_C-MSC40-a.benefits:
The rule helps you prevent unspecified behavior.

"An inline definition provides an alternative to an external definition,
which a translator may use to implement any call to the function in the same
translation unit. It is unspecified whether a call to the function uses
the inline definition or the external definition." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"Since an inline definition is distinct from the corresponding external
definition and from any other corresponding inline definitions in other
translation units, all corresponding objects with static storage duration
are also distinct in each of the definitions." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC40-C. Do not violate constraints
2. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.7.4 Function specifiers

CERT_C-MSC41-a.title: Do not hard code string literals [CERT_C-MSC41-a]
CERT_C-MSC41-a.description:
"Hard coding sensitive information, such as passwords or encryption keys can
expose the information to attackers. Anyone who has access to the executable
or dynamic library files can examine them for strings or other critical data,
revealing the sensitive information.
Hard coding sensitive information also increases the need to manage
and accommodate changes to the code." [CERT-MSC41-C]

This rule detects string literals that are embedded directly in code.
The rule does not check if detected strings contain sensitive information.
CERT_C-MSC41-a.benefits:
Attackers can easily extract information from hard-coded strings.
This rule improves code security by helping you avoid hard-coding
string literals.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MSC41-C. Never hard code sensitive information
2. 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-798: Use of Hard-coded Credentials
3. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-002485: The application must not store sensitive information in hidden fields
APSC-DV-003110: The application must not contain embedded authentication data
APSC-DV-003280: Default passwords must be changed

CERT_C-MSC41.title: CERT-MSC41_C (RULE) Never hard code sensitive information [CERT_C-MSC41]

CERT_C-POS30-a.title: Avoid overflow due to reading a not zero terminated string [CERT_C-POS30-a]
CERT_C-POS30-a.description:
The rule detects cases of possible overflow due to running a string function on a string that
was not properly zero terminated.

The rule will report violation when the parameter passed to certain functions(see PARAMETERS section)
is not zero terminated. There are cases when such behavior may not lead to overflow or undefined
behavior(e.g. using 'snprintf' function with specific format and correct maximum number of bytes).
CERT_C-POS30-a.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
references: CWE-665: Improper Initialization

CERT_C-POS30-b.title: The values returned by functions 'read' and 'readlink' shall be used [CERT_C-POS30-b]
CERT_C-POS30-b.description:
"Traditionally, strings are represented as a region of memory containing
data terminated with a NULL character. Older string-handling methods frequently
rely on this NULL character to determine the length of the string. If a buffer
that does not contain a NULL terminator is passed to one of these functions,
the function will read past the end of the buffer." [CWE-170]

Functions 'read', 'readlink' read data and place it into a buffer, but do not
append the null character at the end. Thus, the content of the buffer might not
be properly null-terminated. Passed such buffer to a function which assumes
a null-terminated string (for example strlen, strcpy) might cause application
crashes or other serious problems.

Functions 'read', 'readlink' upon successful completion return the count
of bytes placed in the buffer. This value can be used to check if the length
of string read into buffer is correct.

This rule reports a violation if 'read' or 'readlink' are called, but the return
value is not used in any way. The return value should be, for example, assigned
to a variable, returned from a function, checked in selection or iteration
statement, passed to a function etc.
CERT_C-POS30-b.benefits:
This rule detects code which ignores 'read' or 'readlink' return value, which
could result in a string buffer being not null-terminated.

"If a null character is omitted from a string, then most string-copying functions
will read data until they locate a null character, even outside of the intended
boundaries of the string. This could:
 - cause a crash due to a segmentation fault
 - cause sensitive adjacent memory to be copied and sent to an outsider
 - trigger a buffer overflow when the copy is being written to a fixed-size
   buffer" [CWE-170]
references: 1. CWE-170: Improper Null Termination
cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/170.html
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS30-C. Use the readlink() function properly
3. 'readlink' specification
4. 'read' specification

CERT_C-POS30-c.title: Use of possibly not null-terminated string with functions expecting null-terminated string [CERT_C-POS30-c]
CERT_C-POS30-c.description:
"Traditionally, strings are represented as a region of memory containing
data terminated with a NULL character. Older string-handling methods frequently
rely on this NULL character to determine the length of the string. If a buffer
that does not contain a NULL terminator is passed to one of these functions,
the function will read past the end of the buffer." [CWE-170]

This rule reports a violation if it detects that:
* a buffer was created by a function which is known to create not
  null-terminated buffers (for example 'readlink', 'read', 'fread'), and
* the buffer was passed to a function which is known to expect a null-terminated
  buffer (for example 'strlen', 'strcpy', 'rename'), and
* the buffer was not used between those two calls, and
* the buffer was not initialized by null characters '\0'
CERT_C-POS30-c.benefits:
"If a null character is omitted from a string, then most string-copying functions
will read data until they locate a null character, even outside of the intended
boundaries of the string. This could:
 - cause a crash due to a segmentation fault
 - cause sensitive adjacent memory to be copied and sent to an outsider
 - trigger a buffer overflow when the copy is being written to a fixed-size
   buffer" [CWE-170]
references: 1. CWE-170: Improper Null Termination
cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/170.html
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS30-C. Use the readlink() function properly

CERT_C-POS30.title: CERT-POS30_C (RULE) Use the readlink() function properly [CERT_C-POS30]

CERT_C-POS33-a.title: Avoid using the 'vfork()' function [CERT_C-POS33-a]
CERT_C-POS33-a.description:
"Using the 'vfork()' function introduces many portability and security issues.
There are many cases in which undefined and implementation-specific behavior
can occur, leading to a denial-of-service vulnerability." [CERT-POS33-C]

This rule detects the use of the 'vfork()' function from the standard library.
The 'fork()' function should be used instead of 'vfork()' in all cases.
CERT_C-POS33-a.benefits:
This rule prevents using the 'vfork()' function, which may lead to race
conditions or a denial-of-service vulnerability.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/avoid-vfork.html
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS33-C. Do not use vfork()
4. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions
APSC-DV-002400: The application must restrict the ability to launch Denial of Service (DoS)
attacks against itself or other information systems
APSC-DV-003320: Protections against DoS attacks must be implemented

CERT_C-POS34-a.title: Usage of system properties (environment variables) should be restricted [CERT_C-POS34-a]
CERT_C-POS34-a.description:
This rule detects code that uses system properties, or environment variables:
a violation is reported on each call of getenv()/putenv() functions from
stdlib.h header.

There are multiple issues and implementation defined behavior related to
environment variables:

- The string pointer returned by getenv() may be modified (overwritten) by
  subsequent calls to the getenv() or other environment modifying functions.
  The string shall not be modified by the program. If you keep using it without
  making a copy you may end up with invalid (dangling) pointer or a pointer to
  a different string then the one returned by the initial getenv() call. Even
  making copy immediately is not completely safe - there's a race window
  between getenv() call and copying the string.

- Depending on the platform the variables may either be or not be
  case-sensitive. The matching conditions are not well defined, for example
  "VAR=value" might be matched on some systems but not on others.

- Names of environment variables are not standard so depending on their
  presence or values or setting them might not give expected results.

- The functions for altering the environment are implementation defined,
  for example putenv() is not a standard C99 function. putenv() need not be
  reentrant (and therefore not required to be thread-safe)

- Attackers can often change the value of environment variables, so limited
  trust should be placed in their values.

- Certain variables expected to be present in the environment can cause
  insecure program behavior if they are missing or improperly set.
CERT_C-POS34-a.benefits:
By controlling use of environment variables in the applications, they are made
more secure. Environment variables can be used as security holes.
If a malicious user passes a specifically crafted string into a running program
via an environment variable, a buffer overflow attack can be launched.
On the other hand, if a program writes sensitive information
(such as a password) into an environment variable with putenv, it can be
captured and used to gain unauthorized privileges or access to sensitive data.
CERT_C-POS34-a.example_repair:
Review each use of getenv/putenv functions and if it is correct
suppress the violation.
references: 1. The Open Group Base Specifications
2. Robert C. Seacord
CERT CS 15-392 Secure Programming - Environment Variables
3. Build Security In - GetEnv
4. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ENV02-C. Beware of multiple environment variables with the same effective
name
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS34-C. Do not call putenv() with a pointer to an automatic variable as
the argument
7. OWASP API Security Top 10-2019
API3-Excessive Data Exposure
8. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-002485: The application must not store sensitive information in hidden fields

CERT_C-POS34-b.title: Do not call putenv() with a pointer to an automatic variable as the argument [CERT_C-POS34-b]
CERT_C-POS34-b.description:
"The "putenv()" function does not create a copy of the string supplied to it as an argument;
rather, it inserts a pointer to the string into the environment array. If a pointer to 
a buffer of automatic storage duration is supplied as an argument to "putenv()", the memory 
allocated for that buffer may be overwritten when the containing function returns and stack 
memory is recycled."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, POS34-C. Do not call "putenv()" with a pointer to an 
automatic variable as the argument]

This rule detects when the POSIX function "putenv()" is called with a pointer to an
automatic variable as the argument.
CERT_C-POS34-b.security_relevance:
"Providing a pointer to a buffer of automatic storage duration as an argument to "putenv()" may 
cause that buffer to take on an unintended value. Depending on how and when the buffer is used, 
it can cause unexpected program behavior or possibly allow an attacker to run arbitrary code."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, POS34-C. Do not call "putenv()" with a pointer to an 
automatic variable as the argument]
CERT_C-POS34-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and prevents an attacker from running arbitrary code.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS34-C. Do not call putenv() with a pointer to an automatic variable as the argument

CERT_C-POS34.title: CERT-POS34_C (RULE) Do not call putenv() with a pointer to an automatic variable as the argument [CERT_C-POS34]

CERT_C-POS35-b.title: Avoid race conditions while checking for the existence of a symbolic link [CERT_C-POS35-b]
CERT_C-POS35-b.description:
When called on a specific file name, the POSIX lstat() function collects information
about a symbolic link rather than its target. It determines whether a file name
corresponds to a symbolic link or an actual file. Opening a file after the call to
lstat() without additional verification leads to a time-of-check, time-of-use (TOCTOU)
race condition between the call to lstat() and the subsequent call to open().
To avoid this, the POSIX fstat() function should be called on the opened file and
the file information returned by lstat() and fstat() should be compared.

This rule detects calls to the lstat() function that precede the opening of a file and
are not followed by a call to the fstat() function before the file is read or modified.
The rule can be parameterized to trigger when a file descriptor is passed to a third-party
function.
CERT_C-POS35-b.security_relevance:
The time-of-check, time-of-use race condition that occurs between the call to lstat()
and the subsequent call to open() may enable an attacker to provide malicious input into
a program that reads from the file or obtain sensitive information from a program that
writes to that file.
CERT_C-POS35-b.benefits: Prevents race conditions when checking for the existence of a symbolic link.
CERT_C-POS35-b.parameters:
When the "Report when a file descriptor is passed to a third-party function" option is
enabled, the rule is applied to file descriptors that are passed to third-party functions.
 
A violation is reported when:
- a file descriptor has been created by opening a file that lstat() was called on,
- passing this file descriptor to a third-party function is not preceded with a call to
the fstat() function.
references: OWASP API Security Top 10-2019
API3-Excessive Data Exposure
2023 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
2022 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
CWE-59: Improper Link Resolution Before File Access ('Link Following')
CWE-367: Time-of-check Time-of-use (TOCTOU) Race Condition
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS35-C. Avoid race conditions while checking for the existence of a symbolic link
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.

CERT_C-POS35.title: CERT-POS35_C (RULE) Avoid race conditions while checking for the existence of a symbolic link [CERT_C-POS35]

CERT_C-POS36-a.title: Observe correct revocation order while relinquishing privileges [CERT_C-POS36-a]
CERT_C-POS36-a.description:
"In case of set-user-ID and set-group-ID programs, when the effective user ID
and group ID are different from those of the real user, it is important to drop
not only the user-level privileges but also the group privileges. While doing
so, the order of revocation must be correct." [CERT-POS36-C]

This rule detects when a call to the setgid() function follows a call to
the setuid() function, and both calls are within one function.
CERT_C-POS36-a.benefits: This rule prevents an attacker from regaining elevated privileges.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS36-C. Observe correct revocation order while relinquishing privileges
2. OWASP Top 10 2017
A5-Broken Access Control
3. OWASP Top 10-2021
A01:2021 - Broken Access Control
4. 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management
5. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-000500: The application must prevent non-privileged users from executing privileged functions to include disabling,
circumventing, or altering implemented security safeguards/countermeasures

CERT_C-POS36.title: CERT-POS36_C (RULE) Observe correct revocation order while relinquishing privileges [CERT_C-POS36]

CERT_C-POS37-a.title: Ensure that privilege relinquishment is successful [CERT_C-POS37-a]
CERT_C-POS37-a.description:
"The POSIX setuid() function has complex semantics and platform-specific
behavior. Because of this complex behavior, desired privilege drops sometimes
may fail. This may be dangerous depending on the sequence of the preceding
privilege changes." [CERT-POS37-C]
This rule triggers when privilege relinquishment is not explicitly checked
after superuser privileges are dropped.
CERT_C-POS37-a.benefits:
"If privilege relinquishment conditions are left unchecked, any flaw
in the program may lead to unintended system compromise corresponding
to the more privileged user or group account." [CERT-POS37-C]

This rule helps you ensure that privilege relinquishment is successful, which
prevents security issues that may be caused by incorrect assigning/releasing
root privileges.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS37-C. Ensure that privilege relinquishment is successful
2. OWASP Top 10 2017
A5-Broken Access Control
3. OWASP Top 10-2021
A01:2021 - Broken Access Control
4. 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Weaknesses On the Cusp
CWE-269: Improper Privilege Management

CERT_C-POS37.title: CERT-POS37_C (RULE) Ensure that privilege relinquishment is successful [CERT_C-POS37]

CERT_C-POS38-a.title: Avoid race conditions when using fork and file descriptors [CERT_C-POS38-a]
CERT_C-POS38-a.description:
bd_trs_DESCRIPTION
"When forking a child process, file descriptors are copied to the child process, which 
can result in concurrent operations on the file. Concurrent operations on the same file 
can cause data to be read or written in a nondeterministic order, creating race 
conditions and unpredictable behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, POS38-C. Beware of race conditions when using 
fork and file descriptors]

This rule detects when a file is accessed by both the parent process and the child process
created with the fork() function.
CERT_C-POS38-a.benefits: Prevents race conditions when new processes are created with the fork() function.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS38-C. Beware of race conditions when using fork and file descriptors
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.

CERT_C-POS38.title: CERT-POS38_C (RULE) Beware of race conditions when using fork and file descriptors [CERT_C-POS38]

CERT_C-POS39-a.title: Use the correct byte ordering when transferring data between systems [CERT_C-POS39-a]
CERT_C-POS39-a.description:
"Different system architectures use different byte ordering, either little
endian (least significant byte first) or big endian (most significant byte
first). When transferring data between systems of different endianness,
the programmer must take care to reverse the byte ordering before interpreting
the data.
The functions htonl(), htons(), ntohl(), and ntohs() can be used to transfer
between network byte ordering (big endian) and the host's byte ordering".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard]

The rule detects when data is transferred between systems without using
the appropriate functions htonl(), htons(), ntohl(), or ntohs().
A violation is reported in the following cases:
- A call to the sending function is not preceded by a call to htonl() or htons().
- The use of a variable that was passed as a buffer to the receiving function
  is not preceded by a call to ntohl() or ntohs().

The rule applies to buffers of the uint16_t and uint32_t types.
CERT_C-POS39-a.security_relevance:
Failing to take care to reverse the byte ordering before interpreting the data
may result in unintended program behavior or program malfunction, such as
printing incorrect results.
CERT_C-POS39-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent incorrect byte ordering.
CERT_C-POS39-a.parameters:
If the "Consider all elements of an array to be converted if at least one of
the elements is converted in a loop" parameter is enabled, the rule assumes
that all elements of an array are converted if at least one element of this
array is converted in a loop.
With this parameter enabled, the rule may not report correct violations if
only a subset of array elements is converted in a loop.
See the test2 function in the EXAMPLE section.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS39-C. Use the correct byte ordering when transferring data between systems

CERT_C-POS39.title: CERT-POS39_C (RULE) Use the correct byte ordering when transferring data between systems [CERT_C-POS39]

CERT_C-POS44-a.title: The 'pthread_kill', 'pthread_sigqueue' and 'tgkill' functions should not be used to send signals to threads [CERT_C-POS44-a]
CERT_C-POS44-a.description:
"Do not send an uncaught signal to kill a thread because the signal kills the
entire process, not just the individual thread." [CERT-POS44-C]

Signal dispositions are process-wide: for signals that do not have the
corresponding handlers installed, the disposition of the signal, 'stop',
'continue', or 'terminate', can affect the whole process.

The rule detects the usage of functions sending signals to a thread:
pthread_kill(), pthread_sigqueue(), or tgkill().
CERT_C-POS44-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent implementation-defined or undefined behavior
caused by premature termination of a process.
references: 1.  SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS44-C. Do not use signals to terminate threads

CERT_C-POS44.title: CERT-POS44_C (RULE) Do not use signals to terminate threads [CERT_C-POS44]

CERT_C-POS47-a.title: The function 'pthread_setcanceltype()' should not be called with 'PTHREAD_CANCEL_ASYNCHRONOUS' argument [CERT_C-POS47-a]
CERT_C-POS47-a.description:
The pthread_setcanceltype() sets the cancelability type of the calling
thread to the value given in the first argument. The new cancellation type
can be set to one of the following:
PTHREAD_CANCEL_ASYNCHRONOUS - The thread can be canceled at any time
PTHREAD_CANCEL_DEFERRED - A cancellation request is deferred until the thread
                          calls a function that is a cancellation point

This rule detects when the pthread_setcanceltype() function is called
with the PTHREAD_CANCEL_ASYNCHRONOUS value.
CERT_C-POS47-a.benefits:
Helps you avoid canceling threads asynchronously (immediately), which may be
dangerous. Most threads are not safe to cancel immediately. If a thread can
be canceled at any time, it cannot safely reserve resources. As a result,
the application has no way of knowing what the state of these resources is when
the thread is canceled.
CERT_C-POS47-a.example_repair:
The function 'pthread_setcanceltype()' should not be called
with the 'PTHREAD_CANCEL_ASYNCHRONOUS' argument
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS47-C. Do not use threads that can be canceled asynchronously
2. 2008 Linux Foundation, written by Michael Kerrisk
Linux Programmer's Manual PTHREAD_SETCANCELSTATE

CERT_C-POS47.title: CERT-POS47_C (RULE) Do not use threads that can be canceled asynchronously [CERT_C-POS47]

CERT_C-POS48-a.title: Do not destroy another thread's mutex [CERT_C-POS48-a]
CERT_C-POS48-a.description:
This rule detects when a lock is destroyed without ensuring that all the threads that may be using
this lock have finished. 
The rule must be parameterized with a list of user-defined functions that are called to ensure
that the threads have finished. If the list is not provided, the rule will report a violation
each time a lock-destroying function is used in code.
CERT_C-POS48-a.security_relevance:
"Destroying a mutex while it is locked may result in invalid control flow and data corruption".
SEI CERT C Coding Standard,
CON31-C. Do not destroy a mutex while it is locked
CERT_C-POS48-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent data corruption and violation of data integrity, as well as maintain
proper control flow.
CERT_C-POS48-a.parameters:
The "Functions that ensure that all the threads have finished" parameter allows you to specify a list
of functions that can be used to check if all the threads that use a lock have finished. When one of these
functions is called before destroying a lock, a violation will not be reported.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON31-C. Do not destroy a mutex while it is locked,
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS48-C. Do not unlock or destroy another POSIX thread's mutex,
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON50-CPP. Do not destroy a mutex while it is locked

CERT_C-POS48-b.title: Do not release a lock that has not been acquired [CERT_C-POS48-b]
CERT_C-POS48-b.description:
This rule detects cases where a lock is released, but it has not been acquired 
in the same method.
CERT_C-POS48-b.benefits: Helps you prevent unlocking another thread's lock.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.13
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.17
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.17 No thread shall unlock a mutex or call cnd_wait() or cnd_timedwait() for a mutex it has not locked before
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS48-C. Do not unlock or destroy another POSIX thread's mutex
CWE-832: Unlock of a Resource that is not Locked

CERT_C-POS48.title: CERT-POS48_C (RULE) Do not unlock or destroy another POSIX thread's mutex [CERT_C-POS48]

CERT_C-POS49-a.title: Use locks to prevent race conditions when modifying bit fields [CERT_C-POS49-a]
CERT_C-POS49-a.description:
When multiple threads access or modify a shared variable, they may inadvertently
access other variables that are adjacent in memory. This rule detects when a bit field
that has another bit field adjacent in the same byte is accessed outside of the critical
section.
CERT_C-POS49-a.benefits: Helps you prevent data corruption due to unintended modifications of adjacent bit fields.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON32-C. Prevent data races when accessing bit-fields from multiple threads
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS49-C. When data must be accessed by multiple threads, provide a mutex and guarantee no adjacent data is also accessed
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON52-CPP. Prevent data races when accessing bit-fields from multiple threads
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.

CERT_C-POS49.title: CERT-POS49_C (RULE) When data must be accessed by multiple threads, provide a mutex and guarantee no adjacent data is also accessed [CERT_C-POS49]

CERT_C-POS50-a.title: Declare objects shared between POSIX threads with appropriate storage durations [CERT_C-POS50-a]
CERT_C-POS50-a.description:
This rule detects when objects are shared between POSIX threads without 
appropriate storage durations. Accessing the stack of a thread from another 
thread can cause invalid memory access, as the referenced stack frame may 
not be valid when the other thread is trying to access it. Regular shared variables
should be protected by a call to the pthread_join(pthread_t thread, void **retval)
method, which is a thread synchronization mechanism.
CERT_C-POS50-a.benefits: Helps you prevents invalid memory access.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON34-C. Declare objects shared between threads with appropriate storage durations
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS50-C. Declare objects shared between POSIX threads with appropriate storage durations

CERT_C-POS50.title: CERT-POS50_C (RULE) Declare objects shared between POSIX threads with appropriate storage durations [CERT_C-POS50]

CERT_C-POS51-a.title: Do not acquire locks in different order [CERT_C-POS51-a]
CERT_C-POS51-a.description:
This rule detects cases where two mutexes are locked in different orders, which
can cause a deadlock. The violation will be reported if there exist at least
two locks and at least two paths on which they are acquired in different orders.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.2
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.2 There shall be no deadlocks between threads
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON35-C. Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS51-C. Avoid deadlock with POSIX threads by locking in predefined order
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON53-CPP. Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order
CWE-833: Deadlock
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
18.3.2 Ensure that order of nesting of locks in a project forms a DAG

CERT_C-POS51.title: CERT-POS51_C (RULE) Avoid deadlock with POSIX threads by locking in predefined order [CERT_C-POS51]

CERT_C-POS52-a.title: Do not use blocking functions while holding a lock [CERT_C-POS52-a]
CERT_C-POS52-a.description:
This rule detects cases where functions that cease thread execution for a certain time
(such as sleep()) are called from critical sections--thus unnecessarily increasing
resource contention between threads.
CERT_C-POS52-a.benefits:
Helps prevent unnecessary resource contention between threads as well as
thread starvation.
CERT_C-POS52-a.parameters:
The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON05-C. Do not perform operations that can block while holding a lock
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS52-C. Do not perform operations that can block while holding a POSIX lock

CERT_C-POS52.title: CERT-POS52_C (RULE) Do not perform operations that can block while holding a POSIX lock [CERT_C-POS52]

CERT_C-POS53-a.title: Do not use more than one mutex for concurrent waiting operations on a condition variable [CERT_C-POS53-a]
CERT_C-POS53-a.description:
"pthread_cond_wait() and pthread_cond_timedwait() take a condition variable
and locked mutex as arguments. These functions unlock the mutex until
the condition variable is signaled and then relock the mutex before returning.
While a thread is waiting on a particular condition variable and mutex,
other threads may only wait on the same condition variable if they also pass
the same mutex as an argument." [CERT-POS53-C]

The rule detects when different calls to:
- cnd_wait() and/or cnd_timedwait(), or to
- pthread_cond_wait() and/or pthread_cond_timedwait()
take the same condition variable, but different mutexes as arguments.
CERT_C-POS53-a.benefits:
The rule helps you prevent undefined behaviors when more than one mutex
is used for concurrent operations of the following functions on the same
condition variable:
- cnd_wait() and/or cnd_timedwait(), or
- pthread_cond_wait() and/or pthread_cond_timedwait().
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.14 Rule 22.19
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.19 A condition variable shall be associated with at most one mutex object
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS53-C. Do not use more than one mutex for concurrent waiting operations
on a condition variable

CERT_C-POS53.title: CERT-POS53_C (RULE) Do not use more than one mutex for concurrent waiting operations on a condition variable [CERT_C-POS53]

CERT_C-POS54-a.title: The value returned by a POSIX library function that may return an error should be used [CERT_C-POS54-a]
CERT_C-POS54-a.description:
"All standard library functions, including I/O functions and memory allocation
functions, return either a valid value or a value of the correct return type
that indicates an error (for example, −1 or a null pointer). Assuming that all
calls to such functions will succeed and failing to check the return value for
an indication of an error is a dangerous practice that may lead to unexpected
or undefined behavior when an error occurs. It is essential that programs
detect and appropriately handle all errors in accordance with an
error-handling policy." [CERT-POS54-C]

This rule detects when values returned from POSIX library functions are not
used. All functions whose names are the same as the names of functions declared
in the POSIX library are detected as POSIX library functions.
CERT_C-POS54-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent unexpected or undefined behavior when an error
occurs during the call to the POSIX library function.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS54-C. Detect and handle POSIX library errors
2. DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC_DV-003235: The application must not be subject to error handling vulnerabilities.

CERT_C-POS54.title: CERT-POS54_C (RULE) Detect and handle POSIX library errors [CERT_C-POS54]

CERT_C-PRE00-a.title: A function should be used in preference to a function-like macro [CERT_C-PRE00-a]
CERT_C-PRE00-a.description:
A function should be used in preference to a function-like macro
if it is permitted by the syntax and constraints of the language standard.
In most circumstances, functions should be used instead of macros. Functions
perform argument type-checking and evaluate their arguments once, thus avoiding
problems with potential multiple side-effects. In many debugging systems,
it is easier to step through execution of a function than a macro.
Nonetheless, macros may be useful in some circumstances.

This rule detects definitions of function-like macros.

See also: CODSTA-03, CODSTA-37, CODSTA-38
CERT_C-PRE00-a.benefits:
"While macros can provide a speed advantage over functions, functions provide
a safer and more robust mechanism. This is particularly true with respect
to the type checking of parameters, and the problem of function-like macros
potentially evaluating parameters multiple times."
[MISRA C++:2008 Rule 16-0-4]
references: 1. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 9 Functions - 9.5 Inline Functions - Rule 35

CERT_C-PRE01-a.title: In the definition of a function-like macro each instance of a parameter shall be enclosed in parentheses unless it is used as the operand of # or ## [CERT_C-PRE01-a]
CERT_C-PRE01-a.description:
"In the definition of a function-like macro each instance of a parameter shall
be enclosed in parentheses. If this rule is not adhered to then when
the preprocessor substitutes the macro into the code the operator precedence
may not give the desired results." [MISRA C:2004 Rule 19.10]

See also: MISRA-096
CERT_C-PRE01-a.benefits:
"If parentheses are not used, then operator precedence may not give the desired
results when macro substitution occurs." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 20.7]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

parameter shall be enclosed in parentheses, unless it is used as the
operand of # or ##

systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-6

PRE01-C. Use parentheses within macros around parameter names

CERT_C-PRE02-a.title: Enclose the entire definition of a function-like macro in parentheses [CERT_C-PRE02-a]
CERT_C-PRE02-a.description:
This rule detects when the entire definition of a function-like macro
is not enclosed in parentheses.
CERT_C-PRE02-a.benefits:
This rule helps you to improve code readability and ensure the correct order
of operations.
CERT_C-PRE02-a.example_violation:
#define abs1(x)  (x)>=0 ? c: -(x)          // Violation
#define abs2(x) ((x)>=0 ? c: -(x))         // OK
#define id(x) x                            // OK
#define func(x) f(x)                       // OK
references: 1. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 96
2. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE02-C. Macro replacement lists should be parenthesized

CERT_C-PRE02.title: CERT-PRE02_C (REC) Macro replacement lists should be parenthesized [CERT_C-PRE02]

CERT_C-PRE06-a.title: Use multiple include guards [CERT_C-PRE06-a]
CERT_C-PRE06-a.description:
Every include file must contain a mechanism that prevents multiple inclusion
of the file.

Following patterns are recognized and accepted:

#ifndef SOME_MACRO
#define SOME_MACRO
// code
#endif

or

#if !defined(SOME_MACRO)
#define SOME_MACRO
// code
#endif

See also: PFO-07, PFO-08, MISRA2004-19_15
CERT_C-PRE06-a.benefits:
Prevents confusion caused by including the same header multiple times,
prevents undefined or erroneous behaviour caused by multiple or conflicting
definitions.
CERT_C-PRE06-a.example_violation:
// file foo.hh
// Violation - no multiple inclusion mechanism present
references: 1. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 4 Source Code in Files - 4.4 Include Files - Rule 7

CERT_C-PRE07-a.title: Trigraphs shall not be used [CERT_C-PRE07-a]
CERT_C-PRE07-a.description:
This rule detects when a trigraph-like sequence appears in the source code,
as well as inside a string or comment.
CERT_C-PRE07-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid confusions

A trigraph-like sequence may be accidentally replaced by another single
character.

CERT_C-PRE30-a.title: Avoid token concatenation that may produce universal character names [CERT_C-PRE30-a]
CERT_C-PRE30-a.description:
"For both object-like and function-like macro invocations, before
the replacement list is reexamined for more macro names to replace,
each instance of a '##' preprocessing token in the replacement list
(not from an argument) is deleted and the preceding preprocessing
token is concatenated with the following preprocessing token.
If the result is not a valid preprocessing token, the behavior
is undefined." [ISO/IEC 14882:2003]

"If a character sequence that matches the syntax
of a universal-character-name is produced by token concatenation,
the behavior is undefined." [ISO/IEC 14882:2003]

This rule detects when the '##' operator used in macro definitions
may produce a universal character name.

See also: MISRA2004-19_13, PREPROC-16
CERT_C-PRE30-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.10.3.3 The ## operator, 5.1.1.2 Translation phases
6.4.3 Universal character names
2. ISO/IEC 14882:2003 Programming languages -- C++
16.3.3 The ## operator, 2.1 Phases of translation
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE30-C. Do not create a universal character name through concatenation

CERT_C-PRE30.title: CERT-PRE30_C (RULE) Do not create a universal character name through concatenation [CERT_C-PRE30]

CERT_C-PRE31-b.title: Assertions should not contain assignments, increment, or decrement operators [CERT_C-PRE31-b]
CERT_C-PRE31-b.description:
"Expressions used as arguments to the standard assert() macro should not have
side effects. The behavior of the assert() macro depends on the definition
of the object-like macro NDEBUG. If the macro NDEBUG is undefined, the assert()
macro is defined to evaluate its expression argument and, if the result of the
expression compares equal to 0, call the abort() function. If NDEBUG is defined,
assert is defined to expand to ((void)0). Consequently, the expression in the
assertion is not evaluated, and no side effects it may have had otherwise take
place in non-debugging executions of the code." [CERT C: PRE31-C]

The rule reports a violation when it detects function-like macros that have
names ending with 'assert', and when the macros' arguments contain an
assignment, increment, or decrement operator.
CERT_C-PRE31-b.benefits: The rule improves maintainability of code and prevents confusions.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE31-C. Avoid side effects in arguments to unsafe macros

CERT_C-PRE31-c.title: Assertions should not contain function calls nor function-like macro calls [CERT_C-PRE31-c]
CERT_C-PRE31-c.description:
"Expressions used as arguments to the standard assert() macro should not have
side effects. The behavior of the assert() macro depends on the definition
of the object-like macro NDEBUG. If the macro NDEBUG is undefined, the assert()
macro is defined to evaluate its expression argument and, if the result of the
expression compares equal to 0, call the abort() function. If NDEBUG is defined,
assert is defined to expand to ((void)0). Consequently, the expression
in the assertion is not evaluated, and no side effects it may have had otherwise
take place in non-debugging executions of the code." [CERT C: PRE31-C]

The rule reports a violation when it detects function-like macros that have
names ending with 'assert', and when the macros' arguments contain a function
call or a function-like macro call. The function call or the function-like macro
call may cause a side effect that may or may not be evaluated.
CERT_C-PRE31-c.benefits: The rule improves maintainability of code and prevents confusions.
CERT_C-PRE31-c.example_repair: If possible, avoid calling function calls and function-like macros in assertions
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE31-C. Avoid side effects in arguments to unsafe macros

CERT_C-PRE31-d.title: Avoid side effects in arguments to unsafe macros [CERT_C-PRE31-d]
CERT_C-PRE31-d.description:
"An unsafe function-like macro is one whose expansion results in evaluating
one of its parameters more than once or not at all. Never invoke an unsafe
macro with arguments containing an assignment, increment, decrement,
volatile access, input/output, or other expressions with side effects
(including function calls, which may cause side effects)." [CERT C: PRE31-C]

The rule reports a violation when an unsafe function-like macro is used with
an argument that contains an assignment, increment, or decrement operator.
CERT_C-PRE31-d.benefits: The rule improves maintainability of code and prevents confusions.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE31-C. Avoid side effects in arguments to unsafe macros

CERT_C-PRE31.title: CERT-PRE31_C (RULE) Avoid side effects in arguments to unsafe macros [CERT_C-PRE31]

CERT_C-PRE32-a.title: Arguments to a function-like macro shall not contain tokens that look like preprocessing directives [CERT_C-PRE32-a]
CERT_C-PRE32-a.description:
"Tokens that look like a preprocessing directive shall not occur within a macro
argument. An argument containing sequences of tokens that would otherwise act
as preprocessing directives leads to undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012, Rule 20.6]

"A preprocessing directive consists of a sequence of preprocessing tokens that
begins with a # preprocessing token that is either the first character in the
source file (optionally after white space containing no new-line characters)
or that follows white space containing at least one new-line character,
and is ended by the next new-line character." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-0-5. Arguments to a function-like macro shall not contain tokens that
look like pre-processing directives
2.  ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.8 Preprocessing directives
3.  ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.10 Preprocessing directives

8.  MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19, Rule 19.9

PRE32-C. Do not use preprocessor directives in invocations of function-like

CERT_C-PRE32.title: CERT-PRE32_C (RULE) Do not use preprocessor directives in invocations of function-like macros [CERT_C-PRE32]

CERT_C-SIG00-a.title: The signal handling facilities of <signal.h> shall not be used [CERT_C-SIG00-a]
CERT_C-SIG00-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'signal()' or 'raise()' global function declared
with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard library,
or locally by the user) is called or when its address is taken.

This rule also detects when identifiers with names of types and macros defined
in <signal.h> are used: sig_atomic_t, SIGABRT, SIGFPE, SIGILL, SIGINT, SIGSEGV,
SIGTERM, SIG_DFL, SIG_IGN, SIG_ERR.
CERT_C-SIG00-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent implementation-defined and undefined behavior.

CERT_C-SIG30-a.title: Properly define signal handlers [CERT_C-SIG30-a]
CERT_C-SIG30-a.description:
This rule analyzes functions registered as signal handlers, and triggers in the following cases: 

 * A signal handler calls a function that is not asynchronous-signal-safe
 * A signal handler accesses a shared object that is not of atomic type

The function is considered to be asynchronous-signal-safe on one of the following conditions:
 * It is required by the standard (see the PARAMETERS section)
 * It calls only asynchronous-signal-safe functions, and does not access any shared objects

The rule reports all signal handler calls to functions that are not asynchronous-signal-safe.

As a rule, shared data should not be accessed in signal handlers.
"The two exceptions (C Standard, 5.1.2.3, paragraph 5) to this rule are the ability to read 
from and write to lock-free atomic objects and variables of type volatile sig_atomic_t. 
Accessing any other type of object from a signal handler is undefined behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]

The rule reports each access to a shared object which is not of atomic type.
CERT_C-SIG30-a.security_relevance:
"Accessing or modifying shared objects in signal handlers can result in race conditions that can leave 
data in an inconsistent state"
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]
CERT_C-SIG30-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
CERT_C-SIG30-a.parameters:
The "Consider asynchronous-signal-safe functions from POSIX standard" parameter allows you to specify 
the Posix Standard the rule should consider, as different standards added or removed requirements for
functions to be asynchronous-signal-safe. Alternatively, you can select the "Strict" option:
"For strictly conforming programs, only the C standard library functions abort(), _Exit(), quick_exit(), 
and signal() can be safely called from within a signal handler."
[SIG30-C. Call only asynchronous-safe functions within signal handlers]

The available options are:
- None
- Strict
- POSIX.1-2001
- POSIX.1-2003
- POSIX.1-2004
- POSIX.1-2008
- POSIX.1-2013
- POSIX.1-2016(default)

See references for more details about the list of signal-asynchronous-free functions required by each standard.

The "Additional functions which are asynchronous-signal-safe" parameter allows to define additional, third-party
functions that the rule should consider to be signal-asynchronous-free. The rule will not trigger when the specified
functions are called from signal handlers.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table:

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name 
  of the type or namespace where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any
  type or namespace, or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
The "Report violations when signal() function is called from within signal handler" parameter allows you to enable
reporting violations when the signal() function is called from within the signal handler.

CERT_C-SIG31-a.description:

The function is considered to be asynchronous-signal-safe on one of the following conditions:
 * It is required by the standard (see the PARAMETERS section)
 * It calls only asynchronous-signal-safe functions, and does not access any shared objects

The rule reports all signal handler calls to functions that are not asynchronous-signal-safe.

As a rule, shared data should not be accessed in signal handlers.
"The two exceptions (C Standard, 5.1.2.3, paragraph 5) to this rule are the ability to read 
from and write to lock-free atomic objects and variables of type volatile sig_atomic_t. 
Accessing any other type of object from a signal handler is undefined behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]

The rule reports each access to a shared object which is not of atomic type.
CERT_C-SIG31-a.security_relevance:
"Accessing or modifying shared objects in signal handlers can result in race conditions that can leave 
data in an inconsistent state"
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]
CERT_C-SIG31-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
CERT_C-SIG31-a.parameters:
The "Consider asynchronous-signal-safe functions from POSIX standard" parameter allows you to specify 
the Posix Standard the rule should consider, as different standards added or removed requirements for
functions to be asynchronous-signal-safe. Alternatively, you can select the "Strict" option:
"For strictly conforming programs, only the C standard library functions abort(), _Exit(), quick_exit(), 
and signal() can be safely called from within a signal handler."
[SIG30-C. Call only asynchronous-safe functions within signal handlers]

The available options are:
- None
- Strict
- POSIX.1-2001
- POSIX.1-2003
- POSIX.1-2004
- POSIX.1-2008
- POSIX.1-2013
- POSIX.1-2016(default)

See references for more details about the list of signal-asynchronous-free functions required by each standard.

The "Additional functions which are asynchronous-signal-safe" parameter allows to define additional, third-party
functions that the rule should consider to be signal-asynchronous-free. The rule will not trigger when the specified
functions are called from signal handlers.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table:

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name 
  of the type or namespace where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any
  type or namespace, or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
The "Report violations when signal() function is called from within signal handler" parameter allows you to enable
reporting violations when the signal() function is called from within the signal handler.
