CERT_C-SIG34-a.title: Properly define signal handlers [CERT_C-SIG34-a]
CERT_C-SIG34-a.description:
This rule analyzes functions registered as signal handlers, and triggers in the following cases: 

 * A signal handler calls a function that is not asynchronous-signal-safe
 * A signal handler accesses a shared object that is not of atomic type

The function is considered to be asynchronous-signal-safe on one of the following conditions:
 * It is required by the standard (see the PARAMETERS section)
 * It calls only asynchronous-signal-safe functions, and does not access any shared objects

The rule reports all signal handler calls to functions that are not asynchronous-signal-safe.

As a rule, shared data should not be accessed in signal handlers.
"The two exceptions (C Standard, 5.1.2.3, paragraph 5) to this rule are the ability to read 
from and write to lock-free atomic objects and variables of type volatile sig_atomic_t. 
Accessing any other type of object from a signal handler is undefined behavior."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]

The rule reports each access to a shared object which is not of atomic type.
CERT_C-SIG34-a.security_relevance:
"Accessing or modifying shared objects in signal handlers can result in race conditions that can leave 
data in an inconsistent state"
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard SIG31-C. Do not access shared objects in signal handlers]
CERT_C-SIG34-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
CERT_C-SIG34-a.parameters:
The "Consider asynchronous-signal-safe functions from POSIX standard" parameter allows you to specify 
the Posix Standard the rule should consider, as different standards added or removed requirements for
functions to be asynchronous-signal-safe. Alternatively, you can select the "Strict" option:
"For strictly conforming programs, only the C standard library functions abort(), _Exit(), quick_exit(), 
and signal() can be safely called from within a signal handler."
[SIG30-C. Call only asynchronous-safe functions within signal handlers]

The available options are:
- None
- Strict
- POSIX.1-2001
- POSIX.1-2003
- POSIX.1-2004
- POSIX.1-2008
- POSIX.1-2013
- POSIX.1-2016(default)

See references for more details about the list of signal-asynchronous-free functions required by each standard.

The "Additional functions which are asynchronous-signal-safe" parameter allows to define additional, third-party
functions that the rule should consider to be signal-asynchronous-free. The rule will not trigger when the specified
functions are called from signal handlers.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table:

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name 
  of the type or namespace where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any
  type or namespace, or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.

reporting violations when the signal() function is called from within the signal handler.

CERT_C-SIG35-a.title: Do not return from a computational exception signal handler [CERT_C-SIG35-a]
CERT_C-SIG35-a.description:
This rule detects returns of functions registered as signal handlers that should never return.
	
"The behavior of a process is undefined after it returns normally from a signal-catching function for
a SIGBUS, SIGFPE, SIGILL, or SIGSEGV signal that was not generated by kill(), sigqueue(), or raise()."
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, 
SIG35-C. Do not return from a computational exception signal handler]
CERT_C-SIG35-a.security_relevance:
Returning from a function that should never return
results in undefined behavior that might be exploited.
CERT_C-SIG35-a.benefits:
Helps you avoid unexpected failures at runtime. The Standard does not define behavior
if a function that should not return returns, which may lead to unexpected behaviors.
CERT_C-SIG35-a.parameters:
The "Values corresponding to signal handlers that should not return" parameter allows you to configure
integer values corresponding to signals. The rule will trigger only if a signal handler is registered
for the specified signal value(s).
The defaults are values commonly used for SIGILL(4), SIGFPE(8) and SIGSEGV(11). You can overwrite them
with values specific to your environment, or add additional, implementation-defined, computational
exception signals, such as SIGBUS.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
SIG35-C. Do not return from a computational exception signal handler

CERT_C-SIG35.title: CERT-SIG35_C (RULE) Do not return from a computational exception signal handler [CERT_C-SIG35]

CERT_C-STR00-a.title: The plain char type shall be used only for the storage and use of character values [CERT_C-STR00-a]
CERT_C-STR00-a.description:
"There are three distinct char types, plain char, signed char and unsigned char.
Signed char and unsigned char shall be used for numeric data and plain char
shall be used for character data. The signedness of the plain char type
is implementation-defined and should not be relied upon.

The permissible operators on plain char types are the simple assignment
operator (=), equality operators( ==, != ) and explicit casts to integral
types. Additionally, the second and third operands of the ternary conditional
operator may both be of plain char type."

The rule reports a violation if parameter, variable or expression of plain char
type is used for numerical data. If a plain char is used as operand of operator
other than assignment (=), equality ( ==, != ), explicit cast or ternary
conditional operator then violation is reported as well.

See also: MISRA2004-6_2
CERT_C-STR00-a.benefits: Rule makes the code more readable and less confusing.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-0-11. The plain char type shall only be used for the storage and use of
character values
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
7.1.6. Use class types or typedefs to abstract scalar quantities and
standard integer types
3. MISRA C++:2008 - Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-12
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT07-C. Use only explicitly signed or unsigned char type for numeric
values
6. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR00-C. Represent characters using an appropriate type
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR04-C. Use plain char for characters in the basic character set

CERT_C-STR00.title: CERT-STR00_C (REC) Represent characters using an appropriate type [CERT_C-STR00]

CERT_C-STR02-a.title: Protect against command injection [CERT_C-STR02-a]
CERT_C-STR02-a.description:
This rule detects cases when data coming directly from the end-user can influence
the code which is executed (for example, to specify the name of the file to
be executed).

The rule supports functions for process execution from the standard C library as well
as from POSIX. For example, it considers the widely used system() function and the exec family
of functions to be dangerous.
CERT_C-STR02-a.security_relevance:
If tainted data appears in the executed file name without verification,
it may enable the execution of custom malicious code that could damage the system.
CERT_C-STR02-a.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

CERT_C-STR02-b.title: Protect against file name injection [CERT_C-STR02-b]
CERT_C-STR02-b.description:
This rule detects cases when possibly tainted data is used as a filename or a path in file
manipulating functions, which could lead to file name injection. The rule supports file manipulation
functions from the standard C and C++ libraries as well as those defined by POSIX.
CERT_C-STR02-b.security_relevance:
Unverified data in file names may enable a potential attacker to gain access
to any file on the system with specially-prepared malicious data.

- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in

CERT_C-STR02-c.title: Protect against SQL injection [CERT_C-STR02-c]
CERT_C-STR02-c.description:
This rule detects possible SQL injections when possibly tainted data reaches functions
that prepare or execute SQL queries, retrieve connections, etc. 

When an application uses data provided by the user (or some other unverified data source)
to construct SQL queries and does not verify/validate such data before it is used,
it is possible for an attacker to alter the SQL statements in a way that the developer
did not intend. As a result, the attacker can take total control of the database or
execute commands on the system.

The rule supports the following database APIs: ODBC, ADO, OLE DB.
CERT_C-STR02-c.security_relevance:
If data that appears in an SQL query has not been validated,
it may enable a malicious user to take control of the database.
CERT_C-STR02-c.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

CERT_C-STR03-a.title: Avoid overflow due to reading a not zero terminated string [CERT_C-STR03-a]
CERT_C-STR03-a.description:
The rule detects cases of possible overflow due to running a string function on a string that
was not properly zero terminated.

The rule will report violation when the parameter passed to certain functions(see PARAMETERS section)
is not zero terminated. There are cases when such behavior may not lead to overflow or undefined
behavior(e.g. using 'snprintf' function with specific format and correct maximum number of bytes).
CERT_C-STR03-a.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.

Signed char and unsigned char shall be used for numeric data and plain char

other than assignment (=), equality ( ==, != ), explicit cast or ternary

CERT_C-STR05-a.title: A string literal shall not be modified [CERT_C-STR05-a]
CERT_C-STR05-a.description:
"The declaration

      char s[] = "abc", t[3] = "abc";

 defines 'plain' char array objects 's' and 't' whose elements are initialized
 with character string literals. This declaration is identical to

      char s[] = { 'a', 'b', 'c', '\0' },
      t[] = { 'a', 'b', 'c' };

 The contents of the arrays are modifiable.

 On the other hand, the declaration

      char *p = "abc";

 defines p with type 'pointer to char' and initializes it to point to an object
 with type 'array of char' with length 4 whose elements are initialized with
 a character string literal. If an attempt is made to use p to modify
 the contents of the array, the behavior is undefined."

"Since string literals are constant, they should only be assigned
 to constant pointers"

The violation is reported by rule if string literal or wide string literal:
  - is modified directly
  - initializes pointer to non-const object
  - is assigned to pointer to non-const object
  - is passed to function as pointer to non-const object or as non-const array
  - is returned from function as pointer to non-const object
  - is casted to pointer to non-const object

CERT_C-STR07-a.title: Avoid using unsafe string functions that do not check bounds [CERT_C-STR07-a]
CERT_C-STR07-a.description:
This rule detects code that uses unsafe string functions:
scanf, fscanf, sscanf, vscanf, vsscanf, vfscanf, realpath,
getopt, getpass, streadd, strecpy, strtrns, getwd, snprintf
According to David A. Wheeler (see reference below),
"C functions users must avoid using dangerous functions
that do not check bounds unless they've ensured
that the bounds will never get exceeded.
The function strlen() should be avoided
unless you can ensure that there will be a terminating NIL character to find.
The scanf() family (scanf(), fscanf(), sscanf(), vscanf(), vsscanf(),
and vfscanf()) is often dangerous to use;
do not use it to send data to a string without controlling the maximum length
(the format %s is a particularly common problem).
Other dangerous functions that may permit buffer overruns
(depending on their use) include realpath(), getopt(), getpass(), streadd(),
strncpy(), and strtrns().
You must be careful with getwd();
the buffer sent to getwd() must be at least PATH_MAX bytes long.
The select() helper macros FD_SET(), FD_CLR(), and FD_ISSET()
do not check that the index fd is within bounds;
make sure that fd >= 0 and fd <= FD_SETSIZE
(this particular one has been exploited in pppd).
Unfortunately, snprintf()'s variants have additional problems.
Officially, snprintf() is not a standard C function
in the ISO 1990 (ANSI 1989) standard,
though sprintf() is, so not all systems include snprintf()
others call sprintf directly what could make horrible thing."
See also: SECURITY-07,SECURITY-12, SECURITY-16
CERT_C-STR07-a.benefits: Prevents the use of functions which may cause overflows.
CERT_C-STR07-a.example_repair: Remove usage of unsafe string functions.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/dangers-c.html
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A27-0-4. C-style strings shall not be used.
3. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications

INT05-C. Do not use input functions to convert character data if they
cannot handle all possible inputs
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR07-C. Use the bounds-checking interfaces for string manipulation

CERT_C-STR07.title: CERT-STR07_C (REC) Use the bounds-checking interfaces for string manipulation [CERT_C-STR07]

CERT_C-STR09-a.title: Expressions with type (plain) char and wchar_t shall not be used as operands to built-in operators other than  =,  ==, != and the unary & operator [CERT_C-STR09-a]
CERT_C-STR09-a.description:
"Expressions with type (plain) char and wchar_t shall not be used as operands
to built-in operators other than the assignment operator =, the equality
operators == and !=, and the unary & operator."
CERT_C-STR09-a.benefits:
"Manipulation of character data may generate results that are contrary
to developer expectations."

CERT_C-STR11-a.title: Do not specify the bound of a character array initialized with a string literal [CERT_C-STR11-a]
CERT_C-STR11-a.description:
"An array of character type may be initialized by a character string literal
or UTF-8 string literal, optionally enclosed in braces. Successive bytes
of the string literal (including the terminating null character if there
is room or if the array is of unknown size) initialize the elements
of the array." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"[I]f the string is intended to be used as a null-terminated byte string,
then the array will have one too few characters to hold the string because
it does not account for the terminating null character. Such a sequence
of characters has limited utility and has the potential to cause
vulnerabilities if a null-terminated byte string is assumed." [CERT-STR11-C]

"A better approach is to not specify the bound of a string initialized
with a string literal because the compiler will automatically allocate
sufficient space for the entire string literal, including the terminating null
character." [CERT-STR11-C]

The rule detects when a character array initialized with a string literal
is declared with an explicitly specified dimension.
CERT_C-STR11-a.benefits: This rule helps you make your code easier to maintain.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR11-C. Do not specify the bound of a character array initialized with a
string literal

CERT_C-STR11.title: CERT-STR11_C (REC) Do not specify the bound of a character array initialized with a string literal [CERT_C-STR11]

CERT_C-STR30-a.title: A string literal shall not be modified [CERT_C-STR30-a]
CERT_C-STR30-a.description:
"The declaration

      char s[] = "abc", t[3] = "abc";

 defines 'plain' char array objects 's' and 't' whose elements are initialized
 with character string literals. This declaration is identical to

      char s[] = { 'a', 'b', 'c', '\0' },
      t[] = { 'a', 'b', 'c' };

 The contents of the arrays are modifiable.

 On the other hand, the declaration

      char *p = "abc";

 defines p with type 'pointer to char' and initializes it to point to an object
 with type 'array of char' with length 4 whose elements are initialized with
 a character string literal. If an attempt is made to use p to modify
 the contents of the array, the behavior is undefined."

"Since string literals are constant, they should only be assigned
 to constant pointers"

The violation is reported by rule if string literal or wide string literal:
  - is modified directly
  - initializes pointer to non-const object
  - is assigned to pointer to non-const object
  - is passed to function as pointer to non-const object or as non-const array
  - is returned from function as pointer to non-const object
  - is casted to pointer to non-const object
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A2-13-4. String literals shall not be assigned to non-constant pointers

CERT_C-STR30-b.title: Do not modify string literals [CERT_C-STR30-b]
CERT_C-STR30-b.description:
This rule reports violations when the contents of a variable
initialized with an unmodifiable string literal are modified.

If the variable of a pointer type is initialized with a string
literal, as in:

    char *p = "abc";

then the pointer is initialized to the address of the string literal.
Attempting to modify a string literal initialized in this way is
undefined behavior.

But in the following declaration, the string literal simply specifies
the initial values of characters to be set in the declared array:

    char p[] = "abc";

Modifying an array initialized in this way is safe.
references: SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR30-C. Do not attempt to modify string literals

CERT_C-STR30.title: CERT-STR30_C (RULE) Do not attempt to modify string literals [CERT_C-STR30]

CERT_C-STR31-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [CERT_C-STR31-a]
CERT_C-STR31-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
CERT_C-STR31-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-STR31-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
CERT_C-STR31-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

CERT_C-STR31-b.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-STR31-b.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:

with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

CERT_C-STR31-c.title: Prevent buffer overflows from tainted data [CERT_C-STR31-c]
CERT_C-STR31-c.description:
The rule detects when code writes to a buffer, but the write operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary. 

To prevent buffer overflow errors, ensure that the destination buffer has sufficient 
size to hold both the input and the null-terminator character.

The rule will report a violation when the following instances are detected:

   * The size of the destination buffer is known or can be approximated to be lower 
     than the input
   * Buffer input is considered to be tainted  
   * Input size was not compared to buffer size
   
Following copy operations are checked:
   * strcpy
   * strcat - violation is reported if a single operation did not fit the buffer (the 
     rule will not track actual buffer input)
CERT_C-STR31-c.security_relevance:
Possible buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to execute 
arbitrary code, enabling the exploiter to gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-STR31-c.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

CERT_C-STR31-d.title: Avoid buffer write overflow from tainted data [CERT_C-STR31-d]
CERT_C-STR31-d.description:
The rule detects when code writes to a buffer and the write operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary.

Specifically, a violation is reported if a 'size' value passed to a function that writes to a buffer
comes from a tainting function (e.g., user input) that can return malicious data, but has not been 
checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types) and not greater than the size
of the buffer.
CERT_C-STR31-d.security_relevance:
A possible buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
CERT_C-STR31-d.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

CERT_C-STR31-e.title: Avoid using unsafe string functions which may cause buffer overflows [CERT_C-STR31-e]
CERT_C-STR31-e.description:
This rule detects code that uses unsafe string functions from C library:
strcpy, strcat, sprintf, vsprintf, gets
According to David A. Wheeler (see reference below),
"C functions users must avoid using dangerous functions
that do not check bounds unless they've ensured
that the bounds will never get exceed.
Functions to avoid in most cases (or ensure protection) include the functions
strcpy(), strcat(), sprintf() (with cousin vsprintf()), and gets().
These should be replaced with functions such as
strncpy(), strncat(), snprintf(), fgets(), respectively."
See also; SECURITY-12,SECURITY-13, SECURITY-16
CERT_C-STR31-e.benefits: Prevents the use of functions which may cause buffer overflows.
references: 1. http://www.dwheeler.com/secure-programs/Secure-Programs-HOWTO/dangers-c.html

CERT_C-STR32-a.title: Avoid overflow due to reading a not zero terminated string [CERT_C-STR32-a]
CERT_C-STR32-a.description:
The rule detects cases of possible overflow due to running a string function on a string that
was not properly zero terminated.

The rule will report violation when the parameter passed to certain functions(see PARAMETERS section)
is not zero terminated. There are cases when such behavior may not lead to overflow or undefined
behavior(e.g. using 'snprintf' function with specific format and correct maximum number of bytes).
CERT_C-STR32-a.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
references: CWE-665: Improper Initialization

CERT_C-STR34-b.title: Cast characters to unsigned char before assignment to larger integer sizes [CERT_C-STR34-b]
CERT_C-STR34-b.description:
For implementations in which the char type is defined to have the same
range, representation and behavior as signed char, the character value
is sign-extended when assigned to the singed integer variable. For character
code 255 decimal, this sign extension results in the value -1 being assigned
to the integer that is indistinguishable from EOF.

It is a compliant solution to cast signed character data to unsigned char
before it is assigned or converted to a larger signed integer type.

This rule detects when an expression of a plain char type that is defined
to have the same range, representation and behavior as signed char is
assigned to a larger integer size.
CERT_C-STR34-b.benefits: The rule prevents conversions that may cause wrong computation results.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR34-C. Cast characters to unsigned char before converting to larger
integer sizes

CERT_C-STR34-c.title: An expressions of the 'signed char' type should not be used as an array index [CERT_C-STR34-c]
CERT_C-STR34-c.description:
An expression of the 'signed char' type used as an array index is converted
to the 'int' type because of integer promotions. After conversion, the character
data can be interpreted as negative values and may result in accessing memory
outside of the array.

This rule detects when an expression of the 'signed char' type is used
as an array index.
CERT_C-STR34-c.benefits:
The rule prevents conversions that may result in accessing memory outside
of an array.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR34-C. Cast characters to unsigned char before converting to larger
integer sizes

CERT_C-STR34-d.title: Cast characters to unsigned char before converting to larger integer sizes [CERT_C-STR34-d]
CERT_C-STR34-d.description:
For implementations in which the char type is defined to have the same
range, representation and behavior as signed char, the character value
is sign-extended when assigned to the singed integer variable. For character
code 255 decimal, this sign extension results in the value -1 being assigned
to the integer that is indistinguishable from EOF.

It is a compliant solution to cast signed character data to unsigned char
before it is assigned or converted to a larger signed integer type.

This rule detects when an expression of the plain char type
that is defined to have the same range, representation and behavior
as signed char is used as the operand of the following operators:
- unary '+', '-'
- binary '+', '-', '*', '/', '%', '<<', '>>'
- compound '+=', '-=', '*=', '/=', '%=', '<<=', '>>=' (only right hand
  operand)
and implicitly promoted to a larger signed type.
CERT_C-STR34-d.benefits: The rule prevents conversions that may cause wrong computation result.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR34-C. Cast characters to unsigned char before converting to larger
integer sizes
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.3.1.8 Usual arithmetic conversions

CERT_C-STR34.title: CERT-STR34_C (RULE) Cast characters to unsigned char before converting to larger integer sizes [CERT_C-STR34]

CERT_C-STR37-a.title: Do not pass incorrect values to ctype.h library functions [CERT_C-STR37-a]
CERT_C-STR37-a.security_relevance:
The use of any other values than in the range of the unsigned char type or
a negative value equivalent to EOF results in undefined behavior.
CERT_C-STR37-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent API violations caused by passing an unexpected
parameter to a function. Such violations could cause runtime errors.
CERT_C-STR37-a.parameters:
If the "Use heuristics to detect calls to functions from the ctype.h header" parameter
is enabled, the rule will use heuristics to determine whether the code contains any calls
to functions from the <ctype.h> header.

The "Sensitive mode" parameter allows you to enable reporting violations
when the value passed to a <ctype.h> library function may possibly be incorrect.
With this option enabled, the rule triggers when it detects incorrect values,
as well as values that may (but may not) be incorrect. This results in more violations
being reported.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.13
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.13 Any value passed to a function in <ctype.h> shall be representable as an unsigned char or be the value EOF
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR37-C. Arguments to character-handling functions must be representable as an unsigned char
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
Rule A21-8-1 Arguments to character-handling functions shall be representable as an unsigned char

CERT_C-STR37.title: CERT-STR37_C (RULE) Arguments to character-handling functions must be representable as an unsigned char [CERT_C-STR37]

CERT_C-STR38-a.title: Do not confuse narrow and wide character strings and functions [CERT_C-STR38-a]
CERT_C-STR38-a.description:
"Passing narrow string arguments to wide string functions or wide string
arguments to narrow string functions can lead to unexpected and undefined
behavior. Scaling problems are likely because of the difference in size
between wide and narrow characters. Because wide strings are terminated
by a null wide character and can contain null bytes, determining the length
is also problematic." [CERT-STR38-C]

This rule detects implicit or explicit conversions of function arguments
between pointers to plain char types and pointers to wchar_t types.
CERT_C-STR38-a.benefits:
The rule prevents unexpected and undefined behaviors. Confusing narrow
and wide character strings may result in buffer overflows or data truncation.
references: 1. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR38-C. Do not confuse narrow and wide character strings and functions

CERT_C-STR38.title: CERT-STR38_C (RULE) Do not confuse narrow and wide character strings and functions [CERT_C-STR38]

CERT_C-WIN00-a.title: Use care to ensure that LoadLibrary() will load the correct library [CERT_C-WIN00-a]
CERT_C-WIN00-a.description:
"Ensure that you specify a fully-qualified filename for the library to ensure
that the correct library is always loaded.
When a library needs to be loaded you should consider using LoadLibraryEx with
the LOAD_WITH_ALTERED_SEARCH_PATH parameter to ensure that the correct library
is loaded."
The rule reports a violation if in code is used the function LoadLibrary
or the function LoadLibraryEx without LOAD_WITH_ALTERED_SEARCH_PATH argument.
CERT_C-WIN00-a.benefits:
The use of this rule prevents than an attacker could inject a Trojan horse DLL
within your process by placing a 'tainted' DLL in a location in the DLL search
path that is found before the intended DLL.
references: 1. https://buildsecurityin.us-cert.gov/bsi/articles/knowledge/coding/766-BSI.html
2. PCI Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) Version 1.2
Requirement 6: Develop and maintain secure systems and applications
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
WIN00-C. Be specific when dynamically loading libraries

5. CWE-426: Untrusted Search Path

CERT_C-WIN30-a.title: Ensure resources are freed [CERT_C-WIN30-a]
CERT_C-WIN30-a.description:
This rule helps to ensure that allocated resources are deallocated on all paths.
Violations of the rule identify execution paths in an application where resources
are leaked.
CERT_C-WIN30-a.security_relevance:
Failure to close resources may lead to starvation, which could have
security implications.
CERT_C-WIN30-a.benefits:
Having a strict policy with respect to closing resources helps to make an application more
robust by preventing resource starvation.
CERT_C-WIN30-a.parameters:
The "Assume resource references can be stored by third-party methods of the following
types" set of parameters allows you to determine how strict the rule is when a reference
to a resource may be stored by a third-party function. For example, if there is a
third-party function SpecialCollection::add() and a resource is passed as a parameter
to this function, it will be stored in a collection and may later be closed by iterating
over the collection. Since Flow Analysis does not know the exact behavior of arbitrary
third-party functions, it behaves in either of the following ways:

   * With "Any functions" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that any third-party
     function may store a reference to a resource if the resource is passed into the function.
     This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource is later closed using
     the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are
     not reported as violations) if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
    
   * With "Functions that accept parameters which could host resource reference" parameter
     enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that a function may store a reference to a resource if
     it takes an additional parameter that can be used to store a pointer or a reference to
     the given resource. This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource
     is later closed using the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false
     negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations) if the function does not store
     a reference to the resource.
   
   * With "Functions with names matching the pattern" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis
     assumes that any third-party method which matches regex may store a reference to
     a resource if the resource is passed into the method. This prevents the rule from reporting
     false positives if the resource is later closed using the stored reference. However,
     this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations)
     if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
   
   * With all the parameters disabled, Flow Analysis takes a more aggressive approach and it
     assumes that third-party methods do not affect resources in any way. This approach
     may lead to some false positives, but it will not overlook any of the real leaks
     that may be missed with enabled parameters.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-b.title: Avoid null pointer dereferencing [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-b]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-b.description: This rule detects cases when a null pointer may be dereferenced.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-b.parameters:
The "Reporting violations where variable is known to be null due to a null check" group of parameters:

Enable or disable this check box to specify which violations are reported:
   * If disabled, the rule will report violations only on variables that are certain to be assigned with a null value.
   * If enabled, there rule will report violations not only for null value assignment, 
     but also when a check for a null value that stipulates null pointer dereferencing at the point of use is detected.

Here is an example of the second type of violations:

    int b = (ptr == 0); /* null check that stipulates null dereferencing at violation point */
    /* ... */
    if (b) {
        /* ... */
        int i = *ptr; /* violation reported if the check box is ticked */
    }

The "Exclude cases when a null check is performed inside:" set of parameters helps you reduce the number of
unwanted violations caused by a null check. For cases when a function or a macro performs a null check,
these parameters determine whether it should be assumed that the corresponding variable can be null
in the callers of the function.

The "a macro invocation" parameter determines how the rule handles null checks in macros.
Consider the following example:
 
    #define FOO(P) if (P == 0) { /* ... */ }
    
    void bar(int* p)
    {
        FOO(p);
        *p = 10; /* Violation or not? */
    }
    
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported for the above example.   

Other parameters in this set are related to the visibility of functions.
Consider the following example:

    int* foo(int* p)
    {
        if (p == 0) {
            /* ... */
        }
        return p; 
    }
    
    void bar(int* p)
    {
        foo(p);
        *p = 10; /* Violation or not? */
    }

Whether the violation inside bar() will be reported or not depends on the visibility of foo()'s 
and parameterization of the rule.

"a called function with internal linkage"
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported in the above example if the foo() function is 
a function with internal linkage (a function that is visible only in the compilation unit where it is defined).
Static global functions and functions from anonymous namespaces are functions with internal linkage. 

"a called global function"
If this option is disabled, a violation will be reported in the above example if the foo() function is
a global non-member function. 

"a called member function of the following visibility:"
This parameter defines the behavior of the rule for cases where foo() is a member function.
If this option is disabled, a violation will always be reported in the above example if foo() is a member function.
If this option is enabled, the following radio group of parameters is available:

   * "public" 
   * "public, protected"
   * "any visibility"

The table below shows all the possible options of the radio group of parameters. Each row represents one of
the parameters. The "+" character indicates that a violation will be reported in the above example.

                                  member function visibility (foo)
Parameterization:                 | public | protected | private |
"public"                          |    -   |     +     |   +     |
"public, protected"               |    -   |     -     |   +     |
"any visibility"                  |    -   |     -     |   -     |



The "Functions that do not accept NULL as their parameters" table allows you to list functions
whose parameters are not allowed to accept null.

The "External functions that may return NULL" table allows you to handle external functions
that can potentially return null.
To achieve better results, the rule provides a predefined set of such null returners from
standard libraries. Those predefined functions are used by default. They cannot be edited
or removed from the list, but they can be enabled/disabled in the "Enabled" column. Select or clear
the check box next to the function name to enable or disable the function.
Additionally, you can extend the set of null returners to include additional functions that
you assume may return null. 

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.

* The 'Non-NULL parameters' column allows you to specify 
  the list of ordinal numbers of the function parameters that are not allowed to accept null.
  List the indexes of parameters separated by a comma or use '*' to specify that all parameters are affected.

A step-by-step example of how to create your own null returner is provided in the 'EXAMPLE' 
section of this help page.

The "Treat "dynamic_cast" conversion as a source of the null value" parameter allows you
to specify if "dynamic_cast" conversion will be considered to be a source of null. 
This option is enabled by default.

class A {virtual void f();};  
class B : public A {void bar();}; 

static void foo(A* p)
{
    B* d = dynamic_cast<B*>(p); // the source of null if the option is enabled
    d->bar(); // a violation is reported if the option is enabled
}

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-c.title: Avoid division by zero [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-c]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-c.description:
This rule detects execution paths where zero may potentially be used as a denominator
in a division operation. Division by zero may lead to divide-by-zero errors and should
be avoided.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-c.benefits:
Division by zero behavior is specified as undefinded in the language standard. For this
reason, it may result in divide-by-zero errors and abnormal program termination.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-c.parameters:
The "Sensitive mode" parameter allows you to enable reporting violations
when divisors are possibly equal to zero.
With this option enabled, Flow Analysis detects not only when the actual division
by zero occurs, but also when it may (but may not) occur. This results in more
violations being reported.

This option is disabled by default.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-d.title: Avoid buffer overflow due to defining incorrect format limits [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-d]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-d.description:
The rule detects cases of possible overflow when writing to a buffer due to either:
 * an incorrectly-defined format specifier for the scanf family of functions, or
 * insufficient size of the buffer to hold resulting string for the sprintf function.
A violation is reported when the write operation can overflow the given buffer.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-d.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-e.title: Avoid overflow due to reading a not zero terminated string [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-e]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-e.description:
The rule detects cases of possible overflow due to running a string function on a string that
was not properly zero terminated.

The rule will report violation when the parameter passed to certain functions(see PARAMETERS section)
is not zero terminated. There are cases when such behavior may not lead to overflow or undefined
behavior(e.g. using 'snprintf' function with specific format and correct maximum number of bytes).
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-e.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
references: CWE-665: Improper Initialization

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-g.title: Avoid overflow when reading from a buffer [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-g]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-g.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-g.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and it is an array
with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is enabled by default.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-i.title: Do not subtract two pointers that do not address elements of the same array [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-i]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-i.description:
This rule detects when two pointers that do not address elements of the same
array are subtracted.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-i.security_relevance:
Subtracting pointers that do not address elements of the same array may cause
undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-i.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior that may be caused by
subtracting pointers that do not address elements of the same array.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-i.parameters:
If "Aggressive mode" is enabled, the rule additionally reports violations 
in cases when Flow Analysis is unable to positively verify that the subtracted
pointers address elements of the same array and there is probability that
reported violations are false positives. Enabling this parameter results in better
bug detection, but it may increase the number of false alarms.
With this parameter disabled, the rule only triggers when Flow Analysis can
conclusively determine that the subtracted pointers do not address elements of
the same array.
references: AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

Rule M0-3-1. Minimization of run-time failures shall be ensured by the use of at least one of: (a) static analysis tools/techniques; (b) dynamic analysis tools/techniques; (c) explicit coding of checks to handle run-time faults

Rule M5-0-17. Subtraction between pointers shall only be applied to pointers that address elements of the same array

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-j.title: Do not compare two unrelated pointers [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-j]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-j.description:
This rule detects when two unrelated pointers are compared.

The rule assumes that two pointers are related if they store addresses of two elements
of the same array. Whether two pointers that point to two elements of the same structure
object are related or not depends on the rule parameterization (see PARAMETERS).
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-j.security_relevance: Comparison of unrelated pointers may cause undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-j.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior that may be caused by
comparing unrelated pointers.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-j.parameters:
If the "Report when the pointers are compared using relational operators"
parameter is enabled, the rule detects when pointers are compared with
the following operators:
	operator>
	operator<
	operator>=
	operator<=
	
If the "Report when the pointers are compared using standard functions" parameter 
is enabled, the rule detects when pointers are compared with the following
operators:
	std::less()
	std::greater()
	std::less_equal()
	std::greater_equal()
	
If the "Treat pointers addressing fields of the same object as related"
parameter is enabled, the rule assumes that that two pointers that address
elements of the same structure object (for example, two fields of the same
object) are related.

If "Aggressive mode" is enabled, the rule additionally reports pointer
comparison in cases when Flow Analysis is unable to positively verify that
the compared pointers are related and there is probability that reported
violations are false positives. Enabling this parameter results in better
bug detection, but it may increase the number of false alarms. 
With this parameter disabled, the rule only triggers when Flow Analysis can
conclusively determine that the compared pointers are not related.
references: AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-k.title: Avoid wraparounds when performing arithmetic integer operations [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-k]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-k.description:
An integer overflow occurs when an integer value is incremented to a value that
is too large to store in the associated representation. When this occurs for
unsigned integers, the value can wrap to become a very small number.
For example: UINT_MAX + 1

Similarly, an integer underflow occurs when an integer value is decremented to
a value that is too small to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs for unsigned integers, the value can wrap to become a very large number.
For example: UINT_MIN - 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on
wrapping, it can have serious consequences if the wrap is unexpected.

This rule reports a violation when an overflow or underflow occurs in the
unsigned integer arithmetic operation.

See also BD-PB-INTUB
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-k.security_relevance:
Integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous if the result value is used in
a sensitive area, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
memory allocation function)
- Loop conditions
- In security-critical code
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-k.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected behavior (see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-k.parameters:
'Violate on overflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
overflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is higher than the
upper boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.

'Violate on underflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
underflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is lower than
zero.
This option is enabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-190: Integer Overflow or Wraparound
CWE-191: Integer Underflow (Wrap or Wraparound)

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-l.title: Avoid signed integer overflows [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-l]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-l.description:
An integer overflow occurs when an integer value is incremented to a value that
is too large to store in the associated representation. When this occurs for
signed integers, it causes undefined behavior.

Similarly, an integer underflow occurs when an integer value is decremented to
a value that is too small to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs for signed integers, it causes undefined behavior.

This rule reports a violation when an overflow or underflow occurs in the signed
integer arithmetic operation.

See also BD-PB-INTWRAP
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-l.security_relevance:
Signed integer overflow leads to undefined behavior.

Integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous if the result value is used in
a sensitive area, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
memory allocation function)
- Loop conditions
- In security-critical code
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-l.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-l.parameters:
'Violate on overflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
overflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is higher than the
upper boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.

'Violate on underflow' - when enabled, violations will be reported when an
underflow occurs, i.e. the result of the arithmetic operation is lower than the
lower boundary value for the given type.
This option is enabled by default.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-m.title: Avoid data loss when converting between integer types [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-m]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-m.description:
This rule detects when casting an integer value to a narrower type results in
a data loss.
A violation is reported when:
* The given value cannot be represented in the result type, and
* The width of the result type is smaller than the width of the source type

See also BD-PB-INTVC
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-m.security_relevance:
Typically, converting an integer to a smaller type results in the truncation of
the high-order bits. If the high-order bits store some data, that data may be
lost. Therefore, the result of integer casting may be incorrect and data loss
makes it impossible to retrieve the correct result. This becomes
security-critical when the result is used to control looping, make a security
decision, or determine the offset or size in behaviors such as memory
allocation, copying, concatenation, etc.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-m.benefits: This rule helps you prevent data loss.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1-m.parameters:
"Aggressive mode" - when enabled, violations will also be reported when the
value being cast is -1.
This option is disabled by default.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1.title: Dir 4.1 (Required) Run-time failures shall be minimized [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_1]

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_10-a.title: Use multiple include guards [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_10-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_10-a.description:
Every include file must contain a mechanism that prevents multiple inclusion
of the file.

Following patterns are recognized and accepted:

#ifndef SOME_MACRO
#define SOME_MACRO
// code
#endif

or

#if !defined(SOME_MACRO)
#define SOME_MACRO
// code
#endif

See also: PFO-07, PFO-08, MISRA2004-19_15
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_10-a.benefits:
Prevents confusion caused by including the same header multiple times,
prevents undefined or erroneous behaviour caused by multiple or conflicting
definitions.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_10-a.example_violation:
// file foo.hh
// Violation - no multiple inclusion mechanism present
references: 1. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 4 Source Code in Files - 4.4 Include Files - Rule 7

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_11-a.title: Validate values passed to library functions [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_11-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_11-a.security_relevance:
Passing a value that is out of the expected range as a function parameter
violates API. This may lead to runtime errors.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_11-a.benefits:
This rule helps prevent API violations caused by passing an unexpected
parameter to a function. Such violations could cause runtime errors.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_11-a.parameters:
If the "Use heuristics to detect calls to functions from the ctype.h header" parameter
is enabled, the rule will use heuristics to determine whether the code contains any calls
to functions from the <ctype.h> header.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_12-a.title: Dynamic heap memory allocation shall not be used [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_12-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_12-a.description:
"The use of dynamic memory can lead to out-of-storage run-time failures,
which are undesirable. The built-in 'new' and 'delete' operators, other than
the placement versions, use dynamic heap memory. The functions 'calloc',
'malloc', 'realloc' and 'free' also use dynamic heap memory." [MISRA C++:2008]

"The identifiers 'calloc', 'malloc', 'realloc', 'aligned_alloc' and 'free'
shall not be used and no macro with one of these names shall be expanded."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

The rule detects calls to the 'calloc()', 'malloc()', 'realloc()',
'aligned_alloc()', and 'free()' functions, as well as the use of 'new' and
'delete' operators.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_12-a.benefits:
"There is a range of unspecified, undefined and implementation-defined
behaviour associated with dynamic memory allocation, as well as a number of
other potential pitfalls. Dynamic heap memory allocation may lead to memory
leaks, data inconsistency, memory exhaustion, non-deterministic behaviour."
[MISRA C++:2008]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_12-a.example_repair:
Modify your code to avoid using the 'new' and 'delete' operators
and the 'calloc()', 'malloc()', 'realloc()', 'aligned_alloc()',
and 'free()' functions.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-a.title: Ensure resources are freed [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-a.description:
This rule helps to ensure that allocated resources are deallocated on all paths.
Violations of the rule identify execution paths in an application where resources
are leaked.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-a.security_relevance:
Failure to close resources may lead to starvation, which could have
security implications.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-a.benefits:
Having a strict policy with respect to closing resources helps to make an application more
robust by preventing resource starvation.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-a.parameters:
The "Assume resource references can be stored by third-party methods of the following
types" set of parameters allows you to determine how strict the rule is when a reference
to a resource may be stored by a third-party function. For example, if there is a
third-party function SpecialCollection::add() and a resource is passed as a parameter
to this function, it will be stored in a collection and may later be closed by iterating
over the collection. Since Flow Analysis does not know the exact behavior of arbitrary
third-party functions, it behaves in either of the following ways:

   * With "Any functions" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that any third-party
     function may store a reference to a resource if the resource is passed into the function.
     This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource is later closed using
     the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are
     not reported as violations) if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
    
   * With "Functions that accept parameters which could host resource reference" parameter
     enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that a function may store a reference to a resource if
     it takes an additional parameter that can be used to store a pointer or a reference to
     the given resource. This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource
     is later closed using the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false
     negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations) if the function does not store
     a reference to the resource.
   
   * With "Functions with names matching the pattern" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis
     assumes that any third-party method which matches regex may store a reference to
     a resource if the resource is passed into the method. This prevents the rule from reporting
     false positives if the resource is later closed using the stored reference. However,
     this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations)
     if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
   
   * With all the parameters disabled, Flow Analysis takes a more aggressive approach and it
     assumes that third-party methods do not affect resources in any way. This approach
     may lead to some false positives, but it will not overlook any of the real leaks
     that may be missed with enabled parameters.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-b.title: Do not use resources that have been freed [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-b]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-b.description:
This rule detects uses of resources that have been freed. In particular, a violation is reported
when a pointer or reference to a freed resource is:

   * returned from a function,
   * passed as a parameter to a function,
   * used in an arithmetic operation,
   * dereferenced, or
   * assigned to a variable/field/array element.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-b.benefits: Prevents strange, often non-deterministic errors related to the use of freed resources.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-b.parameters:
The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to null" check box
unsupresses/suppresses  violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to a null
pointer. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to other pointers" check box
unsuppresses/suppresses violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to other
pointers. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-c.title: Do not free resources using invalid pointers [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-c]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-c.description:
This rule detects attempts to free memory using an invalid pointer.
A violation is reported when the pointer used to free a resource meets any of
the following conditions:

   * It is a pointer to a string constant.
   * It is acquired as a result of a cast of an integer constant to pointer type (wild pointer).
   * It is acquired via an address operation applied to a parameter, local, global
     or static variable (attempt to free resource in non-heap memory).
   * It does not point to the beginning of the allocated chunk.
   * It is not allocated pointer.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-d.title: Do not abandon unreleased locks [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-d]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-d.description:
This rule detects cases where a mutex is locked but not unlocked at an
appropriate place, which could cause an application deadlock.

The list of supported multithreading APIs can be found and modified in test
configuration "Flow Analysis Advanced Settings" -> "Multi-threading".
The rule will report a violation if one of the functions defined as 
"Functions for locking" is used without a proper use of a function defined
as "Functions for unlocking".
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-d.parameters:
The "Locked object may be unlocked from callers up to specified level" parameter
allows you to manage the depth of analysis of the call graph. Lower numbers are 
recommended to enforce the best practice that locking and unlocking occur at the
same nesting level. This practice is required by MISRA C/C++ & JSF standards.

Higher numbers of the parameter value may be preferred for (rare) cases where 
locking and unlocking have to be performed on different levels as well as in the
case where certain functions serve as wrappers to standard locking and unlocking
functions. The parameter of the value is set to 1  by default. This ensures the
detection of most bugs related to direct uses of standard locking functions
as well as the use of custom wrappers around them - without producing
unnecessary false positives.

To demonstrate this, let's assume the following wrapper functions:

void mutex_lock()
{
    pthread_mutex_lock(mutex);
    // do something else
}

void mutex_unlock()
{
    pthread_mutex_unlock(mutex);
    // do something else
}

With the value of parameter set to 0, Flow Analysis will see the call to
pthread_mutex_lock inside the wrapper function "mutex_lock" and no corresponding
call to pthread_mutex_unlock inside the function. Consequently, it will report a 
violation which is in fact a false positive because mutex_lock is not meant to 
call unlocking function by design and pthread_mutex_unlock is called from the 
other wrapper function named "mutex_unlock". Setting the depth to values higher
than 0 prevents false positives for such wrapper functions while still ensuring
that violations will be reported if the code doesn't call "mutex_unlock".
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.13

2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.16

Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.16 All mutex objects locked by a thread shall be explicitly unlocked by the same thread

CON01-C. Acquire and release synchronization primitives in the same module, at the same level of abstraction

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-e.title: Avoid double locking [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-e]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-e.description:
This rule detects cases where a lock is acquired twice without intermediate releasing.
While this is legal for the so-called recursive mutexes, it is not allowed for regular
locks and may cause deadlocks.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-e.parameters:
The "Report on tryLock methods" parameter allows you to specify if the rule will
trigger when the second lock is obtained through a method with the "tryLock"
semantics (e.g. pthread_mutex_trylock).
This option is enabled by default.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-f.title: Do not release a lock that has not been acquired [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-f]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-f.description:
This rule detects cases where a lock is released, but it has not been acquired 
in the same method.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_13-f.benefits: Helps you prevent unlocking another thread's lock.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-a.title: Avoid tainted data in array indexes [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations that may result in out-of-bounds access.
Specifically, it detects cases when an index value that is used to access an array element
comes from a tainting function (e.g. user input) that can return malicious data,
but has not been checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types)
and less than the size of the array.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-a.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter allows you to enable or disable
reporting unvalidated violations. If enabled, Flow  Analysis does not check
whether the path of the violation can be reached from the beginning of
the function. Enabling this parameter may be useful in complex cases when
restricted analysis depth prevents Flow Analysis from completing the violation
validation procedure, but it may result in reporting false positives.
