MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-b.title: Avoid potential integer overflow/underflow on tainted data [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-b]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-b.description:
This rule detects cases when unvalidated input (tainted data) is used in an arithmetic
operation that can result in numeric overflow or underflow.

A numeric overflow is a condition that occurs when the mathematical result of an operation
exceeds the maximum value that can be represented by the result type of the operation.
A numeric underflow occurs when the result of an operation is smaller than the minimum value
that can be represented by the result type of the operation.

A violation is reported at the point of potential overflow.

See also BD-SECURITY-INTOVERF
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-b.security_relevance:
Integer overflow is a serious security threat as it may be used to crash an application or
cause denial of service.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-b.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter allows you to enable or disable
reporting unvalidated violations. If enabled, Flow  Analysis does not check
whether the path of the violation can be reached from the beginning of
the function. Enabling this parameter may be useful in complex cases when
restricted analysis depth prevents Flow Analysis from completing the violation
validation procedure, but it may result in reporting false positives.

APSC-DV-002590: The application must not be vulnerable to overflow attacks.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-c.title: Avoid buffer read overflow from tainted data [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-c]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-c.description:
The rule detects cases when code reads from a buffer and the read operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary.

Specifically, a violation is reported if the 'size' value passed to the function that reads from a buffer
comes from a tainting function (e.g. user input) that can return malicious data, but has not not been
checked and ensured to be non-negative (applicable to signed types) and not greater than the size
of the buffer.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-c.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-c.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-e.title: Protect against command injection [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-e]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-e.description:
This rule detects cases when data coming directly from the end-user can influence
the code which is executed (for example, to specify the name of the file to
be executed).

The rule supports functions for process execution from the standard C library as well
as from POSIX. For example, it considers the widely used system() function and the exec family
of functions to be dangerous.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-e.security_relevance:
If tainted data appears in the executed file name without verification,
it may enable the execution of custom malicious code that could damage the system.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-e.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

This rule detects cases when possibly tainted data is used as a filename or a path in file
manipulating functions, which could lead to file name injection. The rule supports file manipulation
functions from the standard C and C++ libraries as well as those defined by POSIX.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-f.security_relevance:

to any file on the system with specially-prepared malicious data.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-g.title: Protect against SQL injection [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-g]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-g.description:
This rule detects possible SQL injections when possibly tainted data reaches functions
that prepare or execute SQL queries, retrieve connections, etc. 

When an application uses data provided by the user (or some other unverified data source)
to construct SQL queries and does not verify/validate such data before it is used,
it is possible for an attacker to alter the SQL statements in a way that the developer
did not intend. As a result, the attacker can take total control of the database or
execute commands on the system.

The rule supports the following database APIs: ODBC, ADO, OLE DB.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-g.security_relevance:
If data that appears in an SQL query has not been validated,
it may enable a malicious user to take control of the database.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-g.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-h.title: Prevent buffer overflows from tainted data [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-h]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-h.description:
The rule detects when code writes to a buffer, but the write operation can go beyond
the buffer boundary. 

To prevent buffer overflow errors, ensure that the destination buffer has sufficient 
size to hold both the input and the null-terminator character.

The rule will report a violation when the following instances are detected:

   * The size of the destination buffer is known or can be approximated to be lower 
     than the input
   * Buffer input is considered to be tainted  
   * Input size was not compared to buffer size
   
Following copy operations are checked:
   * strcpy
   * strcat - violation is reported if a single operation did not fit the buffer (the 
     rule will not track actual buffer input)
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-h.security_relevance:
Possible buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to execute 
arbitrary code, enabling the exploiter to gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-h.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-i.title: Avoid buffer overflow from tainted data due to defining incorrect format limits [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-i]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-i.description:
This rule detects possible overflow when writing to a buffer as a result of 
insufficient buffer size for holding the string that results from the sprintf function.

To prevent buffer overflow errors, ensure that the destination buffer has sufficient 
size to hold both the input and the null-terminator character.

The rule will report a violation when the following conditions are detected:
   * Destination buffer size is known or when it's smallest size can be approximated  
   * Input size was not compared to buffer size
   * Buffer input is considered tainted
   * Formatting the string did not restrict the size of the input or restrictions are 
     incorrect (allows larger input than size of buffer)
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-i.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to execute 
arbitrary code, enabling the exploiter to gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-i.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators
  
The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-j.title: Protect against environment injection [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-j]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-j.description:
This rule detects cases when data that comes directly from the end-user and
is used to define an environment variable has not been checked.

This rule triggers when tainted data is passed to the following methods:

   * int setenv(const char*, const char*, int)
   * int unsetenv(const char*)
   * int putenv(char*)
   * int _putenv(const char*)
   * int _wputenv(const wchar_t*)
   * errno_t _putenv_s(const char*, const char*)
   * errno_t _wputenv_s(const wchar_t*, const wchar_t*)
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-j.security_relevance:
category: Input-Based Attacks

If unverified data reaches system properties, it may allow an attacker to damage the system.

sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify

   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console

   * Random number generators

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-k.title: Avoid printing tainted data on the output console [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-k]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-k.security_relevance:
Writing tainted user data to the console may allow an attacker to forge console output
or print malicious content on the console.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-k.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter allows you to enable or disable
reporting unvalidated violations. If enabled, Flow  Analysis does not check
whether the path of the violation can be reached from the beginning of
the function. Enabling this parameter may be useful in complex cases when
restricted analysis depth prevents Flow Analysis from completing the violation
validation procedure, but it may result in reporting false positives.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-l.title: Exclude unsanitized user input from format strings [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-l]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-l.description:
This rule detects when possibly tainted data is used as a format
string argument in a string formatting function.

It triggers when tainted data is passed as a format string to one of the 
following functions:

    * printf(const char*, ...)
    * fprintf(FILE*, const char*, ...)
    * sprintf(char*, const char*, ...)
    * snprintf(char*, size_t, const char*, ...)
    * vprintf(const char*, va_list)
    * vfprintf(FILE*, const char*, va_list)
    * vsprintf(char*, const char*, va_list)
    * vsnprintf(char*, size_t, const char*, va_list)    
    * wprintf(const wchar_t*, ...)
    * fwprintf(FILE*, const wchar_t*, ...)
    * swprintf(wchar_t*, size_t, const wchar_t*, ...)
    * vfwprintf(FILE*, const wchar_t*, va_list)
    * vswprintf(wchar_t*, size_t, const wchar_t*, va_list)
    * vwprintf(const wchar_t*, va_list)

The rule can be parameterized (see PARAMETERS).
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-l.security_relevance:
"When an attacker can modify an externally-controlled format string, this can
lead to buffer overflows, denial of service, or data representation problems."
[CWE-134]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-l.benefits: This rule helps you prevent information leaks and denial-of-service attacks.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_14-l.parameters:
The "Sources of tainted data" parameter allows you to specify which data
sources the rule considers to be sources of tainted data. You can specify
the following sources:
   * Files
   * Pipes
   * Stream-oriented APIs (std::istream, CArchive and CFile from MFC)
   * Low-level input (Windows API, POSIX)
   * Console
   * Environment variables
   * Parameters of entry point methods
   * Network
   * Sockets
   * Random number generators

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify functions that can
validate tainted data. The rule assumes that parameters, target objects, and
return values are benign if one of the validating functions is called. 
Fill in the following columns to configure a function.
- 'Enabled' - enables or disables the validating function. If disabled, the rule
    does not consider the function to be a validating function.
- 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' - specifies
    the fully qualified name of the type that declares the function. You can
    use "*" for a function declared in any type.
- 'Function name (wildcard)' - specifies the name of the function.
- '+ definitions in subclasses' - if enabled, the information configured in
    the current row applies to function with the specified name that are defined
    in subclasses of the given class. Note that this applies to both instance
    and non-instance functions.
- "'this' object is validated" - if enabled,  the function cleans the object it
    is called on.
- 'Returns validated data' - if enabled, the function cleans its return value.
- 'Validated parameters' - specifies which function parameters are cleaned by
    the function. List 1-based indexes of parameters separated by a comma or
    use "*" to specify that all function parameters are cleaned.

The "Do not consider numerical data tainted" parameter allows you to specify
how the rule treats numerical data. If enabled, the rule assumes that numerical
data (both primitive data types and objects) is never tainted, even if it
comes from an untrusted source.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_15-a.title: Do not use floating-point expressions which may result in NaN and infinity values [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_15-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_15-a.security_relevance:
The unintentional use of infinity or NaN values can lead to undefined behavior
(e.g. they may be passed to code that was not designed to support them).
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_15-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_3-a.title: Assembly language shall be encapsulated and isolated [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_3-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_3-a.description:
"Where assembly language instructions are required it is recommended
that they be encapsulated and isolated in either
- Assembly language functions;
- C functions (inline functions preferred for C99);
- C macros.
For reasons of efficiency it is sometimes necessary to embed simple assembly
language instructions in-line, for example to enable and disable interrupts.
If this is necessary, then it is recommended that it be achieved by using
macros, or for C99, inline functions."

The rule reports a violation if in one function is used assembly language
instruction and C/C++ code.

See also: CODSTA-114

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_4-a.title: Sections of code should not be "commented out" [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_4-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_4-a.description: This rule detects when a comment contains C or C++ code.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_4-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid mistakes and confusions.

"Where it is required for sections of source code not to be compiled
then this should be achieved by use of conditional compilation
(e.g. #if or #ifdef constructs with a comment). Using start and end
comment markers for this purpose is dangerous because C does not support
nested comments, and any comments already existing in the section of code
would change the effect." [MISRA C:2012]

"Ideally, comments should only be used to explain aspects of the code that
may not be clear from the source code itself. Code that is commented-out may
become out of date, which may lead to confusion when maintaining the code.
A more appropriate method of recording the history of changes in source code
(e.g. a Source Control System) should be used instead of commenting-out."
[MISRA C++:2008]

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-a.title: A typedef for a standard integer type should contain the number indicating its size in its name [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-a.description:
This rule detects when the names of typedefs for the standard integer types
'char' (explicitly declared as 'unsigned' or 'signed'), 'short', 'int', 'long',
and 'long long' do not contain numbers indicating their size in bits.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve the readability of the code.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-a.example_violation:
typedef signed int my_int;       // Violation - the name does not contain any digits
typedef signed int my_int16;     // Violation - if 'int' is not a 16-bit size
typedef signed int my4_int32;    // Violation - the name contains additional digits
typedef const int const_int;     // Violation - the name does not contain any digits

typedef signed int my_int32;     // OK - if 'int' is a 32-bit size
typedef unsigned char uint8_t;   // OK - if 'char' is an 8-bit size

typedef char char_t;             // OK - the rule does not apply
typedef float my_float;          // OK - the rule does not apply

typedef unsigned char bool_type; // OK - exception
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.6
2. MISRA C:2023 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.6
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.6 typedefs that indicate size and signedness should be used in place of the basic integer types
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6.3
5. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.28 Portable Code, AV Rule 209

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid using integer types with unclear storage sizes.

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-c.title: Use typedefs from stdint.h instead of declaring your own in C99 code [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-c]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-c.description:
"For C99, the types provided by <stdint.h> should be used."
[MISRA C:2012 Dir 4.6]

This rule reports a violation on typedef declaration which directly denote one
of following types:
    short, int, long, long long
    signed char, unsigned char
For "char" type, only typedefs to explicitly signed or explicitly unsigned char
type are checked (typedefs to plain char are not checked)

Instead of declaring your own typedefs, you should use typedefs provided by
stdint.h header in C99 standard:
    intN_t  - designates a signed integer type with width N
    uintN_t - designates an unsigned integer type with width N

See also: MISRA2004-6_3, MISRA2004-6_3_b
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-c.benefits:
"Defining a specific-length type whose size is not the same as the implemented
type is counter-productive both in terms of storage requirements and in terms
of portability. Care should be taken to avoid defining types with the wrong
size" [MISRA C:2012 Dir 4.6]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-c.example_violation:
typedef signed short my_short;  /* Violation */
typedef volatile long count;    /* Violation */
typedef signed char schar;      /* Violation */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.6
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.6 typedefs that indicate size and signedness should be used in place of the basic integer types

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-d.title: The names of standard floating-point types should not be used [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-d]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-d.description:
This rule detects when the standard floating-point types 'float', 'double',
and 'long double' are used in the code.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-d.benefits: This rule helps you avoid using floating-point types with unclear storage sizes.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-d.example_violation:
static float f32;                  // Violation

double f1();                       // Violation
void f2( double d )                // Violation
{
    float* pf32;                   // Violation
    long double ld;                // Violation
    ld = (long double) d;          // Violation
}

typedef float float32_t;           // OK - exception
typedef double float64_t;          // OK - exception
typedef float* pfloat32_t;         // Violation - do not define a specific-length type
typedef const float cfloat32_t;    // Violation - do not define a specific-length type
typedef const float32_t cint16_t;  // OK

void f3( float64_t d )             // OK
{
    float32_t* pf32;               // OK
    float64_t ld;                  // OK
    ld = (float64_t) d;            // OK
}
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6.3
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.6
3. MISRA C:2023 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.6
4. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.28 Portable Code, AV Rule 209

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-e.title: A typedef for a standard floating-point type should contain the number indicating its size in its name [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-e]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-e.description:
This rule detects when the names of typedefs for the standard floating-point
types 'float', 'double' and 'long double' (and their complex versions)
do not contain numbers indicating their size in bits.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-e.benefits: This rule helps you improve the readability of the code.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_6-e.example_violation:
typedef float my_float;             // Violation - the name does not contain any digits
typedef float my_float64;           // Violation - if 'float' is not a 64-bit size
typedef float my4_float32;          // Violation - the name contains additional digits
typedef const double const_double;  // Violation - the name does not contain any digits

typedef float float32_t;            // OK - if 'float' is a 32-bit size
typedef double float64_t;           // OK - if 'double' is a 64-bit size

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-a.title: Consistently check the returned value of non-void functions [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-a.description:
This rule detects unchecked returned values for methods that typically check returned values.

Returned values can be checked in one of the following ways:
- the returned value is used in a conditional statement (if, switch, while etc.)
- the returned value is used in a binary expression with >, <, >=, <=, !=, == operators
- the returned value is passed to a validating method as a parameter.

Using the returned value means:
- passing it as a parameter to a method other than a validating method
- dereferencing a pointer, accessing its fields, invoking its methods etc.

The returned value is considered unchecked if:
- it is used before it has been checked
- it has not been checked at all
- it is not used.

This rule works only on a scope of one method. For this reason, the returned values that are
passed further to another method are counted neither as checked nor as unchecked.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-a.security_relevance:
If returned values are unchecked, then an attacker may force the function to fail or return a value
that is not expected. This may lead to a software state that the programmer assumed would not be possible.
For example, if a program calls a function to drop privileges, but does not check the return code to ensure
that privileges were successfully dropped, then the program may continue to operate with the higher privileges.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-a.benefits:
Checking the value returned form a method can prevent unexpected states and conditions that may
lead to crashes or other unintended behaviors.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-a.parameters:
The "Unchecked ratio threshold" parameter allows you to specify when a violation should be reported.
The value expresses the ratio of the number of times returned values are checked to the number of
the method invocations. If the ratio is lower than the specified threshold, the rule will report a violation.
The default value is 0.2. 

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify a list of functions that can be used to check returned values.
The returned value will be considered to be checked if it is passed to one of the listed functions as a parameter.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
references: CWE-252: Unchecked Return Value
MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.7
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.7 If a function returns error information, then that error information shall be tested

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-b.title: Always check the returned value of non-void function [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-b]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-b.description:
This rule detects cases when returned values of functions are not checked.
The list of library functions that should have their returned values checked includes:
- void* bsearch(const void* key, const void* base, size_t num, size_t size,
                 int (*compar)(const void*, const void*))
- void* calloc(size_t num, size_t size)
- int fgetc(FILE* stream)
- char* fgets(char* str, int num, FILE* stream)
- FILE* fopen(const char* filename, const char* mode)
- size_t fread(void* ptr, size_t size, size_t count, FILE* stream)
- void* malloc(size_t size)
- void* aligned_alloc(size_t alignment, size_t size)
- (const)void* memchr ((const)void* ptr, int value, size_t num)
- void* realloc (void* ptr, size_t size)
- (const)char* strchr((const)char * str, int character)
- (const)char* strpbrk((const)char* str1, const char* str2)
- (const)char* strrchr((const)char* str, int character)
- (const)char* strstr((const)char* str1, const char* str2)
- char* strtok(char* str, const char* delimiters)
- (const)wchar_t* wcschr((const)wchar_t* ws, wchar_t wc)
- (const)wchar_t* wcspbrk((const)wchar_t* wcs1, const wchar_t* wcs2)
- (const)wchar_t* wcsrchr((const)wchar_t* ws, wchar_t wc)
- (const)wchar_t* wcsstr((const)wchar_t* wcs1, const wchar_t* wcs2)
- wchar_t* wcstok(wchar_t* wcs, const wchar_t* delimiters(, wchar_t **ptr))
- (const)wchar_t* wmemchr((const)wchar_t* ptr, wchar_t wc, size_t num)

Returned values can be checked in one of the following ways:
- the returned value is used in a conditional statement (if, switch, while etc.)
- the returned value is used in a binary expression with >, <, >=, <=, !=, == operators
- the returned value is passed to a validating method as a parameter.

Using the returned value means:
- passing it as a parameter to a method other than a validating method
- dereferencing a pointer, accessing its fields, invoking its methods etc.

The returned value is considered unchecked if:
- it is used before it has been checked
- it has not been checked at all
- it is not used.

This rule works only on a scope of one method. For this reason, the returned values that are
passed further to another method are counted neither as checked nor as unchecked.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-b.security_relevance:
If returned values are unchecked, then an attacker may force the function to fail or return a value
that is not expected. This may lead to a software state that the programmer assumed would not be possible.
For example, if a program calls a function to drop privileges, but does not check the return code to ensure
that privileges were successfully dropped, then the program may continue to operate with the higher privileges.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-b.benefits:
Checking the value returned form a function can prevent unexpected states and conditions that may
lead to crashes or other unintended behaviors.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7-b.parameters:
The "Analyze library functions" parameter allows you to specify if the default library functions
(listed above) should always have their values checked.

The "Validating functions" parameter allows you to specify a list of functions that can be used to check returned values.
The returned value will be considered to be checked if it is passed to one of the listed functions as a parameter.

The "Additional functions which return value should be checked" parameter allows you to specify additional functions
whose returned values should always be checked.

The "Library functions which return value does not need to be checked" parameter allows you to specify the builtin 
functions whose returned values should not be checked.

The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the tables: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions
  for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide
  the fully qualified name of the type or namespace where the function is declared.
  Use '*' if you want to describe function declared in any type or namespace, or
  a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' column should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' column is used to indicate whether the current row will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class. Note that this
  applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives being reported. In this mode, Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.
references: CWE-252: Unchecked Return Value
MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.7
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.7 If a function returns error information, then that error information shall be tested
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
ERR33-C. Detect and handle standard library errors

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7.title: Dir 4.7 (Required) If a function returns error information, then that error information shall be tested [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_7]

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_8-a.title: If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced within a translation unit, then the implementation of the object should be hidden [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_8-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_8-a.description:
"Hiding the implementation details creates an opaque type that may
be referenced via a pointer whose contents may not be accessed. The
implementation of an object should be hidden by means of a pointer
to an incomplete type." [MISRA C:2012]

This rule reports a violation if the implementation of the structure
or the union is not required in a translation unit. If the structure
is defined in a header file, then its implementation can be required
in one translation unit and not required in the other, so the user
should take care when refactoring the code.
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_8-a.benefits:
"If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced, then the
implementation details of the object are not needed and its contents
should be protected from unintentional changes." [MISRA C:2012]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.8
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.8 If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced within a translation unit, then the implementation of the object should be hidden
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL12-C. Implement abstract data types using opaque types

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_8.title: Dir 4.8 (Advisory) If a pointer to a structure or union is never dereferenced within a translation unit, then the implementation of the object should be hidden [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_8]

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_9-a.title: A function should be used in preference to a function-like macro [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_9-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_9-a.description:
A function should be used in preference to a function-like macro
if it is permitted by the syntax and constraints of the language standard.
In most circumstances, functions should be used instead of macros. Functions
perform argument type-checking and evaluate their arguments once, thus avoiding
problems with potential multiple side-effects. In many debugging systems,
it is easier to step through execution of a function than a macro.
Nonetheless, macros may be useful in some circumstances.

This rule detects definitions of function-like macros.

See also: CODSTA-03, CODSTA-37, CODSTA-38
MISRAC2012-DIR_4_9-a.benefits:
"While macros can provide a speed advantage over functions, functions provide
a safer and more robust mechanism. This is particularly true with respect
to the type checking of parameters, and the problem of function-like macros
potentially evaluating parameters multiple times."
[MISRA C++:2008 Rule 16-0-4]
references: 1. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 9 Functions - 9.5 Inline Functions - Rule 35
2. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.6 Pre-Processing Directives, AV Rule 29
3. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-4
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.9
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.9 A function should be used in preference to a function-like macro where they are interchangeable
6. Misra Guidelines - Rule 93
7. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19, Rule 19.7
8. Scott Meyers, "Effective C++: 55 Specific Ways to Improve
Your Programs and Design", Third Edition, Addison-Wesley,
(C) 2005 Pearson Education, Inc., Chapter 1, Item 2
9. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE00-C. Prefer inline or static functions to function-like macros

MISRAC2012-DIR_4_9.title: Dir 4.9 (Advisory) A function should be used in preference to a function-like macro where they are interchangeable [MISRAC2012-DIR_4_9]

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-a.title: Do not use global variable with different locks set [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-a.description:
Rule checks how many times global variables are used in critical sections. Global 
variables are regularly used in critical sections that hold some set of mutexes. 
When the variables are used in this way and subsequently used outside of a critical 
section or with a different set of locking objects then its possible that that 
variable can be accessed without synchronization, putting the application in an 
incorrect state.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-a.benefits: Prevents unsynchronized access to global variables.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-a.parameters:
"Disallowed usages ratio in critical section" defines the usage threshold for reporting violations.
For example, if a variable is used 10 times in a method - 7 times in a critical section with the
same set of locking objects, and 3 times outside of critical sections or with a different set of
locking objects - 3 violations will be reported when the threshold is set to 0.3. This is because
there is one common pattern of variable usage (it is used 70% of the time in that way), but there
are also 3 other cases when the variable is used in a different way.
    
"Compare usages in critical sections and outside of them regardless the lock object being used" turns off 
checking locking objects. The rule will count usages in all critical sections and compare it to usages 
without critical section.
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-362: Concurrent Execution using Shared Resource with Improper Synchronization ('Race Condition')
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.1
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.1 There shall be no data races between threads
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
18.2.2 Synchronize access to data shared between threads using a single lock
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON43-C. Do not allow data races in multithreaded code
DISA Application Security and Development Security Technical Implementation Guide (STIG)
APSC-DV-001995: The application must not be vulnerable to race conditions.

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-b.title: Variable should be used in context of single critical section [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-b]
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-b.description:
This rule detects when a global variable or method parameter passed as a pointer or reference
is used in more than one critical section in the context of a single method.
Using a variable in many critical sections in a single method does not guarantee the
atomicity of the method. If the method is executed by more than one thread, this
may result in an incorrect state of the synchronized object.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-b.security_relevance:
"When a multi-step operation on a shared resource cannot be guaranteed to execute independent
of interference, then the resulting behavior can be unpredictable. Improper synchronization
could lead to data or memory corruption, denial of service, etc.." [CWE-662]
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-b.benefits: This rule helps you prevent incorrect states of objects in the application.
references: CWE-662: Improper Synchronization
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.1
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.1 There shall be no data races between threads

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-c.title: There shall be no data races between threads [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-c]
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-c.security_relevance:
A data race can cause memory corruption and lead to unexpected, erroneous or erratic 
behavior. Data races typically manifest sporadically and are very hard to reproduce.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-c.benefits: This rule helps you prevent data races between threads.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1-c.parameters:
The "Report on non-guarded use of thread entry point parameter" parameter allows to
specify if the rule should report violation when entry point parameter is used without
any synchronization.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Names of excluded global variables (wildcard)" text field allows to define 
the name pattern for global variables that do not need to be checked.
The default value is an empty string.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.1
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.1 There shall be no data races between threads
CWE-366: Race Condition within a Thread

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1.title: Dir 5.1 (Required) There shall be no data races between threads [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_1]

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_2-a.title: Do not acquire locks in different order [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_2-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_2-a.description:
This rule detects cases where two mutexes are locked in different orders, which
can cause a deadlock. The violation will be reported if there exist at least
two locks and at least two paths on which they are acquired in different orders.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.2
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.2 There shall be no deadlocks between threads
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON35-C. Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS51-C. Avoid deadlock with POSIX threads by locking in predefined order
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON53-CPP. Avoid deadlock by locking in a predefined order
CWE-833: Deadlock
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
18.3.2 Ensure that order of nesting of locks in a project forms a DAG

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_2.title: Dir 5.2 (Required) There shall be no deadlocks between threads [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_2]

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3-a.title: A thread should not be created by another thread [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3-a]
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3-a.description:
This rule detects cases when a thread is created by another thread.

This rule recognizes the following thread-creating functions:

<threads.h> (C Standard Library)
	int thrd_create(thrd_t *thr, thrd_start_t func, void *arg)

<thread> (C++ Standard Library)
	std::thread::thread(Function&& f, Args&&... args)
	
<pthread.h> (POSIX)
	int pthread_create(pthread_t *restrict thread, const pthread_attr_t *restrict attr, void *(*start_routine)(void *), void *restrict arg)

<processthreadsapi.h> (Windows)
	HANDLE CreateThread(LPSECURITY_ATTRIBUTES lpThreadAttributes, SIZE_T dwStackSize, LPTHREAD_START_ROUTINE lpStartAddress, LPVOID lpParameter, DWORD dwCreationFlags, LPDWORD lpThreadId)

If any of these functions is called (directly or indirectly) from a function which itself
is a thread entry point, the rule will report a violation.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3-a.security_relevance:
Dynamic thread creation causes uncertainty about the number of threads running
at any given time, which increases error proneness and reduces analyzability.
Also, the overhead of creating and destroying threads is hard to predict.
MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3-a.benefits: This rule helps you keep a predictable number of threads running concurrently.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.1 Section 7 - Directives, Dir 5.3
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 5.3 There shall be no dynamic thread creation

MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3.title: Dir 5.3 (Required) There shall be no dynamic thread creation [MISRAC2012-DIR_5_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-a.title: An expression of essentially Boolean type should always be used where an operand is interpreted as a Boolean value [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-a.description:
This rule detects cases when the following operands are expressed as integers,
or floating types different than "essentially Boolean" type:
  - the operand of the logical operator '!'
  - the right hand or the left hand operand of logical operators '&&' and '||'
  - the first operand of the conditional operator '?:'

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-a.benefits:
This rule prevents confusion and helps distinguish between integers
and logical values.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-b.title: An operand of essentially Boolean type should not be used where an operand is interpreted as a numeric value [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-b.description:
This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators
have "essentially Boolean" type:
[], +, +=, ++, --, -, -=, *, *=, /, /=, %, %=, <, >, <=, >=, >>, >>=,
<<, <<=, ~, &, &=, |, |=, ^, ^=
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-c.title: An operand of essentially character type should not be used where an operand is interpreted as a numeric value [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-c.description:
"An operand of essentially character type should not be used where an operand
is interpreted as a numeric value. The numeric values of character data are
implementation-defined." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators
have have essentially character type:
[], +(unary), -(unary), *, /, %, >>, <<, ~, &, |, ^, +=, -=, /=, *=,
^=, |=, &=, <<=, >>=, %=

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-c.benefits: This rule prevents confusion and implementation-defined behaviours.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-d.title: An operand of essentially enum type should not be used in an arithmetic operation [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-d.description:
"An operand of essentially enum type should not be used in an arithmetic
operation because an enum object uses an implementation-defined integer type.
An operation involving an enum object may therefore yield a result with an
unexpected type. Note that an enumeration constant from an anonymous enum has
essentially signed type." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators
have essentially enum type:
 +, -, *, /, %, >>, <<, ~, &, |, ^, ++, --, +=, -=, /=, *=, ^=, |=, &=, <<=,
 >>=, %=

This rule assumes that the enumeration type defined as "essentially Boolean"
type is not essentially enum type.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-d.benefits: This rule prevents confusion and implementation defined behaviours.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-e.title: Shift and bitwise operations should not be performed on operands of essentially signed or enum type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-e]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-e.description:
"Shift and bitwise operations should only be performed on operands
of essentially unsigned type. The numeric value resulting from their
use on essentially signed types may be undefined or implementation-defined."
[MISRA C:2012 with Technical Corrigendum 2 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects when the following operands have essentially signed
or essentially enum type:
- the left hand operand of the following operators: >>, <<, <<=, >>=
- the left hand or the right hand operand of the following operators:
  ~, &, |, ^, &=, |=, ^=

This rule assumes that:
- the enumeration type defined as "essentially Boolean" type is not
  essentially enum type.
- the signed type defined as "essentially Boolean" type is not
  essentially signed type.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard, but tools
may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially  Boolean" types.
See "Notes".
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-e.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent developer confusion and undefined
or implementation-defined behaviors.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2012 Technical Corrigendum 2, Technical clarification of MISRA C:2012
Section 3: Clarification of rules, 3.8 Rule 10.1
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-f.title: An operand of essentially signed or enum type should not be used as the right hand operand to the bitwise shifting operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-f]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-f.description:
"The right hand operand of a shift operator should be of essentially unsigned
type to ensure that undefined behaviour does not result from a negative shift."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the right hand operand of a bitwise shift
operator (<<, >>, <<=, >>=) has essentially signed or essentially enum type.

This rule assumes that the enumeration type defined as "essentially Boolean"
type is not essentially enum type.

This rule assumes that the signed type defined as "essentially Boolean" type
is not essentially signed type.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-g.title: An operand of essentially unsigned type should not be used as the operand to the unary minus operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-g]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-g.description:
"An operand of essentially unsigned type should not be used as the operand to
the unary minus operator, as the signedness of the result is determined by the
implemented size of int" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.1]

This rule detects cases when the operand of a unary minus operator
has "essentialy unsigned type".

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-g.benefits: The rule prevents unexpected results which may follow integer promotion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 10.1 Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT31-C. Ensure that integer conversions do not result in lost or
misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-h.title: Floating-point expressions shall not be explicitly (syntactically) tested for equality (==) or inequality (!=) [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-h]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-h.description:
"The inherent nature of floating-point types is such that comparisons
of equality will often not evaluate to true even when they are expected to.
In addition the behaviour of such a comparison cannot be predicted before
execution, and may well vary from one implementation to another."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when a floating-point expression is used as the operand
of the equality (==) or inequality (!=) operator.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-h.benefits: This rule helps you avoid unexpected behavior.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-i.title: Pointers shall not be implicitly compared to NULL [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-i]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-i.description:
This rule detects when a pointer type is converted to a boolean type
or used in a context where a boolean value is expected,
resulting in an implicit comparison to NULL.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-i.benefits:
This rule helps you maintain code clarity by making all comparisons to NULL
explicit, which ensures that the intended behavior is unambiguous.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1-i.parameters:
* "Report implicit conversions from a pointer type to a boolean type":
  if enabled, the rule reports violations when a pointer type is implicitly converted
  to a boolean type, e.g. by an assignment or a function return statement.
* "Report explicit conversions from a pointer type to a boolean type":
  if enabled, the rule reports violations when a pointer type is explicitly converted
  to a boolean type by a cast operation.
* "Report expressions of pointer type used as a conditional statement":
  if enabled, the rule reports violations when the condition in an
  'if', 'while', 'do-while', or 'for' statement is an expression of pointer type.
* "Report expressions of pointer type used as the first operand of a conditional operator":
  if enabled, the rule reports violations when the condition of the '?:' operator
  is an expression of pointer type.
* "Report expressions of pointer type used as an operand of a logical operator":
  if enabled, the rule reports violations when an operand of a logical operator
  ('!', '&&', or '||') is an expression of pointer type.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 11.11 Pointers shall not be implicitly compared to NULL

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1.title: Rule 10.1 (Required) Operands shall not be of an inappropriate essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_2-a.title: Expressions of essentially character type shall not be used inappropriately in addition and subtraction operations [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_2-a.description:
"Expressions of essentially character type shall not be used inappropriately
in addition and subtraction operations.

The appropriate uses are:
1. For the + operator, one operand shall have essentially character type and the
   other shall have essentially signed type or essentially unsigned type having
   a rank lower than or equal to that of int. The result of the operation has
   essentially character type.
2. For the - operator, the first operand shall have essentially character type
   and the second shall have:
   - essentially signed type or essentially unsigned type or essentially
     character type; and
   - a rank lower than or equal to that of int
If both operands have essentially character type then the result
has the standard type (usually int in this case) else the result
has essentially character type." [MISRA C:2012 with Technical Corrigendum 2 Rule 10.2]

This rule checks operands of the following operators:
+ (binary), -(binary) (and appropriate compound assignments: +=,-=)

A violation is reported if the type of one operand is of essentially character
and the type of the other operand is different than the types specified above
in points 1 and 2.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_2-a.benefits:
This rule prevents confusion and ensures that expressions with the essentially
character type are not used arithmetically.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.2

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-a.title: The value of an expression shall not be assigned to an object with a narrower essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-a.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
assignments between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision. The use of stronger
typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model, reduces the likelihood
of these problems occurring." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.3]

This rule detects cases when the value of an expression is assigned to an
object with a narrower essential type. A violation is not reported if an
expression is converted to a different essential type category (such cases
are reported by the CODSTA-163_b rule).

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-a.benefits: This rule prevents unintended results and data loss.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.3

misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-b.title: The value of an expression shall not be assigned to an object of a different essential type category [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-b.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
assignments between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision. The use of stronger
typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model, reduces the likelihood
of these problems occurring." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.3]

This rule detects cases when the value of an expression is assigned to an
object with a different essential type category or if the constant expression
in a switch statement's case label is converted to a different essential type
category.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_3-b.benefits: This rule prevents the loss of value, sign or precision.

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-a.title: Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed shall have the same essential type category [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-a.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
conversions between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision."

"Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions
are performed shall have the same essential type category. This rule applies
to operators that are described in usual arithmetic conversions. This includes
all the binary operators, excluding the shift, logical &&, logical || and comma
operators. The increment and decrement operators are not covered by this rule."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators have
different essential types:
+(binary), -(binary), *, /, %, <, >, <=, >=, !=, ==, &(binary), |, ^, +=, -=,
/=, *=, ^=, |=, &=, %=

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-a.benefits:
"The use of stronger typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model,
allows implicit conversions to be restricted to those that should then produce
the answer expected by the developer." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-b.title: The second and third operands of the ternary operator shall have the same essential type category [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-b.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
conversions between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision."

"Both operands of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions
are performed shall have the same essential type category. The second
and third operands of the ternary operator are covered by this rule."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]

This rule detects cases when the second and third operands of the ternary
operator have different essential types.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_4-b.benefits:
"The use of stronger typing, as enforced by the MISRA essential type model,
allows implicit conversions to be restricted to those that should then produce
the answer expected by the developer." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-a.title: The cast operation to essentially enumeration type is not allowed [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-a.description:
"An explicit cast may be introduced for legitimate functional reasons.
However, some explicit casts are considered inappropriate:
- A cast to an essentially enum type may result in a value that does
  not lie within the set of enumeration constants for that type."
  [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.5]

This rule detect cases when:
- an expression of an essential type other than enum is explicitly cast
  to an essentially enum type
- an expression of an essentially enum type is explicitly cast to a different
  essentially enum type
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-a.benefits:
This rule prevents casts to an enum type which may result in a value that does
not lie within the set of enumeration constants for that type.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.5

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-b.title: Do not cast from or to essentially Boolean type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-b.description:
"An explicit cast may be introduced for legitimate functional reasons.
However, some explicit casts are considered inappropriate:
- In C99, the result of a cast or assignment to _Bool is always 0 or 1.
  This is not necessarily the case when casting to another type which
  is defined as essentially Boolean;
- A cast from essentially Boolean to any other type is unlikely
  to be meaningful" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.5]

This rule detect cases when:
- an expression of an essential type other than Boolean is explicitly cast
  to essentially Boolean type
- an expression of essentially Boolean type is explicitly cast
  to an essential type other than Boolean

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard,
but tools may also provide additional ways of identifying "essentially
Boolean" types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-b.benefits: This rule prevents confusion and helps avoid unnecessary explicit casts.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-c.title: Do not use casts between essentially character types and essentially floating types [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-c.description:
"An explicit cast may be introduced for legitimate functional reasons.
However, some explicit casts are considered inappropriate:
- Converting between floating and character types is not meaningful
  as there is no precise mapping between the two representations."
  [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.5]

This rule detect cases when:
- an expression of an essentially floating type is explicitly cast
  to an essentially character type
- an expression of an essentially character type is explicitly cast
  to an essentially floating type

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-c.benefits: This rule prevents confusion and helps avoid inappropriate explicit casts.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_5-c.example_repair: Do not use casts between essentially character types and essentially floating types.

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_6-a.title: The value of a composite expression shall not be assigned to an object with wider essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_6-a.description:
"The C language allows the programmer considerable freedom and will permit
assignments between different arithmetic types to be performed automatically.
However, the use of these implicit conversions can lead to unintended results,
with the potential for loss of value, sign or precision." [MISRA C:2012]

The rule reports a violation if the value of a composite expression is assigned
to an object with wider essential type.

The terms "composite expression", "essential type" and "assignment" are defined
by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_6-a.benefits: The rule reduces possible developer confusion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

misinterpreted data

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_6.title: Rule 10.6 (Required) The value of a composite expression shall not be assigned to an object with wider essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-a.title: If a composite expression is used as one operand of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed then the other operand shall not have wider essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-a.description:
"Restricting implicit conversions on composite expressions means that sequences
of arithmetic operations within an expression must be conducted in the same
essential type, with the exception that essentially real floating and
essentially complex floating operations may coexist. This reduces possible
developer confusion.

The expression u32a + u16b + u16c is compliant as both additions will notionally
be performed in type uint32_t. In this case only non-composite expressions are
implicitly converted.

The expression (u16a + u16b) + u32c is non-compliant as the left addition is
notionally performed in type uint16_t and the right in type uint32_t, requiring
an implicit conversion of the composite expression u16a + u16b to uint32_t."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.7 with Amendment 3]

The rule reports a violation on a complex expression if:
- the usual arithmetic conversions are performed on its operands, and
- a composite expression is used as one operand and the other operand
  has wider essential type

The terms "composite expression" and "essential type" are defined
by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-a.benefits: The rule reduces possible developer confusion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 10.7, 8.10 The essential type model

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-b.title: If a composite expression is used as one (second or third) operand of a conditional operator then the other operand shall not have wider essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-b.description:
"Restricting implicit conversions on composite expressions means that sequences
of arithmetic operations within an expression must be conducted in the same
essential type, with the exception that essentially real floating and
essentially complex floating operations may coexist. This reduces possible
developer confusion." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.4 with Amendment 3]

The rule reports a violation on a conditional operator if a composite
expression is used as one (second or third) operand and the other
(second or third) operand has a wider essential type.

The terms "composite expression" and "essential type" are defined
by the MISRA C:2012 standard.

See also: CODSTA-156_a
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7-b.benefits: The rule reduces possible developer confusion.

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7.title: Rule 10.7 (Required) If a composite expression is used as one operand of an operator in which the usual arithmetic conversions are performed then the other operand shall not have wider essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_10_8-a.title: The value of a composite expression shall not be cast to a different essential type category or a wider essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_10_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_8-a.description:
This rule reports a violation if the value of a "composite expression" is cast
to a type that has different "essential type category", or is a wider "essential
type".

The terms "composite expression", "essential type category" and "essential type"
are defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_10_8-a.benefits:
By restricting the implicit and explicit conversions that may be applied to
non-trivial expressions, this rule helps avoiding issues caused by:
"- the confusion about the type in which integer expressions are evaluated,
  as this depends on the type of the operands after any integer promotion
- the common misconception among programmers that the type in which
  a calculation is conducted is influenced by the type to which the result
  is assigned or cast. This false expectation may lead to unintended results"
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 10.8]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_1-a.title: Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to a function and any other type than pointer to function [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_1-a.description:
"A pointer to a function shall only be converted into or from a pointer
to a function with a compatible type.

The conversion of a pointer to a function into or from any of:
  - Pointer to object;
  - Pointer to incomplete;
  - void *
results in undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.1]

This rule reports a violation if a function pointer is converted (implicit or
explicit) into or from a type different than function pointer.

The implicit conversion of a pointer is detected in the following cases:
- assignment expression
- comparison expression
- initialization (only simple variables and arrays)
- passing argument to function call
- returning value from function
