Rule 11.1 Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to a function and any other type

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_1-b.title: Conversions shall not be performed between non compatible pointer to a function types [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_1-b.description:
"A pointer to a function shall only be converted into or from a pointer
to a function with a compatible type. If a function is called by means
of a pointer whose type is not compatible with the called function,
the behaviour is undefined." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.1]

This rule reports a violation if a pointer to function is converted
(implicit or explicit) into a pointer to function of a non-compatible type.

The implicit conversion of a pointer is detected in the following cases:
- assignment expression
- comparison expression
- initialization (only simple variables)
- passing argument to function call
- returning value from function

"For two function types to be compatible, both shall specify compatible
return types. Moreover, the parameter type lists, if both are present,
shall agree in the number of parameters and in use of the ellipsis
terminator; corresponding parameters shall have compatible types."
[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_10-a.title: The '_Atomic' qualifier should not be used with the 'void' type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_10-a.description:
"The presence of the _Atomic qualifier designates an atomic type. The size,
representation, and alignment of an atomic type need not be the same as those
of the corresponding unqualified type." [ISO/IEC 9899:2017]

The size and alignment of a pointer to the incomplete _Atomic type 'void' can be
different than the size and alignment of a pointer to a complete _Atomic type,
so conversions between such types can result in undefined behavior.

This rule detects when the 'void' type is declared with the '_Atomic' qualifier.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_10-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined and erroneous program behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_10.title: Rule 11.10 (Required) The _Atomic qualifier shall not be applied to the incomplete type void [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_10]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_2-a.title: Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to an incomplete type and any other type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_2-a.description:
"A pointer to an incomplete type shall not be converted into another type.
A conversion shall not be made into a pointer to incomplete type. Although
a pointer to void is also a pointer to an incomplete type, this rule does
not apply to pointers to void." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.2]

"Pointers to an incomplete type are sometimes used to hide the representation
of an object. Converting a pointer to an incomplete type into a pointer
to object would break this encapsulation." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.2]

This rule reports a violation if a pointer to an incomplete type is converted
(explicit or implicit) into or from a pointer to any other type. An incomplete
type is one that describes an object, but lacks information needed to determine
its size.

The implicit conversion of a pointer is detected in the following cases:

- assignment expression
- initialization (only simple variables and arrays)
- passing argument to function call
- returning value from function
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_2-a.benefits:
The rule prevents undefined behaviours.

"Conversion into or from a pointer to an incomplete type may result in a pointer
that is not correctly aligned, resulting in undefined behaviour.
Conversion of a pointer to an incomplete type into or from a floating type
always results in undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.2]
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.3.4 Cast operators

Rule 11.2 Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to an incomplete type and any other type

EXP57-CPP. Do not cast or delete pointers to incomplete classes

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_2.title: Rule 11.2 (Required) Conversions shall not be performed between a pointer to an incomplete type and any other type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_3-a.title: A cast shall not be performed between a pointer to object type and a pointer to a different object type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_3-a.description:
"Conversion of a pointer to object into a pointer to a different object may
result in a pointer that is not correctly aligned, resulting in undefined
behaviour.
Even if conversion is known to produce a pointer that is correctly aligned,
the behaviour may be undefined if that pointer is used to access an object.
For example, if an object whose type is int is accessed as a short the behaviour
is undefined even if int and short have the same representation and alignment
requirements." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.3 with Amendment 4]

This rule reports a violation when a pointer to an object type is cast as a
pointer to a different object type. This rule applies to the unqualified
types that are pointed to by the pointers.

Rule 11.3 A conversion shall not be performed between a pointer to object type and a pointer to a different object type

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_3.title: Rule 11.3 (Required) A conversion shall not be performed between a pointer to object type and a pointer to a different object type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4-a.title: A conversion should not be performed between a pointer to object and an integer type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4-a.description:
"Conversion of an integer into a pointer to object may result in a pointer
that is not correctly aligned, resulting in undefined behaviour.
Conversion of a pointer or a array to object into an integer may produce a
value that cannot be represented in the chosen integer type resulting
in undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012]

The rule detects when a conversion (explicit or implicit) is performed between
an integral type and a pointer to object type.

Implicit conversion of a pointer is detected in the following cases:
- using an assignment operator,
- using an expression to initialize all or part of an object,
- passing an argument to a function call,
- returning an expression from a function.

The rule checks only conversions to a pointer to object type. Conversions
to pointer to function, pointer to void and to pointer to incomplete types
are not reported.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4-a.example_violation:
/* Examples of incorrect code */
void f1 (int*);
void f2 (int );

int foo (int* ptr1)
{
    int i;
    unsigned char c = ptr1;  /* Violation - implicit conversion
                                from pointer to int type to unsigned
                                char type in initialization */

    int arr1[10];
    unsigned char c1 = arr1; /* Violation - implicit conversion
                                from array of int type to unsigned
                                char type in initialization */

    signed int* ptr2 = c;    /* Violation - implicit conversion
                                from unsigned char type to pointer
                                to int type in initialization */

    i = (int) ptr1;          /* Violation - explicit cast is not allowed */

    f1(0x0FF);               /* Violation - implicit conversion of integer
                                constant to pointer to int type in function
                                call */

    f2(ptr1 - 1);            /* Violation - implicit conversion of expression
                                of pointer to int type to int type in function
                                call */

    return ptr2;             /* Violation - implicit conversion from pointer
                                to int type to int type in function return */
}
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 11.4
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.3 Expressions
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.3.2 Other operands, 6.5 Expressions

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4.title: Rule 11.4 (Advisory) A conversion should not be performed between a pointer to object and an integer type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_5-a.title: A conversion should not be performed from pointer to void into pointer to object [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_5-a.description:
"Conversion of a pointer to void into a pointer to object may result
in a pointer that is not correctly aligned, resulting in undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.5]

The rule reports a violation if a conversion (explicit or implicit) from
a pointer to void type to a pointer to object type is used.

Implicit conversion of pointer is detected by rule in the following cases:
- assignment expression
- initialization (only simple variables and arrays)
- passing argument to function call
- returning value from function

The rule checks only conversions to pointer to object type, so conversions
to pointer to function type and to pointer to incomplete type are not reported.

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_5.title: Rule 11.5 (Advisory) A conversion should not be performed from pointer to void into pointer to object [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_6-a.title: A cast shall not be performed between pointer to void and an arithmetic type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_6-a.description:
"Conversion of an integer into a pointer to void results in behaviour that is
implementation-defined.
Conversion of a pointer to void into an integer may produce a value that cannot
be represented in the chosen integer type resulting in undefined behaviour.
Conversion between any non-integer arithmetic type and pointer to void
is undefined." [MISRA C:2012 with Technical Corrigendum 2 Rule 11.6]

"The type char, the signed and unsigned integer types, and the enumerated
types are collectively called integer types. Integer and floating types
are collectively called arithmetic types." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

This rule detects when an explicit cast is performed between a pointer to
void type and an arithmetic type.

See also: CODSTA-129_b
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined and implementation-defined behaviors.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 11.6
2. MISRA C:2012 Technical Corrigendum 2, Technical clarification of MISRA C:2012
Section 3: Clarification of rules, 3.12 Rule 11.6

6.3.4 Cast operators

6.3.2.3 Pointers, 6.5.4 Cast operators

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_6.title: Rule 11.6 (Required) A cast shall not be performed between pointer to void and an arithmetic type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_7-a.title: A cast shall not be performed between pointer to object and a non-integer arithmetic type [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_7-a.description:
The rule reports a violation if an explicit cast between a pointer to
"object type" and "non-integer arithmetic type" is used.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_7-a.benefits:
"Conversion of an boolean, character or enum type into a pointer to object may
result in a pointer that is not correctly aligned, resulting in undefined
behaviour.

Conversion of a pointer to object into a boolean, character or enum type may
produce a value that cannot be represented in the chosen integer type resulting
in undefined behavior.

Conversion of a pointer to object into or from an floating type results in
undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_8-a.title: A conversion shall not remove any 'const', 'volatile' or '_Atomic' qualification from the type accessed via a pointer or by reference [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_8-a.description:
This rule detects when a 'const', 'volatile' or '_Atomic' qualification is
removed by a conversion from the type accessed via a pointer
or by reference. Both implicit conversions and explicit casts are checked.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_8-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent the issues listed below.

"Any attempt to remove the qualification associated with the addressed type
is a violation of the principle of type qualification.

Some of the problems that might arise if a qualifier is removed from the
addressed object are:
* Removing a 'const' qualifier might circumvent the read-only status of an
  object and result in it being modified
* Removing a 'const' qualifier might result in an exception when the object
  is accessed
* Removing a 'volatile' qualifier might result in accesses to the object
  being optimized away
* Removing an _Atomic qualifier might circumvent the lock status of an object
  and potentially result in memory corruption"
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.8 with Amendment 4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-a.title: Literal zero (0) shall not be used as the null-pointer-constant [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-a.description:
This rule reports a violation if a literal 0 is used as null pointer constant
directly, instead of using NULL or "(void*)0". Please see Notes section for
details.

See also: CODSTA-131, CODSTA-CPP-111, CODSTA-MCPP-04

In modern C++ (C++11 and above), it is recommended to use the rules
CODSTA-CPP-111 and CODSTA-MCPP-04 instead of this rule in order to ensure
that nullptr is the only form of the null pointer constant.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-a.benefits:
"Using NULL rather than 0 makes it clear that a null pointer constant
was intended." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.9]

"The literal 0 is both an integer type and the null-pointer-constant.
To meet developer expectations, NULL should be used as the
null-pointer-constant, and 0 for the integer zero." [MISRA C++:2008 Rule 4-10-2]

"An integer constant expression with the value 0 shall be derived from expansion
of the macro NULL if it appears in any of the following contexts:
  - As the value being assigned to a pointer;
  - As an operand of an == or != operator whose other operand is a pointer;
  - As the second operand of a ?: operator whose third operand is a pointer;
  - As the third operand of a ?: operator whose second operand is a pointer."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.9]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-a.example_repair:
#define NULL 0
#define CALL_FUNC(x) ( getptr( ( x ) ))
void getptr ( int* );

int* foo1 ( int* p1 )
{
  int* v1 = NULL;                   /* Compliant */
  if ( ( void* ) 0 == v1 );         /* Compliant */
  CALL_FUNC( NULL );                /* Compliant */
  p1 ? ( void* ) 0 : p1;            /* Compliant */
  return NULL;                      /* Compliant */
}
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M4-10-2. Literal zero (0) shall not be used as the null-pointer-constant
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 4, Rule 4-10-2
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 11.9
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 11.9 The macro NULL shall be the only permitted form of integer null pointer constant

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-b.title: Use NULL instead of literal zero (0) as the null-pointer-constant [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-b.description:
This rule reports a violation if no "NULL" token is used in a line where
the literal 0 is assigned to or compared with a pointer.

See also: CODSTA-CPP-63, CODSTA-CPP-111, CODSTA-MCPP-04

In modern C++ (C++11 and above), it is recommended to use the rules
CODSTA-CPP-111 and CODSTA-MCPP-04 instead of this rule in order to ensure
that nullptr is the only form of the null pointer constant.
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-b.benefits:
"Using NULL rather than 0 makes it clear that a null pointer constant
was intended." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.9]

The literal 0 can be used both as a null pointer constant and as an integer.
To clarify the developer's intention, it is advised to use the NULL macro
for the null pointer constant, and 0 for the integer zero.

"An integer constant expression with the value 0 shall be derived from expansion
of the macro NULL if it appears in any of the following contexts:
  - As the value being assigned to a pointer;
  - As an operand of an == or != operator whose other operand is a pointer;
  - As the second operand of a ?: operator whose third operand is a pointer;
  - As the third operand of a ?: operator whose second operand is a pointer."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 11.9]
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-b.example_violation:
#define MY_NULL 0
#define CALL_FUNC(x) (getptr( (x) ))
void getptr(int*);

int* foop1(int* p1)
{
    int* v1 = 0;  /* Violation */
    p1 = MY_NULL; /* Violation */
    if(0 == v1);  /* Violation */
    CALL_FUNC(0); /* Violation */

    /* Literal 0 is used as 2nd or 3rd operand of "?:" and the other operand
     * has pointer type */
    p1 ? 0 : p1;  /* Violation */

    return 0;     /* Violation */
}
MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9-b.example_repair:
#define NULL 0
#define MY_NULL NULL
#define CALL_FUNC(x) (getptr( (x) ))
void getptr(int*);

int* foop1(int* p1)
{
    int* v1 = NULL;     /* OK */
    p1 = NULL;          /* OK */
    p1 = MY_NULL;       /* OK */
    if((void*)0 == v1); /* OK */
    CALL_FUNC(NULL);    /* OK */
    p1 ? (void*)0 : p1; /* OK */
    return NULL;        /* OK */
}
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 11.9
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 11.9 The macro NULL shall be the only permitted form of integer null pointer constant

MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9.title: Rule 11.9 (Required) The macro NULL shall be the only permitted form of integer null pointer constant [MISRAC2012-RULE_11_9]

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-a.title: Use parentheses to indicate the precedence of operators in expressions [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-a.description:
"The C language has a relatively large number of operators and their relative
precedences are not intuitive. This can lead less experienced programmers to
make mistakes. Using parentheses to make operator precedence explicit removes
the possibility that the programmer's expectations are incorrect. It also
makes the original programmer's intention clear to reviewers or maintainers of
the code." [MISRA C:2012]

This rule detects when a non-parenthesized binary operator is used as the
operand of another binary or ternary operator. This rule checks the following
binary operators: *, /, %, +, -, <<, >>, <, >, <=, >=, ==, !=, &, ^, |, &&, ||
and the conditional operator ?:. It is allowed to have an operand without
parenthesis when the precedence of the operand is the same as the precedence
of the expression (see table in MISRA C 2012 Rule 12.1).

See also: MISRA2004-12_1_e, FORMAT-25
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve the readability and maintainability of your code.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 8.0.1 Parentheses should be used to make the meaning of an expression appropriately explicit
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 12.1
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 12.1 The precedence of operators within expressions should be made explicit

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-c.title: The operand of the 'sizeof' operator should be enclosed in parentheses [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-c.description:
This rule detects when the operand of the 'sizeof' operator is not enclosed
in parentheses.

See also: FORMAT-23, FORMAT-24
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_1-c.benefits:
This rule helps you improve the readability of the code and avoid mistakes.

"The C language has a relatively large number of operators and their relative
precedences are not intuitive. This can lead less experienced programmers
to make mistakes. Using parentheses to make operator precedence explicit
removes the possibility that the programmer's expectations are incorrect.
It also makes the original programmer's intention clear to reviewers
or maintainers of the code." [MISRA C:2012]
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 8.0.1 Parentheses should be used to make the meaning of an expression appropriately explicit
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

Rule 12.1 The precedence of operators within expressions should be made explicit

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-a.title: Avoid incorrect shift operations [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-a.description:
This rule detects incorrect shift operations. This rule is dependent on several parameterizable
conditions.
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-a.parameters:
"Report violation when negative value is shifted": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation when the left
operand of the shift operator is negative.

"Report violation when shifting by negative value": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation when the right
operand of the shift operator is negative.

"Report violation when shifting by too big value": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation when the right
operand of the shift operator is greater or equal to the number of bits in the left operand.

"Number of bits of the left hand side operand calculated based on its": This parameter determines
how Flow Analysis engine calculates the number of bits of the left operand. Possible options are:
* "Standard type"
* "MISRA C essential type"
* "MISRA C++ underlying type"

"Report violation when shifting will result in overflow": Setting this parameter to true forces a violation 
when the result of the shift operation will cause overflow.

"Report violation when shifting an unsigned value will result in overflow": Setting this parameter to true 
forces a violation when the result of the shift operation will cause overflow and the left operand is of unsigned type.
references: MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-8-1
MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 12.2

Rule 4.1.3 There shall be no occurrence of undefined or critical unspecified behaviour
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 12.2 The right hand operand of a shift operator shall lie in the range zero to one less than the width in bits of the essential type of the left hand operand
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT34-C. Do not shift an expression by a negative number of bits or by greater than or equal to the number of bits that exist in the operand
JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.21 Operators, AV Rule 164
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
4.2.2 Ensure that data loss does not demonstrably occur in an integral expression
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-8-1. The right hand operand of a shift operator shall lie between zero and one less than the width in bits of the underlying type of the left hand operand

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-b.title: The right-hand operand of a constant expression shift operator shall lie between zero and one less than the width in bits of the essential type of the left-hand operand [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-b.description:
This rule detects when both operands of a shift operator are constant
expressions and the right-hand operand has:
- a negative value, or
- a value larger or equal to the width (in bits) of the left-hand operand's
  essential type (in C) or underlying type (in C++).

The term 'essential type' is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
The term 'underlying type' is defined by the MISRA C++:2008 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-b.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.

"If the right-hand operand is negative, or greater than or equal to the width
of the left-hand operand, then the behaviour is undefined.
If, for example, the left-hand operand of a left-shift or right-shift
is a 16-bit integer, then it is important to ensure that this is shifted
only by a number in the range 0 to 15."
[MISRA C:2012]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_2-b.example_repair:
/* This example assumes that 'char' is an 8-bit type, 'short' is a 16-bit type,
   and 'int' is a 32-bit type. */ 
void foo() 
{
   1u << 7;         /* OK - type of '1u' is unsigned char,
                       which is an 8-bit type */

   300 >> 15;       /* OK - type of '300' is signed short,
                       which is a 16-bit type */

   (int)300 >> 16;  /* OK - type of '(int)300' is signed int,
                       which is a 32-bit type */
}

and one less than the width in bits of the underlying type of the left hand
operand.

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_3-a.title: The comma operator shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_3-a.description: This rule detects the use of built-in and user defined comma operators.
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_3-a.benefits:
This rule helps you improve the readability and maintainability of the code.

"Use of the comma operator is generally detrimental to the readability of code,
and the same effect can usually be achieved by other means." [MISRA C++:2023]

5.1.2. Do not rely on the sequence of evaluation within an expression

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-a.title: Integer overflow or underflow in constant expression in '+', '-', '*' operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-a.description:
An integer overflow or wraparound occurs when an integer value is incremented to
a value that is too large to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs, the value may wrap to become a very small or negative number.
For example: INT_MAX + 1

A similar situation happens when an integer value is decremented to a value that
is too small to store in the associated representation. When this occurs, the
value may wrap to become a very large or a positive number.
For example: INT_MIN - 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on wrapping, it
can have serious consequences if the wrap is unexpected.

See also: PB-66_b
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-a.security_relevance:
Unexpected integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-a.benefits:
Unexpected integer overflow generally leads to undefined behavior that may
result in crashes. In the case of overflows involving loop index variables,
the likelihood of infinite loops is also high.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous, as the computed (incorrect)
value can be used in multiple sensitive areas, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
  square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array
  object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
  memory allocation function)
- In security-critical code
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-19-1. Evaluation of constant unsigned integer expressions shall not lead
to wrap-around
2. 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors

expression

systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-19-1

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-b.title: Integer overflow or underflow in constant expression in '<<' operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-b.description:
An integer overflow or wraparound occurs when an integer value is incremented to
a value that is too large to store in the associated representation. When this
occurs, the value may wrap to become a very small or negative number.
For example: INT_MAX << 1

While this may be intended behavior in circumstances that rely on wrapping, it
can have serious consequences if the wrap is unexpected.

See also: PB-66_a
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-b.security_relevance:
Unexpected integer overflow can sometimes trigger buffer overflows that can
be used to execute arbitrary code, as well as lead to memory corruption. This
is usually outside the scope of a program's implicit security policy.
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_4-b.benefits:
Unexpected integer overflow generally leads to undefined behavior that may
result in crashes. In the case of overflows involving loop index variables,
the likelihood of infinite loops is also high.

Unexpected integer overflow can be very dangerous, as the computed (incorrect)
value can be used in multiple sensitive areas, such as:
- Integer operands of any pointer arithmetic, including array indexing
- The assignment expression for the declaration of a variable length array
- The postfix expression preceding square brackets [] or the expression in
  square brackets [] of a subscripted designation of an element of an array
  object
- Function arguments of type size_t or rsize_t (for example, an argument to a
  memory allocation function)
- In security-critical code

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_5-a.title: The 'sizeof' operator shall not have an operand which is a function parameter declared as "array of type" [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_5-a.description:
"The sizeof operator can be used to determine the number of elements
in an array A:

size_t arraySize = sizeof(A)/sizeof(A[0]);

This works as expected when A is an identifier that designates an array,
as it has type 'array of type'. It does not 'degenerate' to a pointer and
sizeof(A) returns the size of the array.

However, this is not the case when A is a function parameter. The Standard
states that a function parameter never has type 'array of type' and a function
parameter declared as an array will 'degenerate' to 'pointer to type'.
This means that sizeof(A) is equivalent to sizeof(int32_t*), which does not
return the size of an array." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 12.5]

This rule detects cases when the operand of a 'sizeof' operator is a function
parameter declared as 'array of type'
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_5-a.benefits:
This rule prevents errors involving incorrect calculation of the size
of the type and improves readability of code.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1. Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 12.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 12.5 The sizeof operator shall not have an operand which is a function parameter declared as "array of type"

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_5.title: Rule 12.5 (Mandatory) The sizeof operator shall not have an operand which is a function parameter declared as "array of type" [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_6-a.title: Members of atomic objects of structure and union types should not be directly accessed [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_6-a.description:
"Accessing a member of an atomic structure or union object results in undefined
behavior.
For example, a data race would occur if access to the entire structure or union
in one thread conflicts with access to a member from another thread, where
at least one access is a modification. Members can be safely accessed using
a non-atomic object which is assigned to or from the atomic object."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

Instead of direct access to a member of an atomic structure or union
the following access functions from the C standard library can be used:
atomic_init(), atomic_store(), atomic_load(), atomic_exchange(),
atomic_compare_exchange().

This rule detects when members of atomic objects of structure or union types
are directly accessed using the '.' or '->' operators.

See also: CODSTA-MC-18
MISRAC2012-RULE_12_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and data races.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.8 Rule 12.6
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 12.6 Structure and union members of atomic objects shall not be directly accessed
3. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.5.2.3 Structure and union members

MISRAC2012-RULE_12_6.title: Rule 12.6 (Required) Structure and union members of atomic objects shall not be directly accessed [MISRAC2012-RULE_12_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_1-a.title: Initializer lists shall not contain persistent side effects [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_1-a.description:
"C99 permits automatic aggregate initializers to contain expressions that are
evaluated at run-time. It also permits compound literals which behave as
anonymous initialized objects. The order in which side effects occur during
evaluation of the expressions in an initializer list is unspecified and the
behaviour of the initialization is therefore unpredictable if those side
effects are persistent."  [MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.1]

The rule reports a violation if initializer list contains:
- assignment operator (simple or compound)
- increment or decrement operator
- use of a volatile object
- allocation or deallocation of memory ('new' statement or 'malloc'-family
  calls or 'free' call)
- call of one of standard library functions which operate on files (for example
  'fopen', 'put', 'flush'), handle signals ('raise', 'signal'), set terminate
  or unexpected handlers ('set_terminate', 'set_unexpected') or handle program
  termination (for example 'abort', 'atexit') or execute commands ('system')
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_1-a.benefits:
The rule prevents writing expressions that can produce different values
depending on order of evaluation that the standard permits.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 13.1, Appendix J
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 13.1 Initializer lists shall not contain persistent side effects
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.5.2.5 Compound literals
6.7.8 Initialization
5.1.2.3 Program execution

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_1.title: Rule 13.1 (Required) Initializer lists shall not contain persistent side effects [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-a.title: The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of evaluation that the standard permits [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-a.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) and also used in another way in a statement, where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

Since the increment, decrement, or assignment operator modifies its operand,
using such an operand more than once within the same expression can produce
undefined results.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A5-0-1. The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of
evaluation that the standard permits
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.1.2. Do not rely on the sequence of evaluation within an expression
3.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS

systems, Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-1

Section 8: Rules, Rule 13.2

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-b.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of function arguments [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-b.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) in a function call argument, and the same object is used
in another argument of the same function call, or the same volatile object
is used in different arguments of the same function call.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-b.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

The order of evaluation of function arguments is unspecified. This means that
if the arguments have side effects, the order in which those side effects occur
is also unspecified. A function call can produce different results depending
on which of the function's arguments is evaluated first.

evaluation that the standard permits

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-c.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of the function designator and function arguments [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-c.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) and also used in another way in a function designator and function
arguments in a function call.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-c.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"If a function is called via a function pointer there shall be no dependence
on the order in which function designator and function arguments are evaluated."
[MISRA-C:2004]

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-d.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of expressions involving a function call [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-d.description:
This rule detects when an object is passed to a function call by a pointer
or a reference, modified (incremented, decremented, or assigned) in that
function, and also used in another way in a statement where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-d.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"Functions may have additional effects when they are called (e.g. modifying some
global data). Dependence on order of evaluation could be avoided by invoking the
function prior to the expression that uses it, making use of a temporary
variable for the value." [MISRA-C:2004]

or assigned) more than once between sequence points.

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-f.title: Do not use more than one volatile between two adjacent sequence points [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-f]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-f.description:
This rule detects when there is more than one modification access or more than
one read access to a volatile-qualified type between two sequence points.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-f.benefits:
This rule helps you ensure that expressions are written in a manner that
consistently produces the same value.

"The volatile type qualifier is provided in C to denote objects whose value can
change independently of the execution of the program (for example an input
register). If an object of volatile qualified type is accessed this may change
its value. C compilers will not optimise out reads of a volatile. In addition,
as far as a C program is concerned, a read of a volatile has a side effect
(changing the value of the volatile). It will usually be necessary to access
volatile data as part of an expression, which then means there may be dependence
on order of evaluation. Where possible though it is recommended that volatiles
only be accessed in simple assignment statements." [MISRA-C:2004]

evaluation that the standard permits

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-g.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of function calls [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-g]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-g.description:
This rule detects when two function calls are used in a statement where the
order of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified, and the called functions
use the same global or static object with least one of them modifying it.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-g.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"Functions may have additional effects when they are called (e.g. modifying some
global data). Dependence on order of evaluation could be avoided by invoking the
function prior to the expression that uses it, making use of a temporary
variable for the value." [MISRA-C:2004]

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-h.title: The value of an expression with atomic type should be independent from thread interleaving [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-h]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-h.description:
If an atomic variable is accessed more than once between two adjacent sequence
points, it can be modified by another thread. This may cause the value of the
expression to depend on the order of read/write operations.

This rule detects when there is more than one modification access to the
parameter or variable with volatile-qualified or atomic type, or there is
more than one read access to the same parameter or variable with atomic type
between two adjacent sequence points.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2-h.benefits:
This rule helps you ensure that expressions are written in a manner that
consistently produces the same value.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.9 Rule 13.2
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 13.2 The value of an expression and its persistent side effects shall be the same under all permitted evaluation orders and shall be independent from thread interleaving
3. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
Annex C: Sequence points

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2.title: Rule 13.2 (Required) The value of an expression and its persistent side effects shall be the same under all permitted evaluation orders and shall be independent from thread interleaving [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_3-a.title: A full expression containing an increment (++) or decrement (--) operator should have no other potential side effects [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_3-a.description:
"The use of increment and decrement operators in combination
with other operators is not recommended because:
  - It can significantly impair the readability of the code;
  - It introduces additional side effects into a statement
    with the potential for undefined behaviour
It is clearer to use these operations in isolation from any
other operators." [MISRA C:2012]

"Accessing a volatile object, modifying an object, modifying
a file, or calling a function that does any of those operations
are all side effects, which are changes in the state of the
execution environment." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

The rule reports a violation if in a full expression ++ or -- operators
are used and at least one more other operation with side effect is present.
The rule assumes that each function call (implicit or explicit) causes
a side effect.

See also: MISRA2004-12_13
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_3-a.benefits: The rule prevents undefined behaviours and improves readability of code.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 13.3
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 13.3 A full expression containing an increment (++) or decrement (--) operator should have no other potential side effects other than that caused by the increment or decrement operator
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.3 Expressions
5.1.2.3 Program execution
4. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.5 Expressions
5.1.2.3 Program execution

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_3.title: Rule 13.3 (Advisory) A full expression containing an increment (++) or decrement (--) operator should have no other potential side effects other than that caused by the increment or decrement operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_4-a.title: The result of an assignment operator should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_4-a.description:
The rule detects when the result of an assignment operator is used
as an operand in another expression.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_4-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and improve the readability
of the code.

"The use of assignment operators, simple or compound, in combination
with other arithmetic operators is not recommended because:
- It can significantly impair the readability of the code;
- It introduces additional side effects into a statement making it more
  difficult to avoid the undefined behaviour" [MISRA C:2012]
references: 1. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.1.2. Do not rely on the sequence of evaluation within an expression
2. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 8.18.2 The result of an assignment operator should not be used

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_5-a.title: The right-hand operand of a logical && or || operator shall not contain side effects [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_5-a.description:
The rule detects when the right-hand operand of the logical operators '&&'
and '||' contains an expression that causes a persistent side effect.
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_5-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent conditional evaluation of the right-hand operand,
which can easily cause problems if the developer relies on a side effect
occurring.

"There are some situations in C or C++ where certain parts of expressions may
not be evaluated. If these sub-expressions contain side effects then those side
effects may or may not occur, depending on the values of other sub expressions.
The operators which can lead to this problem are && and || where the evaluation
of the right-hand operand is conditional on the value of the left-hand operand."
[MISRA C:2012]

contain side effects

sizeof, typeid or a function passed to condition variable::wait

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-a.title: The operand of the sizeof operator shall not contain any expression which has side effects [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-a.description:
"The operand of a sizeof operator may be either an expression or may specify
a type. If the operand contains an expression, a possible programming error
is to expect that expression to be evaluated when it is actually not evaluated
in most circumstances." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.6]

The rule reports a violation if as the sizeof operand is used assignment,
increment, decrement, new, delete or throw operator.

See also: PB-02, PB-22
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-a.benefits:
"Any expressions appearing in the operand of a sizeof operator are not normally
evaluated. This rule mandates that the evaluation of any such expression shall
not contain side effects, whether or not it is actually evaluated."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.6]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M5-3-4. Evaluation of the operand to the sizeof operator shall not contain
side effects

5.1.6. Do not code side effects into the right-hand operands of: &&, ||,
sizeof, typeid or a function passed to condition variable::wait

MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-b.title: Object designated by a volatile lvalue should not be accessed in the operand of the sizeof operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_13_6-b.description:
"The operand of a sizeof operator may be either an expression or may specify
a type. If the operand contains an expression, a possible programming error
is to expect that expression to be evaluated when it is actually not evaluated
in most circumstances." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.6]

The rule reports a violation if an object designated by a volatile lvalue
is accessed in the operand of the sizeof operator.


Exception:

"An expression of the form sizeof ( V ), where V is an lvalue with a volatile
qualified type that is not a variable-length array, is permitted."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 13.6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_1-a.title: A loop counter in a 'for' loop shall not have essentially floating type [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_1-a.description:
"A loop counter shall not have essentially floating type."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.1]

"A loop counter is defined as an object, array element or member of
a structure or union which satisfies the following:

1. It has a scalar type;

2. Its value varies monotonically on each iteration of a given instance of
   a loop; and

3. It is involved in a decision to exit the loop." [MISRA C:2012 Section 8.14]

This rule detects cases when a floating point loop counter is used in a 'for'
loop.
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_1-a.benefits:
"When using a floating-point loop counter, accumulation of rounding errors may
result in a mismatch between the expected and actual number of iterations.
This can happen when a loop step that is not a power of the floating-point
radix is rounded to a value that can be represented." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.1]

"Even if a loop with a floating-point loop counter appears to behave correctly
on one implementation, it may give a different number of iterations on another
implementation." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.1]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 14.1
Section 8: Rules, 8.14: Control statement expressions
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 14.1 A loop counter shall not have essentially floating type

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_1-b.description:

2. Its value varies monotonically on each iteration of a given instance of
   a loop; and

3. It is involved in a decision to exit the loop." [MISRA C:2012 Section 8.14]

This rule detects cases when a floating point loop counter is used in a 'while'
or 'do-while' loop.
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_1-b.benefits:
"When using a floating-point loop counter, accumulation of rounding errors may
result in a mismatch between the expected and actual number of iterations.
This can happen when a loop step that is not a power of the floating-point
radix is rounded to a value that can be represented." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.1]

"Even if a loop with a floating-point loop counter appears to behave correctly
on one implementation, it may give a different number of iterations on another

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-a.title: There shall only be one loop counter in a 'for' loop, which shall not be modified in the 'for' loop body [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-a.description:
"A for loop shall be well-formed" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]

"A loop counter is defined as an object, array element or member of a structure
or union which satisfies the following:

1. It has a scalar type;

2. Its value varies monotonically on each iteration of a given instance of
   a loop; and

3. It is involved in a decision to exit the loop." [MISRA C:2012 Section 8.14]

This rule ensures that there is only one loop counter in a 'for' loop, and that
this loop counter is not modified in the 'for' loop body.
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-a.benefits:
"The 'for' statement provides a general-purpose looping facility. Using
a restricted form of loop makes code easier to review and to analyse."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]
references: 1. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.2.2. Ensure that a loop has a single loop counter, an optional control
variable, and is not degenerate
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, 8.14: Control statement expressions
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 14.2
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 14.2 A for loop shall be well-formed

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-b.title: The first clause of a 'for' loop shall be well-formed [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-b.description:
"A for loop shall be well-formed" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]

"The three clauses of a 'for' statement are the:
- First clause which
  - Shall be empty, or
  - Shall assign a value to the loop counter, or
  - Shall define and initialize the loop counter (C99).
(...)" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]

This rule ensures that the first clause in a 'for' statement is empty
or assigns a value to the loop counter. Additionally, this rule detects
cases when the first clause contains an expression that has a persistent
side effect other than modification of a loop counter or contains
assignments to more than one loop counter.
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-b.benefits:
"The 'for' statement provides a general-purpose looping facility. Using
a restricted form of loop makes code easier to review and to analyse."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 14.2
Section 8: Rules, 8.14: Control statement expressions
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 14.2 A for loop shall be well-formed

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-c.title: The second clause of a 'for' loop shall be well-formed [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-c.description:
"A for loop shall be well-formed" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]

"A loop counter is defined as an object, array element or member of a structure
or union which satisfies the following:
1. It has a scalar type;
2. Its value varies monotonically on each iteration of a given instance of
   a loop; and
3. It is involved in a decision to exit the loop."
[MISRA C:2012 Section 8.14]

"A loop control flag is defined as a single identifier denoting an object with
essentially Boolean type that is used in the Second clause."
[MISRA C:2012 Section 8.14.2]

This rule reports a violation for the following cases:
- The second clause of a 'for' statement does not contain a loop counter.
- The second clause of a 'for' statement contains an object modified inside
  a 'for' loop body (except loop control flags).
- The second clause of a 'for' statement contains an expression that causes
  a persistent side effect.
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-c.benefits:
"The 'for' statement provides a general-purpose looping facility. Using
a restricted form of loop makes code easier to review and to analyse."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 14.2
Section 8: Rules, 8.14: Control statement expressions
Appendix J; Glossary

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-d.title: The third clause of a 'for' statement shall be well-formed [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-d.description:
"A for loop shall be well-formed" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]

"A loop counter is defined as an object, array element or member of a structure
or union which satisfies the following:

1. It has a scalar type;

2. Its value varies monotonically on each iteration of a given instance of
   a loop; and

3. It is involved in a decision to exit the loop." [MISRA C:2012 Section 8.14]

This rule reports a violation if the third clause of a 'for' statement contains
one from the following:
- a comma operator (,)
- an expression other than modification of a loop counter
- an object modified inside a 'for' loop body
- an expression that causes persistent side effect other than modification
  of a loop counter
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_2-d.benefits:
"The 'for' statement provides a general-purpose looping facility. Using
a restricted form of loop makes code easier to review and to analyse."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 14.2]

6.2.4. Only modify a for loop counter in the for expression

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_3-ac.title: Avoid conditions that always evaluate to the same value [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_3-ac]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_3-ac.description:
This rule identifies conditions that always evaluate to the same value. Such conditions
often appear in the course of refactoring and during code evolution - especially when
code is edited by multiple developers. Often, the existence of such conditions indicates
logical errors in code (as shown below in the Example section). Additionally, such conditions
may indicate overly loose assumptions, which results in less efficient code and fragments
of dead code.

For the purpose of this rule, a 'branching condition' is defined as the controlling expression in:
    - if statements (except constexpr if statements)
    - switch statements
    - while loops
    - for loops
    - do...while loops
    - ternary operator

This rule analyzes:

- Branching conditions
    
- Non-branching conditions in the form 'a OP b', where OP is one of operators: ==, !=, >, >=, <, <=, &&, ||
   The result of a non-branching condition can be, for example:
        - assigned to a variable or
        - used as a parameter in a function call or
        - returned from a function
   Violations from non-branching conditions can be disabled by turning off the "Report
   violations on non-branching conditions" parameter (see PARAMETERS).
    
In both cases, if the condition is in the form of a logical AND (a && b) or a logical OR (a || b) operation,
the operands of such an operation are considered as sub-conditions, recursively.
The sub-condition is considered a branching condition or a non-branching condition depending on 
the whole operation.
Violations from sub-conditions can be disabled by turning off the "Report violations on sub-
conditions" parameter (see PARAMETERS).

For example:
bool test(int a)
{
    if (a > 3) {} 
    // "a > 3" is "branching condition"
    
    if (a > 3 || a < 1) {}
    // "a > 3 || a < 1" is "branching condition"
    // "a > 3" and "a < 1" are sub-conditions and "branching conditions"(inherited)
    
    bool b = a > 3;
    // "a > 3" is non-"branching condition"
    
    return a > 3 || a < 1;
    // "a > 3 || a < 1" is non-"branching condition"
    // "a > 3" and "a < 1" are sub-conditions and non-"branching conditions"(inherited)
}
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_3-ac.benefits:
This rule helps you keep your code in good shape and eliminate logical
inconsistencies as soon as they are introduced.
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_3-ac.parameters:
The "Report violations on sub-conditions" parameter enables reporting violations on 
sub-conditions. If the parameter is turned off, the rule will report only on top-level 
conditions.

The "Report violations when a constant value is used as a condition" parameter enables 
reporting violations when a constant value is used as a condition.
The "Except when:" set of parameters helps you reduce the number of violations when 
"Report violations when a constant value is used as a condition" is enabled:
- If "Literal 'true' or '1' is used to define potentially infinite loop" is enabled, the rule 
will not report when literal 'true' or '1' is used as a loop condition.
- If "Literal 'false' or '0' is used as a controlling expression of a do-while loop" is enabled,
 the rule will not report when literal 'false' or '0' is used as a do-while loop condition.
 
The "Report violations on non-branching conditions" parameter enables reporting on 
non-branching conditions.
references: CWE-570: Expression is Always False
CWE-571: Expression is Always True
CWE-665: Improper Initialization

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_4-a.title: Tests of a value against zero should be made explicit, unless the operand is effectively Boolean [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_4-a.description:
"Where a data value is to be tested against zero then the test should be made
explicit. The exception to this rule is when data represents a Boolean value,
even though in C this will in practice be an integer."

An 'effectively Boolean' expression which is either 'Boolean-by-construct'
or 'Boolean-by-enforcement' as defined below.

Boolean-by-construct values are produced by the following operators:
- equality operators (== and !=)
- logical operators (!, && and ||)
- relational operators (<, >, <= and >=)

Boolean-by-enforcement values can be introduced by implementing a specific type
enforcement mechanism using a tool. A Boolean type could be associated with
a specific typedef, and would then be used for any objects that are Boolean."

Rule does not report violation if as an operand of logical operator is used:
- Boolean-by-construct values
- boolean constant
- integer or enum constant defined as '0' or '1'
- variable, parameter or expression of type:
  - bool,
  - typedef to bool,
  - an enumeration type whose name begins with 'bool' (case insensitive)
    or contains 'boolean' (case insensitive) and contains exactly 2 enumeration
    constants
  - typedef whose name begins with 'bool' (case insensitive)
    or contains 'boolean' (case insensitive) to:
    -- a char type defined explicitly as signed or unsigned
    -- a short or int type
    -- an enumeration type which contains exactly 2 enumeration constants
  - reference to above types
MISRAC2012-RULE_14_4-a.benefits:
"This rule is in the interests of clarity, and makes clear the distinction
between integers and logical values."
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 14.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 14.4 The controlling expression of an if statement and the controlling expression of an iteration-statement shall have essentially Boolean type

Chapter 6, Section 13
4. Misra Guidelines - Rule 49
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP20-C. Perform explicit tests to determine success, true and false, and
equality

MISRAC2012-RULE_14_4.title: Rule 14.4 (Required) The controlling expression of an if statement and the controlling expression of an iteration-statement shall have essentially Boolean type [MISRAC2012-RULE_14_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_1-a.title: The goto statement shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_1-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent confusion and improve the readability
and maintainability of the code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_1.title: Rule 15.1 (Advisory) The goto statement should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_2-a.title: The goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the same function body [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_2-a.description:
"Unconstrained use of goto can lead to programs that are extremely difficult
to comprehend, analyse and, for C++, can also lead to the program exhibiting
unspecified behaviour.

However, in many cases a total ban on goto requires the introduction of flags
to ensure correct control flow, and it is possible that these flags may
themselves be less transparent than the goto they replace.

Therefore, the restricted use of goto is allowed where that use will not lead
to semantics contrary to developer expectations. 'Back' jumps are prohibited
as they can be used to create iterations without using the well-defined
iteration statements supplied by the core language."

This rule reports a violation if 'goto' is used to jump to a label which is
declared before the 'goto' location.
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_2-a.benefits:
"Restricting the use of goto so that 'back' jumps are prohibited ensures
that iteration only occurs if the iteration statements provided by the
language are used, helping to minimize visual code complexity."
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-6-2. The goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the same
function body
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.3.1. Ensure that the label(s) for a jump statement or a switch condition
appear later, in the same or an enclosing block
3. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-6-2
4. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.6.3 The goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the function body
5. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 15.2
6. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 15.2 The goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the same function

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_2.title: Rule 15.2 (Required) The goto statement shall jump to a label declared later in the same function [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_3-a.title: Any label referenced by a goto statement shall be declared in the same block, or in a block enclosing the goto statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_3-a.description:
"Unconstrained use of goto can lead to programs that are extremely difficult to
comprehend, analyse and, for C++, can also lead to the program exhibiting
unspecified behaviour.

However, in many cases a total ban on goto requires the introduction of flags
to ensure correct control flow, and it is possible that these flags may
themselves be less transparent than the goto they replace.

Therefore, the restricted use of goto is allowed where that use will not lead
to semantics contrary to developer expectations. Jumping in to nested blocks is
prohibited as it may lead to complex flow graphs."

This rule reports a violation if 'goto' is used to jump into a nested block, or
into a block which does not enclose (directly or indirectly) current block.
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_3-a.benefits:
Preventing jumps between blocks, or into nested blocks, helps to minimize visual
code complexity.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-6-1. Any label referenced by a goto statement shall be declared in the
same block, or in a block enclosing the goto statement
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.3.1. Ensure that the label(s) for a jump statement or a switch condition
appear later, in the same or an enclosing block
3. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-6-1
4. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.6.2 A goto statement shall reference a label in a surrounding block
5. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 15.3
6. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 15.3 Any label referenced by a goto statement shall be declared in the same block, or in any block enclosing the goto statement

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_3.title: Rule 15.3 (Required) Any label referenced by a goto statement shall be declared in the same block, or in any block enclosing the goto statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_4-a.title: For any iteration statement there shall be no more than one break or goto statement used for loop termination [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_4-a.description:
"For any iteration statement there shall be no more than one break or goto
statement used for loop termination."
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_4-a.benefits:
"Restricting the number of exits from a loop is done in the interests of good
structured programming. One break or goto statement is acceptable in a loop
since this allows, for example, for dual outcome loops or optimal coding."
references: 1. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.24 Flow Control Structures, AV Rule 191
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-6-4
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 15.4
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 15.4 There should be no more than one break or goto statement used to terminate any iteration statement

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_4.title: Rule 15.4 (Advisory) There should be no more than one break or goto statement used to terminate any iteration statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_5-a.title: A function shall have a single point of exit at the end of the function [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_5-a.description:
"A function should have no more than one return statement.
When a return statement is used, it should be the final statement
in the compound statement that forms the body of the function.
Early returns may lead to the unintentional omission
of function termination code."
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_5-a.benefits: Complying with this rule leads to safer and more readable code.
references: 1. ISO/DIS 26262
point 8.4.4

Rule 15.5 A function should have a single point of exit at the end

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_5.title: Rule 15.5 (Advisory) A function should have a single point of exit at the end [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-a.title: The statement forming the body of a 'switch', 'while', 'do...while' or 'for' statement shall be a compound statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-a.description:
This rule detects when a 'while', 'do', 'for' or 'switch' statement
is not immediately followed by a compound statement enclosed in
braces.
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-a.benefits:
This rule helps you improve the readability and maintainability of your code.

A developer might mistakenly assume that a sequence of statements forms
the body of an iteration-statement due to their indentation.
Accidentally adding a semicolon after the control expression
is particularly risky, as it results in an empty statement.
Using a block statement clearly specifies which statements are included
in the body.

M6-3-1. The statement forming the body of a switch, while, do ... while or
for statement shall be a compound statement

6.1.1. Enclose the body of a selection or an iteration statement in a
compound statement

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-b.title: The statement forming the body of an 'if' or 'else' statement should be a compound statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-b.description:
This rule detects when an 'if' or 'else' statement is not immediately
followed by a compound statement enclosed in braces.
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_6-b.benefits:
This rule helps you improve the readability and maintainability of your code.

A developer might mistakenly assume that a sequence of statements forms
the body of a selection-statement due to their indentation.
Accidentally adding a semicolon after the control expression
is particularly risky, as it results in an empty statement.
Using a block statement clearly specifies which statements are included
in the body.

MISRAC2012-RULE_15_7-a.title: All 'if...else-if' constructs shall be terminated with an 'else' clause [MISRAC2012-RULE_15_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_7-a.description:
"This rule applies whenever an 'if' statement is followed by one or more
'else-if' statements; the final 'else-if' shall be followed by an 'else'
statement. In the case of a simple 'if' statement then the 'else' statement
need not be included.
The requirement for a final 'else' statement is defensive programming.
The 'else' statement should either take appropriate action or contain
a suitable comment as to why no action is taken."

See also: CODSTA-23
MISRAC2012-RULE_15_7-a.benefits: Rule ensures proper data flow and improves readability and maintainability.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-4-2. All if ... else if constructs shall be terminated with an else
clause
2. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.1.2. Explicitly cover all paths through multi-way selection statements

systems, Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-4-2

Rule 15.7 All if ... else if constructs shall be terminated with an else statement

MSC01-C. Strive for logical completeness

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-a.title: A switch statement shall only contain switch labels and switch clauses, and no other code [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-a.description:
"A switch statement shall only contain switch labels
and switch clauses, and no other code.
Switch label is either a case label or default label.
Switch clause is either a case clause or a default clause.
Case clause is the code between any two switch labels.
Default clause is the code between the default label
and the end of the switch statement."
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-a.benefits: The rule enforces a simple and consistent structure on to the switch statement.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.4.2 The structure of a switch statement shall be appropriate
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.1
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 16.1 All switch statements shall be well-formed
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL41-C. Do not declare variables inside a switch statement before the
first case label

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-b.title: A switch label shall only be used when the most closely-enclosing compound statement is the body of a switch statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-b.description:
"The Standard permits a switch label, i.e. a case label or default label,
to be placed before any statement contained in the body of a switch statement,
potentially leading to unstructured code. In order to prevent this,
a switch label shall only appear at the outermost level of the compound
statement forming the body of a switch statement." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 16.2]

The rule detects nested switch-labels.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-b.benefits: This rule helps you improve readability and maintainability of code.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-4-3. A switch statement shall be a well-formed switch statement
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-4-4. A switch-label shall only be used when the most closely-enclosing
compound statement is the body of a switch statement

6.3.1. Ensure that the label(s) for a jump statement or a switch condition
appear later, in the same or an enclosing block

Rule 16.1 All switch statements shall be well-formed

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-c.title: An unconditional break statement shall terminate every non-empty case clause [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-c.description:
"The last statement in every switch clause shall be a break statement,
or if the switch clause is a compound statement, then the last statement
in the compound statement shall be a break statement."

The rule reports a violation if a non-empty case clause is not terminated
by break or return statement.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-c.benefits:
"Terminating a non-empty case clause in a switch statement with a break
statement eliminates potentially confusing behavior by prohibiting control
from falling through to subsequent statements."
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15.2
2. Misra Guidelines - Rule 61
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.3, Rule 16.1
4. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.

5. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.24 Flow Control Structures, AV Rule 193

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-d.title: An unconditional break statement shall terminate every non-empty default clause [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-d.description:
"The last statement in every switch clause shall be a break statement,
or if the switch clause is a compound statement, then the last statement
in the compound statement shall be a break statement."

The rule reports a violation if a non-empty default clause is not terminated
by break or return statement.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-d.benefits:
Terminating a non-empty default clause in a switch statement with a break
statement in case a future modification turns this into a case clause.
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15.2
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.3, Rule 16.1

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-e.title: Always provide a default branch for switch statements [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-e]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-e.description:
"Every switch statement shall have a default label.
The requirement for a default label is defensive programming.
Any statements following the default label are intended to take
some appropriate action. " [MISRA C:2012 Rule 16.4]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-e.benefits:
Rule implements defensive programming standards which limit possibility of
erroneous code.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.4, Rule 16.1

Rule 16.1  All switch statements shall be well-formed

Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-f.title: A 'default' label shall have a statement or a comment before terminating 'break' [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-f]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-f.description:
"The switch-clause following the 'default' label shall, prior to the terminating
'break' statement, contain either:
* A statement, or
* A comment." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 16.4]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-g.title: A 'default' label, if it exists, shall appear as either the first or the last switch label of a switch statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-g]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-g.description:
If a switch statement has a 'default' label, then the label should be the first
or the last label.

See also: CODSTA-35
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-g.benefits: This rule makes it easy to locate the default label within a switch statement.

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-h.title: Every switch statement shall have at least two switch-clauses [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-h]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-h.description:
"Every switch statement shall have at least two switch-clauses.
A switch statement with a single path is redundant and may be indicative
of a programming error."

A switch-clause in this rule is defined as a group of 'case' and 'default'
labels followed by at least one statement.

See also: CODSTA-35, CODSTA-119, MISRA2004-15_2, MISRA2004-15_5, OPT-21
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_1-h.benefits: Prevents redundant and possibly incorrect code.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.4.2 The structure of a switch statement shall be appropriate

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_2-a.title: A switch label shall only be used when the most closely-enclosing compound statement is the body of a switch statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_2-a.description:
"The Standard permits a switch label, i.e. a case label or default label,
to be placed before any statement contained in the body of a switch statement,
potentially leading to unstructured code. In order to prevent this,
a switch label shall only appear at the outermost level of the compound
statement forming the body of a switch statement." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 16.2]

The rule detects nested switch-labels.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_2-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve readability and maintainability of code.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-4-3. A switch statement shall be a well-formed switch statement

compound statement is the body of a switch statement
3. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.3.1. Ensure that the label(s) for a jump statement or a switch condition
appear later, in the same or an enclosing block
4. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-4-3, Rule 6-4-4
5. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.4.2 The structure of a switch statement shall be appropriate
