MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-a.title: An unconditional break statement shall terminate every non-empty case clause [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-a.description:
"The last statement in every switch clause shall be a break statement,
or if the switch clause is a compound statement, then the last statement
in the compound statement shall be a break statement."

The rule reports a violation if a non-empty case clause is not terminated
by break or return statement.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-a.benefits:
"Terminating a non-empty case clause in a switch statement with a break
statement eliminates potentially confusing behavior by prohibiting control
from falling through to subsequent statements."
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15.2
2. Misra Guidelines - Rule 61
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.3, Rule 16.1
4. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 14 Flow Control Structures - Rule 47
5. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.24 Flow Control Structures, AV Rule 193

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-b.title: An unconditional break statement shall terminate every non-empty default clause [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-b.description:
"The last statement in every switch clause shall be a break statement,
or if the switch clause is a compound statement, then the last statement
in the compound statement shall be a break statement."

The rule reports a violation if a non-empty default clause is not terminated
by break or return statement.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3-b.benefits:
Terminating a non-empty default clause in a switch statement with a break
statement in case a future modification turns this into a case clause.
references: 1. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15.2
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.3, Rule 16.1

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3.title: Rule 16.3 (Required) An unconditional break statement shall terminate every switch-clause [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-a.title: Always provide a default branch for switch statements [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-a.description:
"Every switch statement shall have a default label.
The requirement for a default label is defensive programming.
Any statements following the default label are intended to take
some appropriate action. " [MISRA C:2012 Rule 16.4]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-a.benefits:
Rule implements defensive programming standards which limit possibility of
erroneous code.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.4, Rule 16.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 16.1  All switch statements shall be well-formed
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 16.4 Every switch statement shall have a default label
4. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 14 Flow Control Structures - Rule 48
5. CWE-478: Missing Default Case in Multiple Condition Expression

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-b.title: A 'default' label shall have a statement or a comment before terminating 'break' [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_4-b.description:
"The switch-clause following the 'default' label shall, prior to the terminating
'break' statement, contain either:
* A statement, or
* A comment." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 16.4]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.4, Rule 16.1

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_5-a.title: A 'default' label, if it exists, shall appear as either the first or the last switch label of a switch statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_5-a.description:
If a switch statement has a 'default' label, then the label should be the first
or the last label.

See also: CODSTA-35
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_5-a.benefits: This rule makes it easy to locate the default label within a switch statement.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.5, Rule 16.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_6-a.title: Every switch statement shall have at least two switch-clauses [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_6-a.description:
"Every switch statement shall have at least two switch-clauses.
A switch statement with a single path is redundant and may be indicative
of a programming error."

A switch-clause in this rule is defined as a group of 'case' and 'default'
labels followed by at least one statement.

See also: CODSTA-35, CODSTA-119, MISRA2004-15_2, MISRA2004-15_5, OPT-21
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_6-a.benefits: Prevents redundant and possibly incorrect code.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 9.4.2 The structure of a switch statement shall be appropriate

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-a.title: A switch expression shall not represent a value that is effectively Boolean [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-a.description:
"The Standard requires the controlling expression of a switch statement to have
an integer type. Since the type that is used to implement Boolean values
is an integer, it is possible to have a switch statement controlled by a Boolean
expression. In this instance an if-else construct would be more appropriate."

The rule reports a violation if in a switch condition is used:
- equality operator (==, !=), or
- logical operator (!, &&, ||), or
- relational operator (<, >, <=, >=), or
- parameter/variable/expression of boolean type or reference to boolean type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-a.benefits: The rule prevents using values that are effectively Boolean in switch condition.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-4-3. A switch statement shall be a well-formed switch statement
2. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M6-4-7. The condition of a switch statement shall not have bool type
3. High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
6.1.4. Ensure that a switch statement has at least two case labels,
distinct from the default label
4. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.24 Flow Control Structures, AV Rule 195
5. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6-4-3, Rule 6-4-7
6. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.7
7. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 16.7 A switch-expression shall not have essentially Boolean type
8. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15.4
9. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 63

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-b.title: A switch expression shall not represent a value that is effectively Boolean [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-b.description:
"The Standard requires the controlling expression of a switch statement to have
an integer type. Since the type that is used to implement Boolean values
is an integer, it is possible to have a switch statement controlled by a Boolean
expression. In this instance an if-else construct would be more appropriate."

The rule reports a violation if as switch condition is used variable, parameter
or expression of type:
  - an enumeration type whose name begins with 'bool' (case insensitive)
    or contains 'boolean' (case insensitive) and contains exactly 2 enumeration
    constants
  - typedef whose name begins with 'bool' (case insensitive)
    or contains 'boolean' (case insensitive) to:
    -- a char type defined explicitly as signed or unsigned
    -- a short or int type
    -- an enumeration type which contains exactly 2 enumeration constants
  - reference to above type
MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7-b.benefits: The rule prevents using values that are effectively Boolean in switch condition.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 16.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 16.7 A switch-expression shall not have essentially Boolean type
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 15, Rule 15.4
4. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 63

MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7.title: Rule 16.7 (Required) A switch-expression shall not have essentially Boolean type [MISRAC2012-RULE_16_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-a.title: The identifiers va_list, va_arg, va_start, va_end, va_copy should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-a.description:
"The standard header file <stdarg.h> shall not be #included, and none of the
features that are specified as being provided by <stdarg.h> shall be used.

The Standard lists many instances of undefined behaviour associated with
the features of <stdarg.h>, including:
- va_end not being used prior to end of a function in which va_start was used;
- va_arg being used in different functions on the same va_list;
- the type of an argument not being compatible with the type specified
  to va_arg." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.1 with Amendment 3]

The rule reports violations on identifiers named va_list, va_arg, va_start,
va_end or va_copy. The rule does not check if these identifiers are macros
from stdarg.h header, so violation will be reported even if <stdarg.h> is not
included or the identifiers are defined by the user.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-a.example_repair: Do not use identifiers va_list, va_arg, va_start, va_end and va_copy.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
7.8 Variable arguments <stdarg.h>
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
7.15 Variable arguments <stdarg.h>

Rule 21.10.1 The features of <cstdarg> shall not be used

3 Consequential Amendments
3.1.2 Consistency of terminology regarding header file inclusion
5. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.1
6. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.1 The standard header file <stdarg.h> shall not be used

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-b.description:

The Standard lists many instances of undefined behaviour associated with
the features of <stdarg.h>, including:
- va_end not being used prior to end of a function in which va_start was used;
- va_arg being used in different functions on the same va_list;
- the type of an argument not being compatible with the type specified

va_end. The rule does not check if these identifiers are macros
from stdarg.h header, so violation will be reported even if <stdarg.h> is not
included or the identifiers are defined by the user.

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-c.title: The standard header file <stdarg.h> or <cstdarg> shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_1-c.description:
"The standard header file <stdarg.h> shall not be #included, and none of the
features that are specified as being provided by <stdarg.h> shall be used.

The Standard lists many instances of undefined behaviour associated with
the features of <stdarg.h>, including:
- va_end not being used prior to end of a function in which va_start was used;
- va_arg being used in different functions on the same va_list;
- the type of an argument not being compatible with the type specified
  to va_arg." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.1 with Amendment 3]

The rule detects when the <stdarg.h> or <cstdarg> header is included.

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10-a.title: Functions declared as 'noreturn' shall have the 'void' return type [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10-a.description:
"By definition, use of the _Noreturn function specifier indicates
unambiguously that a function will not return to its caller.
A function that cannot return cannot provide a return value and shall
therefore be defined with a void return type." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects functions whose return type is other than 'void' that are
declared as 'noreturn' with one of the following:
  * "_Noreturn" function specifier
  * "noreturn" macro from stdnoreturn.h
  * __attribute__((noreturn))
  * [[ noreturn ]] attribute
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid unexpected behavior and prevent unexpected failures
at runtime.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10-a.example_violation:
In the following example, the "foo" function declared with the "_Noreturn"
specifier returns an int value.

_Noreturn int foo(void);                                    // Violation
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10-a.example_repair:
To fix the code, change the return type of the "foo" function to "void".

_Noreturn void foo(void);                                   // OK
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.13 Rule 17.10
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.10 A function declared with a _Noreturn function specifier shall have void return type
3. Programming languages -- C ISO/IEC 9899:201x
6.7.4 Function specifiers

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10.title: Rule 17.10 (Required) A function declared with a _Noreturn function specifier shall have void return type [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_10]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_11-a.title: Declare non-returning functions with the attribute that specifies that they do not return [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_11-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_11-a.description:
This rule detects when a non-returning function is declared without
the specifier that indicates that the function does not return, that is:
	* _Noreturn specifier in C (since C11)
	* [[noreturn]] attribute in C++ (since C++11)
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_11-a.benefits:
This rule helps you ensure that a function that does not return is declared
with an appropriate specifier. This allows the compiler to optimize code and
code analyzers to perform a more detailed analysis.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.13 Rule 17.11

Rule 17.11 A function that never returns should be declared with a _Noreturn function specifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_12-a.title: A function identifier shall only be used with either a preceding '&', or with a parenthesised parameter list, which may be empty [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_12-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_12-a.description:
"If the developer writes 'if( f )' then it is not clear whether the intent
is to test if the address of the function is NULL or if a call to the
function f() should be made and the brackets have been unintentionally omitted.
The use of the & (address-of) operator will resolve this ambiguity."
[MISRA C++:2008]

The rule detects when the function identifier is not preceded
by the & (address-of) operator and is not followed by parentheses.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_12-a.benefits:
"A function identifier can implicitly convert to a pointer to a function.
In certain contexts this may result in a well-formed program, but which
is contrary to developer expectations." [MISRA C++:2008]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M8-4-4. A function identifier shall either be used to call the function or
it shall be preceded by &
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 8, Rule 8-4-4
3. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.14 Rule 17.12
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.12 A function identifier should only be used with either a preceding &, or with a parenthesized parameter list
5. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 16
6. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 85

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_12.title: Rule 17.12 (Advisory) A function identifier should only be used with either a preceding &, or with a parenthesized parameter list [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_12]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13-a.title: Do not include any type qualifiers in the specification of a function type [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13-a.description:
"If the specification of a function type includes any type qualifiers,
the behavior is undefined." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

This rule detects when the 'const', 'volatile,' or 'restrict' qualifier is
specified for a function type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13-a.example_repair:
To fix the code, remove the 'const', 'volatile', and 'restrict' qualifiers
specified for the function type.

typedef const int func( void );   // OK

typedef func func_t;              // OK

void f1( void ) {
    func* volatile pfunc;         // OK
}
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features

2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.13 A function type shall not be type qualified
3. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.7.3 Type qualifiers

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13.title: Rule 17.13 (Required) A function type shall not be type qualified [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_13]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_2-a.title: Functions shall not call themselves, either directly or indirectly [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_2-a.description:
"Recursion carries with it the danger of exceeding available stack space, which
can lead to a serious failure. Unless recursion is very tightly controlled,
it is not possible to determine before execution what the worst-case stack
usage could be." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.2]

See also: BD-PB-RECFUN
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_2-a.benefits:
"Unbounded recursion is likely to lead to a stack over-flow and may impact
system timings" [MISRA C++:2008 Rule 7-5-4]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_2-a.example_repair: Do not use recursive function calls.

indirectly

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_3-a.title: Functions shall always have visible prototype at the function call [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_3-a.description:
"Provided that a function call is made in the presence of a prototype,
a constraint ensures that the number of arguments matches the number
of parameters and that each argument can be assigned to its corresponding
parameter." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.3]

The rule requires that prototype (declaration or definition of function
that explicitly declares types of all parameters or uses explicit 'void'
if the function does not have parameters) must be declared before the
function is called.

This rule is enabled only for C language.

See also: MISRA-071_a, MISRA2004-8_2_b, MISRA2004-8_3_b, MISRA2004-16_4,
          MISRA2004-16_5, MISRA2004-16_6
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_3-a.benefits:
"If a function is declared implicitly, a C90 compiler will assume that
the function has a return type of int. Since an implicit function declaration
does not provide a prototype, a compiler will have no information about
the number of function parameters and their types. Inappropriate type
conversions may result in passing the arguments and assigning the return
value, as well as other undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.3]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.3
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.3 A function shall not be declared implicitly
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 8, Rule 8.1
4. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 71
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP37-C. Call functions with the correct number and type of arguments

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_3.title: Rule 17.3 (Mandatory) A function shall not be declared implicitly [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_4-a.description: This rule detects functions that fail to return a value from all exit paths.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_4-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_4-b.title: All exit paths from a function, except main(), with non-void return type shall have an explicit return statement with an expression [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_4-b.description:
"All exit paths from a function with non-void return type shall have
an explicit return statement with an expression.
In a function with non-void return type, return expression gives the value
that the function returns. The absence of a return with an expression leads to
undefined behavior (and the compiler may not give an error)." [AUTOSAR A8-4-2]

This rule detects functions, othen than main(), that fail to return a value
from all exit paths.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_4-b.benefits: The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_5-a.title: The function argument corresponding to a parameter declared to have an array type shall have an appropriate number of elements [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_5-a.description:
"If a parameter is declared as an array with a specified size, the corresponding
argument in each function call should point into an object that has at least as
many elements as the array.

The use of an array declarator for a function parameter specifies the function
interface more clearly than using a pointer. The minimum number of elements
expected by the function is explicitly stated, whereas this is not possible
with a pointer.

A function parameter array declarator which does not specify a size is assumed
to indicate that the function can handle an array of any size. In such cases,
it is expected that the array size will be communicated by some other means,
for example by being passed as another parameter, or by terminating the array
with a sentinel value."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.5]

The rule reports a violation if you call a function declared with an array
parameter and you pass an array which has smaller rank.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_5-a.benefits:
"The use of an array bound is recommended as it allows out-of-bounds checking
to be implemented within the function body and extra checks on parameter
passing. It is legal in C to pass an array of the incorrect size to a parameter
with a specified size, which can lead to unexpected behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.5]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.5 The function argument corresponding to a parameter declared to have an array type shall have an appropriate number of elements

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_5.title: Rule 17.5 (Required) The function argument corresponding to a parameter declared to have an array type shall have an appropriate number of elements [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6-a.title: The declaration of an array parameter shall not contain the 'static' keyword between the [ ] [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6-a.description:
"If the keyword static also appears within the [ and ] of the array type
derivation, then for each call to the function, the value of the corresponding
actual argument shall provide access to the first element of an array with at
least as many elements as specified by the size expression."[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

"Some compilers are able to take advantage of this information to
generate more efficient code for some types of processor. If the guarantee
made by the programmer is not honoured, and the number of elements is less
than the minimum specified, the behaviour is undefined."[MISRA C:2012]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6-a.example_violation:
typedef void foot(int a[static 10]);        /* Violation */

void foo1(void (*fp1)(int a[static 10])) {  /* Violation */
  void (*fp2)(int a[static 10]);            /* Violation */
  fp1 = (void (*)(int a[static 10]))fp2;    /* Violation */
}
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6-a.example_repair: There is no use of this C99 language feature that is compliant with this rule.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.6
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.7.5.3 Function declarators (including prototypes)

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6.title: Rule 17.6 (Mandatory) The declaration of an array parameter shall not contain the static keyword between the [ ] [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-a.title: The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-a.description:
"It is possible to call a function without using the return value, which may be
an error. If the return value of a function is intended not to be used
explicitly, it should be cast to the void type." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.7]

This rule does not check the first operand of comma operator (","). It is
checked by a separate rule: CODSTA-122_b

See also: CODSTA-122_b
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-a.benefits: Rule prevents errors caused by not using or checking function return value.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.7 The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-b.title: The value returned by a function having non-void return type shall be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-b.description:
"It is possible to call a function without using the return value, which may be
an error. If the return value of a function is intended not to be used
explicitly, it should be cast to the void type." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.7]

This rule checks the first operand of comma operator (","). All other cases are
checked by a separate rule: CODSTA-122_a

See also: CODSTA-122_a
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_7-b.benefits: Rule prevents errors caused by not using or checking function return value.

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_8-a.title: A function parameter should not be modified [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_8-a.description:
"A function parameter behaves in the same manner as an object that has automatic
storage duration. While the C language permits parameters to be modified, such
use can be confusing and conflict with programmer expectations. It may be less
confusing to copy the parameter to an automatic object and modify that copy.
With a modern compiler, this will not usually result in any storage or execution
time penalty." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.8]

The rule reports a violation if the parameter is modified inside function.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_8-a.benefits:
"Programmers who are unfamiliar with C, but who are used to other languages,
may modify a parameter believing that the effects of the modification will
be felt in the calling function." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 17.8]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 17.8
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 17.8 A function parameter should not be modified

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_8.title: Rule 17.8 (Advisory) A function parameter should not be modified [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_8]

MISRAC2012-RULE_17_9-a.title: Never return from functions that should not return [MISRAC2012-RULE_17_9-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_9-a.description:
This rule detects returns of functions that are declared with the "noreturn" attribute.

A function that specifies the "noreturn" attribute can prohibit returning by throwing an exception, 
entering an infinite loop, or calling another non-returning function.

The standard functions declared with this attribute: 
    * std::_Exit,
    * std::abort,
    * std::exit, 
    * std::quick_exit,
    * std::unexpected, 
    * std::terminate, 
    * std::rethrow_exception, 
    * std::throw_with_nested,
    * std::nested_exception::rethrow_nested.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_9-a.security_relevance:
Returning from an 'f' function that has been declared with the "noreturn" attribute
results in undefined behavior that might be exploited.
MISRAC2012-RULE_17_9-a.benefits:
Helps you avoid unexpected failures at runtime. The Standard does not define behavior
if a function declared with the "noreturn" attribute returns, which may lead to
unexpected behaviors.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-a.title: Avoid accessing arrays out of bounds [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-a.description:
This rule detects array access operations which may result in out of bounds access.
Specifically, a violation is reported if an execution path with obvious out-of-bounds
access is possible. This occurs when an index value is negative, equal to,
or greater than the array size.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-a.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-a.benefits:
Application of the rule helps to keep the code free from as serious problem as accessing 
an array out of its bounds is. This helps to ensure robustness of the application 
as a whole.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-a.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and its size is
declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the array that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is disabled by default.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-b.title: Avoid accessing arrays and pointers out of bounds [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-b.description:
This rule detects pointer dereference operations that may result in accessing allocated memory that 
is out of bounds. A violation is reported when pointer is dereferenced and points to an array
(address of array variable was assigned to it or array was dynamically created by new[] operator).
Also, a violation is triggered if memory is allocated using new() operator and the pointer is moved
and accessed.

See also : BD-PB-ARRAY
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-b.security_relevance:
The potential to access arrays beyond their boundaries is a severe security threat. If an
application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to execute arbitrary code
and gain full control over the application.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-c.title: A pointer operand and any pointer resulting from pointer arithmetic using that operand shall both address elements of the same array [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-c.description:
This rule reports violations in the following cases:
- a pointer arithmetic operation is performed on a non-array object (see also PARAMETERS)
- the resulting pointer does not address an element of the allocated array
or the element one beyond the end of that array.

This rules applies to the following expressions:
  pointer_expression + integer_expression
  integer_expression + pointer_expression
  pointer_expression - integer_expression
  ++ pointer_expression
  pointer_expression ++
  -- pointer_expression
  pointer_expression --
  & pointer_expression [ integer_expression ]
  & integer_expression [ pointer_expression ]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-c.security_relevance:
"It is undefined behaviour if the result obtained from one of the above expressions 
is not a pointer to an element of the array pointed to by pointer_expression or 
an element one beyond the end of that array".
[MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 5, Rule 5-0-16]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-c.benefits:
The rule helps you avoid unexpected behavior which may lead to failures at runtime. This helps
also to ensure robustness of the application as a whole.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_1-c.parameters:
The "Allow pointer arithmetic on non-array objects, treating them as one-element arrays"
parameter disables reporting violations whenever pointer arithmetic operation is performed
on a non-array object, treating them as they were one-element arrays.

The "Report violations when pointer within the bounds of the structure is out of the field bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the result of the pointer arithmetic exceeds the bounds of the structure whose last field is an
array with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the result of the pointer arithmetic exceeds the bounds of the field, even if it points to
a location within the containing structure object.

The "Report when the pointer addresses one element beyond the end of the array" parameter
enables reporting violations when the resulting pointer points at one element beyond the end
of an array.
For example, with this parameter enabled, the rule will report the following violation:
int32_t a2[ 10 ];
int32_t * p2 = &a2 [ 10 ];   // VIOLATION

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_10-a.title: Pointers to variably-modified array types should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_10-a.description:
"If the two array types are used in a context which requires them to be
compatible, it is undefined behavior if the two size specifiers evaluate
to unequal values." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

This rule detects when a declaration of a parameter or variable
has a pointer to a variably-modified array type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_10-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_2-a.title: Do not subtract two pointers that do not address elements of the same array [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_2-a.description:
This rule detects when two pointers that do not address elements of the same
array are subtracted.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_2-a.security_relevance:
Subtracting pointers that do not address elements of the same array may cause
undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_2-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior that may be caused by
subtracting pointers that do not address elements of the same array.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_2-a.parameters:
If "Aggressive mode" is enabled, the rule additionally reports violations 
in cases when Flow Analysis is unable to positively verify that the subtracted
pointers address elements of the same array and there is probability that
reported violations are false positives. Enabling this parameter results in better
bug detection, but it may increase the number of false alarms.
With this parameter disabled, the rule only triggers when Flow Analysis can
conclusively determine that the subtracted pointers do not address elements of
the same array.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_3-a.title: Do not compare two unrelated pointers [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_3-a.description:
This rule detects when two unrelated pointers are compared.

The rule assumes that two pointers are related if they store addresses of two elements
of the same array. Whether two pointers that point to two elements of the same structure
object are related or not depends on the rule parameterization (see PARAMETERS).
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_3-a.security_relevance: Comparison of unrelated pointers may cause undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_3-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior that may be caused by
comparing unrelated pointers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_3-a.parameters:
If the "Report when the pointers are compared using relational operators"
parameter is enabled, the rule detects when pointers are compared with
the following operators:
	operator>
	operator<
	operator>=
	operator<=
	
If the "Report when the pointers are compared using standard functions" parameter 
is enabled, the rule detects when pointers are compared with the following
operators:
	std::less()
	std::greater()
	std::less_equal()
	std::greater_equal()
	
If the "Treat pointers addressing fields of the same object as related"
parameter is enabled, the rule assumes that that two pointers that address
elements of the same structure object (for example, two fields of the same
object) are related.

If "Aggressive mode" is enabled, the rule additionally reports pointer
comparison in cases when Flow Analysis is unable to positively verify that
the compared pointers are related and there is probability that reported
violations are false positives. Enabling this parameter results in better
bug detection, but it may increase the number of false alarms. 
With this parameter disabled, the rule only triggers when Flow Analysis can
conclusively determine that the compared pointers are not related.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_4-a.title: The +, -, += and -= operators should not be applied to an expression of pointer type [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_4-a.description:
"Array indexing using the array subscript syntax, ptr[expr], is the preferred
form of pointer arithmetic because it is often clearer and hence less error
prone than pointer manipulation. Any explicitly calculated pointer value has
the potential to access unintended or invalid memory addresses. Such behaviour
is also possible with array indexing, but the subscript syntax may ease the
task of manual review.
Pointer arithmetic in C can be confusing to the novice. The expression ptr+1
may be mistakenly interpreted as the addition of 1 to the address held in ptr.
In fact the new memory address depends on the size in bytes of the pointer's
target. This misunderstanding can lead to unexpected behaviour if sizeof is
applied incorrectly." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 18.4]

This rule detects cases when the operands of the following operators have
a pointer type:
 +, -, +=, -=
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_4-a.benefits:
The rule helps you avoid accessing unintended or invalid memory addresses,
improves readability of code and prevents confusions.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 18.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_5-a.title: The declaration of objects should contain no more than 2 levels of pointer indirection [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_5-a.description:
"No more than two pointer declarators should be applied consecutively to a type.
Any typedef-name appearing in a declaration is treated as if it were replaced
by the type that it denotes.
The use of more than two levels of pointer nesting can seriously impair
the ability to understand the behaviour of the code, and should therefore
be avoided." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 18.5]

The rule reports a violation if more than 2 levels of pointer indirection
is used in declaration of object or function.

Typedefs appearing in declarations are treated as if it were replaced by the type
that it denotes.

The array type of function parameter is recognized as pointer, because arrays
are converted to a pointer to the initial element of the array.

See also: CODSTA-05, CODSTA-89, CODSTA-94, CODSTA-95
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_5-a.benefits: The rule improves readability and maintainability of code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-a.title: The address of an object with automatic storage shall not be returned from a function [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-a.description:
"If the address of an automatic object is returned from a function, then
the object containing the address may exist beyond the time when the original
object ceases to exist (and its address becomes invalid)." [MISRA C:2004]

The rule reports a violation if a function returns a reference or a pointer
to a non-static local variable or to a parameter.

See also: MRM-23
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-a.benefits:
"Automatic variables are destroyed at the end of the function call.
Returning a reference or pointer to such a variable allows it
to be used after its destruction, leading to undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C++:2008]

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-c.title: The address of an object with thread storage duration shall not be assigned to an object with static storage duration [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-c.description:
"The address of an object becomes indeterminate when the lifetime of that object
expires. Any use of an indeterminate address results in undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012]

The rule detects when the address of an object with thread storage duration
is assigned to an object with static storage duration.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_6-c.benefits: The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_7-a.title: Flexible array members shall not be declared [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_7-a.description:
The last element of a structure with more than one named member may
have an incomplete array type; this is called a flexible array member.

"The presence of flexible array members modifies the behaviour of the sizeof
operator in ways that might not be expected by a programmer. The assignment
of a structure that contains a flexible array member to another structure
of the same type may not behave in the expected manner as it copies only those
elements up to but not including the start of the flexible array member."

See also: MISRA2004-18_1
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_7-a.example_repair: Do not use flexible array members.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 18.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 18.7 Flexible array members shall not be declared

6.7.2.1 Structure and union specifiers

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_7.title: Rule 18.7 (Required) Flexible array members shall not be declared [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_8-a.title: Variable-length arrays should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_8-a.description:
"Variable-length arrays are specified when the size of an array declared
in a block or a function prototype is not an integer constant expression.
They are typically implemented as a variable size object stored on the stack.
Their use can therefore make it impossible to determine statically the amount
of memory that must be reserved for a stack."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 18.8 with Amendment 4]

This rule detects when a local variable is declared as a variable
length array.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_8-a.benefits:
The rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.

"If the size of a variable-length array is negative or zero, the behaviour
is undefined. If a variable-length array is used in a context in which its type
is required to be compatible with the type of another array, then the size of
the array types shall be identical.
Further, all sizes shall evaluate to positive integers. If these requirements
are not met, the behaviour is undefined. If a variable-length array is used in
the operand of a sizeof operator, under some circumstances it is unspecified
whether the array size expression is evaluated or not."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 18.8 with Amendment 4]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 18.8

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_9-a.title: Do not modify objects with temporary lifetime [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_9-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_9-a.description:
"An array which is a member of a non-lvalue expression with structure
or union type shall not be used as a value, other than as the immediate
postfix-expression operand to a subscript operator. The subscript operator
shall not be used to produce a modifiable lvalue." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

"A non-lvalue expression with structure or union type, where the structure
or union contains a member with array type (including, recursively, members
of all contained structures and unions) refers to an object with automatic
storage duration and temporary lifetime. Its lifetime begins when
the expression is evaluated and its initial value is the value
of the expression. Its lifetime ends when the evaluation of the containing
full expression or full declarator ends. Any attempt to modify an object with
temporary lifetime results in undefined behavior." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

This rule detects access to an array member of a structure or union object
with a temporary lifetime that was created as a result of a function call,
or by using a conditional, assignment, or comma operator, except when it is
used as the immediate postfix-expression operand to a subscript operator
that does not produce a modifiable lvalue.
MISRAC2012-RULE_18_9-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined and erroneous program behavior.
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
6.2.4 Storage durations of objects
2. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.16 Rule 18.9
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 18.9 An object with temporary lifetime shall not undergo array-to-pointer conversion

EXP35-C. Do not modify objects with temporary lifetime

MISRAC2012-RULE_18_9.title: Rule 18.9 (Required) An object with temporary lifetime shall not undergo array-to-pointer conversion [MISRAC2012-RULE_18_9]

MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-a.title: An object shall not be assigned to an overlapping object [MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-a.description:
"The behaviour is undefined when two objects are created
which have some overlap in memory and one is copied to the other."
Rule detects if two different members of the same union are assigned to
each other using the same object.
MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-a.benefits: Rule prevents undefined behaviour and data loss.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-b.description:
"The behaviour is undefined when two objects are created
which have some overlap in memory and one is copied to the other."
Rule detects if two different members of the same union are copied to
each other using the same object.
MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-b.benefits: Rule prevents undefined behaviour and data loss.

MISRAC2012-RULE_19_1-c.security_relevance:
"The behaviour is undefined when two objects are created which have some
overlap in memory and one is assigned or copied to the other."

If enabled, the rule only triggers on calls to the memcpy function.

MISRAC2012-RULE_19_2-a.title: The union keyword should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_19_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_19_2-a.description:
"A union member can be written and the same member can then be read back
in a well-defined manner.
However, if a union member is written and then a different union member
is read back, the behaviour depends on the relative sizes of the members:
- If the member read is wider than the member written then the value
  is unspecified;
- Otherwise, the value is implementation-defined.
The Standard permits the bytes of a union member to be accessed by means
of another member whose type is array of unsigned char. However, since it is
possible to access bytes with unspecified values, unions should not be used."

The rule reports a violation on each 'union' keyword used in code.

See also: MISRA-110, MISRA2004-18_4
MISRAC2012-RULE_19_2-a.benefits: The rule prevents undefined, unspecified and implementation-defined behaviours.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-a.title: A program should not exceed the translation limits imposed by The Standard (c90) [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-a.description:
"A program shall not exceed the translation limits imposed by the
implementation. The minimum translation limits are specified
by The Standard but an implementation may provide higher limits."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 1.1]

This rule detects cases where the minimum translation limits specified
by the C90 standard are exceeded:
- 12 pointer, array, and function declarators (in any combinations) modifying
  an arithmetic, a structure, a union, or an incomplete type in a declaration
- 31 parameters in one function definition
- 31 characters in an internal identifier
- 31 characters in an external identifier (the limit specified in the standard
  is 6, but this rule was set to 31 by default)
- 127 identifiers with block scope declared in one block
- 127 enumeration constants in a single enumeration
- 32767 bytes in an object
- 31 arguments in one function call
- 257 case labels for a switch statement (excluding those for any
  nested switch statements)
- 15 nesting levels of compound statements, iteration control structures,
  and selection control structures
- 127 members in a single structure or union
- 15 levels of nested structure or union definitions in a single
  struct-declaration-list
- 511 external identifiers in one translation unit

See also: CODSTA-174_b_c90
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-a.benefits: This rule prevents undefined behaviors and improves readability of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-a.example_repair: Do not exceed translation limits of an implementation.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 1.1
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
5.2.4.1 Translation limits
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 1.1 The program shall contain no violations of the standard C syntax and constraints, and shall not exceed the implementation's translation limits

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-b.title: A program should not exceed the translation limits imposed by The Standard (c99) [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-b.description:
"A program shall not exceed the translation limits imposed by the
implementation. The minimum translation limits are specified
by The Standard but an implementation may provide higher limits."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 1.1]

This rule detects cases where the minimum translation limits specified
by the C99 standard are exceeded:
- 12 pointer, array, and function declarators (in any combinations) modifying
  an arithmetic, a structure, a union, or an incomplete type in a declaration
- 4095 external identifiers in one translation unit
- 63 characters in an internal identifier
- 31 characters in an external identifier
- 63 levels of nested structure or union definitions in a single
  struct-declaration-list
- 1023 case labels for a switch statement (excluding those for any
  nested switch statements)
- 65535 bytes in an object
- 1023 members in a single structure or union
- 1023 enumeration constants in a single enumeration
- 511 identifiers with block scope declared in one block
- 127 parameters in one function definition
- 127 arguments in one function call
- 127 nesting levels of compound statements, iteration control structures,
  and selection control structures

See also: CODSTA-174_b_c99
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-b.benefits: This rule prevents undefined behaviors and improves readability of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-b.example_repair: Do not exceed translation limits of an implementation.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 1.1
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
5.2.4.1 Translation limits
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 1.1 The program shall contain no violations of the standard C syntax and constraints, and shall not exceed the implementation's translation limits

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-c.title: A program should not exceed the translation limits imposed by The Standard (c90) [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-c.description:
"A program shall not exceed the translation limits imposed by the
implementation. The minimum translation limits are specified
by The Standard but an implementation may provide higher limits."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 1.1]

This rule detects cases where the minimum translation limits specified
by the C90 standard are exceeded:
- 31 characters in a macro name
- 1024 macro identifiers simultaneously defined in one translation unit
  (this rule reports violations if more than 1024 macros are defined
   in one file)
- 509 characters in a logical source line
- 509 characters in a character string literal or wide string literal
- 8 nesting levels of conditional inclusion
- 32 nesting levels of parenthesized expressions or declarators
  (in the C90 standard the limit for declarators is 31 nesting levels,
   but this rule checks the same limit (32) for declarators and expressions)
- 31 parameters in one macro definition
- 31 arguments in one macro invocation
  (this rule reports violations only on invocations of macros that are
  defined in the same file)

See also: CODSTA-174_a_c90
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-c.benefits: This rule prevents undefined behaviors and improves readability of code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-d.title: A program should not exceed the translation limits imposed by The Standard (c99) [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-d.description:
"A program shall not exceed the translation limits imposed by the
implementation. The minimum translation limits are specified
by The Standard but an implementation may provide higher limits."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 1.1]

This rule detects cases where the minimum translation limits specified
by the C99 standard are exceeded:
- 63 characters in a macro name
- 4095 macro identifiers simultaneously defined in one translation unit
  (this rule reports violations if more than 4095 macros are defined
   in one file)
- 4095 characters in a logical source line
- 4095 characters in a character string literal or wide string literal
- 63 nesting levels of conditional inclusion
- 63 nesting levels of parenthesized expressions or declarators
- 127 parameters in one macro definition
- 127 arguments in one macro invocation
   (this rule reports violations only on invocations of macros that are
   defined in the same file)

See also: CODSTA-174_a_c99
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-d.benefits: This rule prevents undefined behaviors and improves readability of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_1-d.example_repair: Do not exceed translation limits of an implementation.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-a.title: Avoid division by zero [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-a.description:
This rule detects execution paths where zero may potentially be used as a denominator
in a division operation. Division by zero may lead to divide-by-zero errors and should
be avoided.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-a.benefits:
Division by zero behavior is specified as undefinded in the language standard. For this
reason, it may result in divide-by-zero errors and abnormal program termination.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-a.parameters:
The "Sensitive mode" parameter allows you to enable reporting violations
when divisors are possibly equal to zero.
With this option enabled, Flow Analysis detects not only when the actual division
by zero occurs, but also when it may (but may not) occur. This results in more
violations being reported.

This option is disabled by default.
references: CWE-369: Divide By Zero

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-b.title: Avoid use before initialization [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-b.description:
Objects with static storage duration are always initialized - either explicitly or implicitly.
Objects with automatic storage duration, if not initialized explicitly, have indeterminate value.
Reading the value of such an object results in undefined behavior.

This rule reports a violation when:
 - a non-initialized object is used, or
 - a non-initialized element of an array is used, or
 - a non-initialized field of a class/struct is used, or
 - a field of a non-initialized union object is used

See also the PARAMETERS section for other situations when the rule may report violations.

For the purpose of this rule, an object is non-initialized when:
 - it is an uninitialized object of numeric, enum or union type, or
 - it is an array where all elements are non-initialized, or
 - it is a class/struct object with all non-static members non-initialized
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-b.security_relevance:
Reading the value of a non-initialized object results in undefined behavior.
Additionally, it can allow an attacker to control the behavior of the program, or to gain access to sensitive data.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-b.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-b.parameters:
The "Report when non-initialized object is passed to a third-party function as const" 
parameter allows you to specify if the rule should trigger when a non-initialized object 
is passed by pointer or reference to const to a function whose source code is not available.
Since such an object cannot be modified inside the function, it is assumed that its value will be read.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Report when non-initialized structure is copied" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a non-initialized object of class/struct/union type is:
 - used as the rhs operand of the assignment operator, or
 - passed to a function by value, or
 - returned from a function by value, or
 - thrown by value.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Report when partially-initialized object is copied" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a partially-initialized object is:
 - used as the rhs operand of the assignment operator, or
 - passed to a function by value, or
 - returned from a function by value, or
 - thrown by value.

The "Report when potentially non-initialized element of an array is used" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a potentially non-initialized element of an array is used.
This parameter is disabled by default.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-c.title: Do not use resources that have been freed [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-c.description:
This rule detects uses of resources that have been freed. In particular, a violation is reported
when a pointer or reference to a freed resource is:

   * returned from a function,
   * passed as a parameter to a function,
   * used in an arithmetic operation,
   * dereferenced, or
   * assigned to a variable/field/array element.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-c.benefits: Prevents strange, often non-deterministic errors related to the use of freed resources.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-c.parameters:
The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to null" check box
unsupresses/suppresses  violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to a null
pointer. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to other pointers" check box
unsuppresses/suppresses violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to other
pointers. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-d.title: Avoid overflow when reading from a buffer [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-d.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-d.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and it is an array
with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is enabled by default.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-f.title: The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of evaluation that the standard permits [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-f]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-f.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) and also used in another way in a statement, where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-f.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

Since the increment, decrement, or assignment operator modifies its operand,
using such an operand more than once within the same expression can produce
undefined results.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-g.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of function arguments [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-g]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-g.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) in a function call argument, and the same object is used
in another argument of the same function call, or the same volatile object
is used in different arguments of the same function call.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-g.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

The order of evaluation of function arguments is unspecified. This means that
if the arguments have side effects, the order in which those side effects occur
is also unspecified. A function call can produce different results depending
on which of the function's arguments is evaluated first.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-h.description:
This rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) and also used in another way in a function designator and function
arguments in a function call.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-h.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"If a function is called via a function pointer there shall be no dependence
on the order in which function designator and function arguments are evaluated."
[MISRA-C:2004]

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-i.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of expressions involving a function call [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-i]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-i.description:
This rule detects when an object is passed to a function call by a pointer
or a reference, modified (incremented, decremented, or assigned) in that
function, and also used in another way in a statement where the order
of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-i.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"Functions may have additional effects when they are called (e.g. modifying some
global data). Dependence on order of evaluation could be avoided by invoking the
function prior to the expression that uses it, making use of a temporary
variable for the value." [MISRA-C:2004]

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-j.title: Between sequence points an object shall have its stored value modified at most once by the evaluation of an expression [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-j]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-j.description:
The rule detects when an object is modified (incremented, decremented,
or assigned) more than once between sequence points.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-j.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A5-0-1. The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of
evaluation that the standard permits
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
5.1.2. Do not rely on the sequence of evaluation within an expression
3.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-k.title: Do not use more than one volatile between two adjacent sequence points [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-k]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-k.description:
This rule detects when there is more than one modification access or more than
one read access to a volatile-qualified type between two sequence points.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-k.benefits:
This rule helps you ensure that expressions are written in a manner that
consistently produces the same value.

"The volatile type qualifier is provided in C to denote objects whose value can
change independently of the execution of the program (for example an input
register). If an object of volatile qualified type is accessed this may change
its value. C compilers will not optimise out reads of a volatile. In addition,
as far as a C program is concerned, a read of a volatile has a side effect
(changing the value of the volatile). It will usually be necessary to access
volatile data as part of an expression, which then means there may be dependence
on order of evaluation. Where possible though it is recommended that volatiles
only be accessed in simple assignment statements." [MISRA-C:2004]
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A5-0-1. The value of an expression shall be the same under any order of
evaluation that the standard permits

5.1.2. Do not rely on the sequence of evaluation within an expression

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-l.title: Avoid code that depends on the order of evaluation of function calls [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-l]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-l.description:
This rule detects when two function calls are used in a statement where the
order of evaluation of sub-expressions is unspecified, and the called functions
use the same global or static object with least one of them modifying it.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-l.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior and inconsistent return values
across different program states.

"Functions may have additional effects when they are called (e.g. modifying some
global data). Dependence on order of evaluation could be avoided by invoking the
function prior to the expression that uses it, making use of a temporary
variable for the value." [MISRA-C:2004]

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-m.title: The address of an object with automatic storage shall not be returned from a function [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-m]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-m.description:
"If the address of an automatic object is returned from a function, then
the object containing the address may exist beyond the time when the original
object ceases to exist (and its address becomes invalid)." [MISRA C:2004]

The rule reports a violation if a function returns a reference or a pointer
to a non-static local variable or to a parameter.

See also: MRM-23
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-m.benefits:
"Automatic variables are destroyed at the end of the function call.
Returning a reference or pointer to such a variable allows it
to be used after its destruction, leading to undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C++:2008]

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-n.title: The address of an object with automatic storage shall not be assigned to another object that may persist after the first object has ceased to exist [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-n]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-n.description:
"If the address of an automatic object is assigned to another automatic object
of larger scope, or to a static object then the object containing the address

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-o.title: The left-hand operand of a right-shift operator shall not have a negative value [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-o]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-o.description:
The rule reports a violation message if as left-hand operand of right-shift
operator is used one from the following options:
- constant with a negative value
- unary operator '-' followed by parameter, variable or expression
- parameter or variable of signed type
- expression of signed type that contains parameter, variable, dereferenced
  pointer or function call of signed type
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_3-o.benefits:
Rule prevents implementation-defined behaviours.
"For e1 >> e2, if e1 has a signed type and a negative value, the value
of (e1 >> e2) is implementation-defined."
references: 1. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.21 Operators, AV Rule 164.1
2. General Principles of Software Validation;
Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff
Document issued on: January 11, 2002

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-j.title: The '__STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__' macro should not be defined to the value other than '0' [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-j]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-j.description:
"Updates to the C Standard have introduced new language features. . .
- Other than defining __STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__ to '0', the facilities of Annex K
 (Bounds-checking interfaces) shall not be used." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

"The functions, macros, and types declared or defined in K.3 and its subclauses
are declared and defined by their respective headers if __STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__
is defined as a macro which expands to the integer constant 1 at the point
in the source file where the appropriate header is first included."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

The rule detects when the '__STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__' macro is defined
to a value other than '0'.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-j.benefits:
"Use of the language features restricted by this rule may have instances
of undefined, unspecified or implementation-defined behaviour associated
with them. In addition, features may also exhibit well defined behaviour
that does not meet developer expectations." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-j.example_repair:
You can fix your code by defining the '__STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__' macro to 0.

#define  __STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__ 0              /* OK */

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-k.description:
"Updates to the C Standard have introduced new language features. The following
shall not be used:
- Other than defining __STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__ to '0', the facilities of Annex K
(Boundschecking interfaces) shall not be used." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

"1 The header <stddef.h> defines a type.
 2 The type is rsize_t which is the type size_t."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011, Annex K]

This rule detects when the 'rsize_t' type is used in code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-k.benefits:
"Use of the language features restricted by this rule may have instances
of undefined, unspecified or implementation-defined behaviour associated

that does not meet developer expectations." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

 2 The type is errno_t which is the type int."

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_4-m.description:
"Updates to the C Standard have introduced new language features. The following
shall not be used:
- Other than defining __STDC_WANT_LIB_EXT1__ to '0', the facilities of Annex K
(Boundschecking interfaces) shall not be used." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

The Annex K specifies following macros:
- 'RSIZE_MAX' defined the <stdint.h> header

This rule detects when 'RSIZE_MAX', 'L_tmpnam_s' and 'TMP_MAX_S' identifiers
are used in the code.

"Use of the language features restricted by this rule may have instances

that does not meet developer expectations." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-a.title: The static storage class specifier shall be used in definitions and declarations of objects and functions that have internal linkage [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-a.description:
The C standard specifies that if an identifier declared with the storage-class
specifier 'extern' in a scope in which a prior declaration of that identifier
is visible, the linkage of that identifier is the same as the linkage of the
prior declaration.

For example, if a function is first declared as 'static' and then defined with
'extern' or without any specifier at all, the linkage will be internal. This
can be confusing: if you see only the definition, you might wrongly assume
that the function has external linkage.

It is a good practice to apply the 'static' storage class specifier to all
objects and functions with internal linkage.

See also: CODSTA-81
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-a.benefits:
Mitigates the risk of incorrectly interpreting linkage of objects and
functions.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-b.title: Storage type modifiers shall be associated with the type, not the variable or the function [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-b.description:
"The placement of a storage-class specifier other than at the beginning
of the declaration specifiers in a declaration is an obsolescent feature."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

The C/C++ Standard allows any order of specifiers in a declaration.
If a specifier (auto, extern, static, register) appears in a declaration,
it should not be placed directly before variable or function name.

This rule detects when one from the following specifiers: 'auto', 'extern',
'static', 'register' is placed directly before a name in the variable
or the function declaration.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-b.benefits: This rule improves the readability of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-b.example_violation:
int static i;       // Violation
void extern f1();   // Violation
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-b.example_repair:
static int i;       // OK
extern void f1();   // OK

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-c.title: Function types shall be in prototype form [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-c.description:
This rule checks if a function types are in prototype form.
It reports a violations if a declaration of a function type:
- is in K&R style, or
- has an empty parameter list

See also: MISRA2004-16_3, CODSTA-158
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-c.benefits:
"The mismatch between the number of arguments and parameters, their types
and the expected and actual return type of a function provides potential
for undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012]

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-d.title: Do not use the macro ATOMIC_VAR_INIT [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-d.description:
"The macro ATOMIC_VAR_INIT is an obsolescent feature."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2017]

This rule detects when the 'ATOMIC_VAR_INIT' identifier is used in the code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-d.benefits:
"Features are declared as obsolescent by the C Standard when they are superseded
by safer or better alternatives, or are considered to exhibit undesirable
behaviour. Features declared as obsolescent by a particular version of the C
Standard may be withdrawn in a later version" [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-d.example_repair: Do not use the ATOMIC_VAR_INIT macro.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-e.title: Do not use the 'bool', 'false' or 'true' identifiers in the #undef directive [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-e]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-e.description:
"The ability to undefine and perhaps then redefine the macros bool, true,
and false is an obsolescent feature." [ISO/IEC 9899:2017]

This rule detects when 'bool', 'false' or 'true' is used as an identifier
in the #undef directive.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-e.benefits:
"Features are declared as obsolescent by the C Standard when they are superseded
by safer or better alternatives, or are considered to exhibit undesirable
behaviour. Features declared as obsolescent by a particular version of the C
Standard may be withdrawn in a later version" [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-e.example_repair: Do not use the 'bool', 'false' or 'true' identifiers in the #undef directive.

MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-g.title: Do not pass 0 value as a size argument to the realloc function [MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-g]
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-g.description:
"Invoking realloc with a size argument equal to zero is an obsolescent feature."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2017]

"Obsolescent language features shall not be used."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects cases when zero value is passed to the realloc function 
as a size parameter.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-g.security_relevance:
Passing zero value as a size parameter to the realloc function is an
obsolescent feature and may cause different implementation-defined behaviors.
MISRAC2012-RULE_1_5-g.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent obsolescent realloc function usage which leads to 
implementation-defined results.

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_1-a.title: #include statements in a file should only be preceded by other preprocessor directives or comments [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_1-a.description:
This rule detects when an #include directive in a file is preceded by code
other than preprocessor directives.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_1-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid situations where executable code is used before
the #include directive, which can result in unexpected or undefined behavior.

It is recommended to have all the #include directives grouped together near
the head of the file.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

preprocessor directives or comments

systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-1

Rule 19.0.3 #include directives should only be preceded by preprocessor
directives or comments
6. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.1

Rule 20.1 #include directives should only be preceded by preprocessor
directives or comments

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10-a.title: The # and ## preprocessor operators should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10-a.description:
The order of evaluation associated with multiple #, multiple ## or a mix
of # and ## preprocessor operators is unspecified. In some cases it is
therefore not possible to predict the result of macro expansion.
The use of the ## operator can result in code that is obscure.
Compilers have been inconsistent in the implementation of these operators.

The rule reports a violation if # or ## preprocessor operators are used.

See also: PREPROC-16
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10-a.benefits: Rule prevents unspecified behavior caused by the use of # and ## operators.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10-a.example_violation:
#define stringer( x ) printf( #x "\n" ) /* Violation */
#define concat( x, y ) ( x##y = 0)      /* Violation */
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10-a.example_repair: Do not use # and ## preprocessor operators.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-3-2. The # and ## operators should not be used
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.8.3.2 The # operator, 6.8.3.3 The ## operator
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.10.3.2 The # operator, 6.10.3.3 The ## operator
4. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-3-2
5. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.3.1 The # and ## preprocessor operators should not be used
6. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.10
7. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.10 The # and ## preprocessor operators should not be used
