MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10.title: Rule 20.10 (Advisory) The # and ## preprocessor operators should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_10]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_11-a.title: A macro parameter immediately following a # operator shall not immediately be followed by a ## operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_11-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_11-a.description:
"The order of evaluation associated with multiple #, multiple ## or a mix
of # and ## preprocessor operators is unspecified. In particular, the result of
a # operator is a string literal and it is extremely unlikely that pasting this
to any other preprocessing token will result in a valid token."

This rule detects macro parameters immediately following a # operator that are
immediately followed by a ## operator.

See also: MISRA2004-19_13
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_11-a.example_repair:
#define M1( x )    #x              /* OK */
#define M( x, y )  M1(x) ## y      /* OK */
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.8.3.2 The # operator
2. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.10.3.2 The # operator
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.3.2 A macro parameter immediately following a # operator shall not be immediately followed by a ## operator
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.11
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.11 A macro parameter immediately following a # operator shall not immediately be followed by a ## operator

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_11.title: Rule 20.11 (Required) A macro parameter immediately following a # operator shall not immediately be followed by a ## operator [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_11]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_12-a.title: A macro parameter used as an operand to the # or ## operators, which is itself subject to further macro replacement, shall only be used as an operand to these operators [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_12-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_12-a.description:
"A macro parameter that is used as an operand of a # or ## operator is not
expanded prior to being used. The same parameter appearing elsewhere in the
replacement text is expanded. If the macro parameter is itself subject to macro
replacement, its use in mixed contexts within a macro replacement may not meet
developer expectations." [MISRA C:2012, Rule 20.12]

The rule scans all files in current translation unit. First it finds all #define
directives and collects following data:
  1) defined identifiers
  2) defined identifiers of function-like macros with parameters which are used
     as operands of "#" or "##" and elsewhere in the replacement text;
     information which parameters are used in such way is also collected

Next, the translation unit files are scanned again. If a function-like call
of identifier from step #2 is found, and an identifier from step #1 is passed
as part of parameter collected in step #2, a violation is reported.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_12-a.benefits:
Prevents confusion caused by using both resolved and not resolved macro
identifiers.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.3.3 The argument to a mixed-use macro parameter shall not be subject to further expansion
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.12
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.12 A macro parameter used as an operand to the # or ## operators, which is itself subject to further macro replacement, shall only be used as an operand to these operators

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_12.title: Rule 20.12 (Required) A macro parameter used as an operand to the # or ## operators, which is itself subject to further macro replacement, shall only be used as an operand to these operators [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_12]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_13-a.title: Preprocessing directives shall be syntactically meaningful even when excluded by the preprocessor [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_13-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_13-a.description:
"When a section of source code is excluded by preprocessor directives,
the content of each excluded statement is ignored until a #else, #elif
or #endif directive is encountered (depending on the context).
If one of these excluded directives is badly formed, it may be ignored
without warning by a compiler with unfortunate consequences.
The requirement of this rule is that all preprocessor directives shall be
syntactically valid even when they occur within an excluded block of code.
Compilers are not always consistent in enforcing this ISO requirement."
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-0-8. If the # token appears as the first token on a line, then it shall
be immediately followed by a pre-processing token
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-8
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.0.1 A line whose first token is # shall be a valid preprocessing directive
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.13
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.13 A line whose first token is # shall be a valid preprocessing directive
6. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19, Rule 19.16

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_13.title: Rule 20.13 (Required) A line whose first token is # shall be a valid preprocessing directive [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_13]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_14-a.title: All #else, #elif and #endif preprocessor directives shall reside in the same file as the #if or #ifdef directive to which they are related [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_14-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_14-a.description:
"When the inclusion and exclusion of blocks of statements is controlled
by a series of preprocessor directives, confusion can arise
if all of the relevant directives do not occur within one file."
This rule requires that the number of preprocessor directives #if/ifdef/ifndef
matches the number of #endif directives in the file.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_14-a.benefits: Rule improves good code structure and prevents maintenance problems.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-1-2. All #else, #elif and #endif pre-processor directives shall reside
in the same file as the #if or #ifdef directive to which they are related
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-1-2
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.1.2 All #else, #elif and #endif preprocessor directives shall reside in the same file as the #if, #ifdef or #ifndef directive to which they are related
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.14
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.14 All #else, #elif and #endif preprocessor directives shall reside in the same file as the #if, #ifdef or #ifndef directive to which they are related
6. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19, Rule 19.17

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_2-a.title: The ', ", /* or // characters shall not occur in a header file name [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_2-a.description:
"It is undefined behaviour if the ', ", /* or // characters are used
between < and > delimiters or the ', /* or // characters are used
between the " delimiters in a header name preprocessing token."

See also: NAMING-48, MISRA2004-19_2

Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-2-4

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_2-b.title: The \ character should not occur in a header file name [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_2-b.description:
"It is undefined behaviour if the \ character is used between < and >
delimiters or between the " delimiters in a header name preprocessing token."

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_3-a.title: The #include directive shall be followed by either a <filename> or "filename" sequence [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_3-a.description:
"The #include directive shall be followed by either a <filename>
or "filename" sequence."

See also: PREPROC-09

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_3-a.example_violation:
/* Following statement is not compliant and may not compile */

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-a.title: A macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword in C90 [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-a.description:
"Using macros to change the meaning of keywords can be confusing. The behaviour
is undefined if a standard header is included while a macro is defined with the
same name as a keyword." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 20.4]

"The program shall not have any macros with names lexically identical
to keywords currently defined prior to the inclusion." [ISO/IEC 9899:1990]

The rule reports a violation if the name of defined macro is the same
as the keyword defined in C90 standard.

Following keywords are recognized:
  auto  double  int  struct  break  else  long  switch  case  enum  register
  typedef  char  extern  return  union  const  float  short  unsigned  continue
  for  signed  void  default  goto  sizeof  volatile  do  if  static  while
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-a.example_violation:
/* The following example is non-compliant because it alters the behaviour
 * of the 'int' keyword. Including a standard header in the presence of this
 * macro results in undefined behaviour. */

#define int some_other_type          /* Violation */
#include <stdlib.h>

/* The following example shows that it is non-compliant to redefine the keyword
 * 'while' but it is compliant to define a macro that expands to statements. */

#define while( E ) for ( ; ( E ) ; ) /* Violation */
#define unless( E ) if ( ! ( E ) )   /* OK */
#define seq( S1, S2 ) do { \
    S1; S2; } while ( false )        /* OK */
#define compound( S ) { S; }         /* OK */
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-a.example_repair: Do not define a macro with the same name as a keyword.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.4 A macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.1.1 Keywords, 7.1.2 Standard headers

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-b.title: A macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword in C99 [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-b.description:
"Using macros to change the meaning of keywords can be confusing. The behaviour
is undefined if a standard header is included while a macro is defined with the
same name as a keyword." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 20.4]

"The program shall not have any macros with names lexically identical
to keywords currently defined prior to the inclusion." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

The rule reports a violation if the name of defined macro is the same
as the keyword defined in C99 standard.

Following keywords are recognized:
  auto  break  case  char  const  continue  default  do  double  else  enum
  extern  float  for  goto  if  inline  int  long  register  restrict  return
  short  signed  sizeof  static  struct  switch  typedef  union  unsigned  void
  volatile  while  _Bool  _Complex  _Imaginary
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-b.example_violation:
/* The following example is non-compliant because it alters the behaviour
 * of the 'int' keyword. Including a standard header in the presence of this
 * macro results in undefined behaviour. */

#define int some_other_type          /* Violation */
#include <stdlib.h>

/* The following example shows that it is non-compliant to redefine the keyword
 * 'while' but it is compliant to define a macro that expands to statements. */

#define while( E ) for ( ; ( E ) ; ) /* Violation */
#define unless( E ) if ( ! ( E ) )   /* OK */
#define seq( S1, S2 ) do { \
    S1; S2; } while ( false )        /* OK */
#define compound( S ) { S; }         /* OK */

#define inline                       /* Violation */
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4-b.example_repair: Do not define a macro with the same name as a keyword.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.4 A macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.4.1 Keywords, 7.1.2 Standard headers

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4.title: Rule 20.4 (Required) A macro shall not be defined with the same name as a keyword [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_5-a.title: #undef shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_5-a.description:
"#undef should not normally be needed. Its use can lead to confusion with
respect to the existence or meaning of a macro when it is used in the code."
[MISRA-C:2004]

This rule detects when the #undef directive is used in code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_5-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve code readability and maintainability.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_5-a.example_repair: You can fix your code by removing the #undef directive.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A16-0-1. The pre-processor shall only be used for unconditional and
conditional file inclusion and include guards, and using the following
directives: (1) #ifndef, (2) #ifdef, (3) #if, (4) #if defined, (5) #elif,
(6) #else, (7) #define, (8) #endif, (9) #include
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
16.1.1. Use the preprocessor only for implementing include guards, and
including header files with include guards

6.  MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.5
7.  MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.5 #undef should not be used

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_6-a.title: Arguments to a function-like macro shall not contain tokens that look like preprocessing directives [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_6-a.description:
"Tokens that look like a preprocessing directive shall not occur within a macro
argument. An argument containing sequences of tokens that would otherwise act
as preprocessing directives leads to undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012, Rule 20.6]

"A preprocessing directive consists of a sequence of preprocessing tokens that
begins with a # preprocessing token that is either the first character in the
source file (optionally after white space containing no new-line characters)
or that follows white space containing at least one new-line character,
and is ended by the next new-line character." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-0-5. Arguments to a function-like macro shall not contain tokens that
look like pre-processing directives
2.  ISO/IEC 9899:1990 Programming languages -- C
6.8 Preprocessing directives
3.  ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.10 Preprocessing directives
4.  MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-5
5.  MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.3.5 Tokens that look like a preprocessing directive shall not occur within a macro argument
6.  MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_6.title: Rule 20.6 (Required) Tokens that look like a preprocessing directive shall not occur within a macro argument [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_7-a.title: In the definition of a function-like macro each instance of a parameter shall be enclosed in parentheses unless it is used as the operand of # or ## [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_7-a.description:
"In the definition of a function-like macro each instance of a parameter shall
be enclosed in parentheses. If this rule is not adhered to then when
the preprocessor substitutes the macro into the code the operator precedence
may not give the desired results." [MISRA C:2004 Rule 19.10]

See also: MISRA-096
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_7-a.benefits:
"If parentheses are not used, then operator precedence may not give the desired
results when macro substitution occurs." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 20.7]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-0-6. In the definition of a function-like macro, each instance of a
parameter shall be enclosed in parentheses, unless it is used as the
operand of # or ##
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-6
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.3.4 Parentheses shall be used to ensure macro arguments are expanded appropriately
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.7
5. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19, Rule 19.10
6. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 96
7. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
PRE01-C. Use parentheses within macros around parameter names

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_7.title: Rule 20.7 (Required) Expressions resulting from the expansion of macro parameters shall be enclosed in parentheses [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8-a.title: The controlling expression of a #if or #elif preprocessing directive shall evaluate to 0 or 1 [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8-a.description:
"The controlling expression of a #if or #elif preprocessing directive shall
evaluate to 0 or 1" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 20.8]

This rule detects only cases when the controlling expression of #if or #elif
a) does not evaluate to 0 or 1, and b)contains one from the following:
- a single positive or negative integer constant,
- a single macro identifier that expands to a single positive or negative
  integer constant,
- a simple arithmetic expression that contains exactly one operator (+,-,/,
  or *) and has two operands (a single integer constant or a macro that expands
  to a single integer constant)
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8-a.benefits:
"Strong typing requires the controlling expression of conditional inclusion
preprocessing directives to have a Boolean value." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 20.8]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8-a.example_violation:
#if    2                         /* Violation */
#elif -1                         /* Violation */
#endif

#define MACRO_10 10
#if MACRO_10                     /* Violation */
#endif

#if    1 + 2                     /* Violation */
#elif  5 - 3                     /* Violation */
#endif

#define MACRO_1 1
#define MACRO_2 2

#if    1 + MACRO_1               /* Violation */
#elif  MACRO_1 * MACRO_2         /* Violation */
#endif
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8-a.example_repair:
/* Example of compliant code */

#if    0                         /* OK */
#elif  1                         /* OK */
#endif

#define MACRO_1 1
#if MACRO_1                      /* OK */
#endif

#if    2 - 1                     /* OK */
#elif  0 + 1                     /* OK */
#endif

#define MACRO_1 1
#define MACRO_2 2

#if    MACRO_2 - 1               /* OK */
#elif  MACRO_1 < MACRO_2         /* OK */
#endif
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.8
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.8 The controlling expression of a #if or #elif preprocessing directive shall evaluate to 0 or 1

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8.title: Rule 20.8 (Required) The controlling expression of a #if or #elif preprocessing directive shall evaluate to 0 or 1 [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_8]

MISRAC2012-RULE_20_9-b.title: Do not use in preprocessor directives #if and #elif macros not defined in translation unit [MISRAC2012-RULE_20_9-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_9-b.description:
"If an attempt is made to use an identifier in a preprocessor directive,
and that identifier has not been defined, the preprocessor will sometimes
not give any warning but will assume the value zero.
'#ifdef', '#ifndef' and 'defined()' are provided to test the existence
of a macro, and are therefore excluded.

Note that preprocessing identifiers may be defined either by use of #define
directives or by options specified at compiler invocation. However the use
of the #define directive is preferred." [MISRA C:2004 Rule 19.11]

The violation is reported by the rule if the macro identifier is used
in #if/#elif directive (except as operands to the 'defined' operator)
and it is possible the macro is not defined.

No violation is reported if in other files in current translation unit there is
a #define for that macro or a check if the macro is defined or not.

Please see Notes section for detailed description of the above algorithm.

See also: MISRA2004-19_11
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_9-b.benefits:
The rule prevents using undefined macros in #if and #elif directives, which may
be undetected if the preprocessor does not give any warnings.
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_9-b.example_violation:
#if M > 0        /* Violation - macro was not defined */
#endif

#define M 1
#undef M
#if M > 0        /* Violation - macro was undefined before the use */
#endif

#ifdef X
  #define M 1
#endif
#if M > 0        /* Violation - macro was defined conditionally */
#endif

#if defined(M) && defined(N)  /* Logical expressions are not fully supported */
  #if M > 0                   /* Violation */
  #endif
#endif

#if defined(Q)
  #define M 1
#elif defined(R)
  #define M 2
#endif
#if M > 1   /* Violation - macro was defined conditionally */
#endif
MISRAC2012-RULE_20_9-b.example_repair:
#define M1 1

#if M1 > 0        /* OK - macro was defined */
#endif

#undef M1

#if defined(M1) && M1 > 0  /* OK - macro is used only if it was defined */
#endif

#ifdef M1
  #if M1 > 0      /* OK - macro is used only if it was defined */
  #endif
#endif

#ifndef M1
//...
#else
  #if M1 > 0      /* OK - macro is used only if it was defined */
  #endif
#endif

#if defined(Q)
  #define M1 1
#elif defined(R)
  #define M1 2
#else
  #define M1 3
#endif
#if M1 > 1   /* OK - macro was defined unconditionally */
#endif

#ifndef M2
#error M2 is not defined
#endif
#if M2 > 0 /* OK - #error directive breaks preprocessing if M2 is not defined */
#endif

#if !defined(M3)
#define M3 1
#endif
#if M3 > 1   /* OK - M3 must be defined */
#endif

/* header.h: */
#define M4 1
#ifndef M5
#if defined(M6)
#endif
#endif
/* source.c: */
#include "header.h"
#if M4 > 1  /* OK - there is #define M4 in header.h */
#endif
#if M5 > 1  /* OK - there is #ifndef M5 in  header.h */
#endif
#if M6 > 1  /* OK - there is #if defined(M6) in header.h */
#endif
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M16-0-7. Undefined macro identifiers shall not be used in #if or #elif pre-
processor directives, except as operands to the defined operator
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 16, Rule 16-0-7
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 19.1.3 All identifiers used in the controlling expression of #if or #elif preprocessing directives shall be defined prior to evaluation
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 20.9
5. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.9 All identifiers used in the controlling expression of #if or #elif preprocessing directives shall be #define'd before evaluation
6. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 19
7. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 97

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_1-a.title: Do not #define or #undef identifiers with names which start with underscore [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_1-a.description:
"It is generally bad practice to #undef a macro which is defined in the
standard library. It is also bad practice to #define a macro name which
is a C reserved identifier or C keyword which is the name of any macro,
object or function in the standard library.
For example, there are some specific reserved words and function names
which are known to give rise to undefined behaviour if they are redefined or
undefined, including defined, _ _LINE_ _, _ _FILE_ _, _ _DATE_ _, _ _TIME_ _,
_ _STDC_ _, errno and assert. Generally, all identifiers that begin with
the underscore character are reserved." [1]

This rule checks if the following reserved names are #define'd or #undef'ined:
- identifiers that begin with the underscore character

See also: MISRA2004-20_1_b, MISRA2004-20_1_e, CODSTA-92, CODSTA-92_a,
          CODSTA-92_b
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A17-0-1. Reserved identifiers, macros and functions in the C++ standard
library shall not be defined, redefined or undefined
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 17, Rule 17-0-1
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 21.1
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 20.15 #define and #undef shall not be used on a reserved identifier or reserved macro name
5. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 20

_ _STDC_ _, errno and assert. Generally, all identifiers that begin with
the underscore character are reserved." [1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_10-a.title: The standard header files <time.h> or <ctime> shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_10-a.description:
"The standard header file <time.h> shall not be #included, and none of the
features that are specified as being provided by <time.h> shall be used.

The time and date functions have unspecified, undefined
and implementation-defined behaviours associated with them."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 21.10 with Amendment 3]

The rule detects when the <time.h> or <ctime> header is included.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_10-a.benefits:
The rule helps you prevent unspecified, undefined and implementation-defined
behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_12-a.title: The features provided by <fenv.h> should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_12-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_12-a.description:
"The standard header file <fenv.h> shall not be #included, and none of the
features that are specified as being provided by <fenv.h> shall be used.
Calling the fesetenv or feupdateenv functions with invalid arguments results
in undefined behaviour.
Calling the 'fesetround' function should be done with care because:
1. Setting the rounding mode may have unexpected consequences, e.g., setting
   the current rounding mode to upwards does not guarantee that the result
   of evaluating an expression is an upward approximation to the value
   of the expression over the reals.
2. Several implementations of functions declared in <math.h> have been designed
   to support round-to-nearest only: if such functions are called when
   a different rounding mode is set, the results can be unpredictable."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 21.12 with Amendment 3]

The rule detects use of the following global functions:
- feclearexcept
- fegetenv
- fegetexceptflag
- fegetround
- feholdexcept
- feraiseexcept
- fesetenv
- fesetexceptflag
- fesetround
- fetestexcept
- feupdateenv

The rule detects the following identifiers:
- FE_ALL_EXCEPT
- FE_DFL_ENV
- FE_DIVBYZERO
- FE_DOWNWARD
- FE_INEXACT
- FE_INVALID
- FE_OVERFLOW
- FE_TONEAREST
- FE_TOWARDZERO
- FE_UNDERFLOW
- FE_UPWARD
- FENV_ACCESS
- fenv_t
- fexcept_t
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_12-a.benefits:
"In some circumstances, the values of the floating-point status flags
are unspecified and attempts to access them may lead to undefined behaviour.
The order in which exceptions are raised by the 'feraiseexcept' function
is unspecified and could therefore result in a program that has been designed
for a certain order not operating correctly." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 21.12]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13-a.title: Do not pass incorrect values to ctype.h library functions [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13-a.security_relevance:
The use of any other values than in the range of the unsigned char type or
a negative value equivalent to EOF results in undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent API violations caused by passing an unexpected
parameter to a function. Such violations could cause runtime errors.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13-a.parameters:
If the "Use heuristics to detect calls to functions from the ctype.h header" parameter
is enabled, the rule will use heuristics to determine whether the code contains any calls
to functions from the <ctype.h> header.

The "Sensitive mode" parameter allows you to enable reporting violations
when the value passed to a <ctype.h> library function may possibly be incorrect.
With this option enabled, the rule triggers when it detects incorrect values,
as well as values that may (but may not) be incorrect. This results in more violations
being reported.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.13
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.13 Any value passed to a function in <ctype.h> shall be representable as an unsigned char or be the value EOF
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
STR37-C. Arguments to character-handling functions must be representable as an unsigned char
AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
Rule A21-8-1 Arguments to character-handling functions shall be representable as an unsigned char

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13.title: Rule 21.13 (Mandatory) Any value passed to a function in <ctype.h> shall be representable as an unsigned char or be the value EOF [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_13]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14-a.title: The Standard Library function memcmp shall not be used to compare null terminated strings [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14-a.description:
This rule detects when the Standard Library function 'memcmp' is used
to compare null terminated strings.
This rule assumes that "null terminated strings" are:
- string literals,
- arrays of char type that contain a null character.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14-a.security_relevance:
"If memcmp is used to compare two strings and the length of either is less 
than n, then they may compare as different even when they are logically the same 
(i.e. each has the same sequence of characters before the null terminator) as the 
characters after a null terminator will be included in the comparison even though 
they do not form part of the string." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.14]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid unexpected failures at runtime caused by passing 
an unexpected parameter to the memcmp function. Such violation could cause
runtime errors.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.14
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.14 The Standard Library function memcmp shall not be used to compare null terminated strings

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14.title: Rule 21.14 (Required) The Standard Library function memcmp shall not be used to compare null terminated strings [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_14]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_15-a.title: The pointer arguments to the Standard Library functions 'memcmp', 'memmove' and 'memcmp' shall be pointers to qualified or unqualified versions of compatible types [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_15-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_15-a.description:
"The standard Library functions 'memcpy, 'memmove' and memcmp perform a byte by
byte copy, move or comparison of the first n bytes of the two objects pointed at
by s1 and s2." (s1 and s2 are first and second parameter of these functions)
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.15]

This rule detects cases when types of the first and the second argument passed
to functions memcpy, memmove or memcmp are pointers to incompatible types
(ignoring cv-qualifiers).
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_15-a.benefits:
This rule prevents possible mistakes.
"An attempt to call of these functions ('memcpy', 'memmove' or 'memcmp')
with arguments which are pointers to different types may indicate a mistake"
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.15].
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.15

6.2.7 Compatible type and composite type
6.7.5.1 Pointer declarators

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_15.title: Rule 21.15 (Required) The pointer arguments to the Standard Library functions memcpy, memmove and memcmp shall be pointers to qualified or unqualified versions of compatible types [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_15]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_16-a.title: The pointer arguments to the Standard Library function 'memcmp' shall point to either a pointer type, an essentially signed type, an essentially unsigned type, an essentially Boolean type or an essentially enum type [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_16-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_16-a.description:
This rule detects cases when an argument passed to the 'memcmp()' function
is a pointer to one of the following types:
- essentially character,
- essentially floating,
- struct/union,
- function,
- void.

The term "essential type" is defined by the MISRA C:2012 standard.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_16-a.benefits:
The Standard Library function 'memcmp' performs a byte by byte comparison
of the first n bytes of the two objects pointed at by the first and the second
argument. This may cause the following problems:
- Structures may be not equal, even when their members hold the same values.
  They may contain padding with an indeterminate value between their members
  and 'memcmp' will include this in its comparison. It cannot be assumed that
  the padding will be equal, even when the values of the structure members are
  the same.
- Unions have similar concerns along with the added complication that they may
  incorrectly be reported as having the same value when the representation
  of different, overlapping members are coincidentally the same.
- The same values of objects with essentially floating type may be stored using
  different representations.
- Essentially char arrays are most frequently used to store character string,
  an attempt to compare such arrays using 'memcmp' may indicate an error
  as the number of characters to be compared will be determined by the value
  of the 'size_t' argument rather than the location of the null characters
  used to terminate the strings. The result may therefore depend on the
  comparison of characters which are not part of the respective strings.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 21.16
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

Rule 21.16 The pointer arguments to the Standard Library function memcmp shall point to either a pointer type, an essentially signed type, an essentially unsigned type, an essentially Boolean type or an essentially enum type

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_16.title: Rule 21.16 (Required) The pointer arguments to the Standard Library function memcmp shall point to either a pointer type, an essentially signed type, an essentially unsigned type, an essentially Boolean type or an essentially enum type [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_16]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-a.title: Avoid overflow due to reading a not zero terminated string [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-a.description:
The rule detects cases of possible overflow due to running a string function on a string that
was not properly zero terminated.

The rule will report violation when the parameter passed to certain functions(see PARAMETERS section)
is not zero terminated. There are cases when such behavior may not lead to overflow or undefined
behavior(e.g. using 'snprintf' function with specific format and correct maximum number of bytes).
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-a.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
references: CWE-665: Improper Initialization

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-b.title: Avoid overflow when writing to a buffer [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-b.security_relevance:
The possibility of buffer overflow is a severe security threat.
If an application has a vulnerability of this kind, it can be exploited to
execute arbitrary code and gain full control over the application.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_17-b.parameters:
The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is the last field of the structure and it is an array
with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the buffer that is accessed out of bounds is a field of the structure, but the access points to
a location within the structure.

This option is enabled by default.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_18-a.title: The size_t argument passed to any function in string.h shall have an appropriate value [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_18-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_18-a.security_relevance:
"Incorrect use of a function listed above may result in a read or write access
beyond the bounds of an object passed as a parameter, resulting in undefined
behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_18-a.benefits:
This rule helps prevent API violations caused by incorrect use of a function.
Such violations could cause runtime errors.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_18-a.parameters:
If the "Report violations when the zero-terminated string object is safely
used" option is enabled, the rule will also trigger when the "num" argument
is greater than the size of the zero-terminated string object passed
as one of the following:
    * any pointer parameter to the 'strncmp' function
    * a source pointer parameter to the 'strncat' or 'strncpy' function

The "Report violations when access within the bounds of the structure is out of the array bounds"
parameter enables reporting violations in two cases:
- the pointer parameter points out of bounds of the object which is the last field of the structure
and which also is an array with size declared to have a length of 0 or 1,
- the pointer parameter points out of bounds of the object which is an array field of the structure,
but still points to a location within the structure.
This option is disabled by default.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_19-a.title: The pointers returned by the Standard Library functions 'localeconv', 'getenv', 'setlocale' or, 'strerror' shall only be used as if they have pointer to const-qualified type [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_19-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_19-a.description:
"The 'localeconv' function returns a pointer of type 'struct lconv*'. This
pointer shall be regarded as if it had type 'const struct lconv*'.
A 'struct lconv' object includes pointers of type 'char*' and the 'getenv',
'setlocale', and 'strerror' functions each return a pointer of type 'char*'.
These pointers are used to access strings (null terminated arrays of type char).
For the purpose of this rule, these pointers shall be regarded as if they had
type 'const char*'

The Standard states that undefined behaviour occurs if a program modifies:
- The structure pointed to by the value returned by 'localeconv';
- The strings returned by 'getenv', 'setlocale' or 'strerror'.

The Standard does not specify the behaviour that results if the strings
referenced by the structure pointed to by the value returned by 'localeconv'
are modified. This rule prohibits any changes to these strings as they are
considered to be undesirable." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]

This rule detects the following cases:
- the pointers returned by the functions 'localeconv', 'getenv', 'setlocale'
or 'strerror' are assigned to pointers to non-const-qualified types,
- the members of the structure 'lconv' referenced by the function 'localeconv'
are assigned to pointers to non-const-qualified types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_19-a.benefits:
"Treating the pointers returned by the various functions as if they were
const-qualified allows an analysis tool to detect any attempt to modify an
object through one of the pointers. Additionally, assigning the return values
of the functions to const-qualified pointers will result in the compiler
issuing a diagnostic if an attempt is made to modify an object."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_19-b.description:
"The 'localeconv' function returns a pointer of type 'struct lconv*'. This
pointer shall be regarded as if it had type 'const struct lconv*'."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]

"Whilst the use of a const-qualified pointer gives compile time protection
of the value returned by 'localeconv', the same is not true for the strings
it references. Modification of these strings can be detected by an analysis
tool" [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]

This rule detects when a pointer to the const-qualified 'lconv' structure
is used to access the member of the 'lconv' structure and a string that
the member points to is modified.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_19-b.benefits:
"The Standard does not specify the behaviour that results if the strings
referenced by the structure pointed to by the value returned by 'localeconv'
are modified. This rule prohibits any changes to these strings as they are
considered to be undesirable." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.19]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_2-a.title: The names of standard library macros, objects and functions shall not be reused [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_2-a.description:
"Where new versions of standard library macros, objects or functions are used
by the programmer (e.g. enhanced functionality or checks of input values) the
modified macro, object or function shall have a new name. This is to avoid any
confusion as to whether a standard macro, object or function is being used or
whether a modified version of that function is being used. So, for example, if
a new version of the sqrt function is written to check that the input is not
negative, the new function shall not be named 'sqrt', but shall be given a new
name." [1]

Rule checks if the following reserved names are used:
- identifiers that begin with the underscore character

See also: MISRA2004-20_2_a, MISRA2004-20_2_b, NAMING-33, CODSTA-92,
          CODSTA-92_a, CODSTA-92_b, CODSTA-93
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_2-a.example_violation:
// Example of code which results in a violation:
int _name();    // Violation
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_2-a.example_repair:
// Example of code which does not result in a violation:
int my_name(); // OK
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

DCL51-CPP. Do not declare or define a reserved identifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_20-a.title: Pointers returned by certain Standard Library functions should not be used following a subsequent call to the same or related function [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_20-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_20-a.description:
This rule detects when a pointer returned by one of the Standard Library functions listed below
is used following a subsequent call to the same function.

"The Standard Library functions asctime, ctime, gmtime, localtime, localeconv, getenv, setlocale
and strerror return a pointer to an object within the Standard Library. Implementations are 
permitted to use static buffers for any of these objects and a second call to the same function 
may modify the contents of the buffer. The value accessed through a pointer held by the program 
before a subsequent call to a function may therefore change unexpectedly".
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 21.20]

<time.h>
    char* asctime (const struct tm* timeptr)
    char* ctime (const time_t* timer)
    struct tm* gmtime (const time_t* timer)
    struct tm* localtime (const time_t* timer)
    
<locale.h>
    struct lconv* localeconv ()
    char* setlocale (int category, const char* locale)
    
<stdlib.h>
    char* getenv (const char* name)
    
<string.h>
    char* strerror (int errnum)
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_20-a.security_relevance:
Subsequent calls to the same function may change the value referenced by the pointer that is returned
by the first call. Relying on such a value following a subsequent call may cause unexpected results.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_20-a.benefits:
Following this rule helps you prevent changes of values that may be caused by subsequent calls
the same function. This could result in incorrect application behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_21-a.title: The 'system()' function from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_21-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_21-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'system()' global function declared with external
C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard library, or locally by
the user) is called or when its address is taken.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_21-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined or implementation-defined behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_22-a.title: All arguments to any type-generic macros declared in <tgmath.h> shall have appropriate essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_22-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_22-a.description:
"The operand arguments passed to the type-generic macros defined in <tgmath.h>
shall have essentially signed, essentially unsigned or essentially floating
(either essentially real floating or essentially complex floating) type.

Arguments to the following macros shall not have essentially complex floating
type: atan2, cbrt, ceil, copysign, erf, erfc, exp2, expm1, fdim, floor, fma,
fmax, fmin, fmod, frexp, hypot, ilogb, ldexp, lgamma, llrint, llround, log10,
log1p, log2, logb, lrint, lround, nearbyint, nextafter, nexttoward, remainder,
remquo, rint, round, scalbn, scalbln, tgamma, trunc.
The final parameter to the 'frexp' and 'remquo' macros is for output, and is not
considered an operand.

Arguments of non-arithmetic types are not convertible to any of the
corresponding real types defined for the macros defined in <tgmath.h>.
Attempting to use them therefore results in undefined behaviour.

Passing an essentially complex floating argument to one of the macros listed
in the amplification results in undefined behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when arguments passed to calls of macros from the <tgmath.h>
header have a different essential type than essentially signed, essentially
unsigned or essentially floating type, or when arguments passed to macros atan2,
cbrt, ceil, copysign, erf, erfc, exp2, expm1, fdim, floor, fma, fmax, fmin,
fmod, frexp, hypot, ilogb, ldexp, lgamma, llrint, llround, log10, log1p, log2,
logb, lrint, lround, nearbyint, nextafter, nexttoward, remainder, remquo, rint,
round, scalbn, scalbln, tgamma, trunc have an essentially complex floating type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_22-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.19 Rule 21.22
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.22 All operand arguments to any type-generic macros declared in<tgmath.h> shall have an appropriate essential type

7.25 Type-generic math <tgmath.h>

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_22.title: Rule 21.22 (Mandatory) All operand arguments to any type-generic macros declared in <tgmath.h> shall have an appropriate essential type [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_22]

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_23-a.title: All arguments to any multi-argument type-generic macros declared in <tgmath.h> shall have the same standard type [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_23-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_23-a.description:
"All operand arguments passed to any of the multi-argument macros defined
in <tgmath.h> shall have the same standard type, after integer promotion
has been applied to any integer arguments.
The final parameter to the 'frexp' and 'remquo' macros is for output, and is
not considered an operand.
On platforms with extended real types, the essentially real floating types
may not be able to be strictly ordered by precision. In this case there
is a risk that if two arguments have different types, the deduced common
real type may be an unexpected size or silently lose precision."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when arguments passed to calls of macros: atan2, copysign,
fdim, fma, fmax, fmin, fmod, hypot, ldexp, nextafter, nexttoward, pow,
remainder, remquo, scalbn, scalbln from the <tgmath.h> header have different
standard types.

This rule is applied only if both arguments have essentially signed, essentially
unsigned or essentially floating types.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_23-a.benefits:
"Ensuring that the types used to determine the corresponding real type
for the whole call expression are consistent makes the relationship
between the input values and the result clearer."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule helps you improve the readability and consistency of the code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_24-a.title: The random number generator functions 'rand()' and 'srand()' should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_24-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_24-a.description:
"The C Standard Library function 'rand()' makes no guarantees as to the quality
of the random sequence produced. The C Standard warns that the numbers generated
by some implementations of the 'rand()' function have a short cycle and the
results can be predictable. Applications with particular requirements should use
a generator that is known to be sufficient for their needs."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when calls to the 'rand()' and 'srand()' functions are used.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_24-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid using functions, which produce predictable numbers
and have limited randomness.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_25-a.title: Only 'memory_order_seq_cst' should be used as an explicit memory order argument [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_25-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_25-a.description:
"The enumerated type memory_order specifies the detailed regular (non-atomic)
memory synchronization operations and may provide for operation ordering.
Its enumeration constants are as follows: memory_order_relaxed,
memory_order_consume, memory_order_acquire, memory_order_release,
memory_order_acq_rel, memory_order_seq_cst" [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"The functions not ending in '_explicit' have the same semantics as the
corresponding '_explicit' function with 'memory_order_seq_cst' for the
memory_order argument." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

The 'memory_order_seq_cst' enumeration is defined in the Standard as the default
memory order for objects with atomic types. Using of this order does not cause
undefined behaviors that can occur when another argument is used as an explicit
memory order argument in Standard Library functions for performing atomic
operations.

This rule detects when the following functions:
- atomic_store_explicit,
- atomic_load_explicit,
- atomic_exchange_explicit,
- atomic_compare_exchange_strong_explicit,
- atomic_compare_exchange_weak_explicit,
- atomic_fetch_add_explicit,
- atomic_fetch_sub_explicit,
- atomic_fetch_or_explicit,
- atomic_fetch_xor_explicit,
- atomic_fetch_and_explicit,
- atomic_flag_test_and_set_explicit,
- atomic_flag_clear_explicit,
- atomic_thread_fence,
- atomic_signal_fence
are called with a memory order argument other than 'memory_order_seq_cst'.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_25-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.12 Rule 21.25
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.25 All memory synchronization operations shall be executed in sequentially consistent order

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_26-a.title: The Standard Library function mtx_timedlock() shall not be invoked on a mutex that does not support timeout [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_26-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_26-a.description:
This rule detects cases when the mtx_timedlock() function is called on a mutex 
variable that does not support timeout.

This rule recognizes global mutexes of type mtx_t or mtx_t*.

The first argument of the mtx_timedlock() function shall be a mutex variable
which supports timeout. To ensure this, the mtx_timed value should be used for
mutex initialization. Usually it is achieved by calling:

mtx_init(&mtx, mtx_timed)

or

mtx_init(&mtx, mtx_timed | mtx_recursive)
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_26-a.security_relevance:
Calling the function mtx_timedlock() on a mutex variable that does not 
support timeout is undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_26-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_26-a.parameters:
When the "Report if both timed and non-timed mutex initializations are visible" 
parameter is enabled, the rule will report a violation if it finds at least one
non-timed mutex initialization, even if timed initialization also can be found.

This option is enabled by default.

When the "Report if no mutex initialization is visible" parameter is enabled, 
the rule will report a violation if it cannot find any mutex initialization and
such mutex is used in the mtx_timedlock() function.

This option is disabled by default.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 21.26
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 21.26 The Standard Library function mtx_timedlock() shall only be invoked on mutex objects of appropriate mutex type

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_3-a.title: Dynamic heap memory allocation shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_3-a.description:
"The use of dynamic memory can lead to out-of-storage run-time failures,
which are undesirable. The built-in 'new' and 'delete' operators, other than
the placement versions, use dynamic heap memory. The functions 'calloc',
'malloc', 'realloc' and 'free' also use dynamic heap memory." [MISRA C++:2008]

"The identifiers 'calloc', 'malloc', 'realloc', 'aligned_alloc' and 'free'
shall not be used and no macro with one of these names shall be expanded."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 2]

The rule detects calls to the 'calloc()', 'malloc()', 'realloc()',
'aligned_alloc()', and 'free()' functions, as well as the use of 'new' and
'delete' operators.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_3-a.benefits:
"There is a range of unspecified, undefined and implementation-defined
behaviour associated with dynamic memory allocation, as well as a number of
other potential pitfalls. Dynamic heap memory allocation may lead to memory
leaks, data inconsistency, memory exhaustion, non-deterministic behaviour."
[MISRA C++:2008]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_3-a.example_repair:
Modify your code to avoid using the 'new' and 'delete' operators
and the 'calloc()', 'malloc()', 'realloc()', 'aligned_alloc()',
and 'free()' functions.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_4-a.title: The facilities provided by <setjmp.h> should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_4-a.description:
"The standard header file <setjmp.h> shall not be #included, and none of the
features that are specified as being provided by <setjmp.h> shall be used."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 21.4 with Amendment 3]

"'setjmp' and 'longjmp' allow the normal function call mechanisms to be
bypassed. Their use may lead to undefined and unspecified behaviour."
[MISRA C:2012]

The rule detects the use of the following facilities:
- the 'longjmp' function
- the 'jmp_buf' type
- the 'setjmp' macro
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_4-a.benefits: The rule helps you prevent implementation-defined and undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_4-a.example_repair: Do not use the facilities from the <setjmp.h> header.

This rule detects when the 'signal()' or 'raise()' global function declared
with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard library,
or locally by the user) is called or when its address is taken.

This rule also detects when identifiers with names of types and macros defined
in <signal.h> are used: sig_atomic_t, SIGABRT, SIGFPE, SIGILL, SIGINT, SIGSEGV,
SIGTERM, SIG_DFL, SIG_IGN, SIG_ERR.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_5-b.benefits: The rule helps you prevent implementation-defined and undefined behavior.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_7-a.title: The 'atof', 'atoi', 'atol' and 'atoll' functions from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_7-a.description:
This rule detects when the 'atof()', 'atoi()', 'atol()' or 'atoll()' global
function declared with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib'
standard library, or locally by the user) is called or when its address
is taken.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_7-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior. Using these functions results in
undefined behavior when the string cannot be converted.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-a.title: The 'abort()' function from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-a.description:
Calling the 'abort()' standard library function leads to implementation-defined
behavior.

This rule detects when the 'abort()' global function declared with external

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-c.title: The 'quick_exit()' and '_Exit()' functions from the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' library shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-c.description:
"The 'std::abort()', 'std::quick_exit()', and 'std::_Exit()' functions are used
to terminate the program in an immediate fashion. They do so without calling
exit handlers registered with 'std::atexit()' and without executing destructors
for objects with automatic, thread, or static storage duration. Because these
functions can leave external resources, such as files and network
communications, in an indeterminate state, they should be called explicitly only
in direct response to a critical error in the application." [CERT-ERR50-CPP]

This rule detects when the 'quick_exit()' or '_Exit()' global function
declared with external C linkage (in the 'stdlib.h' or 'cstdlib' standard
library, or locally by the user) is called or when its address is taken.

See also: PB-75
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_8-c.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent implementation-defined behavior. It allows you
to ensure that open streams with unwritten buffered data are flushed, open
streams are closed, and temporary files are removed.

MISRAC2012-RULE_21_9-a.title: The library functions bsearch and qsort of <stdlib.h> shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_21_9-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_9-a.description:
"If the comparison function does not behave consistently when comparing
elements, or it modifies any of the elements, the behaviour is undefined."

"The bsearch function returns a pointer to a matching element of the array,
or a null pointer if no match is found. If two elements compare as equal,
which element is matched is unspecified."

"The qsort function shall return an integer less than, equal to, or greater
than zero if the first argument is considered to be respectively less than,
equal to, or greater than the second. If two elements compare as equal,
their order in the resulting sorted array is unspecified."

"The implementation of qsort is likely to be recursive and will therefore
place unknown demands on stack resource. This is of concern in embedded
systems as the stack is likely to be a fixed, often small, size."

The rule reports a violation if the function 'bsearch' or 'qsort' of <stdlib.h>
library is called.
MISRAC2012-RULE_21_9-a.benefits: The rule prevents unspecified and undefined behaviours.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_1-a.title: Ensure resources are freed [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_1-a.description:
This rule helps to ensure that allocated resources are deallocated on all paths.
Violations of the rule identify execution paths in an application where resources
are leaked.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_1-a.security_relevance:
Failure to close resources may lead to starvation, which could have
security implications.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_1-a.benefits:
Having a strict policy with respect to closing resources helps to make an application more
robust by preventing resource starvation.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_1-a.parameters:
The "Assume resource references can be stored by third-party methods of the following
types" set of parameters allows you to determine how strict the rule is when a reference
to a resource may be stored by a third-party function. For example, if there is a
third-party function SpecialCollection::add() and a resource is passed as a parameter
to this function, it will be stored in a collection and may later be closed by iterating
over the collection. Since Flow Analysis does not know the exact behavior of arbitrary
third-party functions, it behaves in either of the following ways:

   * With "Any functions" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that any third-party
     function may store a reference to a resource if the resource is passed into the function.
     This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource is later closed using
     the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are
     not reported as violations) if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
    
   * With "Functions that accept parameters which could host resource reference" parameter
     enabled, Flow Analysis assumes that a function may store a reference to a resource if
     it takes an additional parameter that can be used to store a pointer or a reference to
     the given resource. This prevents the rule from reporting false positives if the resource
     is later closed using the stored reference. However, this may also lead to some false
     negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations) if the function does not store
     a reference to the resource.
   
   * With "Functions with names matching the pattern" parameter enabled, Flow Analysis
     assumes that any third-party method which matches regex may store a reference to
     a resource if the resource is passed into the method. This prevents the rule from reporting
     false positives if the resource is later closed using the stored reference. However,
     this may also lead to some false negatives (real leaks are not reported as violations)
     if the function does not store a reference to the resource.
   
   * With all the parameters disabled, Flow Analysis takes a more aggressive approach and it
     assumes that third-party methods do not affect resources in any way. This approach
     may lead to some false positives, but it will not overlook any of the real leaks
     that may be missed with enabled parameters.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_10-a.title: Properly use errno value [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_10-a.security_relevance:
The improper use of errno may result in failing to detect an error condition or in 
incorrectly identifying an error condition when none exists.
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, Rule 12: Error Handling (ERR), ERR30-C]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_10-a.parameters:
If the "Report a violation when errno is not set to 0 before an errno-setting-function call"
option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specifiedin point 1) (see the DESCRIPTION section).
This covers Rule 22.8 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

If the "Report a violation when errno is not tested against 0 after an errno-setting-function call"
option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specified in point 2) (see the DESCRIPTION section)
This covers Rule 22.9 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

If the "Report a violation when errno is tested against 0 after calling a function that is not
an errno-setting-function" option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specified in point 3)
(see the DESCRIPTION section)
This covers Rule 22.10 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

"Additional errno setting functions" allows user to define additional functions which should be considered
errno-setting-functions.
The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name of the type or namespace
  where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace,
  or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
* The "Correct return value constraint" should be specified when function returns an out-of-band error indicator, 
  which means that when error occurs, it returns value that cannot be returned by any successful call.
  The errno value does not have to be tested against 0 if the check of such return value is present instead.
  Possible values are : >A / >=A / <A / <=A / ==B / !=B, where A is constant, B is constant/"true"/"false",
  e.g. ">=-1", "!=0", "==true"

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11-a.title: Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11-a.description:
"The C Standard, 7.26.5.6 [ISO/IEC 9899:2011], states that a thread shall not be joined 
once it was previously joined or detached. Similarly, subclause 7.26.5.3 states that a thread 
shall not be detached once it was previously joined or detached".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, CON39-C. Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached]

This rule detects code flow paths where a thread object that is joined or detached was previously
joined or detached.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11-a.security_relevance:
"Joining or detaching a previously joined or detached thread is undefined behavior".
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, CON39-C. Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior (see SECURITY RELEVANCE).
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics

Rule 22.11 A thread that was previously either joined or detached shall not be subsequently joined nor detached

CON39-C. Do not join or detach a thread that was previously joined or detached

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11.title: Rule 22.11 (Required) A thread that was previously either joined or detached shall not be subsequently joined nor detached [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_11]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_12-a.title: Thread objects, thread synchronization objects, and thread-specific storage pointers shall only be accessed by the appropriate Standard Library functions [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_12-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_12-a.description:
"Thread objects and thread synchronization objects are expected to be
unique for the corresponding thread and synchronization resources.
Thread-specific storage pointers are identified by unique keys.
Any direct manipulation (copy, assignment, etc.) may result in undefined
behaviour." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4]

This rule detects when:
- an expression of the 'thrd_t' type is used in a different way than
  as an argument to the 'thrd_detach()', 'thrd_join()' or 'thrd_equal()'
  function call,
- an expression of the 'tss_t' type is used in a different way than
  as an argument to the 'tss_delete()', 'tss_get()' or 'tss_set()'
  function call,
- an expression of the 'mtx_t' or 'cnd_t' type is used,
- an expression of the struct or union type containing a member of the
  'thrd_t', 'mtx_t', 'cnd_t' or 'tss_t' type is used,
- an expression of a pointer to the 'thrd_t', 'mtx_t', 'cnd_t' or 'tss_t'
  type is assigned to a pointer to a different type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_12-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid undefined behavior.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.14 Rule 22.12
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.12 Thread objects, thread synchronization objects, and thread-specific storage pointers shall only be accessed by the appropriate Standard Library functions

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_12.title: Rule 22.12 (Mandatory) Thread objects, thread synchronization objects, and thread-specific storage pointers shall only be accessed by the appropriate Standard Library functions [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_12]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_13-a.title: Thread objects, thread synchronization objects and thread-specific storage pointers shall not have automatic storage duration [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_13-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_13-a.description:
When an object of automatic storage duration is shared between threads or used
to control concurrent execution, then it can be accessed outside of its
lifetime, which can result in undefined behavior.

This rule detects when objects of type 'thrd_t', 'mtx_t', 'cnd_t', and 'tss_t'
are declared with automatic storage duration.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_13-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior when objects with automatic
storage duration are accessed outside of their lifetime.

6.2.4 Storage durations of objects

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-a.title: A thread synchronization object should not be initialized by a thread [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-a.security_relevance:
Initializing a thread synchronization object inside of a thread can cause 
undefined behavior. It can cause a thread synchronization object to be accessed 
before it is initialized.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent threads from accessing thread synchronization objects
with indeterminate state.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
