Rule 22.14 Thread synchronization objects shall be initialized before being accessed

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-b.title: The second argument passed to the 'mtx_init()' function call should be either 'mtx_plain', 'mtx_timed', 'mtx_plain | mtx_recursive', or 'mtx_timed | mtx_recursive' [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-b.description:
"The 'mtx_init' function creates a mutex object with properties indicated
by type, which must have one of the six values:
- 'mtx_plain' for a simple non-recursive mutex,
- 'mtx_timed' for a non-recursive mutex that supports timeout,
- 'mtx_plain | mtx_recursive' for a simple recursive mutex, or
- 'mtx_timed | mtx_recursive' for a recursive mutex that supports timeout."
[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

If the 'mtx_init' function is called with the second argument with a value
different than listed above, then behavior is undefined.

This rule detects when the 'mtx_init' function is called with the second
argument different than 'mtx_plain', 'mtx_timed', 'mtx_plain | mtx_recursive'
or 'mtx_timed | mtx_recursive'.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14-b.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.14 Rule 22.14
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.14 Thread synchronization objects shall be initialized before being accessed
3. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
7.26.4.2 The mtx_init function

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14.title: Rule 22.14 (Mandatory) Thread synchronization objects shall be initialized before being accessed [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_14]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_15-a.title: A thread synchronization object should not be destroyed by a thread [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_15-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_15-a.security_relevance:
Destroying a thread synchronization object or a thread-specific storage pointer 
inside of a thread can cause undefined behavior, because another thread might 
still have access to the destroyed resource.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_15-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior when thread synchronization 
objects or thread-specific storage pointers are destroyed.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.15
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.15 Thread synchronization objects and thread-specific storage pointers shall not be destroyed until after all threads accessing them have terminated

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_15.title: Rule 22.15 (Required) Thread synchronization objects and thread-specific storage pointers shall not be destroyed until after all threads accessing them have terminated [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_15]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_16-a.title: Do not abandon unreleased locks [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_16-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_16-a.description:
This rule detects cases where a mutex is locked but not unlocked at an
appropriate place, which could cause an application deadlock.

The list of supported multithreading APIs can be found and modified in test
configuration "Flow Analysis Advanced Settings" -> "Multi-threading".
The rule will report a violation if one of the functions defined as 
"Functions for locking" is used without a proper use of a function defined
as "Functions for unlocking".
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_16-a.parameters:
The "Locked object may be unlocked from callers up to specified level" parameter
allows you to manage the depth of analysis of the call graph. Lower numbers are 
recommended to enforce the best practice that locking and unlocking occur at the
same nesting level. This practice is required by MISRA C/C++ & JSF standards.

Higher numbers of the parameter value may be preferred for (rare) cases where 
locking and unlocking have to be performed on different levels as well as in the
case where certain functions serve as wrappers to standard locking and unlocking
functions. The parameter of the value is set to 1  by default. This ensures the
detection of most bugs related to direct uses of standard locking functions
as well as the use of custom wrappers around them - without producing
unnecessary false positives.

To demonstrate this, let's assume the following wrapper functions:

void mutex_lock()
{
    pthread_mutex_lock(mutex);
    // do something else
}

void mutex_unlock()
{
    pthread_mutex_unlock(mutex);
    // do something else
}

With the value of parameter set to 0, Flow Analysis will see the call to
pthread_mutex_lock inside the wrapper function "mutex_lock" and no corresponding
call to pthread_mutex_unlock inside the function. Consequently, it will report a 
violation which is in fact a false positive because mutex_lock is not meant to 
call unlocking function by design and pthread_mutex_unlock is called from the 
other wrapper function named "mutex_unlock". Setting the depth to values higher
than 0 prevents false positives for such wrapper functions while still ensuring
that violations will be reported if the code doesn't call "mutex_unlock".
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.13
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.16
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.16 All mutex objects locked by a thread shall be explicitly unlocked by the same thread
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
CON01-C. Acquire and release synchronization primitives in the same module, at the same level of abstraction
CWE-667: Improper Locking

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_16.title: Rule 22.16 (Required) All mutex objects locked by a thread shall be explicitly unlocked by the same thread [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_16]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_17-a.title: Do not release a lock that has not been acquired [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_17-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_17-a.description:
This rule detects cases where a lock is released, but it has not been acquired 
in the same method.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_17-a.benefits: Helps you prevent unlocking another thread's lock.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.13
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.17
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.17 No thread shall unlock a mutex or call cnd_wait() or cnd_timedwait() for a mutex it has not locked before
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS48-C. Do not unlock or destroy another POSIX thread's mutex
CWE-832: Unlock of a Resource that is not Locked

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_17.title: Rule 22.17 (Required) No thread shall unlock a mutex or call cnd_wait() or cnd_timedwait() for a mutex it has not locked before [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_17]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_18-a.title: Avoid double locking [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_18-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_18-a.description:
This rule detects cases where a lock is acquired twice without intermediate releasing.
While this is legal for the so-called recursive mutexes, it is not allowed for regular
locks and may cause deadlocks.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_18-a.parameters:
The "Report on tryLock methods" parameter allows you to specify if the rule will
trigger when the second lock is obtained through a method with the "tryLock"
semantics (e.g. pthread_mutex_trylock).
This option is enabled by default.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 7: Directives, Dir 4.13
MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.18
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.18 Non-recursive mutexes shall not be recursively locked
High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
18.3.1 Within the scope of a lock, ensure that no static path results in a lock of the same mutex
SEI CERT C++ Coding Standard
CON56-CPP. Do not speculatively lock a non-recursive mutex that is already owned by the calling thread

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_18.title: Rule 22.18 (Required) Non-recursive mutexes shall not be recursively locked [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_18]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_19-a.title: Do not use more than one mutex for concurrent waiting operations on a condition variable [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_19-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_19-a.description:
"pthread_cond_wait() and pthread_cond_timedwait() take a condition variable
and locked mutex as arguments. These functions unlock the mutex until
the condition variable is signaled and then relock the mutex before returning.
While a thread is waiting on a particular condition variable and mutex,
other threads may only wait on the same condition variable if they also pass
the same mutex as an argument." [CERT-POS53-C]

The rule detects when different calls to:
- cnd_wait() and/or cnd_timedwait(), or to
- pthread_cond_wait() and/or pthread_cond_timedwait()
take the same condition variable, but different mutexes as arguments.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_19-a.benefits:
The rule helps you prevent undefined behaviors when more than one mutex
is used for concurrent operations of the following functions on the same
condition variable:
- cnd_wait() and/or cnd_timedwait(), or
- pthread_cond_wait() and/or pthread_cond_timedwait().
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, 2.2.14 Rule 22.19
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.19 A condition variable shall be associated with at most one mutex object
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
POS53-C. Do not use more than one mutex for concurrent waiting operations
on a condition variable

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_19.title: Rule 22.19 (Required) A condition variable shall be associated with at most one mutex object [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_19]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-a.title: Do not use resources that have been freed [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-a.description:
This rule detects uses of resources that have been freed. In particular, a violation is reported
when a pointer or reference to a freed resource is:

   * returned from a function,
   * passed as a parameter to a function,
   * used in an arithmetic operation,
   * dereferenced, or
   * assigned to a variable/field/array element.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-a.benefits: Prevents strange, often non-deterministic errors related to the use of freed resources.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-a.parameters:
The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to null" check box
unsupresses/suppresses  violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to a null
pointer. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to other pointers" check box
unsuppresses/suppresses violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to other
pointers. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources
references: 2023 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
2022 CWE Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors
CWE-416: Use After Free
CWE-415: Double Free
CWE-675: Multiple Operations on Resource in Single-Operation Context

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-b.title: Do not free resources using invalid pointers [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2-b.description:
This rule detects attempts to free memory using an invalid pointer.
A violation is reported when the pointer used to free a resource meets any of
the following conditions:

   * It is a pointer to a string constant.
   * It is acquired as a result of a cast of an integer constant to pointer type (wild pointer).
   * It is acquired via an address operation applied to a parameter, local, global
     or static variable (attempt to free resource in non-heap memory).
   * It does not point to the beginning of the allocated chunk.
   * It is not allocated pointer.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 22.2
MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

Dir 4.13 Functions which are designed to provide operations on a resource should be called in an appropriate sequence
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.2 A block of memory shall only be freed if it was allocated by means of a Standard Library function
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
MEM34-C. Only free memory allocated dynamically
CWE-590: Free of Memory not on the Heap
CWE-761: Free of Pointer not at Start of Buffer

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2.title: Rule 22.2 (Mandatory) A block of memory shall only be freed if it was allocated by means of a Standard Library function [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20-a.title: A thread-specific storage should not be created by a thread [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20-a.description:
This rule detects cases when a thread-specific storage pointer is 
created inside of a thread.

For a list of recognized thread-creating functions, see the rule BD-TRS-THRTHR.

This rule recognizes the following creation functions for 
thread-specific storage pointers:

<threads.h> (C Standard Library)
    int tss_create( tss_t* tss_key, tss_dtor_t destructor )
    
<pthread.h> (POSIX)
    int pthread_key_create(pthread_key_t *key, void (*destructor)(void*));

<processthreadsapi.h> (Windows)
    DWORD TlsAlloc();

If any of these functions is called (directly or indirectly) from a function which itself
is a thread entry point, the rule will report a violation.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20-a.security_relevance:
Creating a thread-specific storage pointer inside of a thread can cause 
undefined behavior. It can cause a thread-specific storage pointer to be accessed
before it is created.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20-a.benefits:
This rule helps you prevent threads from accessing thread-specific storage pointers 
with indeterminate state.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
2.2 Section 8 - Rules, Rule 22.20
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.20 Thread-specific storage pointers shall be created before being accessed

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20.title: Rule 22.20 (Mandatory) Thread-specific storage pointers shall be created before being accessed [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_20]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_3-a.title: The same file shall not be opened for read and write access at the same time on different streams [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_3-a.security_relevance:
Writing to a read-only stream has no defined behavior in the language specification.
For this reason, it is considered unsafe and it may cause segmentation fault errors.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_3-a.benefits:
Helps avoid unexpected failures at runtime. The standard does not define behavior 
in the case of attempts to write and read the same file file on different streams, which may 
lead to unexpected behaviors.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 22.3
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.3 The same file shall not be open for read and write access at the same time on different streams

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_3.title: Rule 22.3 (Required) The same file shall not be open for read and write access at the same time on different streams [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_4-a.title: Avoid writing to a stream which has been opened as read only [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_4-a.security_relevance:
Writing to a read only stream has no defined behavior in the language specification.
For this reason it is considered unsafe and it may cause segmentation fault errors.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_4-a.benefits:
Helps avoid unexpected failures at runtime. The standard does not define behavior in the case of attempts 
to write to a read only file, which may lead to unexpected behaviors.
references: MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 22.4
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.4 There shall be no attempt to write to a stream which has been opened as read-only

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_4.title: Rule 22.4 (Mandatory) There shall be no attempt to write to a stream which has been opened as read-only [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-a.title: A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-a.description:
"A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced directly or indirectly
(e.g. by a call to memcpy or memcmp)." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]

"The direct manipulation of a FILE object is prohibited as this may be
incompatible with its use as a stream designator." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]

This rule detects cases when a pointer to a FILE object is dereferenced
directly (i.e. used as the operand 'a' in expressions: '*a', 'a->b', 'a[b]').
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-a.benefits:
"The Standard (C90 Section 7.9.3(6), C99 Section 7.19.3(6) ) states that the
address of a FILE object used to control a stream may be significant
and a copy of the object may not give the same behaviour. This rule ensures
that such a copy cannot be made." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-a.example_repair: Do not dereference pointers to FILE objects.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 22.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.5 A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced
3. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO38-C. Do not copy a FILE object

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-b.title: A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced by a library function [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-b.description:
"A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced directly or indirectly
(e.g. by a call to memcpy or memcmp)." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]

This rule detects cases when a pointer to a FILE object is used as one
of the following:
- the first argument in a call to the 'memset', 'memchr' or 'realloc' function
- the first or the second argument in a call to the 'memcpy', 'memcmp' or 'memmove'
  function
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-b.benefits:
"The Standard (C90 Section 7.9.3(6), C99 Section 7.19.3(6) ) states that the
address of a FILE object used to control a stream may be significant
and a copy of the object may not give the same behaviour. This rule ensures
that such a copy cannot be made." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 22.5]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5-b.example_repair:
Do not use pointers to a FILE object as arguments in calls to library functions
'memset', 'memchr', 'realloc', 'memcpy', 'memcmp' or 'memmove'.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 22.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.5 A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5.title: Rule 22.5 (Mandatory) A pointer to a FILE object shall not be dereferenced [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_6-a.title: Do not use resources that have been freed [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_6-a.description:
This rule detects uses of resources that have been freed. In particular, a violation is reported
when a pointer or reference to a freed resource is:

   * returned from a function,
   * passed as a parameter to a function,
   * used in an arithmetic operation,
   * dereferenced, or
   * assigned to a variable/field/array element.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_6-a.benefits: Prevents strange, often non-deterministic errors related to the use of freed resources.
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_6-a.parameters:
The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to null" check box
unsupresses/suppresses  violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to a null
pointer. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report violation when pointer to freed resource is compared to other pointers" check box
unsuppresses/suppresses violations where a pointer to a freed resource is compared to other
pointers. This may be allowed by the code writing policy and application design.
By default, such violations are not reported.

The "Report unvalidated violations" parameter can result in more violations,
but possibly also more false positives, being reported. In this mode Flow 
Analysis will not check whether the path of the violation can be reached from
the beginning of the function. In some complicated cases, restricted analysis
depth may prevent Flow Analysis from completing the violation validation
procedure. If this happens, violation can be found only with this parameter 
turned on.

The types of resources handled by this rule can be viewed and edited in test configuration section:
Static Analysis -> Flow Analysis Advanced Settings -> Resources

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7-a.title: The macro EOF should be compared with the unmodified return value from the Standard Library function [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7-a.description:
This rule detects comparisons of the EOF macro with the value returned
by a Standard Library function capable of returning EOF that has been
converted to another type before the comparison.


The list of function that are capable of returning EOF:
	int fclose ( FILE * stream )
	int fflush ( FILE * stream )
	int fgetc ( FILE * stream )
	int fputc ( int character, FILE * stream )
    int fputs ( const char * str, FILE * stream )
	int fputws (const wchar_t* ws, FILE* stream)
	int fscanf ( FILE * stream, const char * format, ... )
	int fwscanf (FILE* stream, const wchar_t* format, ...)
	int getc ( FILE * stream )
	int getchar ( void )
	int putc ( int character, FILE * stream )
	int putchar ( int character )
	int puts ( const char * str )
	int scanf ( const char * format, ... )
	int sscanf ( const char * s, const char * format, ...)
	int swscanf (const wchar_t* ws, const wchar_t* format, ...)
	int ungetc ( int character, FILE * stream )
	int vfscanf ( FILE * stream, const char * format, va_list arg )
	int vfwscanf (FILE* stream, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
	int vscanf ( const char * format, va_list arg )
	int vsscanf ( const char * s, const char * format, va_list arg )
	int vswscanf (const wchar_t* ws, const wchar_t* format, va_list arg)
	int wscanf (const wchar_t* format, ...)
	int wctob (wint_t wc)
	int wscanf (const wchar_t* format, ...)
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7-a.security_relevance:
"An EOF return value from these functions is used to indicate that a stream
is either at end-of-file or that a read or write error has occurred. The EOF
value may become indistinguishable from a valid character code if the value
returned is converted to another type. In such cases, testing the converted
value against EOF will not reliably identify if the end of file has been
reached or if an error has occurred". 
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Rule 22.7]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7-a.benefits:
This rule helps you ensure that the comparison between the EOF macro and
the value returned by the functions listed above is reliable.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 1 Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012
Section 2: New rules, Rule 22.7
MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 22.7 The macro EOF shall only be compared with the unmodified return value from any Standard Library function capable of returning EOF
SEI CERT C Coding Standard
FIO34-C. Distinguish between characters read from a file and EOF or WEOF

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7.title: Rule 22.7 (Required) The macro EOF shall only be compared with the unmodified return value from any Standard Library function capable of returning EOF [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_22_8-a.title: Properly use errno value [MISRAC2012-RULE_22_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_8-a.security_relevance:
The improper use of errno may result in failing to detect an error condition or in 
incorrectly identifying an error condition when none exists.
[SEI CERT C Coding Standard, Rule 12: Error Handling (ERR), ERR30-C]
MISRAC2012-RULE_22_8-a.parameters:
If the "Report a violation when errno is not set to 0 before an errno-setting-function call"
option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specifiedin point 1) (see the DESCRIPTION section).
This covers Rule 22.8 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

If the "Report a violation when errno is not tested against 0 after an errno-setting-function call"
option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specified in point 2) (see the DESCRIPTION section)
This covers Rule 22.9 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

If the "Report a violation when errno is tested against 0 after calling a function that is not
an errno-setting-function" option is enabled, the rule will trigger for cases specified in point 3)
(see the DESCRIPTION section)
This covers Rule 22.10 from Additional security guidelines for MISRA C:2012.

"Additional errno setting functions" allows user to define additional functions which should be considered
errno-setting-functions.
The following columns allow you to define different aspects of functions in the functions table: 

* The 'Enabled' column can be used to temporarily disable some of the defined functions for Flow Analysis.

* The 'Fully qualified type name or namespace (wildcard)' column should be used to provide the fully qualified name of the type or namespace
  where the function is declared. Use '*' if you want to describe a function declared in any type or namespace,
  or a global function declared outside of any type or namespace.

* The 'Function name (wildcard)' should contain the name of the described function.

* The '+ definitions in subclasses' is used to indicate whether the current entry will apply
  to functions with the given name defined in subclasses of the given class.
  Note that this applies to both instance and non-instance functions.
  
* The "Correct return value constraint" should be specified when function returns an out-of-band error indicator, 
  which means that when error occurs, it returns value that cannot be returned by any successful call.
  The errno value does not have to be tested against 0 if the check of such return value is present instead.
  Possible values are : >A / >=A / <A / <=A / ==B / !=B, where A is constant, B is constant/"true"/"false",
  e.g. ">=-1", "!=0", "==true"

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-a.title: A generic selection should only be expanded from a macro [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-a.description:
"A generic selection expression should only be expanded from a function-like
macro, with one of the macro's arguments expanded into the controlling expression
of the generic selection." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects generic selections that are used outside of the macro
definitions.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-a.benefits:
"Generic selections are useful to implement type queries or checks, or generic
functions. If the generic selection is applied directly rather than expanded
from a macro body, the type of the operand is already known locally and querying
it is not necessary." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-a.example_violation:
int x;
int y = _Generic(x, int: 1, default : 0);                      // Violation
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.1 A generic selection should only be expanded from a macro

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-b.title: A generic selection used in a macro definition should have a macro parameter in the controlling expression [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-b.description:
"A generic selection expression should only be expanded from a function-like
macro, with one of the macro's arguments expanded into the controlling
expression of the generic selection." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when a generic selection is used in a macro defintion
and no macro parameter is used in its controlling expression.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-b.benefits: This rule helps you improve readability and maintainability of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1-b.example_violation:
int x;
#define GEN(P) _Generic(x, int: 1, default : 0);               // Violation
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Rule 23.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.1 A generic selection should only be expanded from a macro

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1.title: Rule 23.1 (Advisory) A generic selection should only be expanded from a macro [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_2-a.title: A generic selection that is not expanded from a macro shall not contain potential side effects in the controlling expression [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_2-a.description:
"The controlling expression of a generic selection is never evaluated. It is
only used for its type, and usually has to be repeated in order to be combined
with the result value in some way.
If an expression is specified which syntactically contains a side effect, that
effect will not be applied, even if the expression has a type that would cause
it to be evaluated by 'sizeof', such as a VLA."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

The rule detects potential side effects in controlling expressions of generic
selections that are not expanded from macros.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_2-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid developer confusion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.2
2. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
5.1.2.3 Program execution
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.2 A generic selection that is not expanded from a macro shall not contain potential side effects in the controlling expression

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_2.title: Rule 23.2 (Required) A generic selection that is not expanded from a macro shall not contain potential side effects in the controlling expression [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3-a.title: A generic selection shall indicate at least one non-default association [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3-a.description:
"A generic selection that consists only of a usable 'default' association
does not do anything useful; the default association's result value
is evaluated unconditionally." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects generic selections containing only a default association.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid unnecessary code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3-a.example_repair:
You can fix the code by adding non-default associations.

#define cbrt(X) _Generic((X),           \
                    default: cbrt,      \
                    long double: cbrtl, \
                    float: cbrtf        \
                )(X)
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.3
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.3 A generic selection should contain at least one non-default association

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3.title: Rule 23.3 (Advisory) A generic selection should contain at least one non-default association [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_4-a.title: A generic association shall list an appropriate type [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_4-a.description:
"Since the Standard does not impose a constraint limiting the types in the
association list to the possible types of values after conversion, it is
possible to list associations for types that would never be eligible for
selection.
Value conversion removes top-level qualification and 'decays' array and function
values into pointers. Therefore, such types can never match the type of the
converted value of the controlling expression. Listing them is not a constraint
violation, but serves no useful purpose, and is almost certainly an error.
This only affects object types, not qualification on pointed-to types.
Listing an unnamed structure or union in the association list is a violation
of this rule because every occurrence of a struct-declaration-list creates
a distinct type, therefore it can only be matched by a default association."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects generic associations with the following types:
- a const qualified object type
- a volatile qualified object type
- an atomic object type
- an array type
- a function type
- an unnamed structure or union definition
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_4-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid errors and developer confusion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.4 A generic association shall list an appropriate type

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_4.title: Rule 23.4 (Required) A generic association shall list an appropriate type [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_5-a.title: A generic selection should not depend on implicit pointer type conversion [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_5-a.description:
"The controlling expression of a generic selection undergoes value conversion
before having its type compared to any of the entries in the association list,
but this is the only conversion applied to its type. It can then only match
an association exactly, or select the default association."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

"This rule applies when a default association is selected for a controlling
expression whose type could, in other contexts (such as when passed as an
argument to a function), be implicitly converted to another type explicitly
listed in the association list." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_5-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid developer confusion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.5 A generic selection should not depend on implicit pointer type conversion

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_5.title: Rule 23.5 (Advisory) A generic selection should not depend on implicit pointer type conversion [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-a.title: The controlling expression of a generic selection shall have an essential type that matches its standard type [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-a.description:
"The association selected by a generic selection must be in line with the type
system under which the code was designed, in order to be useful and consistent.
Code written under MISRA guidelines is subject to the essential type system,
and therefore if the selected type is not consistent with the essential type
of the controlling expression, the generic selection will have violated the
type system used for the design.
Because generic selections choose a standard type by name, they can expose
this difference if used with an argument that has inconsistent essential
and standard types." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

The rule detects when the essential type of the controlling expression
of a generic selection is different than its standard type.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid developer confusion.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-a.example_repair:
You can fix the code by ensuring that the essential type of the controlling
expression is the same as its standard type.

#define GENERIC(X) (_Generic ((X) \
 , int: 1                         \
 , default: 2))

void f1()
{
    short s = 1;
    GENERIC ((int)(s + 1));            // OK
    GENERIC (10);                      // OK - Exception
}
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.6
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Appendix D Essential types
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.6 The controlling expression of a generic selection shall have an essential type that matches its standard type

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-b.title: Enumerated types should not be mixed with integer types in generic selections [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-b.description:
"Because an enumerated type is compatible with its implementation-defined
underlying type, it is not distinguishable from the underlying type by
_Generic (although distinguishing different enumerated types is possible
because this compatibility is not transitive). Attempting to list both would
be a constraint violation. Therefore, using an enumerated type as the
controlling expression to a generic selection that lists the underlying type,
or vice versa, is always a violation." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

"Each enumerated type shall be compatible with char, a signed integer type,
or an unsigned integer type. The choice of type is implementation-defined,
but shall be capable of representing the values of all the members of the
enumeration." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

The rule detects when:
- the controlling expression of a generic selection has an enumerated type
  and a char, a signed integer type or an unsigned integer type is used
  in the association list, or
- the controlling expression of a generic selection has a char, a signed
  integer type or an unsigned integer type and an enumerated type is used
  in the association list
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid developer confusion.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6-b.example_repair:
To fix the code, do not use enumerated types in controlling expressions
and the association lists of generic selections.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.6
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.6 The controlling expression of a generic selection shall have an essential type that matches its standard type
3. Programming languages -- C ISO/IEC 9899:2011
6.7.2.2 Enumeration specifiers

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6.title: Rule 23.6 (Required) The controlling expression of a generic selection shall have an essential type that matches its standard type [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7-a.title: A generic selection that is expanded from a macro shall evaluate its argument only once [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7-a.description:
"If the controlling expression of a generic selection is expanded from a macro
argument, it should also be expanded elsewhere in the macro body so that it is
evaluated exactly once in the entire expanded macro body. The number of times
that the expression is evaluated should not depend on which association is
selected, and should be consistent for all associations in the generic
selection." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when the macro parameter that is used in the controlling
expression of a generic selection occurs exactly once in each generic
association, or occurs exactly once outside of the generic selection
in the macro body.

This rule additionally reports when more than one macro parameter is used
in the controlling expression of a generic selection.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7-a.benefits:
"The controlling expression of a generic selection is never evaluated.
It is only used for its type, and usually has to be repeated in order
to be combined with the result value in some way.
If an expression is specified which syntactically contains a side effect,
that effect will not be applied. If the generic selection is (as is usually
the case) the result of a macro expansion which uses one of the macro's
arguments to select the type, this non-evaluation is concealed from the
invoking code." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule helps you improve the readability of the code and prevents
unexpected behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7-a.example_violation:
// Violation
#define M1(X) _Generic((X)    \
    , float: id1(X)           \
    , double: id2(X)          \
    , default: id3(0))

// Violation
#define M2(X) _Generic((X)    \
    , float: id1(X)           \
    , double: id2(X)          \
    , default: id3(X)) + X
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7-a.example_repair:
// OK
#define M1(X) _Generic((X)    \
    , float: id1(X)           \
    , double: id2(X)          \
    , default: id3(X))

// OK
#define M2(X) _Generic((X)    \
    , float: id1              \
    , double: id2             \
    , default: id3)(X)

// OK - exception
#define M3(X) _Generic((X)    \
    , float: 0                \
    , double: 1               \
    , default: 2)
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.7 A generic selection that is expanded from a macro should evaluate its argument only once

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7.title: Rule 23.7 (Advisory) A generic selection that is expanded from a macro should evaluate its argument only once [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8-a.title: The default association shall be placed first or last in the association list [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8-a.description:
"A default association shall only appear as either the first generic
association in the association list, or the last - this rule only applies
when a generic selection contains a default association."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when a generic selection contains a default association
that is not first or last in the association list.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8-a.benefits:
"Having a default association appear as either the first or last in the
association list makes it easy to locate, clarifying the intent of the
selection structure." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule helps you improve the readability of the code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8-a.example_repair:
To fix your code, move the default association to the first or last
position in the association list.

// OK
#define cbrt(X) _Generic((X),           \
                    default: cbrt,      \
                    long double: cbrtl, \
                    float: cbrtf        \
                )(X)
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.21 Rule 23.8
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 23.8 A default association shall appear as either the first or the last association of a generic selection
3. Programming languages -- C ISO/IEC 9899:2011
6.5.1.1 Generic selection

MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8.title: Rule 23.8 (Required) A default association shall appear as either the first or the last association of a generic selection [MISRAC2012-RULE_23_8]

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-a.title: There shall be no unreachable code in "else" block [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-a.description:
"This rule refers to code which cannot under any circumstances be reached,
and which can be identified as such at compile-time. Code that can be reached
but may never be executed is excluded from the rule (e.g. defensive programming
code). A portion of code is unreachable if there is no control flow path from
the relevant entry point to that code."

The rule reports a violation if in an if-else construct the condition
of 'if' statement always evaluates to true and 'else' statement contains
a code (that is unreachable).

See also: MISRA2004-13_7 (group)
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-a.benefits: This rule helps you avoid useless code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-b.title: There shall be no unreachable code after 'return', 'break', 'continue', 'goto', 'throw' statements, and after calls to functions with the 'noreturn' attribute [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-b.description:
"This rule refers to code which cannot under any circumstances be reached,
and which can be identified as such at compile-time. Code that can be reached
but may never be executed is excluded from the rule (e.g. defensive programming
code). A portion of code is unreachable if there is no control flow path from
the relevant entry point to that code."
This rule detects unreachable code after 'return', 'break', 'continue', 'goto'
statements, and after calls to functions with the 'noreturn' attribute.

See also: MISRA2004-14_1_a, MISRA2004-14_1_c, MISRA2004-14_1_d, MISRA2004-14_1_e,
MISRA2004-14_1_f, MISRA2004-14_1_g
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid useless code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-c.title: There shall be no unreachable code in "if/else/while/for" block [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-c]

"This rule refers to code which cannot under any circumstances be reached,
and which can be identified as such at compile-time. Code that can be reached
but may never be executed is excluded from the rule (e.g. defensive programming
code). A portion of code is unreachable if there is no control flow path from
the relevant entry point to that code."

a constant value is used.

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-e.title: There shall be no unreachable code in 'for' loop [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-e]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-e.description:
"This rule refers to code which cannot under any circumstances be reached,
and which can be identified as such at compile-time. Code that can be reached
but may never be executed is excluded from the rule (e.g. defensive programming
code). A portion of code is unreachable if there is no control flow path from
the relevant entry point to that code."
The rule reports a violation if a 'for' loop contains in condition single
relational expression 'VAR < FB',  'VAR <= FB', 'FB  > VAR', 'FB >= VAR'
that result is always false, where FB is a constant or a const variable
and VAR is a variable assigned in for-init-statement.
If a result of for-condition is always false then a body is never executed.
See also: MISRA2004-14_1_a, MISRA2004-14_1_b, MISRA2004-14_1_c,
MISRA2004-14_1_d, MISRA2004-14_1_f, MISRA2004-14_1_g

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-f.title: There shall be no unreachable code after 'if' or 'switch' statement [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-f]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-f.description:
"This rule refers to code which cannot under any circumstances be reached,
and which can be identified as such at compile-time. Code that can be reached
but may never be executed is excluded from the rule (e.g. defensive programming
code). A portion of code is unreachable if there is no control flow path from
the relevant entry point to that code."
The rule reports a violation if:
- the code is after 'if/else' construction, where each branch has unconditional
  'return'
- the code is after switch which has unconditional 'return' inside each 'case'
  and 'default'
See also: MISRA2004-14_1_a, MISRA2004-14_1_b, MISRA2004-14_1_c,
          MISRA2004-14_1_d, MISRA2004-14_1_e, MISRA2004-14_1_g

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-g.title: There shall be no unreachable code after "if" or "switch" statement inside while/for/do...while loop [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-g]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-g.description:
"This rule refers to code which cannot under any circumstances be reached,
and which can be identified as such at compile-time. Code that can be reached
but may never be executed is excluded from the rule (e.g. defensive programming
code). A portion of code is unreachable if there is no control flow path from
the relevant entry point to that code."
The rule reports a violation on the code inside loop if:
- the code is after 'if/else' construction, where each branch has unconditional
  'break', 'continue' or 'return'
- the code is after switch which has unconditional 'return' inside each 'case'
  and 'default'
See also: MISRA2004-14_1_a, MISRA2004-14_1_b, MISRA2004-14_1_c,
          MISRA2004-14_1_d, MISRA2004-14_1_e, MISRA2004-14_1_f

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-h.title: Avoid unreachable methods [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-h]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-h.description:
This rule detects when a function is unreachable or only reachable via
unreachable functions.

Functions declared with the maybe_unused attribute are not reported.
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-h.benefits: Unreachable functions indicate poor code quality or bugs in the program.
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_1-h.parameters:
"Report on functions which are called only from unreachable functions". 
Setting to true defines function unreachability as transitive. As a result,
also functions that are called from unreachable functions will be reported.

"Report on non-member functions:" group defines rule behavior on non-member functions 

"with external linkage"
If set to true, the rule will trigger on unreachable external linkage functions.

"with internal linkage"
If set to true, the rule will trigger on unreachable internal linkage functions.

"Report on member functions:" group defines rule behavior on member functions 

"with public access"
If set to true, the rule will trigger on unreachable public member functions.

"with protected access"
If set to true, the rule will trigger on unreachable protected member functions.

"with private access"
If set to true, the rule will trigger on unreachable private member functions.

"virtual member functions"
If set to false, the rule will not trigger on any virtual functions regardless of
their accessibility and rule's accessibility parameterization.

"Ignore methods from template classes".
If set to true, the rule will not trigger on unreachable methods from template
classes. Virtual methods of such classes are instantiated even if they are 
not called.
references: CWE-561: Dead Code

Section 8: Rules, Rule 2.1

Rule 0.2.4 Functions with limited visibility should be used at least once

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-a.title: All non-null statements shall either have at least one side-effect however executed or cause control flow to change [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-a.description:
"Any statement (other than a null statement) which has no side effect and does
not result in a change of control flow will normally indicate a programming
error." [MISRA-C:2004]

"Accessing a volatile object, modifying an object, modifying a file,
or calling a function that does any of those operations are all side effects,
which are changes in the state of the execution environment."
[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

"The expression in an expression statement is evaluated as a void expression
for its side effects.
A null statement (consisting of just a semicolon) performs no operations."
[ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

This rule detects when an evaluation of an expression in an expression
statement does not cause a side effect.
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-a.benefits:
This rule helps you avoid code defects that may be caused by using statements
that neither change control flow, nor have side effects.

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-b.title: Avoid unused values [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-b.description:
This rule detects when a local variable or function parameter is assigned with 
a value that is never subsequently read because either:
- variable is unused, i.e. there is no possible path on which the variable is
  subsequently used, or
- value is overwritten, i.e. the variable is always assigned a new value before
  it is read
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-b.security_relevance:
"This weakness could be an indication of a bug in the program or a deprecated
variable that was not removed and is an indication of poor quality. This could
lead to further bugs and the introduction of weaknesses." [CWE-563]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-b.benefits: This rule helps you avoid unused values to improve code quality and security.
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_2-b.parameters:
The "Reporting on variable declarations" group of parameters allows you to
specify how the rule should behave regarding variable declarations.
- If the "Report on variable declarations" parameter is enabled, the rule
  checks variable declarations. With this parameter disabled, no violations
  are reported on variable declarations.
- If the "including unused non-initialized primitives and pointers" parameter
  is enabled, the rule triggers on declarations of variables of primitive and
  pointer types, which are not assigned any explicit value, if these variables
  are not used on any reachable path.
  Note: This parameter requires the "Reporting on variable declarations"
  parameter to be enabled.
- If the "including overwritten values of primitives" parameter is enabled,
  the rule triggers on declarations of primitive type variables if the variables
  are initialized with values that are never subsequently used, because they are
  overwritten on all paths where they are used.
  Note: This parameter requires the "Reporting on variable declarations"
  parameter to be enabled.

The "Reporting on function parameters" group of parameters allows you to
specify how the rule should behave regarding function parameters.
- If the "Report on function parameters" parameter is enabled, the rule checks 
  function parameters.
- If the "including overwritten initial values" parameter is enabled, the
  rule triggers on unused(overwritten) initial values of function parameters.
  Note: This parameter requires the "Report on function parameters" parameter
  to be enabled.
  
If the "Report when null value is assigned to a pointer" parameter is enabled,
the rule triggers when null is assigned to a pointer.

If the "Report when there is at least one path where the value of the variable
is not used" parameter is enabled, the rule triggers when there is one or more
paths where the value of a non-volatile variable is never used.
references: CWE-563: Assignment to Variable without Use

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_3-a.title: A function should not contain unused type declarations [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_3-a.description:
"If a type is declared but not used, then it is unclear to a reviewer if the
type is redundant or it has been left unused by mistake."[MISRA C:2012 Rule 2.3]

This rule detects cases when a function contains unused type declarations.
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_3-a.benefits: This rule improves legibility and increases efficiency of code.

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_7-a.title: There should be no unused parameters in functions [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_7-a.description: The rule detects parameters that are not used inside the body of a function.
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_7-a.benefits:
This rule helps you increase the efficiency and legibility of the code.

"Most functions will be specified as using each of their parameters.
If a function parameter is unused, it is possible that the implementation
of the function does not match its specification." [MISRA C:2012]

MISRAC2012-RULE_2_8-a.title: A project should not contain unused variables with external linkage [MISRAC2012-RULE_2_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_2_8-a.description:
"Variables declared and never used in a project constitute noise and may
indicate that the wrong name has been used somewhere. Removing these
declarations reduces the possibility that they may later be used instead
of the correct variable." [MISRA C++:2008 Rule 0-1-3]

This rule detects variables with external linkage that are not used in
a testing scope.

MISRAC2012-RULE_3_1-a.title: The character sequence /* shall not be used within a C-style comment [MISRAC2012-RULE_3_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_3_1-a.description:
This rule reports a violation if "/*" is embedded in "/*" comment.

A C-style comment begins with /* and continues until the first */
is encountered.

C does not support the nesting of comments even though
some compilers support this as a language extension.

MISRA C:2012 standard says:
   If a comment starting sequence /* occurs within a /* comment,
   is it quite likely to be caused by a missing */ comment ending sequence.

See also: COMMENT-11, COMMENT-12
MISRAC2012-RULE_3_1-a.benefits: Detects possible accidental ommision of "*/" end-comment marker.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines

comment

systems, Chapter 6, Section 2, Rule 2-7-1

MISRAC2012-RULE_3_2-a.title: Line-splicing shall not be used in // comments [MISRAC2012-RULE_3_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_3_2-a.description:
"Line-splicing occurs when the \ character is immediately followed by a new-line
character.

If the source line containing a // comment ends with a \ character in the source
character set, the next line becomes part of the comment. This may result in
unintentional removal of code." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 3.2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_4_1-a.title: Octal and hexadecimal escape sequences shall be terminated [MISRAC2012-RULE_4_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_4_1-a.description:
"An octal or hexadecimal escape sequence shall be terminated by either:
  - The start of another escape sequence, or
  - The end of the character constant or the end of a string literal.
There is potential for confusion if an octal or hexadecimal escape sequence
is followed by other characters. For example, the character constant '\x1f'
consists of a single character whereas the character constant '\x1g' consists
of the two characters '\x1' and 'g'. The manner in which multi-character
constants are represented as integers is implementation-defined.
The potential for confusion is reduced if every octal or hexadecimal escape
sequence in a character constant or string literal is terminated."

See also: MISRA2004-4_1
MISRAC2012-RULE_4_1-a.example_violation:
/* Examples of incorrectly terminated escape sequences: */
int c1 = '\141t';            /* Violation */
const char *s1 = "\x41g";    /* Violation */
s1 = "\01 \02";              /* Violation */
MISRAC2012-RULE_4_1-a.example_repair:
/* Examples of correctly terminated escape sequences: */
int c1 = '\141\t';           /* OK */
const char *s1 = "\x41" "g"; /* OK */
s1 = "\001 \002";            /* OK */
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.4.4.4 Character constants
6.4.5 String literals
2. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 5.13.2 Octal escape sequences, hexadecimal escape sequences and universal character names shall be terminated

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_1-a.title: External identifiers shall be distinct [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_1-a.description:
"This rule requires that different external identifiers be distinct within the
limits imposed by the implementation.

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and on the version of
the C language that is being used:
- In C90 the minimum requirement is that the first 6 characters of external
  identifiers are significant but their case is not required to be significant;
- In C99 the minimum requirement is that the first 31 characters of external
  identifiers are significant, with each universal character or corresponding
  extended source character occupying between 6 and 10 characters.

In practice, many implementations provide greater limits. For example it is
common for external identifiers in C90 to be case-sensitive and for at least
the first 31 characters to be significant." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.1]

This rule detect cases when different external identifiers have the same first
31 characters of their names.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_1-a.benefits: Keeping external identifiers distinct can help you avoid undefined behaviors.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.1 External identifiers shall be distinct

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_1.title: Rule 5.1 (Required) External identifiers shall be distinct [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_2-a.title: Identifiers declared in the file scope and in the same name space shall be distinct (c90) [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_2-a.description:
"Identifiers declared in the same scope and name space shall be distinct.
The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and on the version
of the C language that is being used. In C90 the minimum requirement is that
the first 31 characters are significant." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.2]

This rule detects cases when identifiers declared in the file scope
and in the same name space have the same first 31 characters of their names.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_2-a.benefits:
"If two identifiers differ only in non-significant characters, the behaviour is
undefined. If portability is a concern, it would be prudent to apply this rule
using the minimum limits specified in The Standard." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.2]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.2

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_3-a.title: Identifier declared in a local or function prototype scope shall not hide an identifier declared in a global or namespace scope [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_3-a.description:
"If an identifier is declared in an inner scope but is not distinct
from an identifier that already exists in an outer scope, then the inner-most
declaration will 'hide' the outer one. This may lead to developer confusion."

The rule checks if identifiers declared in local or function prototype scope
have the same name as an identifier declared in a global or namespace scope.

See also: CODSTA-CPP-83, CODSTA-CPP-84, CODSTA-CPP-85, MISRA2004-5_1
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_3-a.benefits: Hiding names of global identifiers can lead to errors and confusions.

identifier declared in an outer scope

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-a.title: The name of a macro should be distinct from the names of its parameters(c90) [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-a.description:
"This rule requires that, when a macro is being defined, its name be distinct
from:
- the names of the other macros that are currently defined; and
- the names of their parameters.

It also requires that the names of the parameters of a given macro be distinct
from each other but does not require that macro parameters names be distinct
across two different macros.

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and the version
of the C language that is being used:
- In C90 the minimum requirement is that the first 31 characters
  are significant" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.4]

This rule detects cases when:
- a macro has the same name (or the first 31 characters of the name) as one of
  its parameters;
- the parameters of a macro have the same names (or the first 31 characters of
  their names).
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-a.benefits:
Macros and identifiers whose names are the same may confuse the developer and
lead to undefined behavior of the program. This rule helps keep macro names and
identifiers distinct.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-a.example_violation:
#define MACRO1_01(MACRO1_01) MACRO1_01 + 1      /* Violation */

/*      1234567890123456789012345678901********* Characters */
#define MACRO2_______________________0311( \
        MACRO2_______________________0311)      /* Violation - reported in
                                                               line above */

#define MACRO3____( \
        MACRO3_________________________X, \
        MACRO3_________________________Y)       /* Violation - reported two
                                                               lines above */
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-a.example_repair:
#define MACRO1_01(MACRO1_02) MACRO1_02 + 1      /* OK - different name of
                                                        parameter */

#define MACRO2_______________________0311( \
        MACRO2_______________________0411)      /* OK - difference in first
                                                        31 characters */

#define MACRO3____( \
        MACRO3_________________________X, \
        MACRO3________________________XY)       /* OK */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.4 Macro identifiers shall be distinct

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-b.title: The name of a macro should be distinct from the names of its parameters(c99) [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-b.description:
"This rule requires that, when a macro is being defined, its name be distinct
from:
- the names of the other macros that are currently defined; and
- the names of their parameters.

It also requires that the names of the parameters of a given macro be distinct
from each other but does not require that macro parameters names be distinct
across two different macros.

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and the version
of the C language that is being used:
- In C99 the minimum requirement is that the first 63 characters are significant
with each universal character or extended source character counting as a
single character." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.4]

This rule detects cases when:
- a macro has the same name (or the first 63 characters of the name) as one of
  its parameters;
- the parameters of a macro have the same names (or the first 63 characters of
  their names).
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-b.benefits:
Macros and identifiers whose names are the same may confuse the developer and
lead to undefined behavior of the program. This rule helps keep macro names and
identifiers distinct.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.4 Macro identifiers shall be distinct

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-c.title: The name of a macro should be distinct from the names of other macros that are currently defined(c90) [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-c.description:
"This rule requires that, when a macro is being defined, its name be distinct
from:
- the names of the other macros that are currently defined; and
- the names of their parameters.

It also requires that the names of the parameters of a given macro be distinct
from each other but does not require that macro parameters names be distinct
across two different macros.

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and the version
of the C language that is being used:
- In C90 the minimum requirement is that the first 31 characters
  are significant" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.4]

This rule detects cases when:
- the name of a macro and the name of another currently-defined macro
  or of its parameter are the same or the first 31 characters of their names
  are identical.
- the name of a parameter of a macro and the name of another currently-
  defined macro are the same or the first 31 characters of their names
  are identical.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-c.benefits:
Macros and identifiers whose names are the same may confuse the developer and
lead to undefined behavior of the program. This rule helps keep macro names and
identifiers distinct.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-c.example_violation:
#define macro1        10
#define macro1(X) X + 10                       /* Violation */

#define macro2(param1_01) param1_01
#define param1_01 10                           /* Violation */

#define param2_02 10
#define macro3(param2_02) param2_02            /* Violation */

/*      1234567890123456789012345678901********* Characters */
#define macro4_01____________________01X XX
#define macro4_01____________________01Y YY    /* Violation */
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-c.example_repair:
#define macro1        10
#undef macro1
#define macro1(X) X + 10                       /* OK */

#define macro2(param2_01) param2_01
#define param1_01 10                           /* OK */

#define param2_02 10
#define macro3(param3_01) param3_01            /* OK */

/*      1234567890123456789012345678901********* Characters */
#define macro4_01____________________01X XX
#define macro4_01____________________02Y YY    /* OK */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.4 Macro identifiers shall be distinct

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-d.title: The name of a macro should be distinct from the names of other macros that are currently defined(c99) [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-d]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-d.description:
"This rule requires that, when a macro is being defined, its name be distinct
from:
- the names of the other macros that are currently defined; and
- the names of their parameters.

It also requires that the names of the parameters of a given macro be distinct
from each other but does not require that macro parameters names be distinct
across two different macros.

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and the version
of the C language that is being used:
- In C99 the minimum requirement is that the first 63 characters
  are significant" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.4]

This rule detects cases when:
- the name of a macro and the name of another currently defined macro
  or of its parameter are the same or the first 63 characters of their names
  are identical.
- the name of a parameter of a macro and the name of another currently
  defined macro are the same or the first 63 characters of their names
  are identical.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4-d.benefits:
Macros and identifiers whose names are the same may confuse the developer and
lead to undefined behavior of the program. This rule helps keep macro names and
identifiers distinct.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.4
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.4 Macro identifiers shall be distinct

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4.title: Rule 5.4 (Required) Macro identifiers shall be distinct [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-a.title: The names of macros that exist prior to preprocessing should be distinct from the identifiers that exist after preprocessing (c90) [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-a.description:
"This rule requires that the names of macros that exist prior to preprocessing
be distinct from the identifiers that exist after preprocessing. It applies to
identifiers, regardless of scope or name space, and to any macros that have
been defined regardless of whether the definition is still in force when the
identifier is declared.

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and the version
of the C language that is being used:
- In C90 the minimum requirement is that the first 31 characters
  are significant;
- In C99 the minimum requirement is that the first 63 characters
  are significant, with each universal character or extended source
  character counting as a single character." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.5]

This rule detect cases when the name of the macro before preprocessing and
the name of the identifier after preprocessing are the same or the first 31
characters of their names are identical.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-a.benefits:
"Keeping macro names and identifiers distinct can help to avoid developer
confusion." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.5]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-a.example_violation:
#define Sum(x, y) ( ( x ) + ( y ) )
int Sum;                                       /* Violation */

#define ID1_member_identifier_number_01Y(x) x
int ID1_member_identifier_number_01X(X, Y);    /* Violation - the same first
                                                              31 characters */

#define ID2_struct DEF_STR
#undef ID2_struct
struct ID2_struct {};                          /* Violation */

#ifdef MAC
  #define ID3_local LocalName
#endif

int ID3_local;                                 /* Violation - reported if 'MAC'
                                                              is not defined */
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-a.example_repair:
#define Sum(x, y) ( ( x ) + ( y ) )
int SumL;                                       /* OK - different name */

#define ID1_member_identifier_number_0YY(x) x
int ID1_member_identifier_number_0XX(X, Y);     /* OK - difference in first
                                                        31 characters */

#define ID2_struct DEF_STR
#undef ID2_struct
#define ID2_struct DEF_STR1

struct ID2_struct {};                           /* OK - macro expands
                                                        to 'DEF_STR1' */
#define MAC 10

#ifdef MAC
  #define ID3_local LocalName
#endif

int ID3_local;                                  /* OK - macro expands
                                                        to 'LocalName' */

The definition of distinct depends on the implementation and the version

- In C90 the minimum requirement is that the first 31 characters
  are significant;
- In C99 the minimum requirement is that the first 63 characters
  are significant, with each universal character or extended source
  character counting as a single character." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 5.5]

This rule detect cases when the name of the macro before preprocessing and
the name of the identifier after preprocessing are the same or the first 63
characters of their names are identical.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-b.benefits:
"Keeping macro names and identifiers distinct can help to avoid developer

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5-b.example_repair:

                                                                          OK */

                                                        to 'DEF_STR1' */

  #define ID3_local LocalName
#endif
