                                                        to 'LocalName' */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.5 Identifiers shall be distinct from macro names

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5.title: Rule 5.5 (Required) Identifiers shall be distinct from macro names [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_6-a.title: A typedef name shall be a unique identifier [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_6-a.description:
"A typedef name shall be unique across all name spaces and translation units.
Multiple declarations of the same typedef name are only permitted by this rule
if the type definition is made in a header file and that header file
is included in multiple source files."
[MISRA C:2012]

This rule detects typedefs whose names are duplicated or reused as other
identifiers (including other typedefs). 

Since this rule checks if the typedef name is unique across all name spaces,
it is recommended to use it for C code. For C++ code we recommend
using the GLOBAL-UNIQUETYPEDEF rule, which takes into account qualifications
of identifiers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent developer confusion.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.6
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.6 A typedef name shall be a unique identifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_6.title: Rule 5.6 (Required) A typedef name shall be a unique identifier [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_6]

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_7-a.title: A tag name shall be a unique identifier [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_7-a.description:
"The tag name shall be unique across all name spaces and translation units.
All declarations of the tag shall specify the same type.
Multiple complete declarations of the same tag are only permitted by this rule
if the tag is declared in a header file and that header file is included
in multiple source files."
[MISRA C:2012]

This rule detects classes, structures, unions and enumerations whose names 
are duplicated or reused as other identifiers.

Since this rule checks if the tag name is unique across all name spaces,
it is recommended to use it for C code. For C++ code we recommend
using the GLOBAL-UNIQUETYPE rule, which takes into account qualifications
of identifiers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_7-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent developer confusion and undefined behavior.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.7 A tag name shall be a unique identifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_7.title: Rule 5.7 (Required) A tag name shall be a unique identifier [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-a.title: Identifiers that define objects with external linkage shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-a.description:
"Regardless of scope, no identifier with static storage duration should
be re-used across any source files in the project. This includes objects
or functions with external linkage and any objects or functions with static
storage class specifier."
[AUTOSAR A2-10-5]

This rule detects non-member objects with external linkage whose names are
reused as object names or as other identifiers.
This rule ignores declarations of objects with the explicit 'extern' keyword.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-a.benefits:
"Enforcing uniqueness of identifier names in this manner helps avoid confusion."
[MISRA C++ 2012]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A2-10-5. An identifier name of a function with static storage duration or a
non-member object with external or internal linkage should not be reused
2. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 2, Rule 2-10-5
3. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.8
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 5.8 Identifiers that define objects or functions with external linkage shall be unique

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-b.title: Identifiers that define functions with external linkage shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-b.description:
"Regardless of scope, no identifier with static storage duration should
be re-used across any source files in the project. This includes objects
or functions with external linkage and any objects or functions with static
storage class specifier."
[AUTOSAR A2-10-5]

This rule detects functions with external linkage whose names are reused as
function names or as other identifiers.
The rule assumes that two functions are duplicates only if both have the same
signature and both are definitions.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8-b.benefits:
"Enforcing uniqueness of identifier names in this manner helps avoid confusion."
[MISRA C++ 2012]

A2-10-5. An identifier name of a function with static storage duration or a
non-member object with external or internal linkage should not be reused

Section 8: Rules, Rule 5.8
4. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8.title: Rule 5.8 (Required) Identifiers that define objects or functions with external linkage shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_8]

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-a.title: Identifiers that define objects with internal linkage shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-a.description:
"Regardless of scope, no identifier with static storage duration should
be re-used across any source files in the project. This includes objects
or functions with external linkage and any objects or functions with static
storage class specifier."
[AUTOSAR A2-10-5]

This rule detects non-member objects with internal linkage whose names are
reused as object names or as other identifiers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-a.benefits:
"Enforcing uniqueness of identifier names in this manner helps avoid confusion."
[MISRA C++ 2012]

A2-10-5. An identifier name of a function with static storage duration or a

MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-b.title: Identifiers that define functions with internal linkage shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-b.description:
"Regardless of scope, no identifier with static storage duration should
be re-used across any source files in the project. This includes objects
or functions with external linkage and any objects or functions with static
storage class specifier."
[AUTOSAR A2-10-5]

This rule detects functions with internal linkage whose names are reused as
function names or as other identifiers.
The rule assumes that two functions are duplicates only if both have the same
signature and both are declarations or definitions.
MISRAC2012-RULE_5_9-b.benefits:
"Enforcing uniqueness of identifier names in this manner helps avoid confusion."
[MISRA C++ 2012]

MISRAC2012-RULE_6_1-a.title: Bit fields shall only be defined to be of type unsigned int or signed int [MISRAC2012-RULE_6_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_6_1-a.description:
"Bit fields shall only be defined to be of type unsigned int or signed int.
Using int is implementation-defined because bit fields of type int can be
either signed or unsigned. The use of enum, short or char types for bit fields
is not allowed because the behaviour is undefined." [MISRA C:2004]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 6.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 6.1 Bit-fields shall only be declared with an appropriate type
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6.4
4. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 111
5. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
INT12-C. Do not make assumptions about the type of a plain int bit-field
when used in an expression

MISRAC2012-RULE_6_1.title: Rule 6.1 (Required) Bit-fields shall only be declared with an appropriate type [MISRAC2012-RULE_6_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_6_2-a.title: Named bit-fields with signed integer type shall have a length of more than one bit [MISRAC2012-RULE_6_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_6_2-a.description:
This rule reports a violation if a named bit-field with signed type is declared
with width smaller than 2 bits.

With signed type one bit is used for the sign bit, which leaves 0 bits for the
value, so it can not hold meaningful values.

Such code is most likely an error.
MISRAC2012-RULE_6_2-a.benefits: Rule prevents the potential pitfalls and erroneous code.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M9-6-4. Named bit-fields with signed integer type shall have a length of
more than one bit
2. ISO/IEC 14882:2003 Programming languages -- C++
Section 9.6 Bit-fields
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
Section 6.7.2.1 Structure and union specifiers
4. MISRA C++:2008 Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 9, Rule 9-6-4
5. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 12.2.3 A named bit-field with signed integer type shall not have a length of one bit
6. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 6.2
7. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 6.2 Single-bit named bit-fields shall not be of a signed type
8. MISRA Guidelines for the use of the C language in vehicle based software
Rule 112
9. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 6, Rule 6.4

MISRAC2012-RULE_6_2.title: Rule 6.2 (Required) Single-bit named bit fields shall not be of a signed type [MISRAC2012-RULE_6_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3-a.title: A member of a union shall not be declared as a bit-field [MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3-a.description:
"The exact bitwise position of a bit field within a storage unit
is implementation defined. Therefore, if two bit fields are declared
such that they fit within the same storage unit of a union, the compiler
is not required to overlay them over one another beginning from the starting
bit of the storage unit.
If the union is used for type-punning, it is therefore unclear which bits
of the previously-stored value will be accessed by the bit field.
If the union is not intended to be used for type-punning, there is no point
in declaring the members as bit fields, because no space will be saved
(a complete storage unit will need to be allocated within the union anyway)."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects member variables in unions declared as bit-fields.
MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent implementation-defined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3-a.example_repair: Do not declare member variables in unions as bit-fields.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.3 Rule 6.3

Rule 6.3 A bit-field shall not be declared as a member of a union

MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3.title: Rule 6.3 (Required) A bit field shall not be declared as a member of a union [MISRAC2012-RULE_6_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_1-a.title: Octal constants (other than zero) shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_1-a.description:
"Any integer constant beginning with a '0' (zero) is treated as octal.
Because of this, it is possible for a zero-prefixed constant that is intended
to be a decimal number to be incorrectly entered as an octal number, contrary
to developer expectations.

The integer constant zero (written as a single numeric digit) is, strictly
speaking, an octal constant, but is a permitted exception to this rule."
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_1-a.benefits:
Rule increases readability and maintainability.
Rule prevents using implementation-defined values.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M2-13-2. Octal constants (other than zero) and octal escape sequences
(other than "\0" ) shall not be used
2.  High Integrity C++ Coding Standard
2.5.2. Do not use octal constants (other than zero)

Chapter 4.18 Constants, AV Rule 149

Rule 5.13.3 Octal constants shall not be used

Section 8: Rules, Rule 7.1

Rule 7.1 Octal constants shall not be used

Chapter 6, Section 7, Rule 7.1
9.  Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 19
10. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
DCL18-C. Do not begin integer constants with 0 when specifying a decimal
value

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_1.title: Rule 7.1 (Required) Octal constants shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_2-a.title: A 'U' suffix shall be applied to all constants of unsigned type [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_2-a.description:
"The type of an integer constant is a potential source of confusion,
because it is dependent on a complex combination of factors including:
- The magnitude of the constant
- The implemented sizes of the integer types
- The presence of any suffixes
- The number base in which the value is expressed (i.e. decimal, octal or
  hexadecimal)

Signedness of constants should be explicit. Consistent signedness is an
important principle in constructing well formed expressions. If a constant
is of an unsigned type, it is helpful to avoid ambiguity by applying a “U”
suffix. When applied to larger values, the suffix may be redundant (in the
sense that it does not influence the type of the constant); however its
presence is a valuable contribution towards clarity."

The rule checks if integer constant of unsigned type has 'U' or 'u' suffix.

See also: MISRA-018_a, MISRA-018_b, MISRA-018_c, MISRA-018_d, MISRA2004-10_1_a
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_2-a.example_violation:
/* the type depends from the implemented size of the various integer types */
long lVar = 2147483648;     /* Violation */
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 5.13.4 Unsigned integer literals shall be appropriately suffixed
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 7.2
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 7.2 A "u" or "U" suffix shall be applied to all integer constants that are represented in an unsigned type
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 10
5. Origin: Misra Guidelines - Rule 18

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_2.title: Rule 7.2 (Required) A "u" or "U" suffix shall be applied to all integer constants that are represented in an unsigned type [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_3-a.title: Use capital 'L' instead of lowercase 'l' to indicate long [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_3-a.description:
"Using the uppercase suffix 'L' removes the potential ambiguity between
'1' (digit 1) and 'l' (letter 'el') when declaring literals."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 7.3]

This rule detects when the lowercase 'l' is used in a literal suffix.
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_3-a.benefits: The rule prevents confusions and improves the readability of code.
references: 1. JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.4 Environment, AV Rule 14
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 7.3
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 7.3 The lowercase character "l" shall not be used in a literal suffix

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_3.title: Rule 7.3 (Required) The lowercase character "l" shall not be used in a literal suffix [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_4-a.title: A string literal shall not be modified [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_4-a.description:
"The declaration

      char s[] = "abc", t[3] = "abc";

 defines 'plain' char array objects 's' and 't' whose elements are initialized
 with character string literals. This declaration is identical to

      char s[] = { 'a', 'b', 'c', '\0' },
      t[] = { 'a', 'b', 'c' };

 The contents of the arrays are modifiable.

 On the other hand, the declaration

      char *p = "abc";

 defines p with type 'pointer to char' and initializes it to point to an object
 with type 'array of char' with length 4 whose elements are initialized with
 a character string literal. If an attempt is made to use p to modify
 the contents of the array, the behavior is undefined."

"Since string literals are constant, they should only be assigned
 to constant pointers"

The violation is reported by rule if string literal or wide string literal:
  - is modified directly
  - initializes pointer to non-const object
  - is assigned to pointer to non-const object
  - is passed to function as pointer to non-const object or as non-const array
  - is returned from function as pointer to non-const object
  - is casted to pointer to non-const object
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A2-13-4. String literals shall not be assigned to non-constant pointers
2.  General Principles of Software Validation;
Final Guidance for Industry and FDA Staff
Document issued on: January 11, 2002

6.1.4 String literals

6.4.5 String literals

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5-a.title: Arguments of integer-constant macros should be decimal, octal, or hexadecimal constants with appropriate values [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5-a.description:
This rule detects when the argument of a macro INT(N)_C or UINT(N)_C (where (N)
is 8,16,32,64, or MAX) fails to satisfy any of the following conditions:
- The argument must be an unsuffixed integer constant (decimal, octal, or
  hexadecimal).
- The value of the argument must not exceed the width indicated by the name of
  the macro.
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5-a.example_repair:
To fix the code, use macro arguments that are unsuffixed integer constants with
values that do not exceed the width indicated by the name of a given macro.

#include <stdint.h>
void f1() {
    INT32_C  ( 10 );                      // OK
    UINT8_C  ( 100 );                     // OK
    INT32_C  ( 20 );                      // OK
    INT32_C  ( 0xa );                     // OK
    INT16_C  ( -1 );                      // OK
    UINT16_C ( 256 );                     // OK
    INT16_C  ( -129 );                    // OK
    INT32_C  ( 0x10001 );                 // OK
}
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.4 Rule 7.5
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 7.5 The argument of an integer constant macro shall have an appropriate form
3. ISO/IEC 9899:2011 Programming languages -- C
7.20.4 Macros for integer constants

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5.title: Rule 7.5 (Mandatory) The argument of an integer constant macro shall have an appropriate form [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_7_6-a.title: The small integer variants of the minimum-width integer constant macros should not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_7_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_6-a.description:
"The macro INTN_C(value) shall expand to an integer constant expression
corresponding to the type int_leastN_t. The macro UINTN_C(value) shall
expand to an integer constant expression corresponding to the type
uint_leastN_t." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"Each invocation of one of these macros shall expand to an integer constant
expression suitable for use in #if preprocessing directives. The type of the
expression shall have the same type as would an expression of the corresponding
type converted according to the integer promotions. The value of the expression
shall be that of the argument." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

Due to integer promotions, the results of macro expansions may be of type 'int'
instead of the expected smaller integer type when a macro for minimum-width
integer constants with '8' or '16' in the name is used. In this case, the
macro name may be confusing to the user.

This rule detects when identifiers with names of the small integer variants of
the minimum-width integer constant macros (INT8_C, UINT8_C, INT16_C, UINT16_C)
are used.
MISRAC2012-RULE_7_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected behavior and confusions.

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-a.title: Whenever a function is declared or defined, its type shall be explicitly stated [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-a.description:
If return types are not explicitly declared, functions will implicitly receive
int as the return type. However, the compiler will still generate a warning
for a missing return type. To avoid confusion, functions that do not return
a value should specify void as the return type. This rule detects if you do not
explicitly declare return types in functions.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.1 Types shall be explicitly specified
3. Misra Guidelines - Rule 75
4. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 8
5. [Ellemtel 92] "Programming in C++: Rules and Recommendations"
Ellemtel Telecommunication Systems Laboratories, Sweden.
From: 6 Style - 6.2 Functions - Rec. 20
From: 9 Functions - 9.4 Return types and Values - Rule. 33

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-b.title: Whenever an object is declared or defined, its type shall be explicitly stated [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-b.description:
The rule reports violations on declarations of parameters and variables where
the type is not explicitly stated.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-b.example_violation:
extern x;     /* Violation  */
const y;      /* Violation  */
z;            /* Violation  */

void foo(const i);  /* Violation  */
void foo(const i){} /* Violation  */
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1-b.example_repair:
extern int x;     /* OK  */
const int y;      /* OK  */
int z;            /* OK  */

void foo(const int i);  /* OK  */
void foo(const int i){} /* OK  */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.1
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.1 Types shall be explicitly specified
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 8

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1.title: Rule 8.1 (Required) Types shall be explicitly specified [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_1]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10-a.title: An inline function shall be declared with the static storage class [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10-a.description:
"If an inline function is declared with external linkage but not defined
in the same translation unit, the behaviour is undefined. A call to an inline
function declared with external linkage may call the external definition
of the function, or it may use the inline definition. Although this should
not affect the behaviour of the called function, it might affect execution
timing and therefore have an impact on a real-time program." [MISRA C:2012]

"Any function with internal linkage can be an inline function. For a function
with external linkage, the following restrictions apply: If a function
is declared with an inline function specifier, then it shall also be defined
in the same translation unit." [ISO/IEC 9899:1999]

The rule reports a violation if a function is declared as inline and is not
declared as static.

The rule is enabled only for C language (In C++ the same restrictions apply
to inline functions with internal and external linkage).
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10-a.example_violation:
/* The code is compilable with compilers which support
   the 'inline' function specifier */
inline int fn1();                 /* Violation - non-static inline function */
inline int fn1(){ return 0; }     /* Violation - non-static inline function */
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10-a.example_repair:
/* The code is compilable with compilers which support
   the 'inline' function specifier */
static inline int fn1();             /* OK */
static inline int fn1(){ return 0; } /* OK */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.10
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.10 An inline function shall be declared with the static storage class
3. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.4 Function specifiers

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10.title: Rule 8.10 (Required) An inline function shall be declared with the static storage class [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_10]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_11-a.title: When an array with external linkage is declared, its size should be explicitly specified [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_11-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_11-a.description:
This rule detects declarations of arrays with external linkage without
a specified size.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_11-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior and improves the safety of code.
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
A3-1-4. When an array with external linkage is declared, its size shall be
stated explicitly
2. MISRA C++:2008 - Guidelines for the use of the C++ language in critical
systems, Chapter 6, Section 3, Rule 3-1-3
3. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 6.0.2 When an array with external linkage is declared, its size should be explicitly specified
4. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.11

Rule 8.11 When an array with external linkage is declared, its size should be explicitly specified

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a.title: Within an enumerator list, the value of an implicitly-specified enumeration constant shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a.description:
This rule reports when an implicitly-specified enumeration constant
has the same value as another enumeration constant from the same enumeration.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a.benefits:
The rule improves the readability of code and prevents unexpected behavior.

"If implicitly-specified and explicitly-specified constants are mixed within
an enumeration list, it is possible for values to be duplicated.
Such duplication may be unintentional and may give rise to unexpected behaviour.
This rule requires that any duplication of enumeration constants be made
explicit, thus making the intent clear." [MISRA C++:2023]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a.example_violation:
enum En1 {E11 = 1, E12, E13 = 2};      // Violation
enum En2 {E21 = -2, E22, E23 = -1};    // Violation
enum En3 {E31, E32, E33 = E31};        // OK if the exception is allowed, Violation otherwise
enum En4 {E41, E42 = E31};             // Violation - constant from another enum
enum En5 {E51 = 1, E52, E53 = E51 + 1};// Violation - the exception permits
                                       // using only the name of the constant
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a.example_repair:
enum En1 {E11 = 1, E12 = 1, E13 = 2};    // OK
enum En2 {E21 = -2, E22 = -1, E23 = -1}; // OK
enum En3 {E31 = 0, E32, E33 = E31};      // OK
enum En4 {E41 = 1, E42 = E41};           // OK
enum En5 {E51 = 1, E52, E53 = E52};      // OK if the exception is allowed, Violation otherwise
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12-a.parameters:
* "Allow exception for explicitly-specified enumeration constants": if enabled,
  it permits to define an implicitly-specified enumeration constant if the
  other enumeration constants with the same value have explicitly-specified
  values using only the name of an (even implicitly-specified) enumeration
  constant from the same enumeration. Such definition makes it unambiguous that
  the equality of these constants has been introduced intentionally.
references: 1. MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 11.6.3 Within an enumerator list, the value of an implicitly-specified
enumeration constant shall be unique
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.12
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.12 Within an enumerator list, the value of an implicitly-specified enumeration constant shall be unique

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12.title: Rule 8.12 (Required) Within an enumerator list, the value of an implicitly-specified enumeration constant shall be unique [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_12]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-a.title: A pointer parameter in a function prototype should be declared as pointer to const if the pointer is not used to modify the addressed object [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-a.description:
"A pointer parameter in a function prototype should be declared as pointer to
const if the pointer is not used to modify the addressed object. The const
qualification should be applied to the object pointed to, not to the pointer,
since it is the object itself that is being protected."
See also: CODSTA-14, CODSTA-CPP-43, CODSTA-CPP-53, MISRA-104
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-a.benefits:
Rule prevents unintentional change of data and improves precision in the
definition of the function interface.
references: 1.  AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M7-1-2. A pointer or reference parameter in a function shall be declared as
pointer to const or reference to const if the corresponding object is not
modified
2.  JOINT STRIKE FIGHTER, AIR VEHICLE, C++ CODING STANDARDS
Chapter 4.13 Function, AV Rule 118

systems, Chapter 6, Section 7, Rule 7-1-2
4.  MISRA C++:2023 Guidelines for the use of C++17 in critical systems
Rule 10.1.1 The target type of a pointer or lvalue reference parameter should be const-qualified appropriately

Rule 8.13 A pointer should point to a const-qualified type whenever possible

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-b.title: Declare a type of parameter as typedef to pointer to const if the pointer is not used to modify the addressed object [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-b.description:
The rule reports a violation if a parameter in a function prototype is declared
as typedef to pointer to non-const object and the pointer is not used to modify
the addressed object. Then the type of parameter could be changed to typedef
to pointer to const object.
See also: CODSTA-14, CODSTA-CPP-43, CODSTA-CPP-53, MISRA-104
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_13-b.benefits:
Rule prevents unintentional change of data and improves precision in the
definition of the function interface.

modified

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_14-a.title: The restrict type qualifier shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_14-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_14-a.description:
"When used with care the restrict type qualifier may improve the efficiency
of code generated by a compiler. It may also allow improved static analysis.
However, to use the restrict type qualifier the programmer must be sure that
the memory areas operated on by two or more pointers do not overlap.
There is a significant risk that a compiler will generate code that does not
behave as expected if restrict is used incorrectly."

The rule reports a violation on each use of 'restrict' type qualifier in code.
Any compiler extensions (e.g. __restrict) are not reported.

The rule is enabled only for C language (C++ standard does not define 'restrict'
type qualifier).
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_14-a.example_violation:
/* The code is compilable with compilers which support
   the 'restrict' type qualifier */
typedef int* restrict INT_R;     /* Violation */
INT_R var1;                      /* OK - Violation on typedef declaration */
void foo(void* restrict p){}     /* Violation */

/* If 'restrict' is not a type qualifier (C90) this defines a variable */
  int restrict = 0;              /* Violation */
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.3.1 Formal definition of restrict
2. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.14
3. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.14 The restrict type qualifier shall not be used
4. SEI CERT C Coding Standard
EXP43-C. Avoid undefined behavior when using restrict-qualified pointers

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_14.title: Rule 8.14 (Required) The restrict type qualifier shall not be used [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_14]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-a.title: Explicit alignment specifiers in all declarations of an object with external linkage shall be the same [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-a.description:
"If the definition of an object has an alignment specifier, any other
declaration of that object shall either specify equivalent alignment
or have no alignment specifier. If the definition of an object does
not have an alignment specifier, any other declaration of that object
shall also have no alignment specifier." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"If any declaration (including the definition) of an object includes an explicit
alignment specification, all other declarations of the same object shall also
include the same explicit alignment specification.
Two alignment specifications are the same if they are lexically identical after
macro expansion.
This rule applies both to alignments specified directly by an expression,
and by naming a type." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when declarations of the same variable with external linkage
have different alignment specifiers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve code consistency and maintainability.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.5 Rule 8.15
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.15 All declarations of an object with an explicit alignment specification shall specify the same alignment
3. Programming languages -- C ISO/IEC 9899:2011
6.7.5 Alignment specifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-b.title: Explicit alignment specifiers in all declarations of an object with internal linkage shall be the same [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-b.description:
"If the definition of an object has an alignment specifier, any other
declaration of that object shall either specify equivalent alignment
or have no alignment specifier. If the definition of an object does
not have an alignment specifier, any other declaration of that object
shall also have no alignment specifier." [ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"If any declaration (including the definition) of an object includes an explicit
alignment specification, all other declarations of the same object shall also
include the same explicit alignment specification.
Two alignment specifications are the same if they are lexically identical after
macro expansion.
This rule applies both to alignments specified directly by an expression,
and by naming a type." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule detects when declarations of the same variable with internal linkage
have different alignment specifiers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15-b.benefits:
"Since an object with internal linkage is only accessible by name from within
a single translation unit, there is no direct risk of incompatible alignment
specifications causing undefined behaviour. However, this rule reduces the risk
of accidentally introducing undefined behaviour during maintenance if the code
is later modified to give an object external linkage instead."
[MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

This rule helps you improve code consistency and maintainability.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.5 Rule 8.15
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.15 All declarations of an object with an explicit alignment specification shall specify the same alignment
3. Programming languages -- C ISO/IEC 9899:2011
6.7.5 Alignment specifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15.title: Rule 8.15 (Required) All declarations of an object with an explicit alignment specification shall specify the same alignment [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_15]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16-a.title: The zero-alignment specifier should not be used in an object declaration [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16-a.description:
"The alignment requirement of an entity is the strictest nonzero
alignment specified by its alignment-specifiers, if any; otherwise,
the alignment-specifiers have no effect." [ISO/IEC 14882:2017]

"If the alignment of an object is important, it should be specified
explicitly." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

The rule detects zero-alignment specifiers in object declarations.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve code readability and maintainability.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16-a.example_violation:
#include <stdalign.h>

#define SIZE 0

alignas (SIZE) int g1;                           // Violation
alignas (0) int g2;                              // Violation
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16-a.example_repair:
You can fix the code by removing the zero-alignment specifier.

#include <stdalign.h>

int g1;                                          // OK
alignas (8) int g2;                              // OK
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.5 Rule 8.16
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.16 The alignment specification of zero should not appear in an object declaration
3. Programming languages -- C ISO/IEC 9899:2011
6.7.5 Alignment specifier

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16.title: Rule 8.16 (Advisory) The alignment specification of zero should not appear in an object declaration [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_16]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_17-a.title: A declaration of an object should contain at most one explicit alignment specifier [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_17-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_17-a.description:
"When multiple alignment specifiers occur in a declaration, the effective
alignment requirement is the strictest specified alignment."[ISO/IEC 9899:2011]

"If separate conditions require different minimum permitted alignments
for an object, they should be combined explicitly by the expression controlling
the specifier. Otherwise, the conflicting alignment specifiers risk obscuring
the intent of the declaration from a human reviewer." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3]

The rule detects declarations of variables with more than one alignment
specifier. The rule checks the standard alignment specifiers 'alignas'
and '_Alignas'.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_17-a.benefits: This rule helps you improve code readability and maintainability.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 3 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018
Phase 2 - New C11/C18 Features
2.3 Section 8 - Rules, 2.3.5 Rule 8.17
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.17 At most one explicit alignment specifier should appear in an object declaration

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_17.title: Rule 8.17 (Advisory) At most one explicit alignment specifier should appear in an object declaration [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_17]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-a.title: Identifiers shall be given for all of the parameters in a function prototype declaration [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-a.description:
"This rule requires that names be specified for all the parameters
in a declaration. The parameter names can provide useful information
regarding the function interface and a mismatch between a declaration
and definition might be indicative of a programming error."[MISRA C:2012]

See also: CODSTA-158, CODSTA-159
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-a.benefits: The rule improves readability and clarity of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-a.example_repair:
void foo1(int p);       /* OK */

/* Examples not reported by this rule */
void foo2(int){}        /* OK - this code is compilable in C++ only */
void foo3(void);        /* OK */
void foo4(int p, ...);  /* OK */
typedef void foot(int); /* OK - reported by rule CODSTA-158 */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.2
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.2 Function types shall be in prototype form with named parameters
3. MISRA-C:2004 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Chapter 6, Section 16

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-b.title: Function types shall have named parameters [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-b.description:
"This rule requires that names be specified for all the parameters
in a declaration. The parameter names can provide useful information
regarding the function interface and a mismatch between a declaration
and definition might be indicative of a programming error."[MISRA C:2012]

See also: MISRA2004-16_3, CODSTA-159
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-b.benefits: The rule improves readability and clarity of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-b.example_violation:
typedef void foot(int*);        /* Violation */

void foo1(void (*fp1)(int)) {   /* Violation */
  void (*fp2)(float);           /* Violation */
  fp1 = (void (*)(int))fp2;     /* Violation */
}
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-b.example_repair:
typedef void foot(int* p1);       /* OK */

void foo1(void (*fp1)(int p)) {   /* OK */
  void (*fp2)(float f);           /* OK */
  fp1 = (void (*)(int p))fp2;     /* OK */
}

/* Examples not reported by this rule */
void foo2(int);  /* OK - See rule MISRA2004-16_3 */
void foo3();     /* OK - See rule CODSTA-159 */
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.2
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.2 Function types shall be in prototype form with named parameters

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-c.title: Function types shall be in prototype form [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-c.description:
This rule checks if a function types are in prototype form.
It reports a violations if a declaration of a function type:
- is in K&R style, or
- has an empty parameter list

See also: MISRA2004-16_3, CODSTA-158
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2-c.benefits:
"The mismatch between the number of arguments and parameters, their types
and the expected and actual return type of a function provides potential
for undefined behaviour." [MISRA C:2012]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2.title: Rule 8.2 (Required) Function types shall be in prototype form with named parameters [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_2]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-a.title: If objects or functions are declared more than once their types shall be compatible [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-a.description:
"Two types have compatible type if their types are the same. For two qualified
types to be compatible, both shall have the identically qualified version
of a compatible type. For two pointer types to be compatible, both shall
be identically qualified and both shall be pointers to compatible types.
For two array types to be compatible, both shall have compatible element types,
and if both size specifiers are present, and are integer constant expressions,
then both size specifiers shall have the same constant value.
For two function types to be compatible, both shall specify compatible return
types. Moreover, the parameter type lists, if both are present, shall agree
in the number of parameters and in use of the ellipsis terminator;
corresponding parameters shall have compatible types."

The rule reports a violation if two declarations of object or function are not
compatible. The rule assumes that typedefs to the same type are compatible
and signed short/int/long type is the same as short/int/long type.

See also: MISRA2004-8_3_a, MISRA2004-8_3_b, CODSTA-67

DCL40-C. Do not create incompatible declarations of the same function or
object

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-b.title: The identifiers used in the declaration and definition of a function shall be identical [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-b.description:
If identifiers are given for any of the parameters, then the identifiers used
in the declaration and definition shall be identical.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-b.benefits: Rule improves readability and clarity of code.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-b.example_violation:
void foo(int a, int b);
void foo(int x, int y ) {}  /* Violation */
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M8-4-2. The identifiers used for the parameters in a re-declaration of a
function shall be identical to those in the declaration

8.2.1. Make parameter names absent or identical in all declarations

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-c.title: All declarations of an object or function shall have compatible types [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-c]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-c.description:
This rule detects when declarations of an object or function with external
linkage in different translation units do not have compatible types.

"Compatible types are types that, for the purpose of declaration matching,
are treated as the same. Two identical types are compatible but two compatible
types need not be identical. For example, 'short int' and 'short' are
compatible." [MISRA C++:2008 Appendix C: Glossary]

"The easiest way of ensuring object or function types are compatible
is to make the declarations identical." [MISRA C++:2008 Rule 3-2-1]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-c.benefits:
"It is undefined behaviour if the declarations of an object or function
in two different translation units do not have compatible types."
[MISRA C++:2008 Rule 3-2-1]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-c.example_violation:
Assumption: the testing scope contains only 'test1.cpp' and 'test2.cpp'.

/* test1.cpp */
extern int g1;                   /* Violation */
extern short g2[5];              /* Violation */
extern void (*fp)(int*, int*);   /* Violation */
extern int* f1(int*);            /* Violation */
                                    
                              
/* test2.cpp */
extern short g1;                 /* Violation */
extern short g2[4];              /* Violation */
extern void (*fp)(char*, int*);  /* Violation */
extern int* const f1(int*);      /* Violation */
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_3-c.example_repair:
Assumption: the testing scope contains only 'test1.cpp' and 'test2.cpp'.

/* test1.cpp */
extern int g1;                    /* OK */
extern short g2[5];               /* OK */
extern void (*fp)(char*, int*);   /* OK */
extern int* f1(int*);             /* OK */
                                    
                              
/* test2.cpp */
extern int g1;                    /* OK */
extern short g2[5];               /* OK */            
extern void (*fp)(char*, int*);   /* OK */
extern int* f1(int*);             /* OK */

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_4-a.title: A declaration shall be visible when an object or function with external linkage is defined [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_4-a.description:
"If a declaration for an object or function is visible when that object
or function is defined, a compiler must check that the declaration
and definition are compatible. In the presence of function prototypes
checking extends to the number and type of function parameters."
[MISRA C:2012]

This rule reports a violation if an object or a function with external linkage
does not have a declaration that precedes its definition. The rule does not
check if a declaration is compatible. Violations on incompatible declarations
are reported by rule MISRA2004-8_4.

This rule is only enabled for C language and does not report violations
for the 'main' function.

See also: MISRA-023, MISRA-071_a, MISRA2004-8_4

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_4-b.title: If objects or functions are declared more than once their types shall be compatible [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_4-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_4-b.description:
"Two types have compatible type if their types are the same. For two qualified
types to be compatible, both shall have the identically qualified version
of a compatible type. For two pointer types to be compatible, both shall
be identically qualified and both shall be pointers to compatible types.
For two array types to be compatible, both shall have compatible element types,
and if both size specifiers are present, and are integer constant expressions,
then both size specifiers shall have the same constant value.
For two function types to be compatible, both shall specify compatible return
types. Moreover, the parameter type lists, if both are present, shall agree
in the number of parameters and in use of the ellipsis terminator;
corresponding parameters shall have compatible types."

The rule reports a violation if two declarations of object or function are not
compatible. The rule assumes that typedefs to the same type are compatible
and signed short/int/long type is the same as short/int/long type.

See also: MISRA2004-8_3_a, MISRA2004-8_3_b, CODSTA-67

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_5-a.title: An external object or function shall not have more than one non-defining declaration in translation unit [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_5-a.description:
The rule reports a violation if in one translation unit more than one external
declaration of object or function exists.

This rule applies to non-defining declarations only.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_5-a.benefits:
Having only single declaration in one and only one header file ensures
consistency between declaration and definition and between declarations
in different translation units.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_5-a.example_violation:
/* file.h */
extern int x1;           /* Violation */
extern int x2;           /* Violation */
extern void foo1(void);  /* Violation */


/* file.c */
#include "file.h"
extern int x1;           /* Violation */
int x2;                  /* OK - external definition */
int x2;                  /* Violation - external declaration */
extern void foo1(void);  /* Violation */
void foo2(void);         /* Violation */
void foo2(void);         /* Violation */
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_5-a.example_repair:
/* file.h */
extern int x1;           /* OK */
extern int x2;           /* OK */
extern void foo1(void);  /* OK */
void foo2(void);         /* OK */
static void foos(void);  /* OK */

/* file.c */
#include "file.h"
int x1;                  /* OK - external definition */
int x2 = 0;              /* OK - external definition */
void foo1(void){};       /* OK */
void foo2(void){};       /* OK */
static void foos(void);  /* OK */

Rule 8.5 An external object or function shall be declared once in one and only one file

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_6-a.title: An identifier with external linkage shall have no more then one external definition [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_6-a.description:
An identifier with external linkage shall have no more then one external definition [GLOBAL-ONEEXTERNDEF]


DESCRIPTION


"The behaviour is undefined if an identifier is used for which multiple
definitions exist (in different files) or no definition exists at all. Multiple
definitions in different files are not permitted by this rule even if the
definitions are the same. It is undefined behaviour if the declarations are
different, or initialize the identifier to different values."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 8.6]

This rule detects when identifiers with external linkage (objects or functions)
have more than one external definition.

See also: GLOBAL-NOEXTERNDEF

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_6-b.title: An identifier with external linkage shall have external definition [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_6-b]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_6-b.description:
An identifier with external linkage shall have external definition [GLOBAL-NOEXTERNDEF]


DESCRIPTION


"The behaviour is undefined if an identifier is used for which multiple
definitions exist (in different files) or no definition exists at all."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 8.6]

This rule detects when identifiers with external linkage (objects or functions)
do not have any external definition.

See also: GLOBAL-ONEEXTERNDEF

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_7-a.title: Functions and objects should not be defined with external linkage if they are referenced in only one translation unit [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_7-a.description:
This rule detects cases when identifiers with external linkage (objects
or functions) are used only in the translation unit in which they are
defined. Such identifiers should not have external linkage.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_7-a.benefits:
"Restricting the visibility of an object by giving it internal linkage
or no linkage reduces the chance that it might be accessed inadvertently.
Similarly, reducing the visibility of a function by giving it internal
linkage reduces the chance of it being called inadvertently.

Compliance with this rule also avoids any possibility of confusion between
an identifier and an identical identifier in another translation unit
or a library" [MISRA C:2012 Rule 8.7]
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Section 8: Rules, Rule 8.7
2. MISRA C:2025 Guidelines for the use of the C language in critical systems
Rule 8.7 Functions and objects should not be defined with external linkage if they are referenced in only one translation unit

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_7.title: Rule 8.7 (Advisory) Functions and objects should not be defined with external linkage if they are referenced in only one translation unit [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_7]

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_8-a.title: The static storage class specifier shall be used in definitions and declarations of objects and functions that have internal linkage [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_8-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_8-a.description:
The C standard specifies that if an identifier declared with the storage-class
specifier 'extern' in a scope in which a prior declaration of that identifier
is visible, the linkage of that identifier is the same as the linkage of the
prior declaration.

For example, if a function is first declared as 'static' and then defined with
'extern' or without any specifier at all, the linkage will be internal. This
can be confusing: if you see only the definition, you might wrongly assume
that the function has external linkage.

It is a good practice to apply the 'static' storage class specifier to all
objects and functions with internal linkage.

See also: CODSTA-81
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_8-a.benefits:
Mitigates the risk of incorrectly interpreting linkage of objects and
functions.

MISRAC2012-RULE_8_9-a.title: Objects shall be defined at block scope if they are only accessed from within a single function [MISRAC2012-RULE_8_9-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_9-a.description:
"Declaring an object at block scope reduces the possibility that the object
might be accessed inadvertently and makes clear the intention that it should
not be accessed elsewhere.
Within a function, whether objects are declared at the outermost or innermost
block is largely a matter of style." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 8.9 with Amendment 4]

This rule detects when a global variable with internal linkage is only used
inside a single function in a translation unit.
MISRAC2012-RULE_8_9-a.benefits: The rule helps you improve code readability and maintainability.

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_1-a.title: Avoid use before initialization [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_1-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_1-a.description:
Objects with static storage duration are always initialized - either explicitly or implicitly.
Objects with automatic storage duration, if not initialized explicitly, have indeterminate value.
Reading the value of such an object results in undefined behavior.

This rule reports a violation when:
 - a non-initialized object is used, or
 - a non-initialized element of an array is used, or
 - a non-initialized field of a class/struct is used, or
 - a field of a non-initialized union object is used

See also the PARAMETERS section for other situations when the rule may report violations.

For the purpose of this rule, an object is non-initialized when:
 - it is an uninitialized object of numeric, enum or union type, or
 - it is an array where all elements are non-initialized, or
 - it is a class/struct object with all non-static members non-initialized
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_1-a.security_relevance:
Reading the value of a non-initialized object results in undefined behavior.
Additionally, it can allow an attacker to control the behavior of the program, or to gain access to sensitive data.
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_1-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent undefined behavior.
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_1-a.parameters:
The "Report when non-initialized object is passed to a third-party function as const" 
parameter allows you to specify if the rule should trigger when a non-initialized object 
is passed by pointer or reference to const to a function whose source code is not available.
Since such an object cannot be modified inside the function, it is assumed that its value will be read.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Report when non-initialized structure is copied" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a non-initialized object of class/struct/union type is:
 - used as the rhs operand of the assignment operator, or
 - passed to a function by value, or
 - returned from a function by value, or
 - thrown by value.
This parameter is enabled by default.

The "Report when partially-initialized object is copied" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a partially-initialized object is:
 - used as the rhs operand of the assignment operator, or
 - passed to a function by value, or
 - returned from a function by value, or
 - thrown by value.

The "Report when potentially non-initialized element of an array is used" parameter allows you to
specify if the rule should trigger when a potentially non-initialized element of an array is used.
This parameter is disabled by default.

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_2-a.title: The initializer for an aggregate or union shall be enclosed in braces [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_2-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_2-a.description:
"ISO C requires initializer lists for arrays, structures and union types to be
enclosed in a single pair of braces (though the behaviour if this is not done
is undefined). The rule given here goes further in requiring the use
of additional braces to indicate nested structures." [MISRA C:2004 Rule 9.2]

This rule detects when an aggregate initializer for an array, class,
structure or union is not explicitly enclosed in braces.

See also: MISRA2004-9_2_b, MISRA2004-9_2_c
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_2-a.benefits:
"Using braces to indicate initialization of subobjects improves the clarity
of code and forces programmers to consider the initialization of elements
in complex data structures such as multi-dimensional arrays or arrays
of structures." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 9.2]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M8-5-2. Braces shall be used to indicate and match the structure in the
non-zero initialization of arrays and structures

8.4.2. Ensure that a braced aggregate initializer matches the layout of the
aggregate object

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_3-a.title: Arrays shall not be partially initialized [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_3-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_3-a.description:
"If any element of an array object or subobject is explicitly initialized,
then the entire object or subobject shall be explicitly initialized."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 9.3]

This rule detects when an aggregate initializer for an array object
is partially initialized.

See also: MISRA2004-9_2, MISRA2004-9_2_c
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_3-a.benefits:
"Providing an explicit initialization for each element of an array makes
it clear that every element has been considered." [MISRA C:2012 Rule 9.3]
references: 1. AUTOSAR C++14 Coding Guidelines
M8-5-2. Braces shall be used to indicate and match the structure in the
non-zero initialization of arrays and structures

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_3.title: Rule 9.3 (Required) Arrays shall not be partially initialized [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_3]

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_4-a.title: An element of an object shall not be initialized more than once [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_4-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_4-a.description:
"An aggregate initializer shall not contain two designators that refer to the
same sub-object. An aggregate initializer shall not allow the current object to
implicitly initialize an element that has been initialized previously in the
initializer list." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4]

This rule detects cases when an array element or a member of a structure
or an union is initialized more than once. An element can be initialized
more than once only with designated initializers.
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_4-a.benefits:
"Care is required when using designated initializers since the initialization
of object elements can be inadvertently repeated. The C Standard specifies that
the value produced by the syntactically-last initializer referring to an
element in the list is used, overriding any preceding initializers for that
element. The Standard leaves unspecified whether overridden initializers are
evaluated, and therefore whether or not any side effects in the initializing
expressions occur or not." [MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4]
references: 1. ISO/IEC 9899:1999 Programming languages -- C
6.7.8 Initialization

Rule 9.4 An element of an object shall not be initialized more than once

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_4.title: Rule 9.4 (Required) An element of an object shall not be initialized more than once [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_4]

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_5-a.title: Where designated initializers are used to initialize an array object the size of the array shall be specified explicitly [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_5-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_5-a.description:
"Where designated initializers are used to initialize an array object
the size of the array shall be specified explicitly.

If the size of an array is not specified explicitly, it is determined
by the highest index of any of the elements that are initialized."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 9.5]

This rule detects when designated initializers are used to initialize
an array object, but the size of the array is unknown.
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_5-a.benefits:
"When using designated initializers it may not always be clear which
initializer has the highest index, especially when the initializer contains
a large number of elements. To make the intent clear, the array size shall be
declared explicitly. This provides some protection if, during development
of the program, the indices of the initialized elements are changed as it is
a constraint violation to initialize an element outside the bounds of an array."
[MISRA C:2012 Rule 9.5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_5.title: Rule 9.5 (Required) Where designated initializers are used to initialize an array object the size of the array shall be specified explicitly [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_5]

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_6-a.title: An initializer using chained designators shall not contain initializers without designators [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_6-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_6-a.description:
When an aggregate initializer uses in an initializer list a chained designator
to specify an element in a sub-object, but not all elements in that list have
explicit designators, then it can be not clear which element of the object is
being initialized. Using explicit designators for each initializer at the same
level makes the initializer more clear for the programmer.

This rule detects when an element of the object is initialized using
a chained designator and some element at the same level is initialized
without a designator.
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_6-a.benefits: This rule helps you prevent unexpected behavior and confusions.
references: 1. MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018

MISRAC2012-RULE_9_7-a.title: Atomic objects shall be properly initialized before being accessed [MISRAC2012-RULE_9_7-a]
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_7-a.description:
This rule detects cases when atomic objects are used before being properly initialized.

Proper initialization of atomic objects can be done by:
 - initializing objects on declaration
 - using the Standard Library function atomic_init()
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_7-a.security_relevance:
Atomic objects may have an additional state that needs to be initialized. This can be 
done only by initializing objects in declaration or by using the atomic_init() function.
MISRAC2012-RULE_9_7-a.benefits: This rule helps you to properly initialize atomic objects with any additional state.
references: MISRA C:2012 Amendment 4 Updates for ISO/IEC 9899:2011/2018 Phase 3 - Multi-threading and atomics
